collection-of-beauty-rogier-van-der-weyd: The Virgin sits enthroned beneath a Gothic canopy, holding the Christ Child who turns the page of a small book. Painted in oil on panel, the work shows van der Weyden's command of finely modelled drapery and saturated red, set against a flat gold-tooled ground in the manner of Netherlandish devotional images.
collection-of-beauty-weyden-christ-on-th: A T-shaped cross holding the crucified Christ rises before a red cloth of honour and a stone wall, flanked by the white-robed Virgin and Saint John. The reduced palette and stark spatial setting strip away narrative detail, concentrating on grief and bodily presence in a manner unusual for mid-fifteenth-century devotional painting.
collection-of-beauty-hieronymus-bosch-th: When closed, the triptych shows the world on the third day of Creation in grisaille: a translucent globe enclosing a dark, watery earth, with God depicted small in the upper left. The muted tonality contrasts sharply with the saturated colour of the interior panels, framing the work as an unfolding cosmological drama.
collection-of-beauty-sandro-botticelli-m: The enthroned Virgin nurses the Child between Saint John the Baptist and Saint John the Evangelist, set within a vaulted niche dense with lilies, palms and myrtle. Painted for the Bardi chapel in Santo Spirito, Florence, the panel pairs Botticelli's linear elegance with an unusually botanical, symbol-laden setting.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-hare: A young hare crouches alert against a blank ground, its fur built up in fine watercolour and bodycolour strokes that distinguish guard hairs from softer underfur. Among the most exacting nature studies of the Northern Renaissance, the sheet even records the cross-barred reflection of a studio window in the animal's eye.
collection-of-beauty-correggio-ritratto-: A standing woman in dark mourning dress holds a bowl in a wooded landscape, her gaze direct and reserved. Signed with the Latinised "Anton. Laet.", the portrait is sometimes identified as Veronica Gambara and marks Correggio's transition into a more refined, psychologically attentive manner.
collection-of-beauty-patenierchristopher: Saint Christopher wades across a wide river bearing the Christ Child on his shoulder, dwarfed by a panoramic landscape of jagged rocks, distant towns and luminous water. The sweeping high viewpoint is characteristic of Patinir, often credited as the first specialist in Western landscape painting.
collection-of-beauty-venus-and-organist-: Venus reclines nude on a draped bed, her small dog beside her, while an organist turns from his keyboard to gaze at her body. Part of a series Titian produced for European courts, the composition stages a debate between sight and sound as competing routes to the experience of beauty.
collection-of-beauty-venus-and-adonis-by: Venus, seen from behind, twists to embrace the youthful hunter Adonis as he pulls away with his hounds towards the hunt that will kill him. One of the mythological poesie made for Philip II of Spain, the canvas exemplifies Titian's late, freely brushed handling of flesh and landscape.
collection-of-beauty-tizian-danae-receiv: Danaë reclines nude on her bed as Jupiter descends as a shower of gold, while an old nursemaid stretches up an apron to catch the falling coins. Among Titian's poesie for the Spanish court, the scene contrasts youthful flesh and wizened greed in loose, atmospheric paint.
collection-of-beauty-the-triumph-of-deat: An army of skeletons sweeps across a scorched landscape, herding the living towards a vast coffin while gallows, wheels and burning ships fill the horizon. The panel translates the late-medieval danse macabre into a panoramic moral allegory, indebted to Bosch but rendered with Bruegel's denser observation of human types.
collection-of-beauty-canestra-di-frutta-: A wicker basket of grapes, figs, peaches and a withered apple sits at eye level on a bare ledge, projecting slightly into the viewer's space. Often cited as the first autonomous still life in Italian painting, the work is unsparing about wormholes and decay; its plain ground is a later overpaint.
collection-of-beauty-circle-of-pieter-br: Villagers skate and slide on a frozen river beside snow-covered houses, while at right a propped board baited with grain forms a trap for unwary birds. One of around a hundred surviving versions after Pieter Bruegel the Elder's composition, it pairs winter genre with a quiet emblem of human precariousness.
collection-of-beauty-pedro-pablo-rubens-: Susanna twists away from two leering elders who press in from the shadows of her garden bath, her startled body and rumpled drapery filling the canvas. Painted shortly after Rubens's return from Italy, the picture fuses Venetian colour with the muscular Roman figures he had studied there.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-l: Inside a shadowed rocky grotto, Lot's two daughters ply their elderly father with wine, the burning city of Sodom glowing in the distance. The compact, candle-lit composition is typical of Rubens's early Antwerp years, when Caravaggesque tenebrism still inflected his treatment of biblical narrative.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-d: A small child, often identified as Rubens's nephew Philip, looks out from a softly lit ground while gently holding a bird in one hand. The intimate scale and warm flesh tones place the panel within the painter's family portraits, a private counterpart to the grand commissions for which he is better known.
collection-of-beauty-velazquez-el-triunf: A pale, half-nude Bacchus crowns a kneeling soldier with vine leaves while a circle of weather-beaten Spanish drinkers grins out at the viewer. Painted in Madrid before Velázquez's first trip to Italy, the canvas pulls classical mythology down to tavern level with a sober, almost documentary realism.
collection-of-beauty-hercules-lucha-con-: Hercules grapples with the many-headed Hydra of Lerna, his muscular body twisting against the dark serpentine coils. The canvas belongs to a series of ten Labours of Hercules painted for the Hall of Realms in the Buen Retiro Palace, where Zurbarán's stark tenebrism set the hero against an almost theatrical void.
collection-of-beauty-landschaft-mit-kuhe: A pastoral panorama unfolds along a wooded riverbank, with cattle watered in the foreground and duck hunters moving through the reeds beyond. Painted in the artist's final years at his country estate Het Steen, the work belongs to a group of expansive landscapes notable for their loose handling and golden, atmospheric light.
collection-of-beauty-velazquez-de-breda-: Justin of Nassau hands the keys of the Dutch city to the Genoese general Ambrogio Spinola, who lays a conciliatory hand on the defeated commander's shoulder. The forest of upright Spanish pikes that gives the painting its nickname, Las Lanzas, organises the right half of the composition, painted for the Hall of Realms in the Buen Retiro.
collection-of-beauty-landschaft-mit-turm: A small panel showing a fortified tower set on a wooded rise, approached by a slender bridge under the warm light of a lowering sun. The intimate scale and rapid, fluid brushwork are characteristic of the late landscape sketches Rubens made around his estate at Elewijt.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-c: The apostle is shown crucified upside down at his own request, his contorted, foreshortened body filling a tall altarpiece format. Commissioned for St Peter's Church in Cologne, the work was among the artist's last major altarpieces, completed shortly before his death and dispatched to the parish where his father had once lived.
collection-of-beauty-san-jeronimo-leyend: The Church Father is depicted half-length in a rocky grotto, bare-chested and absorbed in his book, a crucifix steadying his left hand. Murillo treats the penitent scholar with restrained naturalism, the warm Sevillian light modelling weathered skin and beard against a deep brown ground.
collection-of-beauty-adoration-of-the-sh: Shepherds press forward to venerate the naked Christ Child held by the Virgin, a lamb bound at the foreground as a foreshadowing of sacrifice. The earthy palette and tender, everyday types reflect Murillo's early debt to Ribera and the Spanish naturalist tradition before his manner softened in later years.
collection-of-beauty-bartolome-esteban-p: Joseph stoops to play with the infant Jesus and a small bird while Mary pauses at her spinning, the goldfinch a traditional emblem of the Passion to come. The domestic warmth and soft chiaroscuro are typical of Murillo's Sevillian devotional pictures, which translated sacred subjects into the rhythms of family life.
collection-of-beauty-mercurius-en-argus-: An engraving after Jordaens showing Mercury raising his sword above the slumbering hundred-eyed giant Argus, with Io transformed into a cow at the left. The print belongs to the wide diffusion of Jordaens' mythologies through reproductive engraving, here cataloged in Hollstein as Dutch 7.
collection-of-beauty-murillo-descanso-en: The Holy Family pauses on its journey into Egypt, Mary cradling the sleeping Christ Child while Joseph watches over them and the donkey grazes nearby. Now in the Hermitage, the picture exemplifies Murillo's mature estilo vaporoso, in which figures emerge from a hazy, golden atmosphere with notably softened contours.
collection-of-beauty-bartolome-esteban-m: The first canonised saint of the Americas kneels in the white and black habit of a Dominican tertiary, gazing on the Christ Child seated on a cushion above her workbasket. Murillo presents the Limeña mystic with the gentle sweetness and silvery half-light that defined his late religious painting.
collection-of-beauty-peinturesmuseefabre: Venus leans toward Vulcan in his forge to plead for arms for her son Aeneas, the Cyclopes labouring at the anvil behind. Submitted as the painter's morceau de réception to the Académie Royale in 1734, the picture is a polished demonstration of the decorative Rococo mythology Natoire would soon deploy at Versailles.
collection-of-beauty-elisabeth-vigee-le-: An allegorical pair: Peace, in classical drapery, gently leads forward Abundance, who carries a sheaf of wheat and a cornucopia. The painting was Vigée Le Brun's reception piece for the Académie Royale, accepted in 1783, and shows her combining Neoclassical subject matter with the warm flesh tones and silken finish of her portrait practice.
collection-of-beauty-the-muscles-of-the-: An anatomical engraving combining a flayed hand after Bernhard Siegfried Albinus with a foot after De Courcelles, the muscles, tendons and bones laid bare for study. Engraved by Robert Bénard for the late eighteenth-century editions of the Encyclopédie, the plate typifies the Enlightenment project of consolidating earlier anatomical illustration into a single compendium.
collection-of-beauty-elisabeth-louise-vi: The actress of the Comédie-Italienne turns toward the viewer in a fur-trimmed coat and broad black hat, a muff held loosely at her side. Vigée Le Brun catches the sitter mid-movement with a fresh, animated brushwork that announces the informal, theatrical portraiture of the late ancien régime.

A double portrait set out of doors, the Marquise de Pezay seated beside the Marquise de Rougé, whose two young sons press in beside her. Painted on the eve of the Revolution, the canvas blends the natural settings and tender maternal grouping that Vigée Le Brun had refined in her portraits of Marie Antoinette and her circle.
collection-of-beauty-wilhelm-alexander-w: A panoramic battle scene of the 1807 siege of Cosel in Silesia, Bavarian troops repulsing a Prussian sortie across an open foreground while the fortress smokes in the distance. Kobell, court painter in Munich, here works in the large-format Schlachtenbild tradition, organising hundreds of small figures within a clear, brightly lit landscape.
collection-of-beauty-suzanna-en-de-ouder: A Neoclassical treatment of the biblical episode in which the chaste Susanna, bathing in her garden, is spied upon by two elders concealed in foliage. The smooth handling of the nude and theatrical contrast of light and shadow reflect the Flemish painter's training in the academic tradition of David.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-rocky-landsc: A study of the Neurathen rock formation in Saxon Switzerland, with weathered sandstone pillars rising against a pale sky. Friedrich drew repeatedly from this region near Dresden, translating direct observation into the meditative, vertical compositions characteristic of his mature Romantic landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-stieler-auguste-str: A half-length portrait of Auguste Strobl, later Auguste Hilber, painted for King Ludwig I of Bavaria's Gallery of Beauties at Nymphenburg. Stieler's polished oil technique, soft modelling and restrained palette typify the Biedermeier court portraiture commissioned for that celebrated cycle of Munich women.
collection-of-beauty-anna-hillmayer-1829: Anna Hillmayer, daughter of a Munich game merchant, sat for this picture as part of Ludwig I's Gallery of Beauties. Stieler departs from aristocratic convention to record a tradesman's daughter with the same idealised serenity and porcelain finish he gave his royal sitters.
collection-of-beauty-keisai-eisen-wasser: A coiling water dragon, or mizuchi, from a late ukiyo-e series devoted to birds, beasts, insects and fish. Eisen, better known for bijin-ga prints of Edo women, here adapts the colour woodblock to a more decorative, naturalist mode in keeping with kacho-e tradition.
collection-of-beauty-stieler-marianna-ma: The Italian noblewoman, translator and longtime confidante of Ludwig I of Bavaria, shown with the soft idealisation reserved for the king's Gallery of Beauties in Munich. Stieler emphasises her dark eyes and intellectual composure, signalling her standing as a scholar rather than mere society sitter.
collection-of-beauty-vincent-van-gogh-18: A nocturnal view of a yellow-awninged café on the Place du Forum in Arles, with cobblestones receding under a sky of large stars. Van Gogh painted it without using black, building the night entirely from saturated yellows, blues and greens — an early experiment in his ambition to render the south at night in pure colour.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-th: Dawn in the Bois de Boulogne: a wounded Pierrot collapses into the arms of the Duc de Guise while a Venetian doge inspects the injury and a black-cloaked Domino reels away. Gérôme's tightly finished, stage-like composition, derived from a version made for the Duc d'Aumale, exemplifies his blend of academic precision and theatrical narrative.
collection-of-beauty-prado-los-desastres: Plate 4 of the Disasters of War, captioned 'The women give courage'. Goya shows female combatants resisting French troops during the Peninsular War, the etching and aquatint biting through the page in his characteristically stark contrasts of black and gnawed white.

Plate 46 of the Disasters of War, 'This is bad'. A friar is set upon amid scattered bodies, the scene drawn with the cramped, shallow space and bitten line that give the series its grim moral force. The plates were withheld until decades after Goya's death.

Plate 47 of the Disasters of War, 'This is how it happened'. A clergyman is dragged and despoiled in the chaos of war, Goya pairing terse caption and unsparing image in the documentary mode that distinguishes the series from any heroic battle tradition.
collection-of-beauty-madame-monet-au-can: Camille Doncieux, the painter's first wife, reclining on a sofa in quiet thought. The intimate domestic subject and muted tonal range belong to Monet's early-1870s figure painting, before his work shifted decisively outdoors toward pure landscape and atmospheric study.
collection-of-beauty-monet-the-thames-at: A view across the Thames toward the Houses of Parliament and Westminster Bridge, dissolved in river mist. Painted during Monet's wartime exile in London, the canvas anticipates by three decades his later Thames series, already setting silhouetted architecture against a pearly, fog-bound sky.
collection-of-beauty-la-debacle-monet-w5: The breakup of ice on the Seine after a hard winter near Vétheuil, with floes drifting past bare riverbanks. Monet returned to this motif repeatedly in 1880, using broken brushwork to register the shifting interplay of water, ice and reflected sky.
collection-of-beauty-gustave-dore-mont-s: A panoramic view in the Vosges of the wooded heights of Mont Sainte-Odile, ringed by the prehistoric drystone enclosure known as the Pagan Wall. Better remembered for his book illustrations, Doré here works at large scale in oil, treating his native Alsace with a dark Romantic grandeur.
collection-of-beauty-gustave-dore-st-joh: Death rides forth as the pale horseman of Revelation, illustrating Saint John's vision in the Apocalypse. The print belongs to Doré's widely circulated Bible illustrations, where dense wood-engraved hatching and theatrical lighting gave Victorian readers their dominant visual image of scripture.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-die-we: A willow standing on the bank of the Epte near Giverny, caught in the pale, cool light of a spring morning. The pastel palette and feathery brushwork are typical of Monet's mid-1880s riverside studies, in which he began to anchor compositions around a single tree as a recurring motif.
collection-of-beauty-les-soleils-jardin-: A dense bed of sunflowers fills the canvas, painted in the artist's own garden at Petit Gennevilliers on the banks of the Seine. The loose handling and saturated yellows reflect a late shift toward Impressionist plein-air practice, after he had largely turned from urban subjects to horticulture.
collection-of-beauty-leve-de-lune-sur-le: From the series One Hundred Aspects of the Moon, the print shows the warlord Cao Cao gazing at the moon rising over Mount Nanping on the eve of the Battle of Red Cliffs. Yoshitoshi distils the Chinese narrative into a spare vertical composition, a hallmark of his late ukiyo-e woodblock work.
collection-of-beauty-2022-06-24-at-13-41: A bare lane leads into the village of Giverny under winter light, with leafless trees and pale fields rendered in muted blues and ochres. Painted shortly after Monet settled in Giverny, the canvas catalogued as Wildenstein 967 belongs to a small group of winter views recording his new surroundings.
collection-of-beauty-vincent-van-gogh-en: A walled wheatfield is shown at sunrise, the sun cresting low above distant hills while curling brushstrokes animate the grain. Painted from the window of his room at the asylum in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence, it is one of a sustained series in which the same enclosed field is observed across seasons and times of day.
collection-of-beauty-lion-tentant-de-sai: A lion stretches from a rocky perch to bat at a butterfly, the towering predator turned momentarily playful. Gérôme treats the animal with the precise anatomical finish of his academic training, while the desert setting reflects the Orientalist subjects he favoured in his late career.
collection-of-beauty-thestarrynightbyvin: A swirling night sky churns over a quiet village dominated by a dark cypress and a slender church spire. Painted from the artist's room at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy, it reworks the view from his window with thick impasto and rhythmic, spiralling brushwork.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-la: Greek warriors in plumed helmets perform the ancient pyrrhic war dance before a seated audience, swords and shields raised in unison. The carefully staged classical reconstruction is typical of Gérôme's late academic manner, in which archaeological detail is mobilised to bring antiquity to vivid life.
collection-of-beauty-aphonse-mucha-rose-: A young woman wreathed in roses stands within a decorative arch, her flowing hair and gown merging with the surrounding blooms. Part of Mucha's Flowers series of four panel decorations, the design exemplifies the Art Nouveau idiom he refined in Paris in the late 1890s.
collection-of-beauty-blauwe-irissen-rp-p: A cluster of blue irises rises against a plain ground in a tall, narrow format suited to the Japanese hashira-e pillar print. Designed by the shin-hanga artist Ohara Koson, the colour woodcut combines the close natural observation of kachō-e bird-and-flower prints with early twentieth-century printmaking technique.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ascomycetes: Plate from Kunstformen der Natur showing a symmetrical arrangement of sac fungi, with cup-shaped fruiting bodies and powdery mildews magnified and framed like ornament. Haeckel's lithographs translated microscopic biology into decorative compositions that fed directly into Art Nouveau design.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-wa: Pale lilies float on a luminous pond crossed by the trailing reflections of weeping willows. The panel forms part of the Grandes Décorations cycle Monet painted at Giverny for the oval rooms of the Musée de l'Orangerie in Paris, where pond, sky and bank dissolve into a continuous surface.
collection-of-beauty-leonardo-da-vinci-v: Saint Anne supports the Virgin on her lap as Mary reaches for the Christ child, who plays with a lamb foreshadowing the Passion. Left unfinished at the artist's death and now in the Louvre, the panel is celebrated for its pyramidal grouping, soft sfumato modelling and atmospheric mountainous landscape.
collection-of-beauty-lowenjagd-peter-pau: Mounted hunters and lions tangle in violent diagonal motion, men and beasts pulled into a single churning mass. This small oil sketch, held by the Alte Pinakothek in Munich, is a preparatory modello for one of Rubens's larger hunt scenes, with the brushwork left openly visible.
collection-of-beauty-rogier-van-der-weyd: Christ on the Cross occupies the central panel, flanked by donor figures and saints in the wings, set against gilded and architectural backgrounds. The triptych shows the precise drapery, restrained gesture and devotional intensity characteristic of fifteenth-century Netherlandish panel painting.
collection-of-beauty-garden-at-bordigher: Painted during Monet's winter trip to the Italian Riviera, this view of a Bordighera garden captures the silvery light he found so disorienting after Normandy. Twisting olives and palms are rendered in feathery, near-pastel strokes, the morning haze softening every contour into vibrating colour.
collection-of-beauty-jupiter-and-antiope: The Haarlem master, better known as a virtuoso engraver, treats the mythological seduction with a sculptural nude and the warm flesh tones he absorbed from his late Italian journey. Jupiter approaches in satyr disguise, the composition borrowed from Northern Mannerist prints Goltzius himself had popularised across Europe.
collection-of-beauty-plage-de-juan-les-p: From Monet's Antibes campaign on the Côte d'Azur, the canvas dissolves pines, sea and Alps into a high-keyed Mediterranean palette of pinks, mauves and turquoise. The artist complained the southern light was too bright to paint, yet returned with dozens of these luminous beach studies.
collection-of-beauty-still-life-with-fru: The Antwerp specialist piles a Baroque larder with grapes, peaches, dead hare and game birds, animated by a thieving monkey, a wary cat and a darting squirrel. Snyders, Rubens's regular still-life collaborator, treats abundance as theatre, a moralising display of plenty under threat.
collection-of-beauty-taj-mahal-by-charle: Made during Bartlett's Asian travels and printed in Tokyo by the publisher Watanabe Shozaburo, the sheet fuses British watercolour atmosphere with Japanese shin-hanga woodblock craft. The marble mausoleum floats above its reflecting pool in flat, graded washes of dusk colour.
collection-of-beauty-the-beach-at-etreta: Monet returned repeatedly to the Normandy coast at Étretat to paint its arched chalk cliffs and the Aiguille needle rising from the surf. Here the wet shingle catches a cold marine light, broken brushwork translating wind, spray and shifting weather into pure sensation.
collection-of-beauty-the-grand-quay-at-h: A working harbour view of Monet's home port, painted the year of the first Impressionist exhibition. Masts, smokestacks and reflections dissolve into rapid, comma-like strokes, the artist treating modern dockside bustle with the same atmospheric urgency he had just brought to Impression, Sunrise.
collection-of-beauty-the-tea-service-oil: An early domestic still life from Monet's Argenteuil years, when he briefly turned from landscape to tabletop subjects. A lacquered tray, porcelain cups and a polished kettle are arranged with Japoniste restraint, the gleaming surfaces reflecting his admiration for Manet and for Edo lacquerware.
collection-of-beauty-winter-night-ekely-: From Munch's late period at Ekely, his estate outside Oslo, where the ageing painter circled the same garden and orchard motifs through every season. Bare trees stand against deep blue snow in raw, scumbled strokes, the loneliness familiar from his earlier symbolist work distilled into pure landscape.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-apullia-i: An early classical landscape by J. M. W. Turner, exhibited at the British Institution and pointedly modelled on Claude Lorrain to challenge the older master's reputation. Sunlit foliage, distant temples and a literary Ovidian subject mark Turner's ambition to inherit the grand European pastoral tradition.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-lake-luce: One of the watercolour studies from Turner's late Swiss tours, when he returned again and again to Lake Lucerne. From the heights above Brunnen the lake opens in pale washes of blue and pearl, mountains dissolving into the atmospheric haze that defined his final manner.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-shipping-: A loose marine sketch from Turner's mature years, when his oils and watercolours of shipping abandoned topographic detail for weather and mood. Hulls and masts emerge from a smear of sea and sky, the kind of working study that fed his later exhibited storm and harbour pictures.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-story-of-: A late mythological landscape by Turner, the Ovidian chase staged across a golden Italianate valley bathed in the swirling light he had brought back from his Roman travels. Figures dwindle beside vast trees and rivers, drama dissolved into atmosphere in the manner of his ageing Claude homages.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-stromy-se: A late Turner storm piece in which a burning ship is almost swallowed by towering surf and smoke. Paint is scrubbed and scraped into vortices of orange and lead grey, the catastrophe rendered as pure elemental force, a hallmark of his final Tate Bequest oils.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-the-lake-: A small oil study made for Turner's Petworth series, painted while he stayed as a guest of the Earl of Egremont. The lake glows under a low sun in flat, almost abstract bands of pink, gold and lavender, anticipating the freedom of his late visionary landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-barcelona-the-new-m: A coastal genre piece whose long, quarrelsome title points to a children's seaside dispute beneath the new moon. Turner places small contesting figures against a vast, luminous estuary, the tiny human anecdote dwarfed by his characteristic sweep of sea and silvery sky.
collection-of-beauty-venice-aristotele-b: The Venetian Romantic Hayez imagines the philosopher in a sober half-length portrait, draped and meditative, painted with the polished historicism that made him the painter of Risorgimento-era Italy. The work hangs in the Gallerie dell'Accademia, the city where Hayez trained.
collection-of-beauty-venice-la-distruzio: Hayez stages the Roman sack of Herod's Temple as operatic Romantic history painting: collapsing columns, fleeing priests and a smoke-darkened sky. The vast canvas, now in the Gallerie dell'Accademia, was conceived as a meditation on tyranny that resonated with Italian audiences under Austrian rule.
collection-of-beauty-01-ichikobu-bridge: Sheet from Hiroshige's late series Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji, showing Edo's Ikkoku Bridge with the sacred peak rising behind the city's tiled rooftops. The print uses the deep cobalt bokashi gradation Hiroshige favoured for sky and water, contrasting urban bustle below with serene distance.
collection-of-beauty-07leucip: A vehement Baroque mythology in which Castor and Pollux carry off the daughters of Leucippus, the rearing horses and twisting nudes locked into Rubens's signature swirling diagonal. Painted in Antwerp at the height of his powers, the picture exemplifies the warm, pearly flesh and muscular drama that defined his manner.
collection-of-beauty-09-the-teahouse-wit: From Hiroshige's Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji, travellers pause at a roadside teahouse while the snow-tipped volcano floats beyond. The graded blue sky and crisp foreground figures show the woodblock master's command of bokashi printing and his eye for the everyday rhythms of Edo travel.
collection-of-beauty-0-entrevue-du-roi-e: Part of Rubens's twenty-four-canvas Marie de' Medici cycle commissioned for the Luxembourg Palace and now in the Louvre. The royal meeting at Lyons is staged as an allegorical pageant, with personifications of the city, putti and a chariot drawn by lions glorifying a politically uneasy marriage.
collection-of-beauty-0-le-sultan-mulay-a: Rubens reworks an earlier portrait by Jan Cornelisz Vermeyen of the Hafsid sultan, lending the Tunisian ruler the broad shoulders, ruddy flesh and lustrous turban he reserved for noble sitters. Such copies after Vermeyen fed Rubens's lifelong fascination with cosmopolitan dress and the wider Mediterranean world.
collection-of-beauty-0-promethee-supplic: A celebrated collaboration in which Rubens painted the writhing Titan and Frans Snyders supplied the savage eagle tearing at his liver. Painted in Antwerp around 1611-12, the canvas is a touchstone of Flemish Baroque cooperation and now anchors the Philadelphia Museum of Art's European collection.
collection-of-beauty-100-views-edo-063: From One Hundred Famous Views of Edo, Hiroshige's late masterwork-series, this Summer sheet looks down from the Suidobashi bridge over the Kanda aqueduct toward the scholar district of Surugadai. A giant carp streamer for Boys' Day floats in the foreground, a hallmark of his daring close-up framing.
collection-of-beauty-100-views-edo-076: An Autumn print from Hiroshige's One Hundred Famous Views of Edo, showing the bamboo yards along the Kanda River where poles were stacked vertically into stark, near-abstract patterns. The diagonal viewpoint and reduced palette typify the formal boldness of his final great series.
collection-of-beauty-10-the-prodigal-son: Dürer's celebrated early engraving sets the parable in a meticulously observed German farmyard, the kneeling youth surrounded by snouting pigs and timber outbuildings. The print, made soon after his first Italian journey, fuses Northern realism with new Renaissance ideas of psychological inwardness.
collection-of-beauty-11-wild-goose-hill-: From Hiroshige's late Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji, the Tone River winds toward the distant snow cone past Wild Goose Hill. Migrating geese punctuate a sky printed in deep bokashi blue, the woodblock gradation that became the artist's signature atmospheric device.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-arcimboldo-w: From Arcimboldo's Four Seasons cycle painted for the Habsburg court, Winter is a gnarled tree-stump head with bark for skin, ivy for hair and a lemon and orange hanging from broken branches. The conceit flattered the emperor as the season that endures and rules over all others.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-de-schovenbi: Painted at the asylum in Saint-Rémy after a Millet print, part of Van Gogh's series translating the older peasant painter into his own thick, rhythmic brushwork. The stooping woman and golden sheaves are reworked in the saturated yellows and ultramarines of his final Provençal year, now in the Van Gogh Museum.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-eugene-ferdi: An Orientalist subject typical of Delacroix's late career, depicting a North African horseman tending to his mount. The painter draws on sketches from his 1832 Moroccan journey, deploying loose, energetic brushwork and a warm, dust-toned palette to evoke movement and Mediterranean light.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-gericault-th: A Romantic head study of a long-haired youth, painted in the years surrounding the Raft of the Medusa. Géricault works in rapid, sculptural brushstrokes against a darkened ground, using strong chiaroscuro to model the face with a directness drawn from his admiration of Caravaggio and the Old Masters.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-gerome-black: A study of an Ottoman irregular soldier, painted after Gérôme's travels through Egypt and the Levant. The figure's embroidered silks and weaponry are rendered with the polished, almost photographic finish that defined French academic Orientalism, set against a neutral studio ground that isolates costume and pose.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-giuseppe-arc: A late composite portrait fusing all four seasons into a single weathered face, with autumnal fruit, withered branches and bare wood standing in for flesh and hair. Arcimboldo, court painter to the Habsburgs in Prague and Vienna, treats the head as both Mannerist puzzle and emblem of time's passage.

Spring personified as a profile bust assembled entirely from blossoms, leaves and budding stems. Painted for the Habsburg court, it belongs to Arcimboldo's celebrated Four Seasons cycle, where Mannerist wit and botanical precision combine to flatter the emperor as ruler over a harmonious natural order.

Summer rendered as a profile head built from ripe produce — peach cheeks, a cucumber nose, garlic teeth, with a straw collar inscribed with the artist's signature. Part of the Four Seasons series for Maximilian II, it pairs Mannerist invention with sharp still-life observation.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-gustave-cail: Painted in the family garden at Yerres, the picture shows the artist's brother Martial reading beneath potted orange trees while a woman strolls in dappled sun. Caillebotte combines Impressionist light with the steep, photographically cropped perspective that distinguishes his contribution to the movement.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-ingres-mme-v: A graphite portrait drawing of Madame Baltard with her young daughter, wife of the architect of Les Halles. Ingres lavishes precise, wiry contour on the heads while leaving the dresses summarily indicated, in keeping with the Neoclassical portrait drawings he produced throughout his Roman and Parisian careers.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-irissen-s005: Painted at Saint-Rémy in the final year of his life, the canvas crops in tightly on a bed of irises set against a pale background. Van Gogh organises the scene as a near-flat tapestry of curling green leaves and violet petals, the heavy impasto and decorative outlines reflecting his interest in Japanese prints.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-jean-leon-ge: An ancient-history scene staging a public auction in a Roman forum, with toga-clad bidders inspecting an enslaved woman on a raised block. Gérôme applies the same archaeological precision and enamelled finish he used for his Orientalist works, here turning a chilly academic eye on classical antiquity.

An allegory of Truth, nude and enraged, climbing from the well into which liars have cast her — painted at the height of the Dreyfus Affair and widely read as Gérôme's bitter comment on public deceit. The polished academic surface gives the mythic figure unsettling photographic immediacy.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-john-constab: A panoramic view down the Stour Valley toward Dedham church and the Suffolk coast, the heart of Constable's native country. Painted late in his life, the picture distils decades of plein-air study into a richly worked oil, with broken touches of white — his signature "snow" — flickering across foliage and sky.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-nihon-fukei-: A view of Uchisange in Okayama, issued in Hasui's series Selected Views of Japan. Working in the shin-hanga revival of ukiyo-e, he uses graded bokashi printing and a quiet, twilight palette to capture a fleeting weather effect over the streetscape.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-peter-paul-r: A nocturnal genre piece in which an elderly woman shields a single flame as a boy lights his candle from hers. Rubens borrows the candlelit motif from Northern tradition and from his Italian study of Caravaggio, modelling the warm, ruddy flesh in fluid Baroque brushwork.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-pierre-puvis: Three pensive nymphs are arranged in a frieze along a pale shore, an Arcadian reverie rather than a specific place. Puvis de Chavannes paints in the chalky, fresco-like matte tonality that made him a touchstone for Symbolists and for the young Picasso and Seurat.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-retrato-de-l: A family portrait, traditionally identified as Holbein's wife Elsbeth with their two eldest children, painted soon after his return to Basel from England. The careworn faces are recorded with Northern Renaissance candour; the panel was later cut down and mounted, accounting for its arched silhouette.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-self-portrai: Painted at the asylum of Saint-Rémy weeks after a severe breakdown, the artist faces the viewer in a green coat against a swirling pale-blue ground. The looping, vibrating brushstrokes that animate background and clothing alike are characteristic of the self-portraits with which Van Gogh tracked his own state of mind.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-suzuki-harun: A young woman beside a kotatsu, part of Harunobu's Eight Parlour Views series that transposes classical "Eight Views" landscape themes into intimate domestic scenes. The cotton padding draped over the heater is rendered in karazuri blind embossing — an unprinted relief technique that mimics the bulk of fresh snow.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-the-chess-ga: Two men hunched over a chessboard in a tiled Cairene interior, a favourite subject of the Austrian Orientalist Ludwig Deutsch. The picture displays his trademark microscopic finish, with carpets, brass trays and mashrabiya screens recorded in jewel-like detail under cool, even daylight.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-theodore-ger: A monumental Roman aqueduct strides across an imagined Italianate landscape at dusk, with bathers and travellers dwarfed by its arches. Painted for a Parisian patron's hôtel particulier, the picture shows Géricault adopting the heroic, Poussin-derived classical landscape mode at the threshold of Romanticism.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-thomas-gains: An early Suffolk-period portrait of a young sitter, painted while Gainsborough was working in the manner of Hayman and the French rococo printmakers. The lightly built features and silvery palette already foreshadow the feathery brushwork of his mature society portraits.

Four children of the Marsham family arranged in a wooded park, gathering nuts. Painted at the end of Gainsborough's career, it pairs the easy informality of his "fancy pictures" with the feathery, broken brushwork and silvery greens that define his late landscape backgrounds.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-van-gogh-das: A view of a suburban café-restaurant on the banks of the Seine, painted during Van Gogh's Paris years when he frequented the riverside towns with Bernard and Signac. The lightened palette and short, divisionist-inflected strokes register his rapid absorption of Impressionist and Neo-Impressionist colour theory.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-van-gogh-gar: A view into the walled garden of the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum, where Van Gogh was a voluntary patient between 1889 and 1890. A solitary figure passes between trees rendered in writhing, rope-like strokes; the picture is one of many in which the cloister grounds become both subject and consolation.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-van-gogh-wei: Painted from a vantage above Arles in the productive summer of 1888, the canvas reads as a tapestry of yellow stubble, ripening crops and red roofs gathered around the town's spires. Van Gogh works in dense, directional brushstrokes that translate Provençal heat into structured pattern.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-vincent-van-: A small, mordant memento mori produced during Van Gogh's studies at the Antwerp academy, where students were drilled in anatomy from articulated skeletons. The flippant cigarette has been read as a sardonic jab at academic propriety as much as a meditation on mortality.

One of several portraits Van Gogh painted of his Arles friend Joseph Roulin, sorter at the local railway post office. The blue uniform and gilt-buttoned coat are flattened against a plain ground, the heavily impastoed beard and ruddy cheeks fixed with the bold contour of a print.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-vittore-carp: Venetian patricians shoot at cormorants from flat-bottomed boats on the still waters of the lagoon. Originally the lower half of a larger panel — its upper half is the Two Venetian Ladies in the Museo Correr — Carpaccio renders the scene in the crystalline Quattrocento detail typical of his narrative cycles.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-wilhelm-von-: Two riders pause on a height above the Tegernsee, the Bavarian Alps shimmering in clear morning air. Kobell, court painter in Munich, works in the crisp, glassy Biedermeier manner for which he is known, isolating sharply lit figures against a luminous, almost enamelled landscape.
collection-of-beauty-1280px-zelfportret-: Painted in Paris, the artist confronts the viewer at his easel with palette and brushes in hand, dressed in a workman's blue smock. The dense, divisionist touches of complementary colour absorbed from Seurat and Signac mark a decisive break from the dark Dutch palette of his earlier self-portraits.

Painted during Van Gogh's Paris years, this self-portrait shows the artist absorbing Impressionist and Pointillist colour theory. Short, hatched strokes of complementary blue and orange radiate from his head, turning the felt hat into a halo of pure pigment. Now in the Van Gogh Museum, Amsterdam.
collection-of-beauty-1529-cranach-allego: A programmatic Lutheran image divided down the middle: on the left, Death and the Devil drive a sinner toward Hell beneath the Tablets of the Law; on the right, John the Baptist directs the same figure to the crucified Christ. Cranach worked closely with Luther in Wittenberg, and this composition became a template for Reformation visual theology.
collection-of-beauty-1596-caravaggio-the: An early half-length picture from Caravaggio's time in the household of Cardinal del Monte, showing an androgynous youth absorbed in playing a lute. The still life of fruit, flowers and a violin is rendered with the same forensic light that became the artist's signature, the figure pulled forward against a dark wall. The New York version is in the Metropolitan Museum.
collection-of-beauty-15-qian-xuan-eight-: A Yuan-dynasty handscroll in ink and light colour on paper, parading eight flowering specimens across a blank silk-pale ground. Qian Xuan, a Song loyalist who refused to serve the Mongol court, cultivated a deliberately archaic boneless manner, letting outline and tinted wash carry the botanical likeness. Held in the Palace Museum, Beijing.
collection-of-beauty-1627-rubens-maria-m: A Nursing Madonna produced through the Antwerp collaboration of Rubens and Jan Brueghel the Younger, with Rubens supplying the warm, fleshy figures and Brueghel the encircling garland of finely observed flowers. Such joint devotional pictures were a speciality of the Antwerp workshops, marrying Catholic Counter-Reformation piety to Flemish virtuoso still life.
collection-of-beauty-1665-girl-with-a-pe: A tronie rather than a portrait: an anonymous young woman turns over her shoulder, lips parted, against a deep glazed-black ground. Vermeer reserves his most expensive pigments, ultramarine and lead-tin yellow, for the turban, and renders the famous teardrop earring with two soft dabs of lead white. In the Mauritshuis, The Hague.
collection-of-beauty-17-the-sea-off-the-: From Hiroshige's late Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji series, published shortly before his death. A foreground of dark, choppy water frames the distant cone of Fuji across the bay; the woodblock printer's bokashi gradients soften sky and sea into bands of graded blue.
collection-of-beauty-1813-schinkel-blick: Better remembered as Berlin's neoclassical architect, Schinkel was also a Romantic landscape painter. Here a high Alpine valley with a humble farmstead is dwarfed by the snow mass of Mont Blanc, the staffage and outbuildings supplying scale for the sublime geology beyond.
collection-of-beauty-1815-schinkel-mitte: An imagined medieval city rises in tiers of Gothic spires above a river, lit by a low theatrical sun. Schinkel uses architecture as nationalist allegory in the wake of the Napoleonic wars, the cathedral silhouette standing in for a recovered German past.
collection-of-beauty-1836-heimkehr-des-p: A Berlin Biedermeier scene in Philhellenist key: a palikar, one of the irregular fighters of the Greek War of Independence, returns to his family. Magnus, a portraitist trained under Karl Begas, treats the costume and embroidery with ethnographic precision while keeping the emotional register restrained.
collection-of-beauty-1859-menzel-student: Menzel records a Berlin student torchlight procession with his characteristic reportorial eye, the crowd compacted into a flickering frieze of faces and smoking brands. He was the great chronicler of Prussian public life, equally at ease with court ceremonies and street gatherings.
collection-of-beauty-1866-gustave-courbe: A reclining nude exhibited at the Salon of 1866, where Courbet's frank naturalism, tousled hair and unidealised flesh, scandalised critics expecting an academic Venus. The bird perched on her outstretched hand provides the slim mythological pretext. Now in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
collection-of-beauty-1869-courbet-die-wo: One of a series of stormy seascapes Courbet painted at Étretat in 1869, the breaking wave thickly built up with the palette knife. He treated water as solid matter, layering grey-green pigment against a leaden sky to register the weight rather than the glitter of the Channel.
collection-of-beauty-1871-vereshchagin-a: A pyramid of human skulls rises on a scorched plain before a ruined city, crows wheeling overhead. Vereshchagin, a veteran of the Russian campaigns in Turkestan, dedicated the canvas "to all conquerors, past, present and to come"; it is the closing image of his Turkestan series.
collection-of-beauty-1872-vereshchagin-p: Part of the Turkestan series, the painting shows the Emir of Bukhara receiving a heap of severed Russian heads as battle trophies in a tiled courtyard. Vereshchagin's archaeological precision in the architecture sharpens, rather than softens, the indictment of war.
collection-of-beauty-1872-vereshchagin-t: Set in Samarkand's Registan square, a victory ceremony unfolds before the great madrasa portals while Russian soldiers' heads stand impaled on poles in the foreground. Vereshchagin pairs the splendour of Timurid tilework with documentary atrocity, a recurring strategy of his Turkestan cycle.
collection-of-beauty-1873-monet-houses-a: Painted in the Paris suburb where Monet lived from 1871 to 1878, the heart of the early Impressionist project. A row of villas behind a flowering garden is rendered in broken touches of green, rose and violet, light flickering across stucco walls.
collection-of-beauty-1878-vereshchagin-s: From Vereshchagin's Balkan series, prompted by his service as an aide to General Skobelev in the Russo-Turkish War of 1877-78. The Shipka Pass, scene of brutal winter fighting, is shown after the battle as a thin scatter of corpses under a white sky.
collection-of-beauty-1887-renoir-nu-dans: A small late-1880s nude from Renoir's so-called "manière aigre", when he tightened his contours under the influence of Ingres and Italian Renaissance drawing. The pearly figure is set against a lightly brushed landscape, oil on canvas, now on long-term loan from the Pearlman Foundation to the Princeton University Art Museum.
collection-of-beauty-18-the-sagami-river: Plate eighteen of Hiroshige's late Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, with the Sagami River winding through Kanagawa Province toward the snow-capped peak. The vertical ōban format compresses foreground rapids and distant cone into a single, telescoped view.
collection-of-beauty-1902-monet-die-wate: From Monet's London series of 1899-1904, painted from his rooms at the Savoy Hotel overlooking the Thames. Waterloo Bridge dissolves into the famous "effet de brouillard", its iron arches reduced to violet silhouettes against a sulphurous sky thickened by coal smoke.
collection-of-beauty-1993-151-1-pma2018: A Monet of 1888, catalogued as Wildenstein 1191 and now in the Philadelphia Museum of Art. By this date the artist was working in extended series, breaking form into mosaics of high-keyed colour to record a single motif under shifting light.
collection-of-beauty-19-the-seven-ri-bea: Plate nineteen of the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, depicting Shichirigahama, the seven-ri stretch of coast in Sagami Province. Enoshima crouches on the horizon while Fuji floats beyond, the printer using bokashi gradient to fade sea into sky.
collection-of-beauty-2022-06-24-at-13-35: A small still life of peaches in a glass jar, catalogued as Wildenstein 952. Monet rarely worked in this format by the mid-1880s, but the close-keyed orange and pink tones and feathery brushwork align it with his contemporary Giverny garden pictures.
collection-of-beauty-20-the-entrance-gat: Plate twenty of Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, showing pilgrims passing through the bronze torii at Enoshima with Fuji floating across Sagami Bay. The composition is built from flat overlapping silhouettes, a hallmark of late-Edo ukiyo-e landscape design.
collection-of-beauty-21-lake-at-hakone: Plate twenty-one of the Thirty-six Views, with the dark glassy surface of Lake Ashi at Hakone reflecting Mount Fuji and the surrounding peaks. Hiroshige was a master of these mirror-symmetry compositions, splitting the sheet between solid landscape and its watery double.
collection-of-beauty-23-the-sea-off-satt: Plate twenty-three of the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. Big stylised waves curl in the foreground off Satta Point on the Tōkaidō road, with Fuji rising serenely behind, an answer to Hokusai's earlier Great Wave composition.
collection-of-beauty-24-the-pine-forest-: From Hiroshige's late Mount Fuji series, the spit of pines at Miho-no-Matsubara curls into Suruga Bay with Fuji on the far shore. The site is bound up with the noh play Hagoromo, and Hiroshige uses bokashi blues and the dense black of pine needles to set its quiet mood.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-bonaparte-an: Subtitled "Œdipe", the picture shows Napoleon on horseback halted before the half-buried Sphinx during the 1798 Egyptian campaign. Gérôme, the leading Orientalist of the Paris Salon, treats sand, granite and uniform with archaeological exactitude, casting the encounter as one civilisational riddle posed to another.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-courbet-late: A monumental allegory of seven years of Courbet's artistic life, gathering critics, patrons, and anonymous figures around the painter at his easel, with a nude muse and small boy looking on. Rejected from the 1855 Exposition Universelle, it became the centrepiece of his self-organised Pavilion of Realism in Paris.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-de-heuvel-va: Painted shortly after the artist's arrival in Paris, this view of the working windmills and gypsum quarries on Montmartre still uses the muted, earth-toned palette he carried over from his Dutch period. The brushwork is loose and matter-of-fact, predating his encounter with Impressionist colour.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-edgar-germai: A pastel of a woman bathing in a shallow tub, viewed from a steep, almost overhead angle drawn from Japanese prints. Part of Degas's bather series shown at the eighth and final Impressionist exhibition in 1886, where critics remarked on the candid, keyhole intimacy of the pose.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-gustave-cour: A small landscape of the Roman bridge over the Vidourle in Languedoc, oil on paper later mounted on panel. Courbet builds the stonework and riverbank with the heavy palette knife and earthy tonal range that defined his realist treatment of provincial French scenery.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-hiroshi-yosh: A woodblock print of cherry blossoms at Kumoi, made in the shin-hanga revival style that Yoshida helped lead. He personally supervised the carvers and printers, layering many impressions of subtly graded pink to capture diffuse spring light.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-hiroshige24-: Shimada Station from Hiroshige's celebrated series The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō, showing travellers being carried across the unbridged Ōi River. The print is reproduced from a later miniature edition by the Takamizawa workshop in Tokyo.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-hiroshige-fi: Descending Geese at Katata, from the Eight Views of Ōmi, depicting boats and a flock settling onto Lake Biwa at dusk. Hiroshige distils the classical Chinese-derived theme into broad bands of graded indigo bokashi, a printing technique that became his signature.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-jean-leon-ge: A late landscape recalling the village of Achères near the forest of Saint-Germain, painted in the final year of Gérôme's life. The academic precision of his Orientalist canvases is here loosened into a quieter, more atmospheric record of the Île-de-France countryside.

A reconstruction of a race in the Circus Maximus, with quadrigas thundering past the spina under the gaze of a packed Roman crowd. Gérôme drew on archaeological sources to render every harness, obelisk, and statue with the meticulous polish that made him a star of Salon history painting.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-jean-louis-t: Four thoroughbreds stretched into the rocking-horse 'flying gallop' pose then conventional in sporting art, set against a low storm-bruised English sky. Géricault painted it in London, where his fascination with horseflesh and equestrian sport reshaped his late Romantic style.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-korenveld-on: A double-square canvas painted at Auvers-sur-Oise in the last weeks of the artist's life, the wheat below worked in horizontal strokes and the heavy clouds above in slate and lilac scumbles. It belongs to a small group of panoramic Auvers wheatfields that pushed his format toward the cinematic.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-ndl-dc-25865: Sun setting over the rooftops of Ichinokura at Ikegami, from Hasui's series Twenty Views of Tōkyō. The shin-hanga print uses long horizontal bands of graded orange and indigo to fix a single, melancholic moment of urban dusk.

A wet street in the Akashi-chō district after rain, lamplight pooling on the pavement. Hasui, working with the publisher Watanabe Shōzaburō, was the master of these reflective night and weather scenes that defined the shin-hanga revival of Japanese woodblock printing.

A cliff-bound view of Ryūga Island off the Oga Peninsula in Akita, from Hasui's third Souvenirs of Travel set. Pine-fringed rocks rise from a deep cobalt sea, printed with the multiple bokashi gradations that gave shin-hanga its photographic depth.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-nihon-fukei-: An early Hasui woodblock from Selected Views of Japan, showing thatched roofs at Kazusa village in former Hizen Province on the Kyūshū coast. The print already shows the publisher Watanabe's house style: precise architecture, soft skies, and water rendered in layered indigo wash.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-ondergesneeu: A bleak field at Auvers with a discarded harrow under fresh snow, freely after a black-and-white print by Jean-François Millet. Van Gogh translated Millet's earthbound peasant subjects into his own thicker, colour-saturated impasto throughout his last year.

A bleak field at Auvers with a discarded harrow under fresh snow, freely after a black-and-white print by Jean-François Millet. Van Gogh translated Millet's earthbound peasant subjects into his own thicker, colour-saturated impasto throughout his last year.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-pieter-brueg: Returning hunters and their lean dogs trudge across a snowy ridge above frozen ponds where villagers skate, the panel that anchors Bruegel's celebrated cycle of the months commissioned by the Antwerp merchant Niclaes Jonghelinck. Now in the Kunsthistorisches Museum in Vienna, it set the template for European winter landscape painting.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-saint-franci: The saint swoons into the arms of an angel after receiving the stigmata on Mount La Verna, a nocturnal vision lit by a single raking source. It is generally taken to be Caravaggio's earliest religious commission of consequence, painted for his patron Cardinal Francesco Maria del Monte in Rome.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-saint-jerome: The aged hermit-scholar bends over his Vulgate translation, his bald head and the skull on the table almost mirrored across the strong diagonal of his outstretched arm. Painted in Rome for Cardinal Scipione Borghese, the work is now in the Galleria Borghese.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-the-big-wave: A page from Hokusai's later, more contemplative woodblock series One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, in which a curling wave frames the distant sacred peak. Printed in monochrome ink with discreet grey gradations, it reprises in book form the motif of his earlier Great Wave.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-the-langlois: A small Provençal drawbridge over the Arles canal, with washerwomen at the bank and a horse-drawn cart crossing above. The subject obsessed Van Gogh in spring 1888, who returned to it in several oils, watercolours, and drawings for its echo of the Dutch canals of his childhood.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-the-taking-o: Judas's kiss seals Christ's arrest as armoured soldiers surge in from the right, their polished steel catching the lantern held by a self-portrait of the painter at the edge of the scene. Lost for nearly two centuries, the canvas was rediscovered in a Jesuit residence in Dublin in 1990 and is now on long-term loan to the National Gallery of Ireland.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-van-dyck-sir: A posthumous portrait of Venetia Stanley, Lady Digby, propped on her pillows in the dawn following her sudden death in 1633. Commissioned by the grieving Sir Kenelm Digby and now at Dulwich Picture Gallery, the picture is unusually intimate among Van Dyck's English court portraits.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-van-gogh-ack: A Provençal field worked by oxen and ploughman, painted while Van Gogh was a voluntary patient at the asylum of Saint-Paul-de-Mausole near Saint-Rémy. The furrows roll across the canvas in his characteristic ribbon-like impasto, an homage to the peasant labour Millet had pictured a generation earlier.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-van-gogh-the: Gnarled olives churn in the foreground while the limestone Alpilles rise behind under a clouded sky, painted from the grounds of Saint-Paul asylum. Now at MoMA, it belongs to the group of about fifteen olive-grove canvases Van Gogh devoted to the trees during his Saint-Rémy year.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-vincent-van-: A still life of grapes, lemons, pears, and apples banked across the table, painted in Paris under the influence of Adolphe Monticelli's heavy paint and the Impressionists' fruit studies. Now at the Art Institute of Chicago, it shows Van Gogh testing a brighter, complementary-colour palette.

A thatched cottage screened by trees in Auvers-sur-Oise, where Van Gogh spent his last seventy days under the care of Dr Paul Gachet. The brisk diagonal strokes and pale, cool palette belong to the prolific summer 1890 burst that produced more than seventy canvases.

Two harvesters nap in the shade of a haystack at noon, freely copied from a Millet print Van Gogh kept in his Saint-Rémy studio. He translated Millet's grey original into a pairing of complementary blue shadows and golden yellow stubble.

A walled vineyard outside Auvers descends toward red-roofed cottages and the church tower beyond. Painted weeks before Van Gogh's death, the canvas balances his late, agitated brushwork with an unusually orderly Northern French motif.

Beached fishing smacks on the Mediterranean sand at Les Saintes-Maries-de-la-Mer, their masts and rigging picked out in clean primary colour. Made on a five-day excursion from Arles in June 1888, the picture marks Van Gogh's first encounter with the sea in the South of France.

A reaper bends to his work amid sun-bleached fields under a churning sky, painted in the final months of Van Gogh's life at Auvers-sur-Oise. Thick parallel strokes of yellow and ochre carry the artist's recurring theme of the harvester as a figure of mortality and quiet acceptance.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-william-holm: A goat staggers across the salt flats of the Dead Sea, its horns bound with the red wool of the Yom Kippur ritual. Hunt painted the scene on site in 1854, hauling his easel to the shore of Oosdoom in pursuit of the Pre-Raphaelite ideal of truth to nature, sharpened by Christian symbolism.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-thumbnail: An unattributed image from the collection. Without a reliable title, artist, or date, no descriptive label can be drawn from the historical record.
collection-of-beauty-2560px-finches-and-: Two finches perch on a slender bamboo branch, rendered in delicate ink and colour on silk. Attributed to the artist-emperor Huizong, the painting exemplifies the Northern Song court's pursuit of meticulous bird-and-flower observation, where each feather and leaf vein is individually described.
collection-of-beauty-25-fuji-on-the-left: Travellers move along the Tokaido highway as Mount Fuji rises to the left, a print from Hiroshige's posthumously published Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. The composition layers near figures against a flattened distance, exploiting the woodblock medium's graded skies and crisp silhouettes.
collection-of-beauty-26-the-oi-river-bet: Porters ford the broad shallows of the Oi River, with Fuji's cone framed beyond. Hiroshige drew the design for the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji issued by Tsutaya Kichizo, deploying the bokashi gradient technique to suggest mist rising off the water.
collection-of-beauty-31-the-otsuki-plain: Travellers cross the open plain of Otsuki with Fuji set against a pale sky, plate 31 of the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. Hiroshige favoured such low horizons to let the mountain dominate, balanced by the human-scale activity of road and field.
collection-of-beauty-32-dog-eye-pass: Hiroshige's view from Inume Pass in Kai Province looks down on a sea of cloud broken by Fuji's summit, plate 32 of his Thirty-six Views series. The composition compresses foreground rocks and distant peak into a single graphic plane, a hallmark of late-Edo landscape design.
collection-of-beauty-33-kogane-plain: A wide view across the Kogane plain in Shimosa, with Fuji blue on the horizon and grasses sketched in the foreground. From Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, the print uses the bokashi gradation to soften sky and distance.
collection-of-beauty-3415-maria-magdalen: The Magdalene rests her cheek on her hand in the conventional pose of melancholia, jar of ointment beside her. Painted during Gentileschi's Roman years, the work fuses Caravaggesque chiaroscuro with the artist's signature interest in solitary, introspective female protagonists.
collection-of-beauty-34-kuroto-bay: Boats drift in Kuroto Bay with Fuji visible across the water, plate 34 of Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. The flat blue expanses and economical drawing of sails reflect the woodblock vocabulary that would soon enthral European Japonisme.
collection-of-beauty-35390682010-4609909: A landscape catalogued as Wildenstein 1449 in Monet's oeuvre. The artist's standard practice in such canvases is to dissolve form into broken touches of complementary colour, recording transient light with a saturated, high-keyed palette.
collection-of-beauty-36-the-hoda-coast: The closing print of Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji shows the rocky Hoda coast in Awa Province, Fuji floating across Edo Bay. Crisp keyblock outlines and indigo seas give the design the graphic clarity that defined his late style.
collection-of-beauty-4344-vis-print-1476: An oil sketch on oak panel of an elderly woman, made as preparatory study for figures populating Rubens's larger religious and mythological compositions. The loose, economical handling typifies the head-studies the Antwerp workshop produced from life models.
collection-of-beauty-500px-gipsen-vrouwe: A plaster cast of a female torso set against a warm ground, one of a series of academic still-lifes Van Gogh painted in Paris while studying at Cormon's atelier. The handling shows him working through colour theory on a deliberately neutral subject.
collection-of-beauty-500px-kuniyoshi-uta: A hawk poised in profile, its plumage detailed in the layered keyblock and colour blocks of an ukiyo-e print. Hawks were a favoured subject of late Edo print designers, the bird's predatory stillness suiting the medium's graphic economy.

A tiger paces against a wind-blown ground, its striped coat picked out in dense keyblock lines. Kuniyoshi, never having seen a live tiger, drew on imported Chinese painting models and his own taste for muscular animal subjects in the warrior-print tradition.
collection-of-beauty-960px-03-sukiyagahs: The Sukiyagashi district of Edo with Fuji rising beyond rooftops, plate three of Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. The print balances the dense urban foreground against the calm geometric mountain that recurs throughout the series.
collection-of-beauty-960px-13-the-tama-r: The Tama River flows beneath a pale Fuji in this thirteenth design from Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views. Rafts and figures animate the middle distance, while bokashi gradients carry the eye from green water to blue mountain.
collection-of-beauty-960px-evening-snow-: Snow settles on the eaves of Terajima village at dusk, a print from Hasui's revival of landscape woodblock under the shin-hanga movement. Hasui worked closely with publisher Watanabe Shozaburo, prizing atmospheric weather and quiet rural corners over the bustle of the old Edo school.
collection-of-beauty-960px-fox-hunt-1893: A red fox flounders through deep snow as a flock of crows descends, the predator suddenly become prey. Painted at Prouts Neck on the Maine coast, this is the largest canvas of Homer's late career, its compressed Japonist composition and bleak palette signalling the harshness of the northern winter.
collection-of-beauty-960px-hanagami-danj: The warrior Hanagami Danjo grapples with a colossal salamander in the Tonta River of Izumo, a scene of legendary heroism. Kuniyoshi delights in muscular anatomy and reptilian texture, characteristic of the musha-e warrior prints that made his reputation.
collection-of-beauty-960px-hiroshige-fut: The wedded rocks of Meoto Iwa stand off the Futami shore, joined by the sacred shimenawa rope, with Fuji visible across the bay. Plate twenty-seven of the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, the design pairs Shinto pilgrimage iconography with Hiroshige's signature distant cone.
collection-of-beauty-960px-hiroshige-sun: Red maples frame a view of Fuji from the slopes of Meguro at sunset, plate ten of Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views. Printed by Tsutaya Kichizo, the design uses heavy bokashi in the sky to evoke the dying autumn light.
collection-of-beauty-960px-hiroshige-ii-: The Kintai Bridge arches in five wooden spans over the Nishiki at Iwakuni, a celebrated feat of Edo carpentry. The print belongs to Hiroshige II's One Hundred Famous Views of the Provinces, continuing the meisho tradition his teacher had popularised.
collection-of-beauty-960px-hiroshige-mt-: From the Misaka Pass in Kai Province, Fuji is doubled by its reflection in Lake Kawaguchi below. Hiroshige's Thirty-six Views design exploits the symmetry as a graphic device, layering pine-clad slopes against the still water.
collection-of-beauty-960px-jean-leon-ger: Napoleon on horseback before the Sphinx and pyramids, a meditation on power and antiquity tied to the 1798 Egyptian campaign. Gerome's polished academic finish and ethnographic eye made him the leading French Orientalist of the Second Empire.
collection-of-beauty-960px-lofty-mt-lu-b: Towering peaks of Mount Lu rise in vertically stacked planes of ink, a hanging scroll painted by Shen Zhou as a birthday tribute to his teacher Chen Kuan. A founder of the Wu School of Ming literati painting, Shen here pays homage to the Yuan master Wang Meng's dense, crinkled brushwork.
collection-of-beauty-960px-museo-del-pra: An artist slumps over his desk as bats and owls swarm from the dark behind him, plate forty-three of Goya's Caprichos. The etching with aquatint, issued in Madrid in 1799, became the central emblem of the series' attack on Spanish superstition and unreason.
collection-of-beauty-960px-pieter-bruege: Hunters and their hounds trudge home over a snowbound ridge, looking down on skaters scattered across frozen ponds. The winter panel of Bruegel's series of months, commissioned by the Antwerp merchant Niclaes Jonghelinck, hangs today in Vienna's Kunsthistorisches Museum.
collection-of-beauty-960px-robert-delaun: Delaunay returned to the Eiffel Tower repeatedly across his career, fragmenting its iron lattice into prismatic planes of colour. This late oil sets the monument against tilting Parisian rooftops, channelling the Orphic Cubism he developed with Sonia Delaunay into a near-abstract hymn to modern engineering.
collection-of-beauty-960px-sesshutoyo: An autumn landscape in the haboku (splashed-ink) idiom that Sesshū absorbed during his 1467 study trip to Ming China. Sparse, axe-cut brushwork conjures rocky cliffs and a lone traveller, leaving wide expanses of paper to read as mist — a hallmark of Muromachi suiboku ink painting.
collection-of-beauty-960px-tokyo-junidai: From the series Twelve Scenes of Tokyo, Hasui frames the temple steps of Atagoyama beneath blossoming cherry. A leading figure of the shin-hanga revival, he pairs traditional woodblock craftsmanship with Western perspective and a cool, atmospheric palette of dusk blues.
collection-of-beauty-960px-van-gogh-le-m: Painted during Van Gogh's Paris years, when he lodged with brother Theo in Montmartre and absorbed Impressionist colour. The windmill of the Moulin de la Galette, a dance-hall landmark also painted by Renoir and Toulouse-Lautrec, appears here in muted earth tones still tied to his earlier Dutch palette.
collection-of-beauty-a-friendly-call: Chase records a sunlit afternoon visit in his Shinnecock summer studio, the parlour a parade of mirrors, framed prints, and white-painted wicker. A leading American Impressionist and influential teacher, he handles light and reflective surfaces with bravura brushwork indebted to Sargent and Whistler.
collection-of-beauty-a-hippopotamus-and-: One of four exotic hunts Rubens painted around 1615 for Maximilian I of Bavaria, blending Flemish pictorial drama with antique pathos. Spear-wielding riders, snarling hounds, and writhing reptiles crash together in a swirling diagonal composition that became a touchstone for Baroque action painting.
collection-of-beauty-a-parisian-lady-edo: A near-life-size portrait of the actress Ellen Andrée in fashionable street dress, her black silhouette set against a flat ground in Manet's signature manner. The picture pursues the figure of the modern Parisienne championed by Baudelaire rather than a sitter's likeness.
collection-of-beauty-a-sunday-on-la-gran: Seurat's manifesto of Neo-Impressionism, built over two years from countless oil sketches and conté studies. Bourgeois Parisians promenade along the Seine in frieze-like stillness, their forms assembled from tiny dots of contrasting pigment — the optical method he called divisionism.
collection-of-beauty-a-view-of-the-archb: Submitted to the Royal Academy in April 1790, the month Turner turned fifteen, this was his exhibition debut. The watercolour shows a topographical precision learned from Thomas Malton, the architecture set at sharp angles to flaunt the teenager's grasp of perspective.
collection-of-beauty-a-colored-version-o: A reworked echo of the famous Kanagawa wave, drawn from the later One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji. Hokusai mirrors the composition, removes the fishing boats, and turns the spume into a flock of birds — a meditation on transformation rendered in his late, more graphic woodblock manner.
collection-of-beauty-a-female-martyr-sai: A half-length saint, traditionally identified as Catherine of Alexandria by the spiked wheel of her martyrdom. Gentileschi favoured strong-willed female protagonists set against deep shadow, building flesh in luminous Caravaggesque chiaroscuro across a career that spanned Rome, Florence, and Naples.
collection-of-beauty-a-sketch-of-the-mit: From the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, Hokusai squeezes the snow-capped peak between the tiled roofs of the Mitsui dry-goods emporium. Kites, tradesmen, and a soaring eagle animate the bustling Edo street, the woodblock print pairing commerce and the sacred mountain in one frame.
collection-of-beauty-accolade-by-edmund-: A medieval-revival fantasy of a queen knighting a young warrior with the flat of a sword, set in a sun-streaked palace hall. The picture is by Edmund Blair Leighton, a late Victorian academic painter whose chivalric scenes drew on Pre-Raphaelite costume study and theatre design.
collection-of-beauty-adachi-moor: A scene from Yoshitoshi's series One Hundred Aspects of the Moon, illustrating the legend of the demon-hag of Adachigahara. The Meiji-era ukiyo-e master combines traditional brushwork with psychological intensity, sharpening a kabuki-derived horror tale into a single moonlit confrontation.
collection-of-beauty-adameveparadisecran: An early treatment of the Fall by the Wittenberg court painter, set within a paradise teeming with stags, lions, and small game. Cranach's slender, mannerist nudes and crisp linear drawing reflect his proximity to Luther's circle, where the subject carried fresh theological weight.
collection-of-beauty-adam-and-eve-uk-cia: Cranach returned to Adam and Eve dozens of times after 1509, refining a courtly Reformation iconography of the Fall. Eve hands the apple across the Tree of Knowledge while a serpent watches and a menagerie of stag, sheep, and roe gather symbolically around the first couple.
collection-of-beauty-admiral-of-the-whit: A swagger portrait of Admiral Lord Rodney, victor over the French at the 1782 Battle of the Saintes, the captured ensign of the Ville de Paris draped behind him. Gainsborough's feathery brushwork and silvery palette soften the formal demands of state portraiture.
collection-of-beauty-adolph-menzel-baupl: Menzel turns a humble Berlin construction site, fringed by drooping willows, into a study of weather and labour. The unposed subject and freshly observed light anticipate the realist vein the Prussian painter would pursue across decades of obsessive sketching from life.
collection-of-beauty-adolph-menzel-das-b: An empty room, white curtains billowing at the open balcony door, a sofa half-glimpsed in a wall mirror. Menzel made the small oil for himself, capturing transient sunlight with the immediacy of a snapshot decades before plein-air Impressionism reached Berlin.
collection-of-beauty-adolph-menzel-eisen: A grimy panorama of the Königshütte rolling mill in Upper Silesia, with workers wrestling glowing iron under shafts of furnace light. Menzel spent months on site making hundreds of drawings, producing one of the first ambitious European paintings to take heavy industry as its subject.
collection-of-beauty-adolph-menzel-flote: Menzel reconstructs an evening concert at Sanssouci, with Frederick the Great playing flute beside C.P.E. Bach at the harpsichord and Quantz, Benda, and the Margravine Wilhelmine listening. Painted under Friedrich Wilhelm IV, it crowns the artist's long Prussian historical project.
collection-of-beauty-adoration-of-the-ma: A Neapolitan-period altarpiece in which the Magi crowd around the Virgin and Child by torchlight. Gentileschi pushes the figures close to the picture plane and stages the gift-giving in heavy raking light, drawing on the Caravaggism she had carried south from Rome.
collection-of-beauty-after-rubens-anne-o: An early seventeenth-century studio copy of a lost Rubens portrait of Anne of Austria, queen consort of Louis XIII. The Spanish-born queen wears stiff court dress and an elaborate ruff, recorded with the smooth porcelain finish typical of Rubens's diplomatic likenesses of European royalty.
collection-of-beauty-after-the-battle-of: From the twenty-canvas Slav Epic that Mucha worked on for nearly two decades, this scene shows the aftermath of the 1410 Polish-Lithuanian victory over the Teutonic Knights. The Art Nouveau poster designer here deploys monumental tempera-and-oil composition to advance a pan-Slavic political mythology.
collection-of-beauty-after-the-attack-pl: Vereshchagin painted the carnage of the Russo-Turkish siege of Plevna from first-hand experience as a war correspondent attached to the Russian army. The unsentimental documentary realism he made his trademark exposes the human cost behind official communiqués of imperial conquest.
collection-of-beauty-albinus-skeleton-w-: A plate from Bernhard Siegfried Albinus's Tabulae sceleti et musculorum corporis humani (Leiden, 1747), engraved by Jan Wandelaar. The skeleton is set in a poetic landscape with a rhinoceros, marrying anatomical exactitude to Enlightenment-era pictorial staging.

Another plate from Albinus's Tabulae sceleti et musculorum, in which Wandelaar layers the superficial muscles over the skeleton in a posed standing figure. The series was prized for its proportional accuracy, achieved with grids and double-perspective drafting unusual for medical illustration of the period.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-altdorfer-: Commissioned by Duke William IV of Bavaria, Altdorfer compresses Alexander's 333 BC victory over Darius III into thousands of tiny figures swarming under a cosmic sky. The Danube School master treats landscape and weather as protagonists, the curving horizon and apocalyptic sun lifting the battle into world-historical drama.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-adam: Dürer's celebrated copperplate engraving applies the canon of human proportion he had absorbed from Jacopo de' Barbari in Nuremberg. Adam and Eve flank the Tree of Knowledge amid a forest stocked with elk, ox, rabbit, and cat — emblems of the four humours soon to be unbalanced by the Fall.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-selb: Painted shortly after his first Italian journey, the twenty-six-year-old Dürer presents himself as a gentleman scholar in tasselled cap and silk-trimmed jacket rather than a craftsman. The Alpine landscape glimpsed through the window signals his new cosmopolitan ambitions for the German artist.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-adam: Two life-size oil panels showing the first humans isolated against black grounds, Dürer's first attempt to model nudes in the Italian manner after his second Venice trip. Each figure is treated as a study in idealized anatomy, paired across the diptych held by the Prado.
