collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-barb: A charcoal portrait of the artist's mother drawn weeks before her death, capturing her gaunt cheekbones and sunken eye in unsparing detail. Dürer's inscription records her age and condition, turning the sheet into a private memorial as much as a likeness.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-der-: A watercolor and gouache study of a severed roller's wing, its ultramarine, turquoise, and rust feathers laid out with near-scientific precision. The sheet exemplifies Dürer's habit of treating natural specimens as objects of sustained, meditative observation.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-head: A late brush drawing of a bearded ninety-three-year-old man Dürer met in Antwerp, prepared as a study for a never-executed Saint Jerome. The economical washes catch sagging skin and patriarchal beard with the artist's signature analytic eye.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-mele: An engraving from Dürer's so-called Master Prints in which a winged figure broods amid scattered tools of geometry, a polyhedron, and a magic square. The image has been read as an emblem of artistic genius stalled by melancholic temperament.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-pray: A brush study in white and grey on blue prepared paper, drawn as a detail for an apostle in the Heller Altarpiece. The folded hands have since detached from their context to become one of the most reproduced devotional images in Western art.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-the-: A small woodcut from the Small Passion series, showing an angel driving Adam and Eve from Eden through a narrow gate. Dürer compresses the drama into densely cut parallel hatching that gives the bodies sculptural weight.

A watercolour and gouache study of a clump of meadow plants, dandelions and grasses, viewed at ground level with botanical exactitude. The work is a touchstone of Renaissance nature study and prefigures modern ecological imagery.

An early woodcut of six men relaxing around a Nuremberg bathhouse pump, several modelled on Dürer's friends and possibly himself. The crowded composition lets him show male anatomy from multiple angles in a vernacular setting.

A woodcut of an Indian rhinoceros gifted to King Manuel I of Portugal, drawn from a written description and a sketch since Dürer never saw the animal. He sheathes it in armour-like plates, an invented anatomy that dominated European imagery of the species for centuries.
collection-of-beauty-albrecht-durer-druc: The only surviving copper printing plate from Dürer's hand, engraved with Christ praying in Gethsemane while the apostles sleep. Held by the Bamberg State Library, the worn matrix attests to the dense burin-work behind his late religious prints.
collection-of-beauty-alexander-iii-recep: A wide-format Russian historical scene of Tsar Alexander III addressing village elders gathered in the Petrovsky Palace courtyard during his coronation. Repin orchestrates the crowd with his characteristic eye for individuated peasant types and ceremonial pomp.
collection-of-beauty-alexandra-and-elena: A double portrait of Grand Duchesses Alexandra and Elena Pavlovna, daughters of Paul I, painted soon after Vigée Le Brun's arrival at the Russian court in exile from revolutionary France. Soft pastel-like flesh and silvery silks display the courtly grace that made her Marie Antoinette's preferred portraitist.
collection-of-beauty-alfons-mucha-1896-a: From the four-panel decorative series The Seasons, this allegorical figure stands among ripening fruit and ochre foliage. Mucha frames her in the slender vertical format and circular halo motif that defined his Parisian poster style.
collection-of-beauty-alfons-mucha-1896-s: Part of Mucha's first Seasons cycle, the figure plays a lyre fashioned from a branch, surrounded by fresh blossoms and songbirds. The pale palette and arabesque hair are signatures of his early Champenois lithographs.

From the Seasons quartet issued by Champenois, a languid woman trails her feet in water beneath ripe poppies. Mucha sets her in the elongated whiplash silhouette that became shorthand for fin-de-siècle Paris.
collection-of-beauty-alfons-mucha-1896-w: Closing the Seasons set, a fur-cloaked woman warms a small bird against a snow-laden branch. The cool blue-grey palette and stylized snowfall demonstrate Mucha's command of decorative colour lithography.
collection-of-beauty-alfons-mucha-1898-d: Part of The Arts panel series, an entranced female personification floats through swirling drapery and autumnal leaves. Mucha treats movement itself as ornament, threading line through the figure in continuous arabesques.
collection-of-beauty-alfons-mucha-1898-t: From the four-panel Flowers series, a virginal figure rises among towering white lilies in a symmetrical decorative niche. Mucha equates the woman with the bloom, fusing botanical study and Art Nouveau allegory.
collection-of-beauty-alfons-mucha-the-mo: From the pair The Moon and the Stars, a pale-skinned figure leans against a glowing lunar disc among night clouds. The cool silvers and indigos show Mucha working in a more nocturnal, dream-laden register than his daylight posters.
collection-of-beauty-along-the-river-dur: A Qing court reinterpretation of the celebrated Song handscroll documenting Qingming festivities along the Bian River. Shen Yuan, an academician under the Qianlong emperor, executes the panorama in fine ink line touched with sparing ochre washes.
collection-of-beauty-amor-vincet-omnia: A naturalistic Cupid in the Berlin Gemäldegalerie, who clambers grinning over scattered emblems of music, war, science, and rule. Caravaggio's tenebrist lighting and the boy's frank nudity made the picture notorious among Roman collectors.
collection-of-beauty-amsterdam-musee-van: Painted during Monet's brief stay in the Dutch bulb fields near Sassenheim, this canvas spreads horizontal bands of red and yellow tulips before distant windmills. The high-keyed palette previews the saturated colour Monet would later push at Giverny.
collection-of-beauty-an-afternoon-stroll: Chase paints his wife and one of their children walking through the dunes at Shinnecock, Long Island, where he ran his summer school of plein-air painting. Loose, sun-bleached brushwork shows his absorption of French Impressionism into an American idiom.
collection-of-beauty-an-experiment-on-a-: A candle-lit demonstration of a vacuum pump, with a cockatoo gasping in its glass globe as a mixed audience reacts in awe, fascination, and dread. Wright transposes religious chiaroscuro onto the new theatre of Enlightenment science.
collection-of-beauty-anbetung-der-konige: Bruegel sets the Adoration of the Magi inside a snowbound Flemish village, the Holy Family barely visible amid woodcutters, skaters, and falling flakes. Painted in tempera on panel, it is one of the earliest snow scenes in Western painting.
collection-of-beauty-andrea-mantegna-the: Christ's body is laid on a marble slab and seen in radical foreshortening from the soles of his pierced feet. Mantegna's hard, sculptural line and reduced palette turn the Lamentation into an exercise in perspectival shock.
collection-of-beauty-andrea-mantegna-014: Saint Sebastian stands bound to a fragmented classical column, his body bristling with arrows against a ruined ancient cityscape. Mantegna fuses humanist antiquarianism with Christian martyrdom, a recurring preoccupation in his Mantuan years.
collection-of-beauty-anna-alexandrovna-g: A Hermitage portrait of the Georgian-born Princess Galitzin, painted during Vigée Le Brun's long Russian exile after the Revolution. Pale silks and a soft sfumato face exemplify the flattering ancien-régime manner she carried across European courts.
collection-of-beauty-annibale-carracci-p: The dead Christ slumps across the Virgin's lap as two grieving angels hold up his arm and a crown of thorns. Carracci's restrained classicism, Bolognese in its rigour, became a key model for seventeenth-century devotional painting.
collection-of-beauty-annibale-carracci-c: An early devotional work from the Bolognese reformer of late-Mannerist painting, the composition tightens onto the suffering Christ as winged attendants steady his body. Carracci leans on the warm chiaroscuro and sculptural drapery he absorbed from Correggio and Venetian sources.
collection-of-beauty-annibale-carracci-r: An ideal river view in which figures crossing a wooden bridge are dwarfed by the broad waterway and distant hills. Carracci helped invent the classical landscape tradition later codified by Poussin and Claude, balancing observed Italian topography with a calm, ordered geometry.
collection-of-beauty-annibale-carracci-t: A monumental treatment of a humble subject, with carcasses, scales and aproned tradesmen rendered at near life size. Carracci's interest in low-life genre, drawn from northern prints and direct observation, gives the scene an unusually frank, reformist realism for its date.
collection-of-beauty-annibale-carracci-s: The chaste Susanna recoils as two elders intrude on her bath, the biblical pretext for a study of startled flesh against shaded foliage. Carracci stages the assault with the firm contour and idealised anatomy he was simultaneously developing for the Farnese ceiling.
collection-of-beauty-anonymous-ten-thous: An eleven-metre handscroll on silk that travels the Yangtze from its mountain source to the sea, opening section by section in the hand of the viewer. Long attributed to the Southern Song master Xia Gui, it strings together mist, gorges, fishing villages and city walls in characteristic axe-cut brushwork.
collection-of-beauty-anonymous-brustbild: A bust-length study of a young man, plainly dressed and turned slightly toward the viewer, kept in the Belvedere as part of the German Romantic holdings. Runge favoured this kind of clear, almost severe portrait pared back to face and gaze.
collection-of-beauty-anthonis-van-dyck-0: The Genoese noblewoman descends a terrace beneath a parasol held by an African page, the pairing a familiar device in van Dyck's Italian portraits of patrician women. Crimson drapery, towering verticals and a reserved, columnar pose set the template for European court portraiture.

The wine-soaked Silenus, fleshy and unsteady, is propped up by satyrs and a maenad in a tightly cropped Bacchic ensemble. The composition descends from Rubens's workshop, where the young van Dyck assimilated the older master's ruddy, full-bodied paint handling.
collection-of-beauty-anthony-van-dyck-ma: A tender likeness of the painter's Scottish wife, posed with a quiet half-smile against a neutral ground. Van Dyck reserves his usual aristocratic finery for a domestic intimacy, the silvery silks softened by his late, more thinly brushed manner.
collection-of-beauty-anthony-van-dyck-ra: Rachel de Ruvigny, Countess of Southampton, is shown as Fortune perched on a globe, a moralised allegory of worldly mutability much favoured in Caroline court portraiture. Cool blue satins and a still, ovular face are vintage van Dyck.
collection-of-beauty-anton-van-dyck-anto: The Genoese patron rides forward beneath a heavy classical arch, a swaggering equestrian formula van Dyck adapted from Rubens and Titian. Now in the Palazzo Rosso, the canvas anchors the painter's six-year Italian sojourn.
collection-of-beauty-anton-van-dyck-sams: Delilah leans forward in shock as Philistine soldiers seize the shorn Samson from her lap, the moment of betrayal pitched between sensuality and violence. Van Dyck reworks Rubens's earlier composition with cooler colour and a more refined, elongated drawing.
collection-of-beauty-arabian-nights-3-by: A finely worked Orientalist scene drawn from the Thousand and One Nights, set inside one of the tiled, latticed interiors Lewis studied during his decade in Cairo. He is best known for the jewel-bright watercolour technique that lets sunlight pick its way across carpets and costume.
collection-of-beauty-arcimboldo-air-copy: Birds of every order roost on and form a human profile, one of four allegories of the elements painted for the Habsburg court. Arcimboldo's composite portraits flatter the encyclopaedic ambitions of Maximilian II while showing off the painter's natural-history library.
collection-of-beauty-arcimboldo-earth: A bust assembled from terrestrial mammals — stag, lion, hare, ox — interlocking to form a crowned Habsburg profile. The conceit, painted at the Prague court, casts imperial dominion as a literal incorporation of the natural world.
collection-of-beauty-arcimboldowater: A profile fashioned from fish, crustaceans, coral and pearls, completing the Vienna set of the four elements. Arcimboldo treats the composite as both witty courtly device and a serious display of zoological knowledge.
collection-of-beauty-argenteuil-a14692: Sailboats lie along the bank of the Seine at Argenteuil, the suburban boating town where Monet settled after the Franco-Prussian War. Quick comma-strokes break the water and sky into the broken, prismatic colour that became the signature of early Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-arnaut-and-his-dog-: A Balkan mercenary in Ottoman service exhales pipe smoke into the muzzle of a patient hound on a sunlit Cairo street. Gérôme's enamel-smooth surface and ethnographic exactitude are typical of his late Orientalist studies of idle, picturesque soldiery.
collection-of-beauty-artemisia-gentilesc: The Argive princess reclines as Jupiter's golden shower falls into her lap, her maid scrambling to gather the coins. The picture, long shared between Artemisia and her father Orazio, channels Caravaggesque tenebrism into a sensuous, half-length nude.

A young Virgin offers her breast to the sleeping Christ Child in a quiet domestic register. Painted near the start of Artemisia's Roman career, it shows her absorbing Caravaggio's bare interiors and her father's silver-cool flesh tones.

The Egyptian queen lies dead across her bed, asp at the breast, in a foreshortened nude that confronts the viewer at close range. The picture sits within the Gentileschi family's repertoire of half-length heroines of antiquity, attributed variously to Artemisia and her father Orazio.

Judith and her servant pause in Holofernes's tent, alerted by a sound outside; a single candle throws Judith's shielding hand into silhouette and glances off the discarded gauntlet. The Detroit canvas is among Artemisia's most theatrical exercises in Caravaggesque candle-light.

The penitent Magdalene twists in her chair, gold gown slipping, hand pressed to her breast in conversion. Artemisia gives the Counter-Reformation subject the muscular, psychologically charged female protagonist that is her hallmark.
collection-of-beauty-artlib-gallery-1387: A requiem on a frozen plain: a priest swings a censer over a field of fallen Russian soldiers after the Russo-Turkish War. Vereshchagin painted the scene from direct experience as a war correspondent, part of his unsparing campaign against martial glamour.
collection-of-beauty-as-the-old-sing-so-: A multi-generational Flemish family bellows around a table while a child blows a pipe, illustrating the proverb inscribed at the top: as the old sing, so the young pipe. Jordaens returned to the boisterous moralised feast many times, this version inscribed Ut genus est genius.
collection-of-beauty-asakusa-honganji-te: A roofer kneels on the great tiled ridge of the Asakusa Hongan-ji while a kite drifts past and Mount Fuji rises in the distance. Plate four of the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji turns the sacred peak into a small constant glimpsed across Edo's working rooftops.
collection-of-beauty-at-first-glance-he-: A glowering giant's face is, on closer look, made up entirely of squirming naked men — a sly anatomical pun on the proverb in the title. Kuniyoshi produced such jigsaw figures to slip past Tempō-era censorship that restricted depictions of named actors and courtesans.
collection-of-beauty-at-the-beer-garden-: A crowded Munich beer garden seethes with waitresses, top hats and bourgeois families under the chestnut trees. Menzel, the chronicler of modern German urban life, packs the canvas with the sharp-eyed, almost photographic detail he gathered in pocket sketchbooks.
collection-of-beauty-auguste-renoir-en-e: A dark-eyed young woman sits outdoors in a loose white blouse, her hair undone — the painter's model and lover Lise Tréhot cast as a summer bohémienne. The picture predates Renoir's full Impressionist break and still owes a debt to Courbet's full-bodied realism.
collection-of-beauty-auguste-renoir-la-g: Bathers and rowboats cluster around the floating café on the Seine at Bougival, the spot where Renoir and Monet spent the summer of 1869 painting side by side. Broken touches of opaque colour on the water mark one of the foundational moments of Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-augustins-le-christ: Rubens stages the Crucifixion with the two thieves flanking Christ, the muscular bodies twisting against a darkened sky in the dynamic Counter-Reformation idiom. The altarpiece, painted for an Antwerp Augustinian context, deploys the diagonal energy and ruddy flesh tones characteristic of his Flemish Baroque workshop.
collection-of-beauty-autoportrait-au-tur: Rigaud, the great portraitist of Louis XIV's court, presents himself in an exotic turban, a studio prop conveying artistic vanity and learned cosmopolitanism. The lustrous fabrics and confident chiaroscuro foreshadow the polished grandeur he would soon bring to royal portraiture.
collection-of-beauty-ave-caesar-morituri: Jean-Leon Gerome's academic vision of the Roman arena shows gladiators saluting the emperor with the famous formula "Hail Caesar, those who are about to die salute you." The painting epitomizes the meticulous archaeological detail and theatrical staging of nineteenth-century French history painting.
collection-of-beauty-b159-rembrandt: A rare still life from Rembrandt's etched oeuvre, depicting a single cone shell (Conus marmoreus) rendered with delicate cross-hatching and drypoint burr. The print attests to the Dutch fascination with exotic naturalia gathered through global trade.
collection-of-beauty-b320-rembrandt: An early self-portrait etching from Rembrandt's Leiden years, the young artist studies his own face under a soft cap, probing expression and shadow. Such prints functioned as exercises in physiognomy and as advertisements of his graphic virtuosity.
collection-of-beauty-back-view-of-fuji-f: Hiroshige reverses the conventional southern view of Fuji, glimpsing the sacred peak from Kai Province on the mountain's far side. Part of his first Thirty-six Views series, the design uses graduated bokashi printing to layer mist, peak, and foreground.
collection-of-beauty-baglione: Baglione's allegory pits divine love against earthly desire, a youthful angel descending upon a slumbering Cupid. The picture famously rivalled Caravaggio's treatment of the same subject and helped fuel the bitter feud between the two Roman painters.
collection-of-beauty-baigneurs-a-asniere: A preparatory study for Une Baignade, Asnieres, this seated nude shows Seurat working out volume through hatched conte crayon on textured paper. The drawing predates his fully pointillist technique but already pursues the luminous tonal gradations he called irradiation.
collection-of-beauty-baldassare-castigli: Raphael portrays his friend, the courtier and author of The Book of the Courtier, in a restrained palette of grey, black, and pearly white. The composed three-quarter pose and steady gaze became a touchstone for European court portraiture, admired and copied by Rembrandt and Rubens.
collection-of-beauty-basket-fishing-in-t: From the Oceans of Wisdom series on traditional fishing methods, Hokusai shows villagers wading the Kinu with woven basket traps. The flattened riverbank and tight figural grouping demonstrate his mature handling of genre subjects in the 1830s.
collection-of-beauty-bathsheba-c-1827-by: Hayez retells the Old Testament episode with a Venetian sensuousness, Bathsheba caught at her bath under King David's distant gaze. The smooth modelling and warm flesh tones reflect the Italian Romantic painter's debt to Titian.
collection-of-beauty-bauernfeind-gustav-: Bauernfeind, the leading German Orientalist of his generation, records a crowded Jerusalem street where pilgrims and traders converge at the gates of Haram al-Sharif. His near-photographic precision rests on sketches gathered during repeated journeys to Palestine.
collection-of-beauty-bazille-and-camille: A figure study made for Monet's lost large-scale Dejeuner sur l'herbe, showing the painter Frederic Bazille and his future wife Camille Doncieux among forest light. The brisk plein-air handling already announces the broken touch that would define Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-beardsley-peacocksk: Beardsley's ink illustration for Oscar Wilde's Salome shows the princess in a sweeping skirt patterned with peacock eyes, confronting Herodias. The economy of black silhouette and decorative line, drawn from Japanese prints, became a touchstone of Art Nouveau graphic design.
collection-of-beauty-bemberg-fondation-t: Monet paints fishing boats hauled up beneath the chalk cliffs of Etretat, a Norman coast he visited repeatedly in the 1880s. The chalky beach and overcast sea are built from short, broken strokes that record changing weather rather than narrative incident.

An early self-portrait by the painter best known for his hushed flower pieces and group portraits of the Parisian avant-garde. The dark ground and concentrated gaze owe much to his close study of seventeenth-century Dutch masters in the Louvre.
collection-of-beauty-besneeuwde-ochtend-: From the Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, Hokusai shows tea-house guests in Edo's Koishikawa district pausing over rice and sake to admire snow-clad Fuji at dawn. The white expanse and thin red sun exploit the limited palette of nishiki-e woodblock printing.
collection-of-beauty-blaas-eugen-die-was: The Italian-Austrian painter de Blaas specialised in genteel scenes of Venetian life, here a young woman pausing with her copper water vessel. His glossy academic finish made such picturesque subjects popular with late-nineteenth-century international collectors.
collection-of-beauty-bodhidharmayoshitos: From One Hundred Aspects of the Moon, Yoshitoshi depicts the Zen patriarch Bodhidharma seated in meditation behind a ruined lattice. The late-Edo printmaker tempers the supernatural subject with naturalistic shading, marking his bridge between ukiyo-e and modern Japanese illustration.
collection-of-beauty-bonneville-savoy-by: An Alpine view from Turner's first Continental tour following the Peace of Amiens, recording the town of Bonneville against the Mont Blanc range. The classical compositional structure recalls Claude Lorrain, whom Turner studied closely as a young Royal Academician.
collection-of-beauty-botticelli-annuncia: Painted for the Cestello convent in Florence, the panel shows Gabriel kneeling before a swaying Virgin whose body bends in a lyrical S-curve. The cool palette and tiled floor demonstrate Botticelli's late, more austere mode under the influence of Savonarola's preaching.
collection-of-beauty-botticelli-incorona: A predella scene from the San Marco altarpiece, showing John the Evangelist receiving his vision on the rocky island of Patmos. The miniature format allowed Botticelli's workshop to compress dramatic landscape and visionary light into a narrow horizontal panel.
collection-of-beauty-botticelli-a-virgem: An early devotional panel from Botticelli's apprenticeship in the orbit of Filippo Lippi, the Madonna and Child are accompanied by an attendant angel offering fruit. The soft modelling and delicate linearity announce the Florentine grace of his mature style.
collection-of-beauty-botticelli-04-louvr: Botticelli treats the popular Florentine subject of the infant Christ greeted by the young John the Baptist, a tondo-like intimacy enclosed within a vertical panel. The crisp contour drawing and Mary's downcast features are hallmarks of his Quattrocento workshop.
collection-of-beauty-botticelli-prado-49: From the four-panel Boccaccio cycle commissioned by Lorenzo de' Medici for the Pucci-Bini wedding, this episode shows the cursed huntsman pursuing his lover through a pine wood at Ravenna. Botticelli unfolds the moral tale across staged tableaux of refined courtly pageantry.
collection-of-beauty-bouquet-of-mallows: A loose still life of pink mallows arranged in a glass vase, painted at Vetheuil during a difficult personal year for Monet. The tabletop dissolves into rapid, comma-like strokes that anticipate the all-over surface of his late garden pictures.
collection-of-beauty-boy-with-a-basket-o: An early Roman work made soon after Caravaggio entered the household of Cardinal del Monte, the youth offers a basket spilling with grapes, peaches, and figs. The naturalistic still life, with its bruised leaves and worm-eaten apples, helped found a new Italian tradition of painting fruit from life.
collection-of-beauty-brooklyn-museum-sno: Kunisada, the prolific Utagawa-school designer, sets a kabuki-style figure against falling snow on the banks of an Edo river. The plush patterned kimono and densely speckled white sky show his command of beauty prints and the expanded palette of nineteenth-century woodblock printing.
collection-of-beauty-brooklyn-museum-stu: Chase, the leading American Impressionist teacher, captures a young model in a borrowed kimono with rapid, fluid brushwork. Such japoniste studio subjects were fashionable in Gilded Age New York and reveal his admiration for Whistler and Sargent.
collection-of-beauty-brooklyn-museum-tab: From his first Souvenirs of Travel series, Hasui depicts the Tsuta hot spring deep in the mountains of Mutsu, framed by stands of beech and birch. The cool tonal harmonies typify the shin-hanga revival of landscape woodblock printing he led with publisher Watanabe Shozaburo.
collection-of-beauty-byam-shaw-omphale-1: The Lydian queen Omphale stands draped in the lion-skin of Hercules, who served her in bondage as punishment for murder. Byam Shaw, a late Pre-Raphaelite, treats the mythological role-reversal with jewel-toned costume detail and decorative outline drawn from his Birmingham School training.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-1840-1: A field at Giverny rimed with hoarfrost, painted soon after Monet settled at the Norman village that would consume the rest of his career. Broken touches of pale violet, rose, and silvery blue register the chill morning light without recourse to white.

From the late Nymphéas cycle, the canvas dissolves the lily pond into floating pads, drowned grasses, and reflected sky. Working at monumental scale in his Giverny studio, Monet abandoned the horizon altogether to let surface and depth fuse into a single chromatic field.
collection-of-beauty-caillebotte-l-homme: A top-hatted bourgeois leans on the wrought-iron railing of a Haussmann balcony, surveying the boulevard below. Caillebotte, an engineer by training, repeatedly used the steep diagonal of Paris's new avenues to investigate the modern viewer's detached urban gaze.
collection-of-beauty-caillebotte-gustave: An unidealised nude sprawls across a rumpled divan, her clothing tossed over the chair beside her. Painted early in Caillebotte's career, the canvas treats the female body with a candid, almost photographic objectivity that was startling to Salon-trained eyes.
collection-of-beauty-caravaggio-i-musici: Four young musicians, one tuning a lute, prepare to perform in a tightly packed half-length composition. Commissioned by Cardinal Francesco Maria del Monte for his Roman household, the picture launched Caravaggio's career and introduced his trademark modelling of flesh against deep shadow.
collection-of-beauty-caravaggio-la-depos: Christ's lifeless body is lowered onto the anointing stone, his arm and shroud dropping toward the viewer. Painted for the Chiesa Nuova in Rome, the altarpiece compresses the mourners into a powerful diagonal lit by Caravaggio's signature raking shaft.
collection-of-beauty-caravaggio-medusa-g: The Gorgon's severed head shrieks the instant of decapitation, her snakes still writhing. Painted on a convex tournament shield as a gift from Cardinal del Monte to the Grand Duke of Tuscany, the work conflates mirror, weapon, and self-portrait.
collection-of-beauty-caravaggio-sette-op: All seven corporal acts of mercy, from burying the dead to Roman Charity, unfold in a single torch-lit Neapolitan alley. Painted for the Pio Monte della Misericordia, the altarpiece set the template for Neapolitan tenebrism that Ribera and the Caravaggisti would extend.
collection-of-beauty-caravaggio-judith-b: The Hebrew widow grips the Assyrian general's hair and saws through his neck while her aged maid waits with a sack. Caravaggio renders the ugly mechanics of beheading with forensic clarity, the spurting blood lit as starkly as the white sheets.
collection-of-beauty-carl-friedrich-seif: Sunlight refracted through the submerged entrance of Capri's Grotta Azzurra fills the cavern with the cobalt glow that made it a Grand Tour pilgrimage site. The picture is in fact by Berlin landscapist Carl Friedrich Seiffert, who specialised in luminous Italian veduta.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-das-a: A weathered statue of blindfolded Lady Justice stands with broken scales while a soldier loiters in shadow behind her, a wry Biedermeier comment on the gap between law and power. The panel was sold under Nazi duress by Jewish collector Leo Bendel and only restituted to his heirs in 2019.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-der-b: A village postman trudges along a sun-drenched lane in the Rosenthal, satchel slung at his hip. Spitzweg, a trained pharmacist who painted in his spare hours, made a career of these affectionately ironic small-town vignettes.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-der-m: A plein-air painter has stretched out on his back beneath a propped umbrella in a forest clearing, paint box at his feet. The compact panel is a gentle self-mockery of the Romantic ideal of the artist communing with nature.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-der-a: A penniless poet huddles under an umbrella in his garret bed, scribbling verses while the roof leaks. Spitzweg's most famous Biedermeier image, now in Munich's Neue Pinakothek, distils the era's fondness for affectionate caricature of the eccentric outsider.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-der-e: An aged hermit has nodded off in his rocky retreat, breviary slipping from his hand. Spitzweg favoured these solitary cassocked figures as Biedermeier emblems of harmless retreat from the noisy modern world.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-im-ha: An odalisque reclines under fretwork screens in a Munich painter's idea of a Turkish harem, the orientalist staffage rendered with Spitzweg's trademark twinkling humour. The artist drew his exotic detail entirely from prints and travel literature, never having visited the East.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-in-th: A small group winds along a high alpine track with the Wendelstein massif rising behind them. Late in his career Spitzweg loosened his touch under Barbizon influence, and the airy mountain palette here is closer to plein-air observation than to his earlier studio finish.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-013: A pair of conspirators in a courtyard puzzle over a love letter snatched from its addressee. The narrative anecdote, the costumed protagonists, and the pinpoint architectural detail are textbook Spitzweg, drawn from a stage tradition rather than direct observation.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-036: A bourgeois family in their Sunday best parades down a country path under a parasol, the children straggling behind. Spitzweg captures the small comedies of Biedermeier ritual with affectionate, miniaturist precision.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-der-s: A frock-coated naturalist tiptoes through tall grass with raised butterfly net, lost in Linnaean reverie. The collector-eccentric, beloved subject of Spitzweg's Biedermeier comedies, is here painted on a vertical panel that exaggerates the man's spindly absurdity.
collection-of-beauty-carl-spitzweg-stand: A hopeful suitor stands beneath a balcony serenading an unseen sweetheart, while neighbours watch from their windows. Spitzweg returned often to this nocturnal Ständchen subject, savouring the gap between Romantic gesture and small-town audience.
collection-of-beauty-carlos-v-en-muhlber: The Habsburg emperor rides out armoured in pike and lance to commemorate his 1547 victory over the Schmalkaldic League at Mühlberg. Painted in Augsburg and now in the Prado, Titian's portrait fixed the equestrian image of sovereign authority that Velázquez and Van Dyck would inherit.
collection-of-beauty-carlyle-maclise-ori: Thomas Carlyle leans against a pillar, top hat in hand, in a full-length character study by his fellow Scot Daniel Maclise. The portrait belongs to the celebrated Fraser's Magazine series of literary lions Maclise drew during the 1830s.
collection-of-beauty-carnegiemuseumofvin: Furrowed wheat fields on the plain of Auvers, the soil glistening after a storm, painted in the final weeks of Van Gogh's life. The high horizon, ribbon-like brushwork, and rain-washed greens belong to the productive Auvers months that ended in July 1890.
collection-of-beauty-carp-leaping-up-a-c: A koi battles upstream against a thundering waterfall, illustrating the Chinese legend that any carp scaling the falls of the Dragon Gate is transformed into a dragon. The motif, emblematic of perseverance and male coming-of-age, was a staple of East Asian ink painting and ukiyo-e print design.
collection-of-beauty-casincaillebotte: Caillebotte's mother, aunt, cousin, and a family friend sit reading and sewing on the lawn at the family's Yerres estate, viewed from above through a steep garden perspective. The bourgeois leisure scene combines Impressionist plein-air light with Caillebotte's almost geometric staging of figures in space.
collection-of-beauty-caspar-david-friedr: Three figures peer over the dizzying white precipices of Rügen onto the Baltic far below, framed by an arch of beech branches. Painted shortly after Friedrich's honeymoon, the picture invests a real coastal panorama with Romantic allegories of marriage, faith, and mortality.

A procession of monks bears a coffin into the snow-rimed ruin of a Gothic abbey, the gnarled oaks around them silhouetted against a pale winter dusk. Friedrich exhibited the canvas in Berlin alongside Mönch am Meer as a Romantic meditation on death and resurrection.

A wayside crucifix stands atop a fir-clad alpine peak, gilded by rays of an unseen setting sun. Originally an altarpiece for the Tetschen chapel, the work provoked the so-called Ramdohr controversy by daring to treat pure landscape as a vehicle of religious revelation.

A solitary figure stands on a pale dune before an immense, near-empty expanse of sea and sky. Friedrich strips the composition to horizontal bands, dwarfing the human silhouette to evoke spiritual confrontation with the infinite — a foundational image of German Romantic landscape.

A weathered oak rises at the centre of a Bohemian valley at dawn, its dead crown set against luminous distant mountains. Friedrich uses the broken tree as a quiet emblem of endurance, paired with the morning panorama as one half of a meditation on the cycle of day and life.

Five figures on a Baltic shore correspond to five sailing vessels at varying distances — an allegory of the ages of human life rendered in Friedrich's late, hushed palette. The painting was made near the artist's birthplace of Greifswald and is now in Leipzig's Museum der bildenden Künste.

Two women and a man on a rocky shore watch sailing ships approach beneath a rising moon, the sky banded violet and gold. Friedrich repeated the moonlit-sea motif throughout his career as a Rückenfigur meditation on faith, return, and the threshold between visible and unseen.

Seen from behind on a rocky outcrop, the wanderer surveys a swirling fog that swallows the peaks below. Friedrich's signature Rückenfigur device invites the viewer into the figure's vantage, making the landscape an interior as much as a topography of the Elbe Sandstone Mountains.
collection-of-beauty-cassatt-mary-lydia-: Cassatt's elder sister Lydia, often her sitter in these years, leans into her work at a vertical tapestry loom in soft afternoon light. The picture belongs to the artist's series of women absorbed in domestic occupations, painted with the loose Impressionist touch she had absorbed in Degas's circle.
collection-of-beauty-cassatt-mary-matern: A bare-shouldered mother cradles her child against her, the two faces close in quiet intimacy. Cassatt returned to the maternité theme repeatedly after 1880, treating the secular subject with the formal gravity of a Renaissance Madonna while keeping her brushwork bright and contemporary.
collection-of-beauty-cats-suggested-as-t: Kuniyoshi, a noted cat lover, replaces every post town on the Tōkaidō highway with a cat whose pose or attribute puns on the station's name. The triptych is a tour de force of Edo wordplay (mitate) and ukiyo-e woodblock printing, made when actor prints had been restricted by Tenpō reform censorship.
collection-of-beauty-cawing-crow-by-ohar: A black crow opens its beak mid-call on a bare branch, the empty ground around it giving the cry pictorial space. Koson specialised in kachō-e (bird-and-flower) prints for the early-twentieth-century shin-hanga revival, exporting subdued, naturalistic studies like this one to Western collectors.
collection-of-beauty-charing-cross-bridg: Monet painted the railway bridge across the Thames repeatedly during winter stays at the Savoy Hotel, working on dozens of canvases at once as the light shifted. The composition dissolves bridge, smoke, and water into veils of mauve and pale orange characteristic of his London series.

One of nearly forty variants Monet made of Charing Cross Bridge, focused here on the trembling reflections beneath the iron span. The series, painted between 1899 and 1904, was the artist's sustained study of London fog as a coloured atmospheric medium rather than a mere obscurant.
collection-of-beauty-charlotte-dubourg-p: Fantin-Latour's sister-in-law sits in dark dress against a neutral ground, hands loosely clasped. The portrait carries the muted tonality and quiet psychological focus the artist learned from his admiration of the Old Masters and his friendship with Whistler; it now hangs in the Musée d'Orsay.
collection-of-beauty-chase-william-merri: Sand, scrub, and pale sky meet under bright Long Island light at Shinnecock, where Chase ran his pioneering open-air summer school from 1891. The painting's broad horizontal bands and feathery brushwork mark his absorption of French Impressionism into an American plein-air idiom.

Chase portrays his fellow expatriate sympathiser in the dandyish black-and-grey register Whistler himself favoured, monocle and cane in hand. The two artists painted each other in London that summer; their friendship soured shortly after over the resulting likenesses.

Dora Wheeler, a designer trained alongside Chase in Munich, sits in a blue gown amid the embroidered hangings of her mother Candace's Associated Artists studio. The portrait won a medal at the 1883 Paris Salon and helped establish Chase's reputation for atmospheric, aesthetic-movement interiors.

Painted in his Fourth Avenue studio late in life, Chase shows himself in a dark suit among the bric-à-brac collection that doubled as his teaching tool. The bravura handling and theatrical self-presentation summarise the persona he cultivated at the Art Students League and Chase School.

An early student work made during Chase's Indianapolis years, the cut watermelon is laid out with the dark ground and dramatic highlights of the Munich-school tradition he would soon study at first hand. The picture predates his shift toward looser, brighter American Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-chase-mrs-meigs-pia: Mrs. Meigs is shown at her piano-organ in a richly furnished aesthetic interior, her white dress glowing against dark panelling. Chase exhibited several such society portraits in the early 1880s, deploying the bravura tonal handling he had absorbed from Frans Hals and Velázquez.
collection-of-beauty-chen-rong-nine-drag: Nine dragons surge through clouds, mist, and crashing water across a long handscroll of ink and wash on paper. Chen Rong, a Southern Song scholar-official, painted the work in 1244; it is celebrated for its splashed-ink dragons and is held by the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston.
collection-of-beauty-chichester-canal-18: The still water of the Sussex canal mirrors a tall-masted ship and a sky drenched in late sun. Turner painted the scene for his patron the Earl of Egremont, one of a set of canvases made for the panelled dining room at Petworth House and now in the Tate.
collection-of-beauty-childe-harold: Inspired by Byron's Mediterranean poem, Turner builds a luminous Italian campagna of crumbling arches, distant hills, and a glowing sky. The picture is part of the artist's long late dialogue with Claude Lorrain, dissolving classical landscape architecture into atmosphere.
collection-of-beauty-chinese-warship-tin: The German-built ironclad Tingyuan, then the largest warship in the Far East, lies at anchor in a Japanese port flying her flags. Kōitsu records the ship's Meiji-era courtesy visit in a colour woodblock print that combines ukiyo-e tradition with the new Western subject matter of steam and steel.
collection-of-beauty-christian-ix-of-den: Tuxen assembles Christian IX — the so-called father-in-law of Europe — with his children and royal grandchildren in the garden hall of Fredensborg Palace. Commissioned for the king's golden wedding anniversary, the group portrait records the Danish dynastic ties that linked the courts of Britain, Russia, and Greece.
collection-of-beauty-claudemonetbanksoft: The Seine winds past the village of Lavacourt across from Vétheuil, where Monet lived from 1878. The riverbank views he painted there mark the brightening palette and broken brushwork of his fully mature Impressionism, made under the strain of his wife Camille's final illness.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-1867-r: Sailboats and onlookers crowd the beach at Sainte-Adresse, the Norman resort where Monet spent the summer with relatives. The crisp horizontals and bright local colour are still indebted to Boudin and Jongkind, but already announce the open-air manner that Impressionism would soon codify.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-1870-l: Painted on the Norman coast during Monet's summer honeymoon with Camille, the breakwater at low tide is laid down in rapid, salt-scratched brushwork. Sand was famously found mixed into the paint of his Trouville canvases, a record of their plein-air making in the wind off the Channel.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-1875-t: A locomotive idles at the Argenteuil station, its dark bulk and red signal lamps set against snow and bare trees. Monet returned often to the new railway as a subject, treating engine smoke and winter light as kindred atmospheric phenomena; the canvas is now at the Musée Marmottan Monet, Paris.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-adolph: The painter's father sits in a straw hat among flowering plants in the family's seaside garden at Sainte-Adresse. Painted during the same productive Norman summer as the Regatta, the picture combines a tender domestic portrait with Monet's emerging interest in dappled outdoor light.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-fishin: Sail-rigged fishing boats slip out of Le Havre on a misty morning, the harbour walls and shipping reduced to muted silhouettes. Monet painted the port of his boyhood repeatedly in the early 1870s, and works of this kind directly preceded Impression, Sunrise of 1872.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-le-gra: Looking down the Grand Canal toward Santa Maria della Salute, Monet renders Venice as drifting reflections of pink and turquoise on water. The view comes from his only visit to the city, in autumn 1908, which yielded a late series exhibited at Bernheim-Jeune in 1912.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-french: An early still life arranging garden blooms alongside ripe pears and grapes on a draped surface. Painted before Monet committed fully to plein-air landscape, the canvas shows him testing dense impasto and saturated colour against the conventions of Salon still-life painting.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-a-corn: An interior view of the Monet family's Argenteuil home, with young Jean standing at the end of a darkened hallway between hanging plants and curtained light. The painting marks one of Monet's rare domestic interiors, balancing silhouetted shadow against a glowing window.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-a-seas: A nocturnal Channel scene with moonlight breaking through cloud over distant sailing vessels. Made during Monet's formative summers on the Normandy coast, the picture follows the marines of Boudin and Jongkind, with feathery brushwork capturing reflected silver on dark water.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-a-walk: Figures stroll through tall summer grass beside the Seine at Argenteuil, the village where Monet lived from 1871 to 1878. The high horizon and broken touch dissolve foreground wildflowers into a tapestry of pure colour, a defining experiment of his Argenteuil years.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-alice-: Alice Hoschedé, who would become Monet's second wife, stands among foliage at Vétheuil. The portrait belongs to a small group of garden figures from this transitional period, in which Monet treats the sitter as one luminous accent within a tangle of green.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-antibe: A view across the Mediterranean toward the snow-capped Alps, made during Monet's four-month campaign on the Côte d'Azur in early 1888. The pink and turquoise palette he developed here pushed his colour further than ever, anticipating the serial canvases of the 1890s.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-argent: A view of the Seine at Argenteuil, the suburban boating town where Monet settled after the Franco-Prussian War. Sailboats and reflections on calm water became his central subject here, painted from a floating studio he converted from a small skiff.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-arriva: Steam billows beneath the iron-and-glass shed of the Gare Saint-Lazare as a locomotive pulls in from the Normandy line. One of twelve canvases Monet made of the station in 1877, the series treated industrial vapour as a modern equivalent of mist on the Seine.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-automn: Riverside trees in russet and gold are doubled in the still surface of the Seine. Painted from the bank at Argenteuil, the picture exploits Monet's preferred device of mirrored reflection, the upper and lower halves of the canvas answering one another in horizontal bands.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-camill: Monet painted his first wife within hours of her death from uterine cancer at age thirty-two, the brushwork dissolving her features into a veil of violet, grey and white. He later confessed unease at finding himself analysing her colour rather than mourning her.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-champ-: A summer wheatfield treated as an almost abstract field of warm strokes under a high sky. The subject anticipates the haystacks Monet would begin painting in series a decade later, where the same agrarian motif became a vehicle for studying changing light.

Crimson poppies scatter through summer grass on the slopes above Vétheuil, where Monet lived in straitened circumstances after leaving Argenteuil. The motif of wildflowers as red dabs against green became a signature device, repeated through the late 1870s.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-cliff-: Two women in white stand on a wind-swept Channel cliff above sailing boats, their parasols echoing the curve of clouds and grass. Painted during a working summer at Pourville in 1882, the canvas balances figural anchor against the broad Norman sky.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-der-ko: A rare portrait by Monet, depicting the white-aproned cook of the Hôtel Blanche at Pourville. Painted during the same Normandy stay that produced the cliff-walk pictures, it captures the chef's ruddy face and twin chins in dense, almost caricatural impasto.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-entree: Boats crowd the harbour mouth of the Norman resort of Trouville, where Monet honeymooned with Camille in the summer of 1870. The pictures from that holiday — sand still embedded in some of the canvases — were the last he made before fleeing the war to London.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-flower: A bank of the Seine near Argenteuil shimmers under summer foliage and scattered blossom. The high-keyed greens and broken touch are characteristic of Monet's most concentrated Impressionist years, when he and Renoir painted side by side along the river.

An orchard at Vétheuil seen through a haze of white spring blossom. Monet treats the subject — long established in Japanese printmaking, which he collected — as an all-over screen of pale flecks against pale ground, the trees almost dissolving into the field.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-grayst: A single haystack rises against a flushed sky from one of Monet's most influential serial campaigns. Working from his Giverny window over the winter of 1890–91, he painted twenty-five Grainstack canvases tracking the same motif through every shift of weather and hour.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-houses: The Palace of Westminster looms through Thames fog at a moment of breaking sun. Monet painted some nineteen Parliament views from a balcony at St Thomas's Hospital between 1900 and 1904, returning to his hotel each evening to refine the series from memory and notes.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-hotel-: Holiday-makers parade beneath a row of fluttering tricolours along the boardwalk of Trouville's grandest hotel. Painted on the eve of the Franco-Prussian War, the picture is a brisk exercise in plein-air reportage, with sand still trapped in the paint surface.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-in-the: Two of Alice Hoschedé's daughters work and read in the dappled woods near Giverny, where Monet had moved in 1883. Blanche, herself a painter, would later marry Monet's son Jean and become his nurse and companion in his final years.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-interi: A lamp-lit domestic interior from Monet's youth, with figures gathered after a meal. Indoor subjects are unusual in his work; here the warm artificial light and dark-keyed palette betray a debt to the realism of Manet and Bazille more than to his later high-colour outdoor practice.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-jardin: An ornamental garden at the Norman seaside resort where Monet's aunt and family summered. The bright flower beds, brisk handling and high horizon look forward to the celebrated Terrace at Sainte-Adresse painted the following summer at the same property.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-jean-p: Monet's son Michel and stepson Jean-Pierre lie among grasses on the banks of the Epte, the small tributary of the Seine that ran past Giverny. The figures' white shirts vanish into a meadow rendered in feather-light strokes — a key example of Monet's late figural work.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-l-ete-: Camille and Jean lie among long grass and poppies under a high summer sky. The picture belongs to a small group of figure-in-meadow compositions Monet made at Argenteuil, treating the family as colour incidents within a continuous field of warm growth.

A bank of red poppies climbs a sunlit slope above figures lost in the grass. The canvas is one of several Monet devoted to the motif of wildflower meadows, the bright dabs of red functioning as both botanical record and pure chromatic accent.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-la-cor: Olive groves and pines tumble toward the Mediterranean from the cliff road outside Monaco. Painted during Monet's early 1884 trip to the Riviera with Renoir, the picture pushes into hotter, more saturated colour than anything he had attempted on the Seine.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-la-man: The vast natural arch of the Manneporte rises from the surf at Étretat, one of three rock formations Monet painted obsessively during his Normandy campaigns of the early 1880s. The view from below dramatises the cliff's mass against breaking sea.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-la-poi: Wagons and figures cross the wet sand below the headland of La Hève near Le Havre, Monet's home town. Shown at the Salon of 1865 — his official debut — the canvas owes much to the marine realism of Boudin and Courbet.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-la-sei: A bend of the Seine at Vétheuil, the village where Monet lived through the bleakest years of his middle career. The river, opposite bank and sky are resolved into three horizontal bands of broken colour, a structural simplicity that points toward his coming serial work.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-le-pal: The Doge's Palace rises from the Venetian lagoon in dissolving pinks and aquamarines, painted during Monet's two-month stay in the city. The facade is treated less as architecture than as a screen for refracted light, with broken brushwork knitting stone, water and sky into a single luminous fabric.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-le-par: One of nineteen views of the Houses of Parliament that Monet worked on from a balcony at St Thomas' Hospital across the Thames. Westminster's Gothic silhouette is silhouetted against a sun-soaked fog, the river burning with reflected colour as atmospheric haze flattens detail into pure tonal envelope.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-le-bat: The floating studio Monet had built at Argenteuil, a small cabined skiff that let him paint directly on the Seine. Reflections double the boat in rippling vertical strokes, an image of the artist's working method as much as a landscape.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-le-pon: The iron railway bridge at Argenteuil cuts across the Seine in flat planes of pale stone, a steam train trailing white smoke above. Monet treats modern infrastructure as a legitimate motif of plein-air painting, balancing its industrial geometry against the soft river bank.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-les-py: The jagged sea stacks off Belle-Île-en-Mer, painted during Monet's bracing autumn campaign on the Breton coast. Granite is built up in dense, crusted strokes against a churning Atlantic, a darker and rougher palette than his Norman seascapes.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-les-ca: Sailboats and rowers drift on the Seine at Argenteuil, the Parisian suburb where Monet lived through the early 1870s and turned regatta-day leisure into a recurring subject. Quick comma-strokes describe water and rigging with equal economy.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-neige-: A muffled village street under fresh snow, part of Monet's sustained study of Argenteuil winters. The white ground is laid in with cool blues and lilacs rather than pure white, a decisive Impressionist break from academic monochrome.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-nymphe: An early canvas in the great water-lily cycle Monet pursued at his Giverny pond for the last three decades of his life. The horizon has already vanished; only floating pads, reflected sky and submerged depth remain, organised purely by colour.

From the Giverny series, the Japanese-style pond seen close-up with no shore in sight. Lily pads punctuate broad zones of mauve and green reflection, the painted surface oscillating between water-skin and pure abstraction.

Held by the Musée Marmottan Monet, which inherited the artist's studio holdings, this Nymphéas frames the pond as an unbroken field of reflected light. Pads cluster as horizontal accents across deep blues and rose, the composition organised by colour weight rather than perspective.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-on-the: A young woman trails her hand in the water from a small skiff, her dress and the boat's interior dissolving into the river's green reflections. The high viewpoint and cropped framing show Monet's lasting absorption of Japanese ukiyo-e composition.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-pond-w: A vertical Giverny canvas in which the lily pond is rotated almost into pure surface pattern. Pinks and violets float over deep greens, the brushwork loose enough that the pads sometimes read as drifting clouds of pigment.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-poplar: From the Poplars series, painted from a flat-bottomed boat moored in the river Epte near Giverny. The slender trunks form a rhythmic vertical lattice, doubled by reflection, that anticipates the decorative serial logic of the later Rouen Cathedrals.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-poppy-: Camille and the young Jean Monet wade through high summer grass at Argenteuil, scarlet poppies dotted across the field in dabs of vermilion. A second figure-pair higher on the slope mirrors the first, knitting two moments into one composition.

A sun-flooded meadow of red poppies near Vétheuil, where Monet lived after Argenteuil. The horizon is pushed high so the field reads as a near-flat carpet of broken colour, a structure he would return to throughout his rural landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-reflec: A late, large-scale Giverny panel related to the Grandes Décorations Monet was preparing for the Orangerie. Clouds are present only as inverted reflections, the painting collapsing sky, water and lily into one immersive plane.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-regatt: Sails crowd the Seine basin at Argenteuil, doubled in vivid downward strokes that read almost as flames on the water. The painting's loose vertical reflections were among the works that helped define the Impressionist handling of moving light on water.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-regnv-: Rain over Étretat: the Norman cliffs and arched rock recede behind a grey curtain of weather, the Channel rendered in broken slate strokes. Monet often returned to Étretat through the 1880s, attracted by its dramatic chalk geology under shifting marine light.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-road-a: A sunken Norman lane cuts down toward the sea at Pourville, where Monet spent productive months in 1882. Banks of grass and gorse frame a small triangle of bright Channel water, a composition organised by the steep diagonal of the path.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-rocks-: A rocky inlet at Port-Domois on Belle-Île, painted during Monet's autumn 1886 campaign on the storm-battered Breton coast. The granite is worked in dense, dragged strokes of plum and ochre against an unusually saturated turquoise sea.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-spring: A young woman, almost certainly Camille, sits reading in dappled shade beneath flowering trees. Pink-white blossoms and green foliage are dissolved into a single shimmering screen of broken touch, an early statement of Monet's plein-air figure painting.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-still-: Game birds laid out on a bare table in muted earth tones, a rare still life from a year of personal hardship for Monet following his wife Camille's death. The handling is broader and more sober than his contemporary garden pictures.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-street: An early view of the seaside resort north of Le Havre, where Monet stayed with his aunt during a formative summer. Architecture is still drawn with relatively firm contour, but the bright Norman light and high-keyed palette already point toward Impressionist practice.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-temps-: The Channel at Pourville under settled weather, painted during Monet's first sustained Norman coast campaign. Long horizontal bands of beach, sea and sky are differentiated almost entirely by close-valued blues and pearly greys.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-ar: The road bridge at Argenteuil, rebuilt after the Franco-Prussian War, frames moored sailboats on the Seine. Monet used the same bridge in several canvases of 1874, varying the viewpoint and weather to test how a single motif behaves under changing light.

Rows of irises beneath espaliered fruit trees in the artist's own garden at Giverny, which he designed and tended as obsessively as he painted it. The diagonal furrows of mauve and violet flowers organise the canvas as a near-abstract chromatic grid.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-cl: The famous Falaise d'Aval at Étretat, with its natural arch piercing the chalk cliff. Monet, who painted this Norman site repeatedly, here works with broken touches of cream, lilac and pale green to render limestone under shifting Channel light.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-fo: The old fortifications of Antibes seen across the bay, from Monet's 1888 Riviera campaign. The southern light pushed his palette toward saturated pinks, turquoises and ochres, the Mediterranean treated as a high-keyed counterpart to his northern coasts.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-ga: A locomotive pulls under the iron-and-glass shed of the Gare Saint-Lazare, steam rolling up to merge with the smoke-stained light. Part of a twelve-canvas series for which Monet secured permission to set up his easel inside the working terminus.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-hi: The road bridge at Argenteuil propped on temporary timber scaffolding after wartime damage. Monet treats the construction site as a fully serious motif, the geometry of beams and trestles balanced against the soft Seine below.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-ho: The Gothic silhouette of Westminster dissolves into a haze of violet and orange across the Thames. Painted from a terrace at St Thomas's Hospital, the canvas belongs to Monet's London series, in which he tracked the same motif under shifting fog, smoke and sun.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-ma: A single black bird perches on a wattle gate in a snowbound Norman garden, casting a faint blue shadow on the drift. One of Monet's earliest essays in coloured shadow, the picture was rejected by the 1869 Salon and now hangs in the Musée d'Orsay.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-ra: A modern iron rail bridge strides across the Seine on concrete piers, a small steam train trailing white smoke above sailing boats. Monet repeatedly painted this junction of leisure and industry during his Argenteuil years.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-ro: A rutted country road leads the eye between hedgerows toward the village rooftops on the Seine. Painted soon after Monet settled at Vétheuil following financial collapse in Paris, the canvas favours dry, broken brushwork over tonal finish.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-va: The dry Ligurian valley opens out beneath ridge-line pines and a distant town, painted on Monet's January journey along the Riviera with Renoir. The high-key palette of pinks, ochres and lilacs reflects his fascination with Mediterranean light.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-wa: Floating pads drift across a mirror of green leaf-shadow, with no sky or bank to anchor the view. The canvas is a study toward the Grandes Décorations Monet eventually donated to the French state for the Orangerie.

Pale dawn light catches the lily pads on the Giverny pond, the water dissolving into broad horizontal sweeps of mauve and cream. The work belongs to the great cycle Monet developed in his late, near-blind years for the Orangerie ovals.

Banks of cloud are reflected on the still pond surface, the lilies riding across them like islands. Painted as Monet wrestled with cataracts, the panel forms part of his bequest of mural-scale Décorations to France.

Two weeping willows frame the composition like proscenium curtains, their trunks pale against drifts of pink and blue water. The mural-scale study belongs to Monet's late Giverny project conceived as continuous, all-encompassing decoration.
Inverted trees and clouds shimmer in the pond, lily pads punctuating the surface as the only signal of where water meets reflection. The picture pushes Monet's late style toward near-abstraction.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-wi: A brick polder mill stands across one of Amsterdam's quieter canals, its sails set against a wind-swept Dutch sky. Monet painted it during his autumn 1874 Dutch trip, a return to the country he had first sketched while waiting out the Franco-Prussian War.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-tulip-: Bands of red and yellow tulips run to the horizon under a brisk sky, with windmill sails turning at the field's edge. Monet visited the Dutch bulb fields in spring 1886 expressly to confront these violently saturated stripes of colour.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-view-o: The riverside village climbs from the Seine to its Gothic church tower, viewed from a boat moored among reeds. Monet rented a house at Vétheuil in 1878 and worked tirelessly along this stretch of water following Camille's death.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-villas: White Italianate villas nestle among twisting umbrella pines and palms above a glittering Mediterranean. Monet wrote to Alice Hoschedé from Bordighera that the colours demanded a palette of diamonds and gemstones to be rendered honestly.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-voir-l: Snow lies along the riverbank at Argenteuil, with the white plain crossed by a lone walker and faint footprints. Monet's winter pictures from this period set warm rooftops and clothed figures against muted blue-grey snow.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-vetheu: The riverside village rises from the Seine to its squat Romanesque church, mirrored in the slow water. Monet painted dozens of variants of this view during his impoverished Vétheuil years, working out of a small studio boat.

Drift ice clogs the Seine beneath the half-frozen village, painted during the brutal winter of 1879-80 when the river broke up in spectacular fashion. The chalky palette of bone-white and pewter records one of the coldest seasons of the century.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-water-: The pond surface fills the canvas edge to edge, lilies massing in clusters of rose and ivory among dark vegetal reflections. Painted as Monet's eyesight deteriorated, the picture's looser, more saturated handling anticipates mid-century abstraction.

Lily pads float in even rhythms across green-blue water, with a pale band of sky-reflection running along the upper edge. The picture comes from the first mature group of Nymphéas exhibited by Durand-Ruel in 1909.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-waterl: A horizontal screen of lily pads hovers over reflected sky on the Giverny pond. By 1906 Monet had abolished the bank entirely, treating the water as a self-contained world the eye could enter at any point.

Clusters of lilies drift on a pond shot through with violet and rose reflection, the pads rendered in short, scumbled strokes. The 1907 group is among Monet's most chromatically daring before his cataracts began to dull his vision.

Lily blossoms sit like white embers on a deep mauve pond, the surface broken only by their slow geometry. The canvas dates from Monet's last full-sighted summers at Giverny before cataract surgery would alter his colour sense.

The bridge's arches dissolve into Thames fog while the smoke-stained palette of the south bank glows behind. Monet worked on the Waterloo and Charing Cross views from a balcony at the Savoy Hotel, juggling many canvases as the light shifted.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-windmi: A girl walks the dyke path between Oostzaan and Zaandam, windmill sails turning over flat polder land. Painted during Monet's 1871 Dutch sojourn after his English exile, the picture's narrow tonal range catches a typical overcast Holland sky.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-woman-: Camille turns under a green parasol while young Jean stands among grasses on a windy bluff, painted from below so the figures rise into a billowing sky. The picture, now in Washington's National Gallery, is Monet's bid to handle the figure outdoors with full plein-air conviction.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-eglise: The Norman cliff-top church catches early sun above a mist-veiled coastline near Dieppe. Monet worked on the Varengeville motif repeatedly during his 1882 stay, treating the small chapel as a study in shifting maritime light.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-etreta: Beached fishing caïques dominate the foreground as the chalk Falaise d'Amont curves out into a green sea. Monet returned to Étretat repeatedly in the 1880s, drawn by the sculpted cliffs Courbet had also painted a generation earlier.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-021: Anglers in small boats work a quiet stretch of the Seine framed by leafy banks, painted from Monet's floating studio. The Poissy years were unhappy ones, but they yielded several of his most luminous river scenes in delicate greens and silvers.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-022: Jeanne-Marguerite Lecadre, a relative, stands among flowering beds in the family garden at Sainte-Adresse near Le Havre. The picture's bright local colour and crisp shadows show Monet absorbing the lessons of Manet and Japanese prints.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-023: Suzanne Hoschedé strides up a grassy slope, parasol angled against the wind that flattens her white dress. The picture revisits the 1875 Argenteuil composition of Camille, now reworked as a near-anonymous figure study with the face deliberately blurred.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-029: A modest fisherman's cottage perched above the Norman coast, painted during Monet's productive campaigns at Pourville and Varengeville. Broken brushwork dissolves cliff, sea, and sky into a single weathered atmosphere, the cottage anchoring the composition against the windblown grass.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-044: One of the late Giverny pond pictures, painted as Monet was preparing the monumental Grandes Décorations now in the Orangerie. Lily pads drift across a mirrored surface in which sky, willow, and water trade places, the paint laid in long, lapping strokes of green, mauve, and rose.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-051: A still life of chrysanthemums in a glazed pot, painted at Argenteuil during a season when Monet turned indoors to floral subjects. Loose, prismatic touches break the petals into shimmering points of colour, treating the bouquet with the same outdoor optics he brought to his river views.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-052: A dense bouquet of sunflowers arranged in a glazed vase, painted in Vétheuil years before Van Gogh took up the same motif in Arles. Monet builds the heavy heads from short, comma-like strokes of chrome yellow and ochre, contrasting their warmth against a cool blue-grey ground.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-camill: An intimate domestic scene of Monet's first wife, Camille Doncieux, seated at an embroidery frame in their Argenteuil home. The painter treats the patterned tapestry, dress, and wall hangings as a single woven field of colour, dissolving figure and interior into a continuous surface of touches.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-le-bat: Monet's floating studio, the converted boat he commissioned in Argenteuil so he could paint the Seine from on the water itself. Reflections of hull, mast, and bankside trees fragment across the surface in short broken strokes, the picture as much about looking from the boat as at it.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-nymphe: A large-scale water-lily canvas of 1915, painted in the studio Monet built at Giverny to accommodate his ever-bigger formats. The pond has lost its banks entirely; tangled reflections of weeping willow descend through deep blues and violets while pads float as flat islands of green.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-cl: A view of the chalk cliffs and natural arches at Étretat on the Normandy coast, a motif Monet returned to repeatedly in the mid-1880s. He works the chalk face in pinks and creams against the green-blue Channel, alert to how the light shifts across the porous stone.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-the-du: Geese gather at a farmyard pond in this rural scene from Monet's Argenteuil years, painted at the moment Impressionism crystallised around the first group exhibition. The whitewashed wall and reflecting water carry the picture's main optical work, dappled with quick, flickering touches.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-vetheu: A view of the Seine-side village of Vétheuil, where Monet settled with his struggling household in 1878. He renders the church and clustered roofs through a screen of shimmering river reflections, treating the whole town as a single envelope of haze and light.
collection-of-beauty-claude-monet-water-: A canvas from the second great wave of Nymphéas pictures, painted as Monet exhibited forty-eight pond views at Durand-Ruel's Paris gallery. Pads drift across reflections of cloud and sky, the water surface flattened into a pure field of colour with no horizon to orient the viewer.
collection-of-beauty-clearing-sky-after-: A plate from Hasui's Twenty Views of Tokyo, depicting the Sensō-ji temple complex at Asakusa under fresh snow. A leading figure of the shin-hanga revival, Hasui rebuilt the older ukiyo-e collaboration of designer, carver, and printer to record a modernising city in soft, atmospheric colour woodblock.
collection-of-beauty-cleopatra-and-caesa: Cleopatra steps from the unrolled carpet to confront Caesar at his writing table, the ancient anecdote staged with the polished historicism Gérôme made his trademark. Smooth porcelain finish, archaeologically researched props, and a controlled tonal palette anchor the picture in academic theatre.
collection-of-beauty-cliffs-at-pourville: One of Monet's 1882 views from the Norman village of Pourville, where he worked along the cliff path between Dieppe and Varengeville. Tall grasses are whipped into rapid green-and-violet strokes, the Channel pushed to a high horizon so that the cliff and sky carry most of the picture's air.
collection-of-beauty-climbing-on-mt-fuji: A late plate from Hokusai's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, showing pilgrims roped along the rocky upper slopes of the sacred peak. Hokusai records the climb itself rather than the silhouette, using the famous bero-ai imported Prussian blue for the cold air at altitude.
collection-of-beauty-confusion-of-tongue: An illustration to Genesis 11 from Doré's English Bible, depicting the workers scattering as the Tower of Babel founders mid-construction. Doré stages the scene with vertiginous architecture and a gulf of stormy sky, exploiting the dense black hatching of wood-engraving to dramatise divine judgement.
collection-of-beauty-constable-seascape-: A small open-air oil sketch of the sea off Brighton, where Constable spent summers for his wife's health. He records a single squall in rapid, loaded brushwork, the sky's grey-violet rain veil treated with the empirical attention to weather he called the chiaroscuro of nature.
collection-of-beauty-constable-salisbury: A late, emotionally charged view of Salisbury Cathedral seen across the water meadows under a rainbow, painted in the wake of Constable's wife's death. Flickering white highlights, the celebrated Constable snow, animate trees and sky in a picture that fused topographic affection with personal grief.
collection-of-beauty-cornard-wood: An early woodland scene from Gainsborough's Suffolk years, made before he turned full-time to portrait commissions in Bath and London. Drawing on Dutch landscape models, particularly Ruisdael, he threads a sandy track through scrubby oaks under a broken English sky.
collection-of-beauty-cornelia-vetterlein: A portrait from Stieler's Munich practice, where he served as court painter to the Bavarian kings. The sitter is set against a smooth dark ground in the artist's signature manner, soft modelling of the face giving way to crisp, jewel-like attention at lace and ribbon.
collection-of-beauty-cornelis-cornelisz-: Ixion plunges into Tartarus among a tangle of rebellious giants in this Haarlem Mannerist tour de force of foreshortened nudes. Cornelis exaggerates musculature and contorted pose under crisp lighting, the kind of athletic, learned composition prized by Karel van Mander's circle.

The defeated Titans tumble headlong from heaven, their nude bodies twisting around each other in a virtuoso lattice of foreshortening. Cornelis treats the mythological subject as an excuse for anatomical display, central to the late-sixteenth-century Haarlem Mannerism he developed with Goltzius and Van Mander.

A monk and a beguine paired in earthy proximity, a stock anticlerical jab popular in late-sixteenth-century Dutch genre. Cornelis softens his Mannerist drawing for the satirical mode, fixing the figures in close focus against a plain dark ground.
collection-of-beauty-cornelis-de-vos-el-: A Flemish baroque procession of Bacchus and Ariadne, with putti, satyrs, and tipsy revellers wreathed in vine. De Vos, best known for his crisp portraits of Antwerp burghers, here works closer to Rubens's mythological idiom, ordering the throng with broad, ruddy modelling.
collection-of-beauty-corner-in-voyer-d-a: A summer corner of an Asnières park from Van Gogh's Paris years, when he absorbed Impressionism and Neo-Impressionism in the company of Bernard and Signac. Short divided strokes of green, lemon, and rose lighten his palette decisively before he carried it south to Arles.
collection-of-beauty-correggio-leda-and-: An erotic mythology painted for Federico II Gonzaga of Mantua as part of a series of Loves of Jupiter intended as a gift to Charles V. Correggio orchestrates Leda's encounter in soft chiaroscuro and a continuous lateral rhythm of figures, the smoky modelling that would inspire Parmigianino and the Carracci.
collection-of-beauty-countess-siemontkow: A society portrait from Vigée Le Brun's twelve-year exile after fleeing revolutionary Paris, when she travelled the courts of Italy, Vienna, and Saint Petersburg. Soft pastel-key flesh tones, a feathered hat, and a deliberately informal pose carry forward the Marie-Antoinette idiom that made her career.
collection-of-beauty-courbet-l-hallali-d: A vast hunting picture, over five metres wide, in which hounds bring down a stag in deep snow under the eye of a mounted huntsman. Courbet draws on his Franche-Comté upbringing and the Flemish hunt tradition, scaling a popular subject up to the format of history painting in keeping with his Realist programme.
collection-of-beauty-courbet-autoportrai: An early self-portrait from Courbet's Romantic-tinged youth, the painter shown smoking a long pipe with hooded eyes and a tilted head. Strong dark-on-dark modelling and a deliberately bohemian air announce the self-mythologising persona he would build through the 1850s.
collection-of-beauty-courtyard-with-luna: A small tin-plate painting from Goya's recovery after the illness that left him deaf, drawing on a violent scene he claimed to have witnessed in Zaragoza. Naked inmates wrestle under a barred opening while a warden flogs them, the cell's harsh chiaroscuro foreshadowing the Black Paintings.
collection-of-beauty-cranach-madonna-und: The Virgin cradles the Christ Child against a dark fir backdrop, a German alpine motif that replaces Italian landscape conventions with Saxon woodland. Cranach's panel, painted for Breslau Cathedral, balances tender intimacy against the spiky verticals of conifer needles, a hallmark of his court style at Wittenberg.
