collection-of-beauty-hieronymus-bosch-tr: The desert hermit Anthony is besieged by a phantasmagoria of hybrid demons, burning villages and blasphemous rites across three panels now in Lisbon. Bosch's most influential triptych, it codified the visual vocabulary of grotesque temptation that fed Bruegel and the later Surrealists alike.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshi-yoshida-hik: Two sailing junks lean before the wind on the dazzled water of the Inland Sea. From the Setonaikai series, the print is one of six in which Yoshida had identical blocks printed in different lighting keys, demonstrating his mastery of multi-impression woodblock colour.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshi-yoshida-kum: Pale clouds of Kumoi cherry blossom hang over a Japanese garden in the soft tonal gradations Yoshida coaxed from up to thirty separate woodblock impressions. Trained in Western oil painting before turning to shin-hanga, he supervised every stage of carving and printing personally.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-traveller: Figures hurry across a wooden bridge as a slate-grey downpour streaks the air in fine parallel lines, a device Hiroshige made his signature for sudden weather. The composition's daring crop and atmospheric mood would later astonish Whistler and Van Gogh.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-53-statio: A daimyō procession sets out across Edo's Nihonbashi bridge at dawn, opening Hiroshige's Tokaido Road series for the publisher Hōeidō. The print establishes the journey's ceremonial scale before the road's weather and incident take over.

Travellers cross the open plain at Hara station while Mount Fuji rises in pink-tinged morning light, breaking the sheet's upper border. The Hōeidō Tokaido is among the most successful print series of the Edo period, sold in tens of thousands of impressions.

Three muffled figures trudge through deep night snow at Kanbara, the white sky bokashi-graded into a heavy grey. Long counted among Hiroshige's finest landscape prints, the design treats silence and stillness as subject in their own right.

Travellers approach a thatched teahouse on the windswept Sarugababa heath outside Futagawa station. Hiroshige builds the scene from broad fields of muted ochre and pine green typical of the Hōeidō Tōkaidō's middle stretch.

Workmen scaffold the Toyokawa River bridge at Yoshida station while Yoshida castle rises beyond. Hiroshige uses the diagonal of the bridgework as a vehicle for his characteristic recession into atmospheric distance.

Serving women aggressively haul reluctant travellers into an inn at Goyu, a stock comic episode of the Tōkaidō. The print is one of several in the series in which Hiroshige slips genre observation into what is nominally a landscape.

An Akasaka inn courtyard is shown in cross-section: maids preparing to receive guests on one side, bathing travellers on the other, separated by a clipped sago palm. The bird's-eye plan demonstrates the architectural curiosity Hiroshige brought to genre interiors.

Station Chiryū from the Tōkaidō series, depicting the early-summer horse fair on a grassy plain where merchants and tethered animals scatter under wide skies. Hiroshige uses subtle bokashi gradations along the horizon to evoke seasonal atmosphere; the Hoeidō publisher seal marks this as part of his career-making 1833 edition.

Station Yokkaichi shows a lone traveler bracing against the wind beside the Mie River, a hat tumbling away across the reeds. The sweeping diagonals and muted blues are characteristic of Hiroshige's Hoeidō Tōkaidō, a series that fixed the iconography of journey and weather in Edo print culture.

The penultimate station of the Tōkaidō, Kusatsu was famed for its meibutsu tateba teahouse where porters rested with fast palanquins. Hiroshige captures the bustle of arriving travelers under banners, his linework crisp and his palette anchored by the deep indigo blues of the early Hoeidō printing.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige11-hakone: Hakone, the most mountainous station of the Tōkaidō, rises in jagged, faceted peaks shaded in flat planes of color. A small daimyō procession threads through the pass beside Lake Ashi, a composition Hiroshige used to dramatize the human scale against terrain.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige19-ejiri: Ejiri, set on Suruga Bay near the Miho pine grove, looks across a calm inlet dotted with sailing boats. Hiroshige flattens the distance into bands of graded color, a hallmark of his ukiyo-e landscape mode that influenced later Western printmakers.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige20-fuchu: Travelers ford the Abe River at Fuchu, carried piggyback or on platforms by the station's licensed porters. The rhythmic figures across the shallow water exemplify Hiroshige's interest in the practical theater of travel along the Tōkaidō road.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige22-okabe: The Okabe station turns inland through the gorge of Utsu Pass, where a stony stream cuts between steep wooded slopes. Hiroshige's compressed verticals and overlapping rocks give the print a closed, intimate atmosphere unusual for the series.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige25-kanaya: Kanaya sits on the western bank of the Ōi River, reached only by porters who carried travelers across its broad flats. Hiroshige looks down on the receding scene from a high vantage, ordering the mountains and water into clean horizontal registers.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige28-fukuroi: At Fukuroi, travelers rest at a tea stand pitched beneath a leafy tree, a kettle steaming on the open fire. The genre vignette is set against a low, level landscape, demonstrating Hiroshige's gift for everyday observation within the Tōkaidō format.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige30-hamamat: Hamamatsu features a great solitary pine on a windswept field, with figures gathered around a small fire at its base. The motif distills Hiroshige's recurring concern with weather and season, the bare branches and grey sky reading as winter on the road.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige38-fujikaw: Fujikawa shows a daimyō procession entering the station under banners, met by villagers prostrated in formal greeting. The print documents the parade etiquette of sankin-kōtai, the alternate-attendance system that drove Tōkaidō traffic in the Edo period.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige39-okazaki: The Yahagi Bridge at Okazaki, said to be the longest on the Tōkaidō, stretches across the river in a single shallow arc. Hiroshige aligns the procession crossing it with the distant castle, framing infrastructure and authority in one elegant span.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige45-ishiyak: Ishiyakushi takes its name from the Buddhist temple visible among trees behind the village fields. Hiroshige's quiet rural composition, with farmers at work and a stand of pines, exemplifies the lyrical pastoral mode he interleaved with the busier station scenes.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige47-kameyam: Kameyama Castle rises above a snow-blanketed slope as a daimyō procession climbs toward the gate. The print is among the most admired snowscapes of the Hoeidō Tōkaidō, its diagonal trail and white expanse drawn with Hiroshige's restrained graphic economy.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige49-sakanos: Sakanoshita lies at the foot of the steep Suzuka pass, here dominated by the cliff known as Fudesute-yama, the 'brush-throwing mountain'. Travelers pause at a teahouse to contemplate the rock face, a scene that joins landscape and legend in Hiroshige's understated manner.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige50-tsuchiy: Tsuchiyama is shown in heavy spring rain, a daimyō procession crossing a swollen stream beneath dripping trees. The slanting rain lines and saturated greens make this one of the most atmospheric prints in the Tōkaidō series.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-1838-two-: Two mandarin ducks float together among reeds, the drake's plumage rendered in patterned blocks of vermilion, ochre, and indigo. Paired oshidori were a long-standing kachō-e symbol of marital devotion; Hiroshige treats the subject with the calligraphic economy of his late bird-and-flower work.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-a-large-w: From the late series Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces, this vertical print shows the Yōrō Falls of Mino plunging through a wooded ravine. Hiroshige exploits the tall format to stretch the cataract into a single white ribbon against deep green cliffs, with figures dwarfed at its base.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-a-shrine-: A small wayside shrine stands half-hidden in a stand of trees on an open moor, its torii a quiet vertical accent in the landscape. Hiroshige favored such modest sacred sites in his rural views, treating them as pauses in the broader rhythm of travel.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-atake-sou: From One Hundred Famous Views of Edo, figures hurry across the Shin-Ōhashi bridge as a sudden summer downpour falls in fine engraved lines over the Sumida. The compressed perspective and slanting rain made the print a touchstone for European Japonisme; Van Gogh later copied it in oil.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-full-moon: The Autumn Moon at Ishiyama Temple, from Eight Views of Ōmi, shows the bright disc rising over Lake Biwa behind the temple's wooded bluff. Hiroshige draws on the classical meisho tradition, pairing a literary scenic theme with his characteristic graded blue skies.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-heavy-rai: Night Rain at Karasaki, from Eight Views of Ōmi, isolates the great pine of Karasaki shrine as wind and rain lash its dark canopy. The near-monochrome palette and dense parallel lines give the print the hush of a literary classical theme treated in late ukiyo-e.
collection-of-beauty-hiroshige-view-of-a: Evening Glow at Seta, from Eight Views of Ōmi, depicts the Karahashi bridge stretching across the southern reaches of Lake Biwa under a flushed sky. Hiroshige uses bokashi gradations to model dusk, a printing technique that gives the series its meditative tonal character.
collection-of-beauty-hodakayama-from-the: From Yoshida's Twelve Scenes in the Japan Alps, this view of Hotaka-dake records the artist's own mountaineering observations of the Northern Alps. Yoshida combined ukiyo-e block techniques with Western tonal modeling, often printing the same blocks in different palettes to capture changing light.
collection-of-beauty-hokusai-1828-cuckoo: A cuckoo darts past a flowering azalea branch, its body sharply foreshortened in Hokusai's compact kachō-e mode. The pairing of a vocal summer bird with the seasonally apt blossom follows the conventions of haiku-illustrated prints, of which Hokusai was a leading designer.
collection-of-beauty-hokusai-1760-1849-o: Chōshi in Shimōsa Province, from Hokusai's Oceans of Wisdom series, shows fishermen working their nets beneath a heaving wall of water. The aggressive curl and sprayed crest reprise, in horizontal format, the breaking-wave motif Hokusai had perfected a few years earlier in the Great Wave.
collection-of-beauty-hokusai-manga-01: A page from the first volume of the Hokusai Manga, the artist's sprawling printed sketchbooks that catalogue figures, animals, landscapes, and curiosities. Issued from 1814, the volumes circulated widely as drawing manuals and later became a key conduit of Japanese form into European art.
collection-of-beauty-hokusai-manga-02: Another page from the Hokusai Manga, the encyclopedic woodblock sketch compendium that eventually ran to fifteen volumes. The loose, observational lines were intended as study material for students, though they read today as a portrait of Edo's everyday visual world.
collection-of-beauty-holbein-danse-macab: The opening plate of Holbein's Dance of Death, designed in Basel in the 1520s, shows the Creation that frames the cycle's moral argument. Cut by Hans Lützelburger from Holbein's miniature designs, the series compresses scriptural and social satire into woodblocks scarcely larger than a thumbprint.

A plate from Holbein's Dance of Death cycle, in which Death intrudes upon a figure of contemporary estate, abbot, merchant, peasant or king. The miniature woodcuts, executed with Hans Lützelburger's exacting cutting, were an early printed vehicle for Reformation-era social critique.

A woodcut from Holbein's celebrated Dance of Death cycle, designed in Basel and cut by Hans Lützelburger. Each compact scene stages Death intruding upon a figure of medieval estate, satirising clerical and worldly vanity with razor-sharp draughtsmanship.

Part of the Dance of Death series first issued in Lyon in 1538, where Death visits every rung of the social ladder. The miniature woodblock concentrates dense narrative into a few square inches, a hallmark of Holbein's reformist printmaking.

Holbein's pocket-sized memento mori reduces a moral sermon to a single arrested gesture: the skeleton's grasp, the victim's surprise. Cut by Lützelburger from Holbein's drawings, the prints circulated widely in Reformation Europe.

The Abbot, plate XIV of the Dance of Death, shows Death yanking the prelate by his crozier, mocking ecclesiastical pomp with characteristic Reformation bite. The dense crosshatching and crowded folds typify Lützelburger's virtuoso cutting of Holbein's designs.

Another scene from the Dance of Death series, in which Death claims a representative of the medieval social order. Holbein's tightly composed woodcuts pair grim humour with precise observation of costume and gesture.

From the Dance of Death cycle published at Lyon, this plate confronts a worldly figure with the universal leveller. Holbein's drawing translates fluently into Lützelburger's miniature woodblock technique, reading clearly at thumbnail scale.

A plate from Holbein's Imagines Mortis, conceived in Basel in the late 1520s. Death's skeletal cameo within an ordered domestic or civic scene gave the series its lasting force as moral and social commentary.

From the Dance of Death cycle, Holbein stages Death as an active intruder rather than an emblem, lunging into a tableau of contemporary life. The series helped fix the iconography of the danse macabre for the print era.

The Advocate, plate XIX of the Dance of Death, finds the lawyer pocketing a coin while Death stands ready at his side. Holbein's reformist satire jabs at venal professions, the woodcut's miniature scale concentrating its sting.

The Temptation, the second plate of Holbein's Dance of Death, depicts the serpent in Eden as the origin of mortality. Holbein opens the cycle with the Fall before Death claims figures across society in succeeding plates.

A plate from the Dance of Death, where Holbein casts Death as a sly partner pulling a complacent sitter from their station. The series, cut by Lützelburger, distilled Reformation moral teaching into pictures small enough to slip into a pocket.

From Holbein's Imagines Mortis, Death disrupts a scene of civic or domestic routine. The block's tight shading and confident outline carry Holbein's drawing into mass circulation across sixteenth-century Europe.
Another plate in the Dance of Death sequence, joining its survey of estates from pope to peasant. Holbein's compositions remain models of pictorial economy, every line bearing narrative weight.

From the Dance of Death, Holbein again pairs the living with the cavorting skeleton, their gestures locked in a brief, fatal duet. The Lyon edition of 1538 became a model for memento mori imagery for centuries.

A scene from Holbein's Dance of Death cycle, in which Death steps into the everyday with grim levity. The series remains a high point of Northern Renaissance printmaking, sharpened by Reformation-era anticlerical wit.

Plate from the Imagines Mortis cycle, where Holbein's Death overturns hierarchies one estate at a time. The miniature woodcut format encouraged private meditation on mortality alongside its public satire.

Another tableau from the Dance of Death sequence, illustrating Death's universal claim. Holbein's draughtsmanship and Lützelburger's exacting cutting give the prints their crisp, almost engraving-like precision.
From the Dance of Death series, a moment of social comedy turned funeral procession. Holbein's tiny woodblocks reward close looking, packing costume detail and pointed satire into a few centimetres.

A Dance of Death plate carrying forward the cycle's procession through every rank of society. The format - small block, pithy scene, accompanying scriptural verse in the original edition - became the template for emblem books to come.

Holbein places Death amid the routines of trade or labour, reminding the viewer that no occupation provides exemption. The Imagines Mortis remained in print across multiple Lyon editions through the sixteenth century.

The Expulsion of Adam and Eve, the third plate in Holbein's Dance of Death cycle, follows the Temptation as the source of human mortality. The angel drives the pair into a thorny world while Death takes up his fiddle to lead them onward.
Another scene from the Dance of Death, marrying biting moral commentary to compact woodcut design. Holbein's cycle gave Reformation Europe its definitive picture of mortality stalking every estate.

From the Dance of Death series, Holbein renders Death as both companion and antagonist, mirroring the gestures of the living. The plates retain a draughtsman's vivacity despite the resistance of the woodblock.
A Dance of Death plate from Holbein's celebrated cycle, the figures locked in a brief contest of will and fate. Lützelburger's cutting captures every flicker of Holbein's pen-line at miniature scale.
Part of Holbein's Imagines Mortis, the small woodcut sets a skeleton against a figure of social rank, a formula the artist varies with inexhaustible invention across the series.
From the Dance of Death, Holbein uses the miniature woodcut as a stage for a quick, devastating morality play. The cycle pairs each image with a biblical citation underscoring its lesson.

Another plate from the Dance of Death, depicting Death intruding on a moment of ordinary life or work. Holbein's Reformation-era satire is leavened by sympathy for the labouring poor among his subjects.

A scene from the Dance of Death cycle, in which Holbein's skeleton mimics or mocks the trade of his victim. The miniature woodcut carries an outsized influence on later memento mori imagery across European print culture.

From the Imagines Mortis series, this plate continues Holbein's procession through the social order. Designed in Basel and printed in Lyon, the cycle balances religious gravity with sharp-edged worldly humour.

Another woodcut from Holbein's Dance of Death, the cycle's closing plates moving toward the Last Judgement. The series remains a touchstone of Northern Renaissance printmaking, both for its design and for Lützelburger's astonishing cutting.

A woodcut from Hans Holbein the Younger's celebrated Dance of Death cycle, designed in the 1520s and first published at Lyon in 1538. Skeletons drag the living from every social rank, the moral message compressed into miniature scenes carved with extraordinary economy of line.

Plate from Holbein's Dance of Death, the cycle of small woodcuts in which Death claims figures from every estate of medieval society. The compact composition is characteristic of Holbein's collaboration with the Lyon block-cutter Hans Lützelburger, whose precise carving gave the prints their incisive bite.

From Holbein's Dance of Death, an unsparing memento mori in miniature woodcut. Death intrudes on daily life with theatrical bluntness, the densely worked black lines reflecting the early sixteenth-century northern fascination with mortality and judgement.

A page from Holbein's Dance of Death, designed in Basel and printed at Lyon in 1538. The series subjected pope, emperor, merchant, and ploughman alike to Death's hand, channelling Reformation-era anxiety through tightly composed woodcut tableaux.

From Holbein's Dance of Death cycle, in which a capering skeleton interrupts the routines of medieval life. The print's terse narrative and crisp black-line carving exemplify the small-format moralising woodcut perfected in early sixteenth-century Basel.

Plate from Holbein's Dance of Death, a sequence of moral vignettes first issued at Lyon in 1538. Each scene confronts a different rank of society with mortality, the figures animated by Holbein's economical draftsmanship and the precision of Hans Lützelburger's cutting.

An image from Holbein's Dance of Death, the German painter's most widely circulated print cycle. Death is staged as both intruder and equaliser, a theme rooted in late medieval European tradition but rendered with Renaissance compositional clarity.

From Holbein's Dance of Death, a series of forty-one woodcuts in which Death appears as skeletal companion to every walk of life. The prints became a touchstone of Reformation-era moral imagery and shaped the iconography of mortality for centuries.

Plate from Holbein's Dance of Death, designed around 1526 and printed in Lyon a decade later. Crammed into a tight rectangular field, the scene typifies the cycle's blend of social satire and pious warning.

From Holbein's Dance of Death, where Death stalks every estate of society with grim impartiality. The miniature woodcut format favoured by Holbein concentrates psychological force into a few square inches of black line.

A late plate in Holbein's Dance of Death cycle, a programme of small woodcuts whose dark humour and moral urgency defined the German Renaissance memento mori. The figures are sharply observed, the architectural settings reduced to telling props.

Plate from Holbein's Dance of Death, the influential moralising sequence designed in Basel in the 1520s. Death descends without warning on princes and labourers alike, a theme intensified by the woodcut's confined format and emphatic shadows.

From Holbein's Dance of Death cycle, in which the skeletal figure of mortality breaks into an everyday scene with sardonic relish. The series helped fix the visual vocabulary of the danse macabre for European print culture.

An image from Holbein's Dance of Death, a Reformation-era meditation on mortality issued in Lyon in 1538. Death's intrusion is staged with theatrical compression, every gesture pared to its essential meaning.

Plate from Holbein the Younger's Dance of Death, where skeletons enact a mocking equality across every social rank. The cut is sharply contoured, drawing on the Basel woodcut tradition that Holbein elevated to a new pictorial intelligence.

From Holbein's Dance of Death sequence, designed about 1526 and widely reprinted across Europe. Death's grip is sudden and absolute; Holbein's command of small-scale figure groups gives each plate the punch of a self-contained drama.
collection-of-beauty-homere-deifie-dit-a: Commissioned for a ceiling in the Louvre, this strictly symmetrical tableau enthrones the blind poet beneath a winged Victory while artists and writers from antiquity to the modern age gather as witnesses. Ingres treats the canvas almost as a bas-relief, marshalling Neoclassical contour and frieze-like balance to assert Homer's status as the wellspring of Western art.
collection-of-beauty-honjo-tatekawa-the-: From Hokusai's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, the print frames the sacred peak between stacks of squared timber on the Tatekawa canal. The geometric scaffolding of beams and ladders contrasts with workers and a kite drifting overhead, a quintessentially Edo conjunction of labour and landscape rendered in cool blue keylines.
collection-of-beauty-hugo-van-der-goes-g: An unfinished panel by the Ghent master, depicting the three kings paying homage to the Christ Child amid a richly textured architectural ruin. Van der Goes builds the scene with sober psychological gravity and minute Netherlandish detail in the brocades, gold vessels, and northern landscape glimpsed beyond.
collection-of-beauty-hugo-van-der-goes-v: A small devotional diptych pairing the Fall of Man with a Lamentation, in which Van der Goes channels acute emotional weight through pale, exhausted flesh and the slumped diagonal of Christ's body. The meticulous Eyckian observation is carried by a temperament closer to mournful introspection than ceremonial display.
collection-of-beauty-hungry-ghosts-scrol: A late Heian-period handscroll depicting gaki, the emaciated hungry ghosts of Buddhist cosmology, condemned by past greed to wander among the living unseen. Ink and colour on paper carry the doctrine in plain pictorial narrative, the suffering specters set against scenes of human everyday life in twelfth-century Japan.
collection-of-beauty-hunt-awakeningconsc: A young woman starts up from her lover's lap in a cluttered London parlour, her face caught at the instant of moral recognition. Hunt loaded the picture with Pre-Raphaelite symbolism, every object on the patterned wallpaper, the discarded glove, the cat at the table chosen to spell out the cost of a kept woman's situation.
collection-of-beauty-hyacinthe-rigaud-gi: A late state portrait of the Genoese patrician and future doge, painted by the leading French portraitist of the age. Rigaud orchestrates a cascade of velvet, silk, and lace around the sitter's confident gaze, the formula of court magnificence he had perfected for Louis XIV transposed to a Ligurian nobleman.
collection-of-beauty-hyacinthe-rigaud-po: Dubois, tutor to the future Louis XV and chief minister under the Regent, sits enveloped in a voluminous wrap of scarlet silk topped by a short fur cape. Rigaud channels the swagger of Louis XIV-era state portraiture into a study of ecclesiastical and political power, the textiles handled with the sumptuous fluency that made him the Bourbon court's favoured face-maker.
collection-of-beauty-idylls-of-the-king-: A wood-engraved illustration to Tennyson's Arthurian cycle, showing Edyrn travelling to Arthur's court with his lady and dwarf. Doré builds the scene from his characteristic stagecraft of looming forest and theatrical light, the dense black tonalities suited to the poem's medieval atmosphere.
collection-of-beauty-ike-taiga-orchids-1: A literati-style study of orchids in ink on paper, drawn with the calligraphic spontaneity that defined Ike no Taiga's Nanga painting. The plant's sparse leaves and blossoms are set down in a few rapid strokes, a Confucian emblem of refined virtue rendered with Edo-period painterly freedom.
collection-of-beauty-ildefonso-altar-the: Reverse panel of the Ildefonso Altar painted for Albert and Isabella of the Spanish Netherlands, in which the Holy Family rests in a luminous garden beneath a fruiting apple tree. Rubens links Eve's tree of knowledge to Christ's redemption through the warm Flemish flesh, billowing draperies, and pearly sky that defined his late Baroque manner.
collection-of-beauty-ilia-efimovich-repi: Eleven harnessed labourers heave a barge upstream across a vast, sun-bleached Volga sandbar, their faces individuated portraits of exhaustion and stoicism. Repin's early masterpiece announced the social conscience of the Peredvizhniki, marrying academic figure construction to a panoramic Russian landscape.
collection-of-beauty-ilja-jefimowitsch-r: A boisterous huddle of Ukrainian Cossacks dictates a profanity-laced letter to the Ottoman sultan, their laughter rolling across the tabletop. Repin worked on the picture for over a decade, building each character from ethnographic studies into a celebration of stubborn Slavic insolence and freedom.
collection-of-beauty-ilya-repin-ceremoni: An immense official commission recording a centenary session of the Russian State Council in the Mariinsky Palace. Repin marshals more than eighty individual portraits into a coherent ceremonial space, his late brushwork looser and more atmospheric than the tightly modelled realism of his earlier work.
collection-of-beauty-ilya-repin-cesar-cu: A bust-length portrait of the composer and music critic César Cui, a member of The Mighty Handful. Repin captures the sitter in a relaxed, contemplative pose with the loose brushwork and warm psychological focus characteristic of his portraits of Russian cultural figures.
collection-of-beauty-ilya-repin-dragonfl: Repin's daughter Vera perches on a rough wooden fence against a luminous summer sky, her dangling legs and bright bow giving the picture its nickname. The high horizon and outdoor light show Repin absorbing plein-air practice into the intimate Russian tradition of family portraiture.
collection-of-beauty-ilya-repin-sadko-go: Repin's graduation piece for the Imperial Academy of Arts depicts the Novgorod merchant-hero Sadko in the underwater kingdom of the Sea Tsar, choosing a bride from a procession of foreign maidens. Painted in Paris, it earned him a gold medal and signalled an early turn toward Russian folkloric subject matter.
collection-of-beauty-ilya-repin-unexpect: A Siberian exile reappears in his family's drawing room, the household frozen mid-reaction in a moment of recognition. Now in the Tretyakov Gallery, the painting is a touchstone of Russian Realism, organising psychology along sharp diagonal sightlines that link the returnee to mother, wife, and uncomprehending children.
collection-of-beauty-imaginary-tour-thro: A Southern Song handscroll, nearly four metres long, leading the viewer through the misted river country at the confluence of the Xiao and Xiang. Painted in monochrome ink on paper, it belongs to the contemplative literati tradition and is held by the Tokyo National Museum.
collection-of-beauty-in-the-conservatory: A fashionable Parisian couple — Monsieur and Madame Guillemet — pause among hothouse foliage, their hands almost meeting on a garden bench. Manet exhibited the canvas at the 1879 Salon, where its crisp drawing and modern subject bridged his Realist roots and Impressionist company.
collection-of-beauty-in-the-water-by-eug: Venetian girls hike up their skirts to wade in the lagoon shallows, a favourite genre subject of Eugen von Blaas. The Austrian-Italian academician built his career on these sun-warmed scenes of Adriatic peasant life, rendered with porcelain finish and bright local colour.
collection-of-beauty-inada-kyuzo-shinsuk: A sheet from Yoshitoshi's series Eimei nijūhasshūku (Twenty-eight Famous Murders with Verse), produced with Yoshiiku in 1866–67. The print exemplifies the late ukiyo-e taste for muzan-e — bloody, theatrical scenes drawn from kabuki and historical legend.
collection-of-beauty-ingresodipusandsphi: Oedipus pauses on a rocky ledge to answer the riddle of the Sphinx, his polished nude profile set against deep shadow. Painted in Rome as part of Ingres's Prix de Rome obligations, it distils his Neoclassical doctrine of pure contour and idealised anatomy.
collection-of-beauty-ingres-atelier-ange: A study sheet from Ingres's mature years preserved at La Piscine museum in Roubaix, related to his Angelica compositions. The drawing shows his disciplined pencil contour and obsessive return to the same Orlando Furioso motif over decades.
collection-of-beauty-ingres-mme-victor-b: A graphite portrait of Adeline Baltard, wife of the architect Victor Baltard, with their young daughter Paule on her lap. Ingres produced hundreds of such silvery pencil portraits for friends and patrons, prized for the precision of facial likeness against lightly indicated dress.
collection-of-beauty-ingres-estudo-de-nu: An academic male nude executed during Ingres's training under David, when he was competing for the Prix de Rome. The figure's smooth modelling and elongated proportions already announce the linear classicism that would define his career.
collection-of-beauty-ingres-academic-stu: A youthful académie made in Jacques-Louis David's studio, drawn from a posed model with painstaking anatomical observation. Such exercises were the bedrock of Ingres's Neoclassical training and prefigure the sculptural torsos in his later history paintings.
collection-of-beauty-ingres-etude-bain-t: A preparatory drawing in the Louvre's Cabinet des dessins for Le Bain turc, the circular canvas Ingres completed at age 82. The sheet shows him reworking the languid female nudes he had drawn from life decades earlier into a single dense Orientalist tableau.
collection-of-beauty-ingres-odalisque-es: An odalisque reclines on a divan while a slave plays the tanbur and a black eunuch keeps watch in a tiled harem chamber. Now at the Harvard Art Museums, this version repeats with new richness Ingres's lifelong fascination with the boneless, sinuous female nude in a faux-Ottoman setting.
collection-of-beauty-isenheim-altarpiece: A photographic record of the Concert of Angels panel from Matthias Grünewald's Isenheim Altarpiece (c. 1512–16), housed at the Musée Unterlinden in Colmar. The original shows angel musicians serenading the Virgin in jewel-coloured robes, part of the great folding altarpiece made for the monastery hospital of St Anthony.
collection-of-beauty-ivan-el-terrible-y-: The tsar cradles the dying son he has just struck down with his iron-tipped staff, blood smearing the Persian carpet. Held by the Tretyakov Gallery, the picture shocked contemporaries and was briefly banned by Alexander III for its raw psychological violence.
collection-of-beauty-j-l-gerome-the-wail: Worshippers gather at the Western Wall in Jerusalem, the great Herodian ashlar towering over them in raking sunlight. Gérôme based the picture on photographs and sketches from his Near Eastern travels, deploying the polished academic finish that defined his Orientalist output.
collection-of-beauty-jean-louis-theodore: Survivors of the wrecked French frigate Méduse strain toward a distant ship, piling living and dead bodies into a single rising pyramid. Géricault interviewed actual survivors and modelled corpses in his studio for this Salon-shaking indictment of incompetent royalist command, now anchoring the Louvre's Romanticism galleries.
collection-of-beauty-jacob-jordaens-nake: An academic figure study of an aged male model, drawn from life with the meaty corporeality typical of the Antwerp Baroque. Jordaens repeatedly used such studies as a stockpile for the satyrs, river gods, and apostles populating his crowded canvases.
collection-of-beauty-jacob-jordaens-prom: Chained to the Caucasus, Prometheus writhes as the eagle of Zeus rends his liver — a punishment renewed each day. Jordaens treats the Titan with brawny, ruddy realism, working in the Rubensian Antwerp idiom of muscular flesh and dramatic chiaroscuro.
collection-of-beauty-jacob-jordaens-psyc: On Olympus, Psyche is offered the cup of immortality that will allow her marriage to Cupid, attended by an assembly of gods. Jordaens stages the Apuleian fable with his characteristic warm palette and tightly clustered, bench-front composition.
collection-of-beauty-jacob-jordaens-selb: A late self-portrait of the Antwerp master, looking directly out at the viewer with the broad, fleshy modelling he applied to all his sitters. The panel is in the Bavarian State Painting Collections in Munich.
collection-of-beauty-jacob-jordaens-the-: A boisterous Twelfth Night feast in which whoever finds the bean in the cake reigns as king while companions roar 'The king drinks!' Jordaens returned to the proverbial subject several times, packing the table with three generations and the wet-eyed comedy of overindulgence.
collection-of-beauty-jacob-wrestles-with: A wood-engraved illustration from Doré's English Bible (1866), depicting Genesis 32 — Jacob struggling through the night with a divine adversary at the ford of the Jabbok. The dense black hatching and theatrical lighting are hallmarks of Doré's Romantic graphic style.
collection-of-beauty-jacopo-tintoretto-m: Christ's first miracle, the turning of water into wine, is staged at the far end of a steeply receding banquet table in the refectory of Santa Maria della Salute, Venice. Tintoretto exploits the long perspective to merge the painted feast with the friars' real dining hall.
collection-of-beauty-jacopo-tintoretto-t: Painted for the chancel of San Giorgio Maggiore in the last year of Tintoretto's life, the scene tilts the table on a sharp diagonal and dissolves the upper room in smoky angelic apparitions. It marks the radical conclusion of his Mannerist experiments with light and off-axis composition.
collection-of-beauty-jacopo-tintoretto-c: A photographic record of Tintoretto's Creation of the Animals (c. 1551–52) for the Scuola Grande della Trinità, now in the Gallerie dell'Accademia, Venice. God the Father sweeps across the sky propelling birds, fish, and beasts into existence in a single rushing diagonal.
collection-of-beauty-james-abbot-mcneill: Old Battersea Bridge looms in silhouette over the Thames at dusk, its piers stretched into a near-Japanese vertical. Whistler reduced river, sky, and timber to a few veils of blue and gold, treating the cityscape as an arrangement of tones in line with his Aesthetic credo.

A Nocturne in pink and grey, in which a stretch of river or shoreline dissolves into a few atmospheric washes. Whistler used these musical titles to insist that subject mattered less than the harmony of tones — the central tenet of his Aestheticist programme.
collection-of-beauty-james-abbott-mcneil: A small etching from Whistler's Venice set, produced after the artist arrived in the city following his bankruptcy. Sparing, calligraphic line on laid paper sketches a moored vessel against open water, the unworked paper standing in for sky and reflection.

Plate from the Thames Set, made when Whistler took rooms at the Angel Inn to study London's working riverfront. A balcony view crowded with rigging, mooring posts and a pair of seamen, etched with the dense, descriptive line he later abandoned for atmospheric suggestion.
collection-of-beauty-james-mcneill-whist: A small Loire-valley landscape from Whistler's late period, painted on a wooden panel during a French sojourn. The title declares the picture's true subject — a tonal duet of green foliage and silvered river — in keeping with Whistler's insistence that paintings be read as musical arrangements of colour.

Painted in London during Whistler's japonisme phase, the canvas dresses Christine Spartali in a loose kimono among Asian carpets and a folding screen. Frederick Leyland later acquired it and hung it in the dining room that became the Peacock Room, now reinstalled at the Freer Gallery.

An oil sketch on wood for an unrealised group portrait, showing Whistler at his easel with the models Joanna Hiffernan and Fumette behind him. The thinly brushed greys and creams demonstrate the tonal economy he was beginning to formulate as a programme.

Painted during the great freeze that gripped London in the winter of 1860, when Whistler was lodging at Wapping. A merchant ship sits locked among broken floes, its rigging set against a smoke-grey sky in the muted, almost monochrome palette he was developing for the river.

Made during Whistler's eight-month visit to Chile, where he had travelled to observe the Spanish bombardment of the port. Warships ride on a flat blue bay beneath a softly graded sky, a dusk study that anticipates his Thames Nocturnes by a decade.

A Thames-side view from the years when Whistler lived on Lindsey Row and was reformulating landscape as colour harmony. Fishermen and barge-poles are reduced to slim vertical accents on a horizontal field of pearly grey water and dusty pink shore.
collection-of-beauty-jan-brueghel-i-pete: From the Five Senses cycle painted with Jan Brueghel the Elder for the Archdukes Albert and Isabella in Brussels. Brueghel laid in the encyclopaedic gallery interior crammed with instruments, music books and singing birds; Rubens contributed the figure of Venus listening to a concert of Cupids.

Sight, the most celebrated of the Five Senses series produced jointly with Jan Brueghel the Elder, sets Venus and Cupid in a kunstkammer crowded with paintings, scientific instruments and rarities. The picture doubles as an inventory of the Archdukes' collection in Brussels.

Brueghel and Rubens collaborated on the Five Senses for the regents of the Spanish Netherlands, this panel devoted to scent. A garden chamber overflows with potted carnations, lilies and roses, civets and a perfumer's still, around a Venus modelled by Rubens.
collection-of-beauty-jan-brueghel-de-oud: A double-handed cabinet picture in which Jan Brueghel the Elder painted the lush menagerie of Eden — leopards, parrots, horses, fish — while Rubens contributed the soft, full-bodied figures of Adam and Eve at the Tree. Now in the Mauritshuis in The Hague.
collection-of-beauty-jan-cossiers-narcis: The Antwerp Baroque painter, a pupil of Cornelis de Vos and a follower of Rubens, takes up the Ovidian theme of the youth fixed by his own reflection. The half-length composition turns on a strong oblique light that picks out flesh and stream against deep shadow.
collection-of-beauty-jan-vermeer-the-art: An allegory of painting in which an artist seen from behind sets brush to a portrait of Clio, the muse of history, identified by her trumpet and laurel. Vermeer kept the canvas in his Delft studio until his death, and the heavy map of the Seventeen Provinces above the model carries political weight.
collection-of-beauty-jan-vermeer-van-del: An early interior in which a young woman reads at a leaded casement, her face echoed in the panes. Recent restoration at the Gemäldegalerie in Dresden uncovered a painted Cupid on the back wall that had been overpainted after Vermeer's death, sharpening the picture's amorous subtext.

A genteel courtship scene in which a man waits attentively while a young woman drains a roemer of wine. The stained-glass roundel at the upper window depicts Temperance with bridle and square, an emblem ironically warning against the very excess being staged below.
collection-of-beauty-jan-van-der-hoecke-: A Rubens-school battlepiece commemorating the Cardinal-Infante Ferdinand's decisive victory over the Swedes in the Thirty Years' War. The cavalry mêlée in the foreground gives way to drifting smoke and a panoramic plain, in the manner Rubens established for Habsburg state imagery.
collection-of-beauty-japanese-anemones-h: Fantin-Latour built much of his reputation on still lifes painted in his Paris apartment for the English market. Here he sets autumn-flowering anemones in a glass vessel against a flat brown ground, modelling the petals with the cool, even illumination he preferred to the Impressionists' broken light.
collection-of-beauty-jean-auguste-domini: An academic history piece showing Virgil reciting the Aeneid before the imperial family at the moment Octavia faints at the mention of her dead son Marcellus. The carefully balanced antique architecture and the chiselled outlines announce Ingres's lifelong allegiance to Davidian Neoclassicism.

A society portrait of Louise de Broglie, comtesse d'Haussonville, that Ingres laboured over for three years and that now hangs in the Frick Collection. Mirror, ribboned slippers and porcelain are arranged with linear precision, while the sitter's improbably elongated arm exemplifies the artist's controlled anatomical license.

Painted in Rome as one of Ingres's required envois to the Académie, the canvas presents a seated nude from behind, head wrapped in a striped turban. The smooth contour of the back, lifted to the surface as pure drawing, became a touchstone for the artist's later Turkish bath subjects.

An exact-size replica of Titian's Venus of Urbino that Ingres made during his Florentine years, copying directly from the original at the Pitti Palace. The exercise was both a personal homage to the High Renaissance and a way of carrying the Venetian model home to France.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-eg: Painted after Gérôme's first journey up the Nile, the canvas shows conscripts being driven across the sand under guard. The high horizon, sharp shadows and ethnographic costume detail are characteristic of his fastidious Orientalist manner.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-po: A staged Roman amphitheatre scene in which a victorious mirmillo stands over his fallen opponent and looks up at the Vestals' thumbs-down verdict. Gérôme drew on his own collection of antique armour for the gladiators' helmets and helped fix Hollywood's mental image of the arena.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-th: A Turkish dignitary mourns a dead tiger laid out on a carpet in his palace court, attended by silent retainers. Gérôme's miniaturist finish — every tile, tassel and whisker rendered without visible brushwork — was central to his reputation among Salon and American collectors.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-le: An Orientalist hammam scene in which a black servant pours water over a seated bather. Gérôme returned often to the bathhouse subject after his travels to Cairo and Constantinople, exploiting marble, brass and steam for cool tonal contrasts and an air of ethnographic reportage.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-ph: Gérôme stages the legendary trial of the courtesan Phryne, disrobed by her advocate Hypereides to sway the Athenian judges. The single sweep of light on her body and the recoiling line of bearded jurors make a deliberately theatrical machine of his archaeological reconstruction.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-py: The Cypriot sculptor embraces his ivory creation as she turns from cold stone to warm flesh from the hips upward. Gérôme also modelled the subject in polychrome sculpture in these years; the painting plays on his own dual practice as painter and sculptor.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-th: First panel of a late narrative pair drawn from an ode attributed to Anacreon, in which a drenched Cupid knocks at the poet's door on a stormy night. Gérôme's tightly drawn classical interior sets up the comic mishap that follows in the second canvas.

The companion to the rainstorm scene: Anacreon has taken the shivering Cupid in and warms him by the hearth, just before the boy will draw his bow and pierce the host's heart. Gérôme stages the moral fable with the archaeological props and porcelain finish of his late manner.

Part of a late illustrative cycle Gérôme devoted to the Greek lyric poet Anacreon, the scene shows the mischievous Cupid escaping after warming himself at the poet's hearth. Drawn in Gérôme's tight academic line, it preserves the anecdotal charm of the original Anacreontic ode.

A late narrative drawing in Gérôme's Anacreon series, picturing the aged poet dozing by the fire as Cupid hovers nearby. The composition leans on Gérôme's signature precision of contour and his appetite for ancient literary conceits rendered in tableau form.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-at: A solitary Muslim worshipper kneels on a prayer rug, the figure isolated against an austere architectural ground. The work belongs to Gérôme's earliest Orientalist forays, marked by his crisp draftsmanship and the smooth, almost lacquered surface he favoured throughout his career.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-ba: A studio portrait of an Ottoman irregular soldier, painted from a model in Paris using costumes Gérôme had collected on his travels. Now in the Metropolitan Museum, the canvas is admired for the embroidered silk turban and the jewel-bright contrast against a dark ground.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-di: The Cynic philosopher squats inside his earthenware tub, lighting a lamp by daylight to search Athens for an honest man. Gérôme surrounds him with the dogs that gave Cynicism its name, treating the anecdote with the archaeological literalism typical of his Greek subjects.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-el: Drawn from Herodotus, the canvas catches the moment Gyges glimpses the queen of Lydia undressing while King Candaules watches from concealment. Gérôme stages the voyeuristic episode as a brightly lit interior, turning ancient text into theatrical tableau.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-ev: Worshippers prostrate themselves on the flat roof of a Cairo mosque as the day's last light slants across the architecture. The picture belongs to Gérôme's prayer series of the 1860s, in which he applied his polished panel technique to ethnographic studies gathered on his Egyptian journeys.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-le: A nude boy displays a python before a row of seated Ottoman soldiers, the tiled wall behind them rendered with archaeological exactitude. Long held up as the emblem of Saidian Orientalism after appearing on the cover of Edward Said's book, it shows Gérôme's appetite for stagecraft and luxurious surface.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-mo: A bather is attended by a dark-skinned servant in the steam of a hammam, the marble basin and brass ewer rendered with Gérôme's ethnographic precision. The subject became a recurring vehicle for him, combining Orientalist setting with academic study of the female nude.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-on: Commissioned by Baltimore collector William T. Walters, the small panel pictures a Nubian handler walking two slim sighthounds through a windswept desert. The trim silhouettes suggest Pharaoh hounds, a subject Gérôme studied during his Egyptian travels of the 1860s.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-po: Two nudes loll beside the marble pool of a tiled harem interior while a guard withdraws into shadow. Gérôme paints the architecture with a near-photographic finish, building atmosphere from the contrast of cool stone and warm flesh.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-pr: An Albanian-Ottoman irregular has dismounted from his caravan and laid a cape on the sand, prostrating himself toward Mecca in the desert's emptiness. Gérôme isolates the figure against vast horizon, a compositional habit that recurs throughout his prayer pictures.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-ri: A horseman rests against the flank of his exhausted mount in the brassy heat of the desert afternoon, barren hills behind. Gérôme exploits a low horizon and brushed-smooth surface to evoke the still, shimmering air of his North African travels.

A small file of riders pushes across the open Egyptian sand under a hard noon sun, the line of barren hills receding behind them. The intimate panel format and tightly drawn silhouettes are characteristic of Gérôme's traveller scenes from the early 1870s.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-th: Gérôme avoids the assassination itself, instead showing senators streaming from the curia while Caesar's body lies abandoned in the foreground. The cool, measured composition and almost photographic finish drew contemporary comparison to a daguerreotype of the ancient world.

A Black eunuch stands sentinel at the lattice of a waterfront kiosk while veiled women look out toward the Bosphorus. Gérôme's love of Ottoman tilework and his crisp architectural perspective shape the picture's mood of guarded retreat.

A buyer inspects the teeth of a stripped female captive in a North African market, surrounded by impassive merchants. Now at the Clark, the work is one of several slave-market scenes Gérôme staged across both ancient Roman and contemporary Ottoman settings.

A cavalryman tramples a tulip bed while soldiers behind him slash at the flower beds, an allegory of the Dutch tulipomania crash of 1636-37. Gérôme's choice of so eccentric an episode reflects his fondness for moral and economic anecdotes drawn from history.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-ti: A Bengal tiger crouches on a rocky escarpment, its eyes locked on unseen prey below. The picture exemplifies Gérôme's late animalier subjects, executed with the same enamelled precision he brought to figure painting.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-yo: Boys at prayer kneel beneath the painted vaults of an Ottoman mosque, the marble floor catching reflected light. Gérôme paints the interior with archaeological care, a habit cultivated on his repeated journeys through Cairo and Istanbul.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-01: A solo dancer raises her arms before seated Ottoman soldiers in a smoky tavern, the firelight glinting off brass and embroidered cloth. The almeh -- an Egyptian singing-dancer -- was one of Gérôme's recurring Orientalist motifs of the 1860s.

A merchant unfurls a crimson carpet for buyers in a sunlit Cairo courtyard, the surrounding walls hung with further wares. Gérôme deploys his tight academic finish to revel in the textile's pile and the architectural geometry overhead.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-co: Now at the Musée d'Orsay, the canvas pictures the three crosses of Calvary as long shadows cast across Jerusalem at the moment of Christ's death. Gérôme adopts a striking near-aerial viewpoint, abstracting the Crucifixion to silhouette and shadow.
collection-of-beauty-jean-paul-flandrin-: Commissioned by King Wilhelm I of Württemberg, the picture reclines an odalisque before a garden vista while a servant plays the lute. Ingres designed and largely painted it, with the landscape backdrop entrusted to his pupil Paul Flandrin.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-ar: A standing Albanian irregular smokes a long-stemmed pipe, his costume bristling with daggers and embroidered braid. Gérôme treats the bashi-bazouk as a costume study, the kind of single-figure ethnographic exercise he repeated through the 1860s.
collection-of-beauty-jean-auguste-domini: A reclining concubine turns her head over a famously elongated back -- Ingres lengthened her spine by several vertebrae in pursuit of an ideal sinuous line. Commissioned by Caroline Murat for the Naples court, the picture was scandalised at the 1819 Salon for its anatomical liberties.

A standing nude pours water from an urn at her shoulder, an allegory of a freshwater spring. Ingres began the picture in Florence around 1820 and worked at it intermittently for decades; its porcelain-smooth contours epitomise his late Neoclassical ideal of feminine form.
collection-of-beauty-jean-leon-gerome-se: Set on the steps of a Roman forum rather than a 19th-century souk, the canvas shows a captive presented to bidders before a backdrop of senators. It is one of six slave-market subjects Gérôme painted, distributing them between ancient Rome and contemporary Istanbul.
collection-of-beauty-jean-auguste-domini: Pauline de Broglie is set against a tapestry-hung wall, her blue satin gown rendered with Ingres's near-fanatical attention to fabric. The portrait was painted late in his career and is now a centrepiece of the Robert Lehman Collection at the Metropolitan Museum.
collection-of-beauty-jerusalem-artichoke: Yellow blooms of helianthus rise on tall stems in a thicket of green, the canvas worked in feathery, broken touches. Painted at Vétheuil during a fertile garden period, it shows Monet's habit of treating cultivated flowers as motifs for studies in light and saturated colour.
collection-of-beauty-jeune-orpheline-au-: A young girl kneels beside a grave, her tear-streaked face turned skyward in mute appeal. Painted as a study for the Salon piece The Massacre at Chios, the work shows Delacroix's early Romantic instinct for raw psychological pathos rendered through loose brushwork and warm, earthen tones.
collection-of-beauty-jheronimus-bosch-de: The central panel of a triptych in the Prado, drawn from the Flemish proverb that the world is a haystack from which each takes what he can. Crowds claw at a giant cart of hay drawn by demons toward hell, Bosch's signature grotesques staging human greed with miniaturist precision.
collection-of-beauty-jheronimus-bosch-01: A surviving fragment of a dismembered triptych depicting passengers crammed into a small boat, drinking, singing, and grasping at food while their craft drifts aimlessly. Bosch borrows the late-medieval Narrenschiff allegory to lampoon clerical vice, populating the scene with a lute-strumming nun and a tonsured friar.
collection-of-beauty-jheronimus-bosch-05: The right wing of the dismantled Wayfarer triptych, now in the National Gallery of Art, Washington. A dying man hesitates between an angel at his pillow and a demon offering a sack of gold, while Death enters at the door — a memento mori rendered with the artist's cool, lapidary palette.
collection-of-beauty-joachim-patinir-st-: The giant ferryman wades a river bearing the Christ Child on his shoulder, the saint's labor dwarfed by Patinir's signature world-landscape stretching to a hazy blue horizon. The Antwerp master pioneered this compositional formula in which figures recede before vast geological panoramas.
collection-of-beauty-joachim-patinir-res: Mary nurses the infant Christ in a meadow as Joseph rests nearby, the Holy Family settled into a sweeping panorama threaded with rivers and jagged blue rock formations. Patinir treats the devotional subject as a pretext for landscape, layering brown foreground, green middle ground, and azure distance.
collection-of-beauty-joachim-patinir-st-: The hermit saint kneels before his crucifix in a fissured wilderness, the lion at his side and a distant village dissolving into atmospheric blue. Patinir uses the eremitic theme to display his trademark world-landscape, with stratified geology dwarfing the figure of penitence.
collection-of-beauty-joachim-patinir-010: Lot and his daughters flee toward foreground rocks while behind them the cities of the plain erupt in apocalyptic fire under a livid sky. Patinir treats the Old Testament cataclysm as a stage for tectonic landscape painting, his cool blue distances offset by the lurid orange of brimstone.
collection-of-beauty-johann-heinrich-fus: The Norse god Thor wrestles the Midgard Serpent in a churning sea, muscles knotted in the elongated, theatrical anatomy Fuseli favored. The Swiss-born painter mined Eddic and Miltonic mythology for charged subjects, exaggerating chiaroscuro and pose for sublime effect.

Odysseus steers his vessel between the six-headed Scylla and the whirlpool Charybdis, his crew contorted in heroic dread. The composition belongs to Fuseli's lifelong cycle of literary illustrations, where storms of muscular figures stage classical and Miltonic catastrophe.

A horseman is overwhelmed by an enormous serpent that coils around him and his rearing mount. Fuseli specialised in nightmare iconography of this kind, drawing on Mannerist precedents to push human and animal anatomy into expressive distortion.

The sea-nymph Thetis bends in mourning over the corpse of her son Achilles, her grief amplified by the painter's sinuous neoclassical line. Fuseli reinterprets the Homeric scene through the proto-Romantic lens of sublime emotion, giving the figures attenuated, almost weightless poise.
collection-of-beauty-johannes-jan-vermee: Vermeer's largest known painting and a rare history piece, made early in his career before he turned to domestic interiors. Christ gestures toward the seated, attentive Mary while Martha presents a basket of bread; the broad handling and warm reds anticipate the artist's mature poise.
collection-of-beauty-johannes-vermeer-ge: A modest stretch of Delft brickwork, with figures absorbed in domestic chores in a doorway and an alley. One of only two surviving cityscapes by Vermeer, the picture distills the painter's quiet attention to weathered surface, mortar, and clouded northern light.
collection-of-beauty-johannes-vermeer-he: A sturdy kitchen maid pours a thin stream of milk into an earthenware bowl, the room hushed by daylight from a leaded window. Vermeer's pointillé highlights flicker on bread crusts and copper, and the lapis-derived blue of her apron exemplifies his costly pigment habits.
collection-of-beauty-johannes-vermeer-th: A scholar in a long robe leans over a celestial globe, his hand brushing its surface as if to set the cosmos turning. Painted as a pendant to The Geographer, the picture reflects Delft's culture of empirical inquiry, its still light and book-strewn study tuned to quiet concentration.

The companion piece to The Astronomer, showing a scholar with dividers paused over a chart, a terrestrial globe behind him. Vermeer drapes the figure in an exotic Japonse rok, framing seventeenth-century Dutch knowledge-making as a contemplative, light-filled pursuit.

The smallest of Vermeer's surviving paintings, focusing tightly on a young woman bent over her bobbins and pins. Threads of red and white spill from a cushion in molten blurs that anticipate optical photography, while her concentrated face is rendered with the artist's signature stillness.
collection-of-beauty-john-atkinson-grims: A wet Leeds thoroughfare at dusk, gas lamps haloed in mist and shopfronts mirrored on the slick pavement. Grimshaw built a career on these meticulously observed urban nocturnes, balancing Pre-Raphaelite detail with a Whistlerian feel for atmospheric blue-greens.
collection-of-beauty-john-constable-1776: An intimate portrait of the painter's fiancée, completed shortly before their long-resisted marriage. Constable, primarily a landscapist, treats her face with a reserved tenderness, the muted palette and soft modelling reflecting the private nature of the commission.
collection-of-beauty-john-constable-flat: A view from a footbridge on the Stour, with a barge being unhitched in the foreground beneath open Suffolk sky. Painted largely en plein air on Constable's family land, the picture exemplifies the truth-to-nature creed that underpinned his six large 'Stour' canvases.
collection-of-beauty-john-constable-harw: A breezy view of the Essex coast, with the squat Low Lighthouse anchoring a beach where small figures gather under a luminous, cloud-stacked sky. Constable's skies are scientifically observed, drawing on his sustained study of meteorology in oil sketches.
collection-of-beauty-john-constable-sali: The cathedral's spire rises through the screen of trees on a property owned by the painter's friend Bishop Fisher, who commissioned the picture. Constable renders the verdant grounds with characteristic flecks of white impasto — his 'snow' that animated foliage and water alike.
collection-of-beauty-john-constable-ston: A late watercolour showing the prehistoric stones beneath a roiling sky split by a double rainbow. Constable, working in his final years, treats the monument as a meditation on time and weather, the broken trilithons set against the moving drama of the English heavens.
collection-of-beauty-john-constable-wive: A panoramic estate view commissioned by Major-General Francis Slater-Rebow, balancing fishermen, swans, a grazing herd, and the distant house. Constable expanded the canvas at the patron's request to include a lawn and outbuildings, evidence of his pragmatic handling of the country-house genre.
collection-of-beauty-john-everett-millai: Titled from a song in Shakespeare's As You Like It, the painting shows a young woman trudging through a bleak snowbound landscape. By the 1890s Millais had moved away from his early Pre-Raphaelite tightness toward a broader, more mood-driven late style.

An autumnal Perthshire backwater under leaden skies, painted from a sandbank on the Tay near Murthly. Millais himself called this his first true landscape, and its damp, unromantic mood anticipates the meditative late landscapes that occupied his final decades.

A snow-covered Scottish vista at twilight, the windows of a distant house glowing against winter blue. Millais's late landscapes increasingly dispense with figures and narrative, trading Pre-Raphaelite detail for atmospheric tonal painting in the manner of his hero Turner.

A view of the Perthshire glen near Dunkeld where Millais routinely summered and painted. The wet leaf-strewn ground and bare birches are handled with the freer, broader brushwork of his maturity, far from the meticulous botanical inventories of his Pre-Raphaelite youth.

Drawn from Tennyson's poem after the Shakespearean character, Mariana stretches wearily at her embroidery beside a stained-glass window, autumn leaves drifting onto the floor. Millais paints the heraldic glass and velvet gown with founding Pre-Raphaelite precision, every fibre and pane separately observed.

Shakespeare's drowning Ophelia drifts among meadow flowers along the Hogsmill River in Surrey. Millais painted the riverbank botany on site over five months, then posed Elizabeth Siddal in a heated bathtub for the figure. Each bloom carries Victorian floral symbolism for grief, innocence and madness.

A young Brunswick cavalry officer takes leave of his sweetheart on the eve of Waterloo, her hand braced against the door he is determined to open. Millais paired the black death's-head hussar uniform against shimmering ivory silk to heighten the parting. Charles Dickens's daughter Kate modelled for the woman.
collection-of-beauty-john-frederick-lewi: A British traveller's tent camp pitched in the Sinai wilderness, with St Catherine's monastery faintly visible in the distance. Lewis drew on a decade of life in Cairo to render carpets, tasselled saddles and Bedouin retainers with miniaturist exactitude. The watercolour fuses Orientalist reportage with the topographical tradition.

A harem interior in Cairo, where a seated lady greets her guests amid mashrabiya screens and a sun-dappled mosaic floor. Lewis lived a decade in an Ottoman house in the city and made the filtered light through latticed windows his signature subject. Every textile, slipper and tile is rendered in jewel-bright watercolour clarity.

An early Scottish genre piece from before Lewis's Mediterranean and Egyptian years, showing crofters welcoming visitors at a Highland hearth. The picture belongs to a brief vogue for Walter Scott-inflected tartan subjects shared with Landseer. Lewis already shows his appetite for textured plaids, deer carcasses and rough stone interiors.

A late riverside scene from Upper Egypt, with figures and pack animals drawn up on a sunlit bank above the Nile. Painted long after Lewis's return to England, it draws on the cache of Cairo sketchbooks he mined for the rest of his career. The high horizon and clear ochre palette typify his desert manner.

A group of Italian pilgrims rests on the road, products of Lewis's residence in Rome during the late 1830s before he settled in Cairo. The picture catalogues regional costume with the same ethnographic precision he would later turn on the Ottoman world. It marks his transition from Spanish to Mediterranean genre subjects.

A late biblical genre piece taking its title from the Epistle of James, set within a sunlit Eastern interior. Lewis casts the scriptural episode in the Cairene domestic vocabulary he had perfected over decades. Mashrabiya light falls across patterned cushions and the bedside attendants in saturated colour.

A young Egyptian woman carries a brass coffee tray through a marble courtyard, ducking below the lintel of a sunlit doorway. Lewis stages the scene to showcase the architectural light effects he studied during his Cairo years. The metalwork, sash and tilework are executed with miniaturist watercolour precision in oils.
collection-of-beauty-john-henry-newman-b: Cardinal Newman, leader of the Oxford Movement and convert to Rome, sits in scarlet biretta and mozzetta near the end of his life. Millais paints the theologian with the brisk, grand-manner brushwork of his late society portraiture. The picture was commissioned soon after Newman received the cardinal's hat from Leo XIII.
collection-of-beauty-john-liston-byam-sh: Eve panel from a triptych on the Fall, with Adam and the serpent occupying the flanking compartments. Byam Shaw extends the late Pre-Raphaelite manner of Burne-Jones and Rossetti into the Edwardian era, with hothouse colour and stylised vegetation. The figure is set against a tapestry of curling leaves and ripe fruit.
collection-of-beauty-john-singer-sargent: A personification of the defeated Synagogue, blindfolded and clutching a broken staff, painted for Sargent's Triumph of Religion mural cycle in the Boston Public Library. The medieval iconographic pairing with a triumphant Church drew sustained protest from Boston's Jewish community. The cycle was left unfinished, partly in the wake of that controversy.

The painter Paul Helleu sketches outdoors beside his wife Alice, the canoe and grass rendered in broken Impressionist touch. Sargent painted the work at Fladbury during a summer of plein-air experiment under Monet's influence. The flushed greens and unfinished edges show him at his most explicitly Impressionist.

A circular ceiling painting for the rotunda of the Boston Museum of Fine Arts, showing the Titan stooping under the celestial sphere while the nymphs of the western garden recline at his feet. Sargent took on the rotunda decoration as a late, demanding mural commission. The composition reads from any vantage on the floor below.

Breton women and children file across the tidal flats of Cancale at low water, baskets balanced on shoulders. The Salon-scale picture established the twenty-two-year-old Sargent in Paris and won a second-class medal in 1878. The wet sand mirrors a high pearly sky in long horizontal bands of grey-blue.

Two girls in white pinafores light Japanese paper lanterns among carnations and tall lilies in a Cotswold garden at dusk. Sargent painted only in the brief twilight window each day across two summers in Broadway. The Tate canvas became the picture that rehabilitated his reputation in England after the Madame X scandal.

A languid female figure reclines on a sofa, head turned away, wrapped in shimmering ivory satin and gauze. Sargent's niece Rose-Marie Ormond was the model for several such drapery studies in the years before the First World War. The picture is essentially a virtuoso exercise in painting white folds against deep shadow.

Two friends bend over a board in a sun-shot garden during one of Sargent's Alpine summers in the Simplon. The painter spent these holidays sketching members of his travelling party in shawls, hats and Kashmiri textiles. The watercolour-like oil handling and high-keyed greens are typical of these informal late landscapes.

A street vendor stands in the cool gloom of a Venetian calle, a string of onions slung from her hand. The picture belongs to Sargent's early Venetian campaigns of 1880-82, when he prowled working-class quarters off the tourist routes. Tonal greys and a single shaft of light recall Velazquez, whom he had recently studied in Madrid.
collection-of-beauty-joseon-kang-huian-g: A Joseon scholar leans on a rocky outcrop, contemplating the flow of water below in the Daoist trope of the recluse-sage. Kang Hui-an, a fifteenth-century court official and painter, helped establish the Korean literati landscape idiom. The brushwork shows clear debts to early Ming academy masters in the Zhe school manner.
collection-of-beauty-joseph-mallord-will: Fishermen drag a boat up onto a windswept beach beneath a heavy, scudding sky. Part of the Iveagh Bequest at Kenwood, the picture sits in Turner's lifelong dialogue with seventeenth-century Dutch marine painters such as van de Velde and Cuyp. Salt spray, wet sand and weather become as much the subject as the labour.

Westminster's old Palace blazes against the night sky on 16 October 1834, watched by a crowd massed on the Thames. Turner sketched the fire from a hired boat and produced two oil versions, of which this is the Philadelphia canvas. The conflagration let him paint exactly the kind of incandescent yellow-and-red sky he had been pursuing for decades.

A vessel keels over on the East Anglian sands at low tide, attended by figures in the wet shallows. The work belongs to a group of Yarmouth sample studies Turner produced for engravers as test pieces in a more vaporous late manner. It is now part of the Turner Bequest at the National Gallery.

A rainbow arcs over Buttermere following a Lake District shower, the slate fells doubled in the still water below. Turner exhibited the picture at the Royal Academy aged twenty-three, after a Cumberland sketching tour. It is one of his earliest essays in the sublime mountain weather effects that would define him.

Local fisherwomen, the poissards, dig for lugworm bait on the broad expanse of Calais beach as the sun sinks behind them. Turner returned to the Channel coast repeatedly, treating Calais as the symbolic threshold between England and the Continent. The sky and wet sand dissolve into a single shimmering field of low light.

Harvesters work an open field on the Earl of Essex's estate at Cassiobury in Hertfordshire. The painting was a private commission produced alongside a series of watercolours of the park. It shows Turner extending his Claudean pastoral mode to a contemporary English country-house setting.

A loose oil sketch toward the large Trafalgar canvas commissioned by George IV for St James's Palace. Turner stitched together multiple moments of the engagement onto a single mast-cluttered composition. The freedom of brushwork here, intended only as a working study, anticipates his later, looser exhibited manner.

Boats tow a damaged sailing ship into the imaginary Dutch harbour of Port Ruysdael, Turner's homage to Jacob van Ruisdael. Painted late in his career, the canvas dissolves rigging and breaking sea into broad sweeps of grey, white and salt. It is one of several pictures in which Turner used invented Dutch place-names as a critical tribute.

Grenoble lies between the Drac and the distant snowfields of Mont Blanc, drawn during Turner's first Continental tour after the Peace of Amiens reopened France. The 1802 Alpine campaign permanently reshaped his sense of geological scale. The picture's stretched horizontal format prefigures his later panoramic mountain views.

A small, intimate study of a figure asleep, head sunk against bedding, kept in Turner's studio and bequeathed to the nation. The looseness of touch is closer to the colour-beginnings of his late watercolours than to anything he exhibited. It belongs to a private corpus of figurative sketches now in the Tate's Turner Bequest.

An Italian hill town rises above the misted Campagna, drawn from sketches Turner made on his second visit to Rome. The thinly washed watercolour leaves the paper itself standing in for the bleached southern light he prized.

A view across the Earl of Egremont's lake, painted under the patronage that gave Turner the run of Petworth. Cool morning haze dissolves park, water and sky into the unified atmosphere typical of his Sussex landscapes.

A long horizontal sweep of Egremont's deer park with Tillington's spire pinning the horizon. One of the studies made in the Petworth studio Egremont set aside for him, the picture is structured almost entirely by golden, grazing light.
An unpeopled stretch of cliff and surf treated as a study in elemental force. The thinly brushed sea and broken light show Turner's late shorthand for shoreline weather.

Merchant vessels ride the chop beside a stone breakwater in a dockside scene from Turner's early career, when he was building a reputation as a marine painter. The handling still leans on the Dutch seventeenth-century tradition of van de Velde.

Sails crowd the Solent off East Cowes, where Turner stayed with the architect John Nash during the 1827 Royal Yacht Club regatta. The picture belongs to a series of studies generated by that summer visit.

An oil sketch on canvas worked up while Turner was Nash's guest at East Cowes Castle. The yachts beating against the wind would be reorganised into the finished regatta picture exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1828.

A second oil sketch from the East Cowes regatta campaign, broadly laid in to test the disposition of the racing yachts. Turner used these large studies almost as colour rehearsals before committing to a finished exhibition picture.

Form is reduced to bands of gold, rose and slate water in a late, near-abstract evocation of dusk. Pictures of this kind, left in his studio at his death, anticipate by half a century the dissolutions of Impressionism.

An English river caught at the end of the day, with rushes and a low bank silhouetted against burning sky. The work belongs to the loose Thames oil sketches Turner made from his boat around Walton and Windsor.

Vessels lie at anchor under the red Devon cliffs of the Teign estuary, a coast Turner sketched repeatedly on his West Country tours. The watercolour links to his preparatory work for the Southern Coast engravings.

A close-quarters view of Nelson's flagship Victory at Trafalgar, taken from the mizen-shrouds rather than the more conventional broadside vantage. Turner had boarded Victory on her return to the Medway and used the sketches he made there to anchor the smoke-wreathed scene.

Estuary shipping gathers where Thames and Medway meet, painted in the tradition of seventeenth-century Dutch sea pieces. Heavy cloud and a low horizon underline Turner's command of English coastal weather.

Night falls on the battlefield as women search the heaped dead by torchlight, three years after the engagement. Turner had walked the ground himself; the picture quotes Byron's Childe Harold and refuses any patriotic triumph.

A poet implores his muse in a cramped attic, an unusually literary genre subject for Turner. Exhibited at the Royal Academy with verses of his own composition, it shows him courting the hierarchy of history painting.

A vertiginous Alpine gorge above the St Gotthard road, recorded on Turner's first Continental tour after the Peace of Amiens reopened Europe. The watercolour fed a series of Swiss subjects in which scale itself becomes the drama.

A quiet reach of the upper Thames painted from the boat Turner had fitted out as a floating studio. The fluid handling belongs to the Thames oil sketches in which he loosened his manner before the public ever saw it.

Most of the panel is given over to evening sky, with foliage massed at the lower edge and Guildford Castle barely indicated. The composition foreshadows Turner's later willingness to let weather dominate topography.

The Doge's Palace and the Riva degli Schiavoni shimmer through lagoon light in a late Venetian view. By his third trip to Venice in 1840 Turner was treating the city's stones as little more than coloured vapour.

The cliff-set Umbrian town floats above its golden plain in a picture begun and finished during Turner's second Roman stay. Exhibited at his studio on the Quattro Fontane, it greeted Italian visitors as a deliberate answer to Claude.
A pure marine, the canvas is given entirely to a churning sea raked by spray. Turner reportedly had himself lashed to a mast in storms; the picture's loose, scumbled paint claims that immediacy.
Cattle wade in the slow water below a country weir in one of Turner's pastoral Thames-valley sketches. The thin oil handling and silvery palette recall his admiration for Cuyp.
A screen of willows leans over still water in an unforced piece of Thames-side observation. It belongs to the group of small oil sketches Turner kept in his studio rather than exhibiting.

The royal castle rides above the river on its chalk bluff, painted from Turner's boat on one of his summer Thames campaigns. Sky and water are set down in long, wet sweeps that leave the paint visibly mobile.

Storm and divine wrath sweep over a darkened Egyptian city as the firstborn die. An early bid for the prestige of Old Master history painting, the picture leans openly on Poussin's biblical compositions.

The ruined Forum, then still grazed and known as the Campo Vaccino, glows under a luminous Roman evening. Painted as a pendant to Ancient Rome, it gave Turner one of his last great meditations on the city's layered time.

The Dutch admiral Maarten Tromp puts his ship about in heavy weather, a subject Turner returned to repeatedly in his last decade. The picture extends his admiration for the seventeenth-century Dutch marine tradition into a high-key, Romantic key.
