Agrippina steps ashore at Brundisium carrying the urn of her murdered husband Germanicus before a stage-set of imperial architecture. Conceived as the Ancient half of a paired Royal Academy exhibit, the canvas is built on Claudean perspective dissolved in Turner's own sun-soaked atmosphere.

The shattered piers of Caligula's pleasure-bridge at Baiae rise above a glassy bay in a moralised view of imperial vanity. Turner exhibited it at the Royal Academy with verses from his own Fallacies of Hope.

A tall-masted barge drifts past the island cemetery of San Michele in the Venetian lagoon. Painted alongside its Royal Academy companion the Dogano, the work converts a place of burial into a study of pearled morning light.

An early topographical watercolour from Turner's Cambridge tour, viewing Clare Hall and the soaring Perpendicular pinnacles of King's College Chapel across the Cam. Drawn at eighteen, the sheet shows the precise architectural draftsmanship that earned him admission to the Royal Academy schools.

A Thames-side prospect of the wooded Cliveden cliffs in Buckinghamshire, the river curling beneath in pale silver. Turner repeatedly returned to the Thames as a quintessentially English subject, here softening topography into atmospheric haze.

An apocalyptic sketch from the Book of Revelation, the spectral rider looming from a vortex of dark pigment. Painted around the time of his wife's death, the canvas pushes Turner's late, near-abstract handling of paint and his fascination with sublime terror.

A glassy Dutch harbour bathed in golden morning light, paying open homage to Aelbert Cuyp, whose paintings Turner studied on his 1817 Low Countries tour. The becalmed packet boat anchors a luminous sky that occupies more than half the canvas.

The thundering Rheinfall in Switzerland, its spray rising into a churning sky encountered on Turner's first Continental tour after the Peace of Amiens. The painting marks his early engagement with the Alpine sublime that would shape his Romantic vocabulary.

A modest coastal cottage on the Kentish cliffs, rendered in the picturesque idiom Turner absorbed from Thomas Girtin and the watercolour tradition. The sheet dates from his teenage years working up topographical views for the print market.

Turner's first oil exhibited at the Royal Academy, depicting a fishing boat tossed beneath a cold moon off the Needles, Isle of Wight. The contrast of silver lunar light and orange lantern glow announces a lifelong preoccupation with luminous opposition.

Estuarine fishermen at low water on the Thames, the horizon line dropped to expose a vast, muted sky. The subdued grey-and-cream palette reflects Turner's study of seventeenth-century Dutch marine painters such as van de Velde.

Tyneside coal-heavers loading colliers under a full moon at Shields, painted as a pendant to a Venetian scene to contrast industrial north and southern lagoon. The torchlit smoke and silvered water typify Turner's late nocturnes.

The ruined Stewart royal palace above its loch in West Lothian, drawn from Turner's Scottish sketching tours undertaken for engraved topographical series. Soft watercolour washes blend the masonry into the surrounding landscape.

A panoramic survey of the capital seen from the hill at Greenwich, with the Queen's House and Wren's Royal Hospital below and St Paul's hazed in coal-smoke distance. The painting belongs to a series celebrating the Thames as the artery of empire.

The Augustinian ruin on the Wey in Surrey, its broken walls reflected in still water. Turner mined such Gothic remains for the picturesque subjects favoured by Romantic patrons and the engraving trade.

The Northumbrian border keep dissolving into rose and gold mist above the Tweed, a motif Turner painted repeatedly across his career. The late oil reduces architecture and cattle to translucent veils of pigment, anticipating modern abstraction.

A house portrait commissioned by the third Earl of Darlington, set in his County Durham deer park with a hunt in the foreground. Turner devotes the upper canvas to a churning, dramatic sky, signalling his shift from topography to Romantic landscape.

Naval shipping anchored at the Nore sandbank off the Kent dockyard, with rigging and bunting catching wet wind. Turner exhibited the canvas at his own gallery, courting comparison with Dutch marine masters in scale and atmosphere.

Working vessels labouring in the Thames estuary chop, sails braced against a low, weather-laden sky. The painting belongs to Turner's productive run of estuary subjects answering his rivalry with the marine tradition of van de Velde.

A paddle-steamer engulfed by a vortex of snow, smoke and sea, exhibited with Turner's claim that he had been lashed to the mast to observe it. Critics dismissed the canvas as 'soapsuds and whitewash', but it remains his definitive statement on the elemental sublime.

A solitary angler beside a wooded stream, painted in Turner's youthful picturesque manner. The watercolour reflects his early apprenticeship copying after Richard Wilson and the British landscape engravers.

An eyewitness response to the conflagration that destroyed the medieval Palace of Westminster, watched by crowds along the Thames. Turner sketched the blaze from a hired boat and translated it into pyrotechnic yellows and reds against the cool Westminster Bridge.

Sussex fisherfolk hauling their catch onto the shingle, with luggers and chalk cliffs receding behind. Turner repeatedly painted Hastings during the Napoleonic years when its beach was both a working port and a fashionable resort.

A bustling Norman harbour bathed in unnaturally Italianate light, criticised at the Royal Academy for its sun-drenched palette. The painting forms part of Turner's continental port series following his post-war travels to France.

Survivors clinging to lifeboats amid mountainous green seas, the doomed vessel rolling beyond. Engraved by Charles Turner, this was the first of the artist's canvases reproduced as a mezzotint, spreading his fame as a painter of catastrophe.

A lateen-sailed fishing boat departing the lagoon at dawn, its painted sail catching pink Adriatic light. Drawn from Turner's third Venetian visit, the canvas was accompanied by lines from his unfinished poem warning of storms ahead.

A reconstructed view of the Doric temple on Aegina, imagined intact above the Aegean, painted from sketches supplied by an architect-traveller as Turner himself never visited Greece. The canvas answered the British vogue for Hellenic antiquity stoked by the Elgin Marbles.

Nelson's flagship shown in three rotated views entering Sheerness with the admiral's body aboard, after Turner sketched her at anchor. The triple study reflects his marine fascination and the national mourning that followed Trafalgar.

A Highland packhorse bridge over the Tummel, recorded on Turner's 1801 Scottish tour that supplied subjects for engraved series and Walter Scott illustrations. The watercolour balances rugged geology against an open, weather-shifting sky.

A fantastical pairing of the water-nymph Undine, drawn from de la Motte Fouqué's novella, with the Neapolitan fisherman-rebel Masaniello. Painted at seventy-one, the canvas dissolves narrative into a swirl of aquatic colour typical of Turner's late square-format mythologies.

Embarking passengers and fishwives crowd the French pier as a squall builds beyond the harbour mouth. Turner had himself crossed to Calais in rough weather in 1802, and he exhibited this large canvas to challenge the marine paintings of his French contemporaries.
collection-of-beauty-joseph-wright-an-ir: A nocturnal interior of a Midlands forge, the white-hot bloom illuminating the ironworker's family. Wright was the painter of the early Industrial Revolution, treating bellows and tilt-hammers with the same chiaroscuro reverence Caravaggio gave to saints.
collection-of-beauty-joseph-wright-of-de: A Tyrrhenian lighthouse silhouetted against a cool, full moon over the Italian coast, recalled from Wright's 1773-75 sojourn near Naples. The painting exemplifies his fascination with competing light sources, here moonlight alone setting the spectral palette.

A silvered view of the Derbyshire valley of Dovedale under a full moon, its limestone crags and quiet river rendered in a cool, near-monochrome palette. Wright pursued nocturnes throughout his career, applying the dramatic chiaroscuro he developed for candlelit interiors to the open landscape.

An aged alchemist kneels before a glowing flask as phosphorus ignites in his laboratory, alluding to Hennig Brand's 1669 discovery. Wright stages the scene with the theatrical single-source illumination that defined his Enlightenment subjects, balancing scientific wonder against gothic shadow.
collection-of-beauty-judit-decapitando-a: The biblical heroine and her maidservant pin the Assyrian general to his bed, blade drawn across his throat as blood spurts onto the linen. Gentileschi heightens the Caravaggesque tenebrism with a tightly compressed composition and the unflinching, muscular cooperation of the two women.
collection-of-beauty-judith-and-her-maid: In the aftermath of the killing, Judith pauses with sword raised while Abra wraps the severed head in a sack. A late Naples-period work, it returns to Gentileschi's signature subject in a quieter, more nocturnal register, lit by a single candle that gilds the drapery.
collection-of-beauty-judische-hochzeit-i: Guests crowd a sunlit Tangier courtyard around dancers and musicians at a Sephardi wedding feast. Drawing on sketches from his 1832 North African journey with the Comte de Mornay, Delacroix translated direct observation into a Romantic ethnography rich in textile color and architectural detail.
collection-of-beauty-kiyonobu-yamanaka-i: Two kabuki actors square off in a confrontational pose typical of aragoto bravura roles. Kiyonobu, founder of the Torii line of theatre artists, defined the early ukiyo-e print with bold contour lines and the hand-applied tan-e orange-red pigment characteristic of pre-polychrome woodblock work.
collection-of-beauty-kagesue-takatsuna-a: Two mounted samurai surge through the rapids of the Uji in their famed race to lead the charge during the Genpei War. Kuniyoshi packs the triptych with churning water and twisting horses, exemplifying the warrior-print genre (musha-e) he revived in the late Edo period.
collection-of-beauty-kaigetsudo-ando-sta: A Yoshiwara courtesan stands alone in a richly patterned kimono, hip cocked in the languid S-curve that became the workshop's hallmark. As founder of the Kaigetsudō school, Ando concentrated entirely on monumental beauties, painted in fluid ink with broad swathes of color and gold on paper.
collection-of-beauty-kajikazawa-in-kai-p: A lone fisherman casts his net from a jagged outcrop while Mount Fuji rises pale beyond the surf. From the Thirty-six Views, the design is among Hokusai's most pared-down, reducing landscape to interlocking triangles printed in the imported Prussian blue (bero-ai) that defines the series.
collection-of-beauty-kalisz-rubens: Christ's body is lowered from the cross in a diagonal cascade of pale flesh and white shroud, supported by mourning saints. Rubens returned to this Counter-Reformation subject repeatedly after his Antwerp Cathedral altarpiece, modelling the dead Christ on classical sculpture studied during his years in Rome.
collection-of-beauty-kanae-yamamoto-1915: A cow rendered in sōsaku-hanga style, its bulk built up from carved planes of color. Yamamoto launched Japan's creative-print movement, insisting that the artist personally design, carve, and print the block, and his rural subjects helped redirect woodblock practice from commercial ukiyo-e toward modernist self-expression.
collection-of-beauty-kanae-yamamoto-1926: A view at the foot of Mount Tokkō in oils, painted after Yamamoto's return from Europe where he absorbed Post-Impressionist palette and brushwork. Better remembered for woodblock prints, he pursued painting alongside his advocacy for the children's free-drawing and farmers' art movements in interwar Japan.
collection-of-beauty-kannon-riding-a-dra: The Buddhist bodhisattva of compassion rides a coiling dragon through cloudbanks, the figure modelled with the volumetric solidity of European academic painting. A Munich-trained pioneer of yōga (Western-style oil painting), Harada provoked debate by giving a Japanese religious subject the visual language of Counter-Reformation altarpieces.
collection-of-beauty-kano-hideyori-maple: Courtiers picnic beneath flame-coloured maples on the slopes of Mount Takao, eating, dancing and flirting in vignettes spread across the gold-ground screen. One of the earliest surviving Japanese genre paintings, it shifts the Kano workshop's brush from Chinese-derived ink subjects toward the everyday life of contemporary Kyoto.
collection-of-beauty-kano-eitoku-rakuchu: A panoramic bird's-eye survey of Kyoto in and around the capital, with thousands of tiny figures threading temples, markets and noble compounds amid gilded clouds. Eitoku codified the rakuchū rakugai zu format for the Momoyama elite; this pair was reportedly given by Oda Nobunaga to Uesugi Kenshin.
collection-of-beauty-kano-sansetsu-froli: Songbirds dart through the gnarled, almost calligraphic branches of a flowering plum and a weeping willow on a gold ground. Sansetsu, the eccentric heir of the Kyoto Kanō line, stylises nature into rhythmic arabesques, pushing the school's bird-and-flower repertoire toward a mannered late-Momoyama elegance.
collection-of-beauty-karl-friedrich-schi: Dawn light spreads across a placid river valley as a small boat puts out from the shore. Although the file references Schinkel, the work belongs to the meditative landscape mode of early-nineteenth-century German Romanticism, where the simple act of daybreak carries the weight of spiritual allegory.

A spired Gothic cathedral rises atop a cliff above the sea, more vision than topography. The motif - architecture as sacred outcropping - belongs to the shared Romantic imagination of Schinkel and Friedrich, where the medieval church stands in for a yearned-for unity of faith, nation and landscape.

A fortified castle commands the bend of a broad river under a luminous, theatrical sky. The composition treats architecture scenographically, the way Schinkel did in his stage designs for Berlin, while the contemplative figures and burnished light belong to the wider Romantic landscape current.

A natural rock arch frames a distant view, conceived as the Nordic pendant to a sunlit Italian landscape. The pairing stages a Romantic dialogue between the sublime German wilderness and the classical south, a polarity that runs through the era's painting and through Schinkel's own architectural imagination.

An allegorical tribute to the Prussian industrial reformer Peter Beuth, shown astride Pegasus as he soars over a panorama of human civilisation and progress. The composition fuses Romantic landscape with Enlightenment allegory, celebrating Beuth's role in the modernisation of the Prussian state.

A vast Gothic cathedral towers over a riverside town at sunset, its silhouette dwarfing the mortal traffic on the quay. The motif of the imagined medieval Dom appealed to Romantic painters as both architectural dream and patriotic emblem after the Napoleonic Wars.
collection-of-beauty-karl-joseph-stieler: The young daughter of a Greek War of Independence hero is shown in embroidered Greek costume, her dark hair loose beneath a tasselled cap. Stieler painted the portrait for Ludwig I of Bavaria's Schönheitengalerie, the Munich gallery of beauties in which the king collected idealised female likenesses.
collection-of-beauty-karolina-lizius: The shoemaker's daughter Karolina Lizius gazes outward in modest dress, painted as one of the bourgeois sitters Ludwig I added to his Schönheitengalerie. Stieler, court painter in Munich, specialised in this restrained Biedermeier portrait formula: smooth finish, neutral ground, demure psychology.
collection-of-beauty-katsushika-hokusai-: Cherry-blossom viewers gather on the bluff at Goten-yama overlooking Edo Bay, with Mount Fuji a faint cone on the horizon. Part of the Thirty-six Views, the print binds a popular hanami spot to the sacred mountain through Hokusai's careful balancing of human ritual and distant geology.

A canary alights on a bowed peony stem against a deep blue sky, accompanied by a haiku. From Hokusai's Large Flowers series for the publisher Eijudō, the print compresses the kachō-e bird-and-flower tradition into a tight diagonal, exploiting the new Prussian blue pigment for luminous backgrounds.

Pilgrims bathe in the falls at Ōyama, where Rōben is said to have purified himself before founding the mountain's temple. From A Tour of the Waterfalls of the Provinces, Hokusai abstracts cascading water into ribbons of stylised line, treating each fall as a graphic emblem of place.

Yoshitsune's retainers wash their horses beneath the Yoshino falls, the cascade splitting into braided ribbons of pale paper. The plate from the Waterfalls series fuses warrior legend with landscape, characteristic of how Hokusai layered narrative into his late topographical print sets.

The Kirifuri ("falling mist") cascade on Mount Kurokami breaks into four white tendrils against dark cliffs, with tiny travellers gazing up from below. Hokusai's Waterfalls series treats Japan's sacred falls as near-abstract patterns, the water carved as flat decorative line rather than naturalistic spray.

Travellers and packhorses pause on the Kisokaidō highway beside the Ono falls, dwarfed by a smooth blue sheet of water. From the Tour of the Waterfalls, the print pairs Hokusai's stylised, almost geometric treatment of cascades with the everyday traffic of one of Edo Japan's great post roads.

From the celebrated series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, this woodblock print depicts the towering Amida Falls cascading from a circular pool said to resemble the head of the Buddha Amida. Hokusai pairs sweeping calligraphic line with flat planes of indigo and ochre, dwarfing tiny travellers beside the rushing water.
collection-of-beauty-kawase-ebisu-11: A quiet harbour scene from the Souvenirs of Travel II series, capturing a wooden boat at the small port of Ebisu on Sado Island. Kawase, a leading shin-hanga artist, renders the wet stone and overcast sky in muted, atmospheric washes that revive the woodblock tradition for the modern era.
collection-of-beauty-kawase-tenno-ji-27: Plate from the Souvenirs of Travel III series, depicting the precincts of the ancient Shitennō-ji temple in Osaka. Kawase's shin-hanga method preserves Edo-period block-cutting techniques while indulging Western sensibilities for soft light and weather, here a damp, lantern-lit dusk.
collection-of-beauty-kawase-zojoji: View of the Tokugawa mortuary temple Zōjō-ji at Shiba, from the Twenty Views of Tokyo series. A solitary figure shelters under a paper umbrella as snow falls against the vermilion gate, a hallmark of Kawase's quiet, weather-soaked urban landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-kenko-shokei-shosho: Ink-painting from the Zen monk-painter's interpretation of the Eight Views of Xiao and Xiang, the canonical Chinese landscape suite of distant temples, mist and migrating geese. Shōkei trained in Kamakura under Geiami and brought a flecked, slightly nervous brush to the Muromachi suiboku tradition.
collection-of-beauty-khnopff-listening-t: A figure buries her face in her hand as a half-glimpsed pianist plays in the corner, the music itself unseen. Khnopff, a founding Belgian Symbolist, isolates inwardness and silence with his characteristic muted palette and severe, almost photographic restraint.
collection-of-beauty-kisokaido14-itahana: The fourteenth station on the inland Kisokaidō highway, from the celebrated series Eisen and Hiroshige produced jointly in the 1830s. Eisen contributes a snowbound village under a heavy grey sky, peasants leaning into the wind in the bokashi-graded foreground.
collection-of-beauty-kitagawa-utamaro-ha: From the Twelve Types of Women's Handicraft series, an elegant ukiyo-e study of a kamiyui dressing a client's elaborate coiffure. Utamaro's signature bijinga close-up, pale ground and supple linework foreground the intimate gestures of female labour in the pleasure quarters.
collection-of-beauty-kitagawa-utamaro-ta: Ohisa, a celebrated Edo teahouse beauty, inspects the back of her coiffure with paired mirrors during the Asakusa autumn festival. Utamaro's precise hairlines and luminous mica-dusted ground typify his bust-portrait okubi-e of fashionable contemporary women.
collection-of-beauty-kiyonaga-bathhouse-: Half of a celebrated diptych depicting women in a public bathhouse, an unusually informal subject from the Tenmei era. Kiyonaga, head of the Torii school, sets his tall, calm figures in the careful spatial recession that reformed bijinga for the next generation.
collection-of-beauty-konrad-witz-004: Saint Christopher wades through dark water bearing the Christ Child, his giant frame sharply lit against a wooded shore. Witz, working in Basel, was an early Northern realist whose granular textures and naturalistic landscape settings broke with International Gothic abstraction.
collection-of-beauty-korinsscreen: A pair of folding screens depicting the rocky pine-clad islets of Matsushima Bay, with stylised waves curling in gold-leaf and malachite green. Kōrin, leading master of the Rinpa school, treats the famed scenic site as flat decorative pattern rather than topographic record.
collection-of-beauty-kunisada-futamigaur: Sunrise over the Wedded Rocks at Futamigaura, the Shinto pilgrimage site on Ise Bay where worshippers greet the dawn. Kunisada, the most prolific Utagawa-school designer of the late Edo period, frames the scene with his characteristic robust figural drawing and vivid bokashi sky.
collection-of-beauty-kuniyoshi-utagawa-a: A waterside Edo scene along the Sumida, the river that defined the city's ferry traffic, festivals and pleasure boating. Kuniyoshi, an Utagawa-school designer best known for warrior prints, here turns to a quieter genre subject in the bold linework typical of late ukiyo-e.
collection-of-beauty-kuniyoshi-utagawa-p: Devotees perform misogi, the Shinto cold-water purification rite, beneath a thundering cascade. Kuniyoshi exploits the vertical ōban format to dramatise the columnar fall of water, a theatrical staging that recalls his celebrated heroic and supernatural compositions.
collection-of-beauty-kuniyoshi-utagawa-t: A hanging scroll depicting the arhat Handaka Sonja, shaven-headed and large-eared, summoning a dragon from his begging bowl amid a coiling rain cloud. Kuniyoshi handles the Buddhist iconography in ink and pale colour with the muscular drawing he refined in his warrior prints.
collection-of-beauty-kuroda-seiki-kohan0: Lakeside, painted at Hakone, shows the artist's wife seated by the water in a yukata, a paper fan in her lap. Kuroda, who trained in Paris, brought plein-air Impressionist colour and the high-key palette of yōga oil painting back to Meiji Japan.
collection-of-beauty-l-embarquement-pour: Lovers prepare to leave the island of Venus in golden afternoon light, a melancholic departure rather than the arrival the title might suggest. Watteau's reception piece for the Académie royale invented the genre of the fête galante, with feathery brushwork drawn from his study of Rubens.
collection-of-beauty-l-enlevement-par-pa: An early figural composition treating the rape of Persephone by Pluto in dark, churning paint and a fevered ochre-and-black palette. Cézanne dedicated the canvas to his friend Émile Zola; it dates from the violent, romantic phase that preceded his Impressionist conversion at Pontoise.
collection-of-beauty-l-enlevement-des-sa: Roman soldiers seize the Sabine women at Romulus's signal in a frieze of taut, sculptural figures arrayed before classical architecture. Poussin choreographs the violence with archaeological detail and disciplined gesture, exemplifying the rational French classicism he forged in Rome.
collection-of-beauty-l-ermitage-par-paul: Cover lithograph for the Symbolist literary review L'Ermitage, depicting a pensive woman wreathed in stylised foliage. Berthon, a pupil of Eugène Grasset, worked in soft chalky tones rather than Mucha's saturated colour, giving his Art Nouveau posters a distinctive fresco-like flatness.
collection-of-beauty-l-ultimo-bacio-di-g: Romeo, foot already on the balcony, embraces Juliet in an exchange of literary tenderness laced with foreboding. Hayez, the leading Italian Romantic painter and a quiet Risorgimento sympathiser, modelled the composition on theatrical tableaux to charge it with patriotic feeling.
collection-of-beauty-le-port-de-zaandam: The Dutch industrial port at Zaandam painted during Monet's exile from the Franco-Prussian War, with windmills and timber yards reflected in the Zaan. The shifting greys and broken brushwork mark his decisive move toward fully Impressionist landscape after the London canvases.
collection-of-beauty-la-bohemienne-endor: A lion sniffs at a sleeping Black musician beside a moonlit river, lute and water jar at her side. Rousseau's flattened forms, dreamlike disjunctions and meticulous storybook clarity show the self-taught douanier inventing a modern visionary mode admired by the Parisian avant-garde.
collection-of-beauty-la-fornarina-by-raf: Long identified as the baker's daughter Margherita Luti, Raphael's lover, the sitter wears an oriental turban and a slim arm-band inscribed with the painter's name. The picture, completed near the end of Raphael's life, has the tactile sfumato modelling and direct erotic charge of his late Roman period.
collection-of-beauty-la-liberte-guidant-: An allegorical Liberty, bare-breasted and clutching the tricolore, leads insurgents over a barricade strewn with the dead during the July Revolution of 1830. Delacroix, the standard-bearer of French Romanticism, fuses contemporary reportage with classical personification in vivid, brushy oil.
collection-of-beauty-la-seine-a-lavacour: A sunlit stretch of the Seine opposite Vétheuil, painted in the years Monet spent there following his wife Camille's death. The exhibition-scale canvas, accepted at the 1880 Salon, shows him reconciling Impressionist broken touch with the broader, calmer compositions of mid-career.
collection-of-beauty-la-seine-a-vetheuil: The Seine at Vétheuil, the riverside village where Monet settled in 1878 after leaving Argenteuil. Soft poplars and reflections built up in short, drily loaded strokes typify the more sober palette of his late-1870s landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-la-vision-de-san-hu: A collaborative panel: Rubens painted the kneeling huntsman Saint Hubert encountering the cross-bearing stag, while Jan Brueghel the Elder provided the meticulous forest setting. Such division of labour between figure-painter and landscape specialist was a hallmark of Antwerp's Baroque studio system.
collection-of-beauty-la-yole-the-skiff-r: Two young women row a vivid orange skiff on the Seine at Chatou, the river populated by sailboats and a distant railway bridge. Renoir's complementary contrast of cobalt water and chrome-orange hull, applied in feathered touches, captures the leisurely Impressionist suburb of the 1870s.
collection-of-beauty-la-famille-lafitte-: A group portrait of the Laffite family by the leading court painter of Louis XIV. Rigaud's studio specialised in elaborate full-length likenesses of French nobility, with rich drapery, polished flesh tones and carefully posed sitters that established the visual vocabulary of Bourbon state portraiture.
collection-of-beauty-la-grande-piscine-d: An Orientalist interior depicting the great bath at Bursa in Ottoman Anatolia. Gérôme combined ethnographic observation from his eastern travels with academic precision, treating tilework, steam and bathers' bodies with the same hard-edged finish that made his Eastern scenes so widely reproduced.
collection-of-beauty-la-route-de-vetheui: A snow-effect view of the road leading into Vétheuil, the Seine-side village where Monet lived from 1878. The picture belongs to his series of winter landscapes painted en plein air, the broken brushwork registering the muted blues and lilacs of low northern light on settled snow.
collection-of-beauty-lac-d-annecy-par-pa: Painted during a summer stay at Talloires, the canvas reduces the Savoyard lake and the Château de Duingt to interlocking planes of blue, green and ochre. Cézanne's structural brushwork and tilted perspective treat water and stone as equivalent geometric facets, prefiguring Cubist analysis of form.
collection-of-beauty-landscape-with-pan-: A collaborative mythological landscape in which Rubens painted the figures of Pan pursuing the nymph Syrinx while Jan Brueghel the Younger supplied the dense reed bed and wooded river setting. Such partnerships between Antwerp masters were a hallmark of Flemish Baroque cabinet painting.
collection-of-beauty-landscape-with-the-: An imaginary landscape staging Christ's parable beneath a thunder-bruised sky. Rembrandt favoured these dramatic 'heroic landscapes' in the late 1630s, dwarfing the small figures of the Samaritan and the wounded traveller within ruined towers, stormlight and tonal contrasts that recall Hercules Seghers.
collection-of-beauty-las-meninas-by-dieg: The Spanish court group portrait centred on the Infanta Margarita, her maids and dwarves, with Velázquez himself at the easel and the king and queen reflected in a distant mirror. The picture is a sustained meditation on vision, painted for Philip IV and now the cornerstone of the Prado.
collection-of-beauty-laurits-tuxen-the-n: A view of the North Sea at Højen on the northern tip of Jutland, painted near Tuxen's home at Skagen. Associated with the Skagen painters, Tuxen here forsakes the colony's clear summer light for a churning storm twilight, with thick impasto registering wind-driven spray.
collection-of-beauty-le-bain-turc-by-jea: A circular tondo crowded with bathing women, drawn together from poses Ingres had refined across half a century of nudes. Painted in his eighties for Prince Napoléon, the work distils his lifelong fascination with sinuous contour and the Orientalist harem trope into a single hothouse composition.
collection-of-beauty-le-chapeau-de-paill: A portrait long mistitled 'The Straw Hat' — the felt hat is in fact black — depicting Susanna Lunden, sister of Rubens's second wife Helena Fourment. The picture is admired for its luminous flesh painting and the deft handling of cool daylight on the sitter's blue dress and tilted hat brim.
collection-of-beauty-le-concert-champetr: A Venetian poesia of two clothed gentlemen and two nude female figures, attended by music, in a pastoral landscape. Begun by Giorgione and likely completed by the young Titian, the picture established the lyrical pastoral genre that would echo through Manet's Déjeuner sur l'herbe.
collection-of-beauty-le-debarquement-de-: From the Marie de' Medici cycle commissioned for the Luxembourg Palace, the canvas shows the queen's arrival at Marseilles in 1600, greeted by an allegorical France while Tritons and Nereids churn the water below. Rubens fuses Habsburg state portraiture with Baroque mythological invention.
collection-of-beauty-le-pont-d-argenteui: A view of the road bridge at Argenteuil, the Seine town that became the Impressionist headquarters in the early 1870s. Monet records the pleasure boats and tree-lined bank with quick comma-strokes, the rebuilt iron-and-stone span symbolising the post-war modernity of suburban leisure.
collection-of-beauty-le-bassin-aux-nymph: From the late Giverny series, the canvas crops the lily pond into a near-abstract field of floating pads and reflected sky. By 1919 Monet's failing eyesight pushed him toward broader, more saturated touches that would culminate in the wraparound Grandes Décorations of the Orangerie.
collection-of-beauty-le-havre-muma-monet: A coastal view of Fécamp on the Normandy chalk cliffs, painted during a productive Channel-side campaign in the early 1880s. Monet works the cliff face and sea in parallel ribbons of pigment, registering the cool grey-green light of the Pays de Caux.
collection-of-beauty-lebrun-self-portrai: Painted in Florence for the Uffizi's gallery of artists' self-portraits during her revolutionary exile, Vigée Le Brun shows herself at the easel sketching Marie Antoinette. The picture asserts both her professional standing as portraitist to the queen and her loyalty in the face of political upheaval.
collection-of-beauty-leda-and-the-swan-b: Gérôme treats the Ovidian myth in his characteristic polished academic manner, isolating the pale figure of Leda against a dim ground for sculptural emphasis. Trained as both painter and sculptor, he often returned to mythological nudes that doubled as exercises in modelled, near-tactile flesh.

An early treatment of the myth from Rubens's Italian years, when his study of Michelangelo and Venetian colour was reshaping his approach to the sensual nude. The composition pairs the swan's white plumage against warm flesh tones in the kind of erotically charged mythology Rubens would refine for decades.
collection-of-beauty-leda-et-le-cygne-pa: A late Venetian rendering of Jupiter's seduction of Leda, painted in Veronese's distinctive cool palette of silver-greys, dusty pinks and shot silks. The picture shows the master's assured handling of the reclining female nude within an intimate, theatrically lit interior.
collection-of-beauty-leighton-alain-char: A romantic medieval scene in which Margaret of Scotland is said to have kissed the lips of the sleeping French poet Alain Chartier in tribute to his eloquence. The High Victorian subject suits Leighton's classicising drapery and carefully balanced courtly grouping.
collection-of-beauty-leighton-god-speed: A medievalising farewell scene of a knight setting forth, his lady extending a token from a balcony above. Leighton's late Academicism distils chivalric narrative into measured silhouettes and warm Mediterranean light borrowed from his studies of Italian quattrocento fresco.
collection-of-beauty-leighton-stitching-: A young woman bends over a heraldic banner she is sewing for war, posed in profile against an architectural setting. Leighton uses the medieval-revival subject to display the sculptural drapery and serene contour that defined his presidency of the Royal Academy.
collection-of-beauty-leighton-the-king-a: Drawn from the Tennyson ballad of King Cophetua, the painting shows the monarch descending his throne to a barefoot beggar girl. Leighton handles the asymmetric encounter with marble-cool surfaces and tightly modelled drapery, in keeping with his Olympian late style.
collection-of-beauty-leo-tolstoy02: A studio portrait of Tolstoy seated in the vaulted room of his estate at Yasnaya Polyana. Repin, the leading realist of the Peredvizhniki, painted the writer repeatedly over decades, capturing here the ascetic peasant dress and inward gaze of Tolstoy's late religious phase.
collection-of-beauty-les-oceanides-les-n: A wood-engraved fantasy of the Oceanid sea nymphs rising through dark surf. Doré, working largely as a print designer, deploys his trademark high-contrast hatching to model luminous bodies against deep velvety blacks, building atmosphere from tonal extremes rather than colour.
collection-of-beauty-lille-pdba-monet-la: Painted after the spectacular Seine ice break-up of January 1880, the canvas shows broken floes drifting past the bare poplars at Vétheuil. The subject — provoked by a thaw following one of the century's harshest winters — gave Monet a chance to study fragmented reflections and pearl-grey winter light.
collection-of-beauty-lille-pdba-rubens-m: A devotional half-length of the penitent Magdalene in ecstatic grief, hair loose and gaze raised toward heaven. Painted in the years of Rubens's mature Counter-Reformation manner, the canvas exploits warm flesh against shadowy ground in the persuasive emotional register favoured by Catholic patrons.
collection-of-beauty-little-red-riding-h: From Doré's celebrated illustrations to Perrault's Contes, the wood engraving stages the child's fateful meeting with the wolf among forest shadows. Doré pioneered cinematic tonal staging in printed fairy tales, and these designs shaped the visual imagination of the genre across Europe.
collection-of-beauty-london-the-houses-o: Part of Monet's Houses of Parliament series painted from his window at St Thomas's Hospital, the canvas dissolves the Gothic Revival silhouette into mauve and orange vapour. The campaigns of 1899–1904 used the Thames fog as a coloured veil through which Monet tracked the changing hours.
collection-of-beauty-los-horrores-de-la-: An allegory painted for the Medici and addressed to the disasters of the Thirty Years' War. Mars, dragged forward by a Fury, tramples the arts and a despairing Europe, while Venus tries vainly to restrain him — Rubens's late, politically engaged history painting at its most charged.
collection-of-beauty-los-nenufares-monet: A study of water lilies drifting on the surface of the Giverny pond, where reflected sky and submerged stems dissolve any fixed horizon. Monet returned to this motif obsessively in his final decades, building dense, broken touches of pigment into a shimmering field of colour.
collection-of-beauty-lot-and-his-daughte: The episode from Genesis 19, in which Lot's daughters ply their father with wine to secure descendants after the destruction of Sodom. Rubens treats the subject with characteristically warm flesh tones and muscular, twisting figures, the canvas later extended at top and bottom in the early eighteenth century.
collection-of-beauty-lot-en-zijn-dochter: Beneath a tree the daughters press wine on their father while Sodom burns in the distance, a small dog and a still life of bread and cheese anchoring the foreground. Goltzius, the great Haarlem engraver turned painter, sets the nudes in cool, sculptural light typical of his late Mannerist style.
collection-of-beauty-louis-francois-bert: The newspaper editor of the Journal des Débats sits forward, hands planted on his thighs, in a pose that became a touchstone for nineteenth-century bourgeois portraiture. Ingres's polished surface and tightly drawn contours register every wrinkle of Bertin's waistcoat without disturbing the granite stillness of the sitter.
collection-of-beauty-louis-xiv-of-france: The Sun King at sixty-three, draped in ermine and blue velvet fleurs-de-lis, his bewigged head turned over a leg cocked in a courtly dance pose. Rigaud's state portrait set the template for European royal imagery, multiplied across embassies and palaces in countless studio replicas.
collection-of-beauty-louise-jane-jopling: The painter and writer Louise Jopling, herself a celebrated society figure, presented in a slim black gown against a muted ground. By this date Millais had moved well beyond his Pre-Raphaelite beginnings into a fluent, Velázquez-inflected portrait manner aimed squarely at fashionable London.
collection-of-beauty-low-tide-at-pourvil: Exposed sand flats stretch beneath the chalk cliffs of the Normandy fishing village, painted on one of Monet's productive 1882 campaigns along the coast. Quick, broken brushwork captures the shifting light and damp air, and X-rays reveal an earlier composition lying beneath the beach.
collection-of-beauty-luca-signorelli-por: A bust-length study of a weathered older man, painted with the linear precision and severe modelling for which the Cortona master was celebrated. Signorelli's interest in anatomical truth, sharpened by his fresco cycles at Orvieto, surfaces in every furrow of the sitter's face.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-der-j: Aged women are carried into a stone basin on the left and emerge as nubile young brides on the right, an allegory of rejuvenation popular at the Saxon court. Cranach unfolds the fantasy across a panoramic landscape with the crisp outlines and porcelain flesh tones of his Wittenberg workshop.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-h: Hercules takes the celestial globe onto his own shoulders so that Atlas may fetch the apples of the Hesperides, an Antique fable updated in slim, elongated nudes. Cranach's late style favours linear elegance and a smooth, almost enamel-like finish over Italianate volume.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-s: The Hebrew judge Samson straddles the lion and pries its jaws apart in the act recounted in Judges 14. Cranach treats the subject with the heraldic clarity and brilliant local colour favoured by his patron, the Elector of Saxony.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-w: Moses leads the Israelites through the wilderness beneath the divine pillar of cloud described in Exodus 13. A workshop production from the busy Cranach studio, the panel keeps the master's compact figure types and bright, decorative landscape.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-j: The Hebrew widow displays the severed head of the Assyrian general she has just decapitated to save her city, set against Cranach's signature inky black ground. Pearled jewels and a slashed velvet bodice betray the panel's origin as a courtly Saxon trophy rather than a devotional image.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-0: Johann Friedrich, the Lutheran champion who would lose his electorate after the battle of Mühlberg, shown here in jewelled chain and flat black cap. Cranach was court painter at Wittenberg for half a century and produced numerous variants of this loyal Reformation portrait.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-a: The first parents stand on either side of the apple tree, slim and pale against a forest dense with deer, lions and stags. Cranach made the Fall a workshop staple, prized by Lutheran patrons as a meditation on sin made flesh.

A naked Venus wears only a wide-brimmed hat and gold chain while a small Cupid, stung by bees, complains of the honey he has stolen. Cranach often paired the subject with a Latin epigram warning that pleasure is mixed with pain.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-c: The personification of Christian charity stands nude, suckling one infant while two others cluster at her feet, the traditional emblem of the virtue. Cranach reduced the iconography to slender contours and unbroken flesh, producing the composition in many small-scale variants.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-d: Naked figures dance in a ring beneath fruit trees in the mythical age of innocence described by Hesiod and Ovid. Cranach turns the classical theme into a playful, decorative idyll suited to the humanist tastes of the Saxon court.

Saint Barbara kneels as her own father raises the sword, the executioner's act that, according to legend, struck him down with lightning. The early panel shows Cranach still close to the dramatic, expressive line of the Danube School.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-h: The Saxon court drives stags into the Elbe before the towers of Schloss Hartenfels, with the Elector and his guests stationed at crossbows along the bank. Cranach made several versions of this hunt, half topographical record and half dynastic propaganda.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-k: The Virgin and Saint John mourn beneath the crucified Christ in a windswept landscape that owes much to the young Cranach's time in Vienna. The agitated drapery and emotional pitch place the panel at the heart of the early Danube School.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-d-a-p: A young Wettin prince stares out in a feathered cap and gold-chained jerkin, the kind of dynastic likeness Cranach produced in pairs for the Saxon nursery. The flat black ground throws the boy's pale features and finely drawn costume into relief.
collection-of-beauty-lucas-cranach-the-e: Adam reaches for the apple Eve has plucked while the serpent watches from the branches above, the trees of paradise teeming with creatures. The panel is among Cranach's most fully developed treatments of the subject, painted for the Lutheran market that prized its theological clarity.
collection-of-beauty-ludwig-deutsch-the-: Mamluk-styled guards examine a weapon in a tiled Cairene interior, every brass boss and silk braid rendered in obsessive detail. Deutsch, an Austrian who settled in Paris, built his reputation on these meticulous Orientalist set-pieces assembled from photographs and studio props.

A black-skinned guard in scarlet livery stands sentinel before a carved mashrabiyya screen, halberd at his shoulder. Deutsch returned repeatedly to the figure of the palace doorkeeper, polishing each surface to an almost photographic enamel finish.

A street vendor of sahleb, the hot orchid-root drink popular in Ottoman Cairo, pours a cup before a tiled wall. Deutsch worked from sketches and props gathered on visits to Egypt, finishing each picture in his Paris studio with jewel-like precision.

A robed man draws contemplatively on a long-stemmed pipe in a sunlit Cairo courtyard, costume and tilework rendered with watchmaker's precision. By the early twentieth century Deutsch's Orientalist tableaux had become almost hyperreal, vivid set pieces of an imagined East.
collection-of-beauty-luisa-maria-amelia-: The Neapolitan princess, later Holy Roman Empress as wife of Francis II, painted during Vigée Le Brun's Italian sojourn after fleeing the French Revolution. The portraitist's flattering palette and soft, naturalistic informality made her a favourite of Europe's exiled royalty.
collection-of-beauty-manet-musica-en-las: A fashionable crowd in top hats and crinolines listens to a band in the Tuileries gardens, the canvas crowded with portraits of Manet's circle, including Baudelaire, Gautier and Fantin-Latour. The flat patches of black coats and abrupt cropping mark Manet's first decisive turn toward painting modern Parisian life.
collection-of-beauty-mb-mon-14-monet-str: A sun-drenched view of the old Roman road on the Italian Riviera, painted during Monet's three-month stay on the Mediterranean in early 1884. Hot pinks, lemons and mineral blues record what he described in letters as the difficulty of capturing the south's blinding light.
collection-of-beauty-mb-mon-16-monet-les: A coastal study of Les Petites-Dalles on the Normandy shore at low tide, where Monet records the chalk cliffs and exposed shingle in feathered, broken brushstrokes. The painting belongs to the artist's recurring engagement with the Channel coast, tracking shifting light and atmosphere over a familiar stretch of beach.
collection-of-beauty-ma-yuan-dancing-and: A handscroll in ink and light colour showing villagers carousing along a path beside gnarled trees, with a vast misted distance left blank to the right. Ma Yuan, a leading Southern Song court painter, was known for his off-centre 'one-corner' compositions, anchoring figures to one edge while suggesting boundless space.

A close detail of the figural group in Ma Yuan's handscroll, showing peasants linked in song with bent knees and raised arms rendered in supple, calligraphic line. The brushwork demonstrates the lean economy and 'axe-cut' angularity associated with Southern Song court painting.
collection-of-beauty-macke-august-promen: Strolling figures pass beneath trees on a sunlit avenue, simplified into faceless silhouettes against blocks of pure colour. Macke, a central member of Der Blaue Reiter, used such promenade scenes to translate everyday Rhineland leisure into the prismatic colour planes he absorbed from Delaunay's Orphism.
collection-of-beauty-macke-seiltanzerin-: A tightrope walker poised above a fairground crowd, the scene built from interlocking translucent diamonds of colour. Painted in the artist's most prolific year, the composition shows Macke fusing his Blaue Reiter sensibility with the prismatic geometry he had absorbed from Robert Delaunay in Paris.
collection-of-beauty-madame-x-madame-pie: Virginie Gautreau stands in profile in a black satin gown, her lavender-powdered skin set against a brown ground in a near-monochrome scheme. Sargent's salon submission caused such scandal in Paris over its eroticised pose and originally fallen strap that the painter eventually decamped to London.
collection-of-beauty-madeleine-undressin: Drawn from Keats's poem, Madeline undresses by moonlight on St Agnes's Eve in the hope of dreaming of her lover. Millais painted the scene by night at Knole House using his wife Effie as model, working in the cool silvered palette characteristic of his later, looser Pre-Raphaelite manner.
collection-of-beauty-madonna-and-child-g: An intimate half-length devotional image of the Virgin nursing the Christ Child, modelled in the heavy chiaroscuro Gentileschi absorbed from Caravaggio and her father Orazio. The fall of light across Mary's bowed head and the infant's body gives the picture a quiet, sculptural gravity rather than overt pathos.
collection-of-beauty-marc-franz-the-tige: A crouching tiger fractured into prismatic facets of yellow and black, its body locked into the surrounding crystalline landscape. Marc, co-founder of Der Blaue Reiter, treated animals as emblems of an unfallen spiritual world, here borrowing Cubist faceting to fuse predator and habitat into a single restless rhythm.
collection-of-beauty-maria-cristina-by-e: A Bourbon princess of the Two Sicilies depicted with the soft, flushed informality Vigée Le Brun favoured for royal sitters. Painted during the artist's long European exile after fleeing Revolutionary France, the portrait carries her trademark blend of neoclassical poise and rococo warmth.
collection-of-beauty-maria-de-tassis-by-: A standing portrait of the Antwerp heiress in a starched lace ruff and black silk gown set with jewels, her hand resting on an ostrich-feather fan. Van Dyck's Genoese-influenced full-length format and silvery handling of fabric established the elegant template he would soon export to the Stuart court.
collection-of-beauty-maria-omringd-door-: Painted as the altarpiece for Rubens's own funerary chapel in St Jacob's Church, Antwerp, the panel surrounds the Virgin and Child with saints in whose features the artist included self-portrait and family likenesses. It is among his last works, executed in the warm, fluid manner of his final Antwerp years.
collection-of-beauty-marie-buloz-pailler: A portrait of the writer Marie Buloz Pailleron painted out of doors at the family estate of Ronjoux, an unusually plein-air approach for a society commission. Sargent, then a young pupil of Carolus-Duran, sets her white dress against deep summer foliage in fluid, Manet-like brushwork.
collection-of-beauty-market-scene-on-a-q: A teeming Antwerp quayside laden with game, fish, fruit and live animals, painted on a near three-metre canvas typical of Snyders's grand market series. The Flemish Baroque specialist often collaborated with Rubens on such still-life pageants, and his workshop reproduced the format for aristocratic dining halls.
collection-of-beauty-marktjaffagustavbau: A bustling market square in Ottoman Jaffa rendered with Bauernfeind's near-photographic precision, every robe, awning and stone of the citadel sharply observed. A trained architect turned Orientalist painter, he based such scenes on extensive study trips and on-site sketches in the Levant.
collection-of-beauty-martin-schongauer-c: An engraving of the procession to Calvary thronged with mounted soldiers, jeering onlookers and dogs winding across a vast horizon. Schongauer, the leading German printmaker before Dürer, brought a refined, silvery line and unprecedented compositional ambition to copperplate engraving.
collection-of-beauty-martin-schongauer-n: An Adoration of the Shepherds set in a ruined Romanesque shed, the rough stonework and spiky straw rendered in the crisp, hatched line for which Schongauer was renowned. His Late Gothic prints circulated widely and were copied closely by the young Albrecht Dürer.
collection-of-beauty-martin-schongauer-t: The Virgin steadies the Christ Child as Joseph reaches up to gather pears from a slender tree, a quiet domestic conceit set in a walled garden. Schongauer's burin builds form through controlled parallel hatching, a technique that became foundational for Northern Renaissance engraving.
collection-of-beauty-mary-cassatt-the-ch: Seen from a steep, raised viewpoint, a woman bathes a small child in a striped basin, their bodies pressed together in tender absorption. Cassatt borrows the high vantage and patterned surfaces from Japanese woodblock prints, which she had studied closely in the Paris exhibition of 1890.
collection-of-beauty-mary-cassatt-the-te: Two women take afternoon tea in a Philadelphia parlour, the silver service gleaming in the foreground while the visitor lifts her cup to obscure her face. Cassatt presents bourgeois female ritual with an Impressionist's looseness of touch, emphasising decorative still-life passages over narrative.
collection-of-beauty-mary-stevenson-cass: A young woman in pale evening dress sits in a theatre box, her image doubled in the curving mirror of the loge behind her. Cassatt was the only American invited to exhibit with the French Impressionists, and her theatre series adopts Degas's spatial play with reflection and electric light.
collection-of-beauty-masked-ball-at-the-: Top-hatted men press around costumed women in dominoes beneath the colonnaded foyer of the old Paris Opéra on rue Le Peletier, painted only months before the building burnt down. Manet compresses the crowd into a frieze of black coats, reading the bourgeois carnival as a study in modern social ritual.
collection-of-beauty-matsue-izumo-576590: A crescent moon hangs over the still moat and tile roofs of Matsue Castle, the lights of the lakeside town reflected in pale washes of water. The print is from Hasui's 'Souvenirs of Travel' series for publisher Watanabe Shōzaburō, a flagship of the early-twentieth-century shin-hanga revival.
collection-of-beauty-matteo-ripa001-morn: A copperplate view of the Kangxi Emperor's summer retreat at Chengde, with mist breaking over the western ridge in fine engraved hatching. Ripa, a Neapolitan missionary at the Qing court, produced the imperial album that introduced Western intaglio printing techniques to China.
collection-of-beauty-max-schmitt-in-a-si: Eakins's friend Max Schmitt rests on his oars on the Schuylkill River at twilight, with the painter himself rowing in the middle distance. The composition is built from rigorous perspective studies of bridges and ripples, reflecting Eakins's Philadelphia commitment to anatomical and optical truth.
collection-of-beauty-medusarubens: The severed head of Medusa lies on a rocky outcrop, her hair a writhing nest of vipers, scorpions and salamanders. Rubens collaborated with the still-life specialist Frans Snyders on the reptilian foreground, and the panel was traditionally kept curtained in the Habsburg collections to spare visitors a sudden shock.
collection-of-beauty-mehmet-ali-pacha-de: The Albanian-born ruler of Egypt sits in a European armchair in turban and richly embroidered Ottoman dress, painted on Wilkie's late tour to the Near East. The Scottish painter died at sea on the return voyage from this commission, and J.M.W. Turner commemorated his burial in 'Peace - Burial at Sea'.
collection-of-beauty-menter-by-repin: A portrait of the Bavarian virtuoso pianist Sophie Menter, then teaching at the Saint Petersburg Conservatory, captured with the loose painterly bravura Repin reserved for his musical and literary friends. The Russian Realist treated such cultural sitters as documents of the late imperial intelligentsia.
collection-of-beauty-menzel-adolph-frede: Frederick II strides into a candlelit Silesian hall after the battle of Leuthen with a casual greeting to startled Austrian officers billeted there. Menzel built his career as the great visual chronicler of the Prussian king, working from meticulous reconstructions of period uniforms and interiors.
collection-of-beauty-menzels-schwester-e: A small, intimate study of the painter's sister Emilie asleep in a chair, her head fallen to one shoulder beneath a lamp. Menzel's private domestic oils, made off the public radar, anticipate Impressionism in their sketchy handling and unsentimental observation of momentary light.
collection-of-beauty-michelangelo-carava: A youthful musician tunes a lute at a stone table strewn with flowers, sheet music and ripe fruit, painted for Cardinal Francesco Maria del Monte. Caravaggio's tenebrist contrast and forensically observed still life details — dewdrops, a cracked pear — anchor the figure in a charged, palpable present.
collection-of-beauty-michelangelo-libyan: A red-chalk preparatory study for the Libyan Sibyl on the Sistine Chapel ceiling, drawn from a male studio model whose torsion and shoulder muscles Michelangelo refined toward the finished figure. Detail studies of the toes and a turning head fill the sheet, now held by the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
collection-of-beauty-mikhail-nesterov-00: The young Bartholomew, future St Sergius of Radonezh, encounters a black-clad monk beneath a slender oak in a hushed Russian landscape. Nesterov's debut religious canvas joins thin, lyrical brushwork with a pale northern palette, founding the devout pastoral mode he would pursue across his career.

An aged ascetic pads along the shore of a still northern lake, drawing the eye into Nesterov's signature blend of Orthodox spirituality and quiet Russian landscape. Painted at the outset of his religious phase, the work was acquired by Pavel Tretyakov and now hangs in his Moscow gallery.
collection-of-beauty-millais-john-everet: Painted when Millais was barely seventeen and exhibited at the Royal Academy, this dramatic history scene shows the conquistador wresting Atahualpa from his throne amid scattering attendants. The crowded composition and bright local colour announce the precocious draughtsmanship he would soon channel into the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood.
collection-of-beauty-millais-das-tal-der: Two nuns dig a grave at twilight in a walled cemetery, one resting on her spade as a coffin-shaped cloud drifts overhead. Begun outdoors at Kinnoull near Perth, the painting marks Millais's shift from minute Pre-Raphaelite finish toward broader, mood-saturated landscape.
collection-of-beauty-millais-uberfahrt-n: An old sea captain sits stiffly by a window while his daughter reads aloud from a logbook, a Union Jack draped across his knees. Millais distilled Britain's Arctic ambitions into domestic drama; the canvas became one of the most reproduced images of Victorian patriotic sentiment.
collection-of-beauty-millais-order-of-re: A Highland wife presents a release order to a soldier guarding her wounded Jacobite husband, who collapses against her with their child asleep on his shoulder. Effie Ruskin posed for the wife — the sittings precipitated her separation from John Ruskin and eventual marriage to Millais.
collection-of-beauty-moitessier-ingres-1: Madame Moitessier stands in black velvet and lace, a heavy gold bracelet anchoring her gloved wrist. Ingres laboured on his Moitessier portraits across more than a decade, prizing the linear contour and porcelain finish that define his late society style; this version belongs to the National Gallery of Art in Washington.
collection-of-beauty-monet-claude-houses: Westminster's Gothic silhouette dissolves into the Thames mist while gulls wheel through a violet, sulphur-tinted sky. Begun on the terrace of St Thomas's Hospital during Monet's 1899-1901 London campaigns and completed in Giverny, the canvas belongs to a series tracking the same view across hours and weather.
collection-of-beauty-monet-claude-hut-in: A tarred fisherman's hut stands above the bared sands of the Normandy coast, painted on a windy summer return to Trouville. Quick, scrubbed strokes record the silvered tideline and the lift of grasses on the dune, hallmarks of Monet's plein-air practice.
collection-of-beauty-monet-pond-at-montg: Painted at the Hoschedé estate of Rottembourg, where Monet was commissioned to produce four large decorative panels, a still pond mirrors poplars and a strolling figure in flickering verticals of green. The Montgeron sojourn deepened the relationship that would, after Camille's death, lead to his second marriage with Alice Hoschedé.
collection-of-beauty-monet-barque-marmot: A varnished rowing skiff rests among reeds on the Epte at Giverny, its hull doubled in dark green water. The riverbank boats became a private motif of the late 1880s, and the painting now belongs to the Musée Marmottan Monet, formed from the bequest of the artist's son Michel.
collection-of-beauty-monet-mer-agitee-a-: Heavy surf breaks against the Normandy chalk cliffs at Etretat, the famous arches receding behind a churn of jade and white. Monet returned repeatedly to this stretch of coast in the early 1880s, working on multiple canvases at once to chase shifting light; this one hangs in the Musée des Beaux-Arts de Lyon.
collection-of-beauty-monet-poly-marmotta: A weather-beaten Belle-Île fisherman, Hippolyte 'Poly' Guillaume, sits with arms folded, his red sash and seaman's cap painted in the same broken touches as the rocks Monet was studying on the Brittany island. The portrait, an unusual late venture into character study, entered the Musée Marmottan via the artist's family.
collection-of-beauty-monet-water-lily-po: Water lilies float on the green-blue surface of the Giverny pond, the bank dissolved in a tapestry of brushstrokes that releases the picture from any horizon. Painted during Monet's late, near-abstract Nymphéas campaign and now installed in Tadao Ando's Chichu Art Museum on Naoshima, the canvas is housed in pure natural light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-boat-lying-at: A stranded fishing boat tilts on the Normandy beach at Fécamp, its hull dark against shimmering wet sand and a high pearl sky. The on-the-spot rendering shows Monet's growing interest in the sea coast, a subject he would mine across the next two decades.
collection-of-beauty-monet-cliffs-near-d: Chalk cliffs rear above a narrow band of restless sea, the grasses on top whipped by a Channel wind. Monet spent the spring of 1882 working at Pourville and Varengeville, applying short, dragged strokes to capture the fissured stone and saline atmosphere of the Norman coast.
collection-of-beauty-monet-das-nachtesse: Camille and the infant Jean sit at the candlelit family table while a guest leans in from the doorway, the room warmed by lamp glow on porcelain and pewter. An early experiment with artificial light and intimate genre, painted at Étretat during a precarious winter for the young household.
collection-of-beauty-monet-die-zaan-bei-: Refugee from the Franco-Prussian war, Monet found in the Dutch waterways at Zaandam a flat horizon punctuated by sails and clapboard houses. Cool blues and ochre dominate the four-month Holland sojourn, in which he painted some two dozen views along the Zaan.
collection-of-beauty-monet-fruhling-in-g: Fruit trees in blossom rise above a meadow of high grass at the artist's Giverny estate, the pink froth picked out with rapid stippled touches. Spring orchards became one of Monet's most consistent local motifs after he settled in the village in 1883.
collection-of-beauty-monet-garten-der-in: From a high window of the Louvre, Monet looked across the Pont des Arts to the dome of the Institut and the formal parterre of the Garden of the Princess. One of three Paris views painted from the same elevated vantage in 1867, it shows top-hatted strollers and carriages reduced to crisp coloured marks.
collection-of-beauty-monet-grainstacks-w: A rimed haystack stands in a frosted field outside Giverny, its conical bulk caught between a violet shadow and a chilled mauve sky. The work belongs to the Grainstacks series with which Monet inaugurated his rigorous serial method, painting the same motif under successive light conditions.
collection-of-beauty-monet-impression-su: The harbour of Le Havre at dawn dissolves into vapour, masts and cranes ghosted behind an orange sun whose reflections crackle across the water. Exhibited at the first independent show of 1874, its title supplied a hostile critic with the term 'Impressionism' that would name the movement.
collection-of-beauty-monet-in-der-barke: Three of Alice Hoschedé's daughters drift on the Epte in a Norwegian skiff, their pale dresses caught between dark river plants and sun-laced water. The high viewpoint and crop, learnt from Japanese prints, push the figures toward decorative pattern.
collection-of-beauty-monet-nenufares: Pads of green and lavender float across a pond surface that fills the canvas, sky and bank registering only as reflections among the lilies. With this 1903-08 series Monet refined the immersive water-garden vision that would culminate in the Orangerie cycles.
collection-of-beauty-monet-red-boats-arg: Sailing skiffs with vermilion hulls cluster against the willow-lined bank of the Seine at Argenteuil, where Monet kept his studio boat. The flickering reflections and high-keyed palette typify the river scenes he painted alongside Renoir and Manet during the suburb's pivotal Impressionist summers.
collection-of-beauty-monet-small-country: On a winter trip to the Italian Riviera, Monet found at Bordighera a tangle of olives, palms and ochre walls under bright Mediterranean light. He complained of the difficulty of catching the dazzle in pigment, and worked rapidly across some forty canvases during the three-month campaign.
collection-of-beauty-monet-the-church-at: The little Saint-Valéry church perches on its eroding clifftop at Varengeville-sur-Mer, painted from the gully below with the Channel framed beyond. Monet returned to the motif from several angles during his 1882 Pourville stay, treating the building as a fixed point against shifting weather.
collection-of-beauty-monet-tulpenfelder-: Bands of red, yellow and white tulips stripe the polder near Sassenheim, a windmill anchoring the flat horizon. Monet's brief 1886 Holland trip yielded five tulip-field canvases, in which the riot of cultivated colour gave him a ready-made experiment in pure chroma.
collection-of-beauty-monet-wildenstein-1: Catalogued in Daniel Wildenstein's monumental Monet raisonné, this canvas belongs to the artist's late Normandy period, when he returned repeatedly to the cliffs near Pourville and Varengeville. Loose, broken brushwork dissolves form into atmospheric colour, the hallmark of his mature Impressionist practice.

From Monet's Giverny years, this work reflects his sustained meditation on water, reflection, and shifting light around the property he had transformed into a living motif. The palette favours violet-blues and greens applied in dense, woven strokes that flatten depth into surface.

An early Monet study from his formative years on the Channel coast, when he absorbed Boudin's plein-air practice and Jongkind's atmospheric handling of sky and sea. The brushwork is comparatively descriptive, anticipating the looser facture of the following decade.
collection-of-beauty-monet-dahlias: A garden subject from Monet's Argenteuil period, when his suburban plot became a recurring laboratory for floral colour and dappled shade. Thick, comma-shaped strokes register each bloom as a discrete patch of pigment rather than a botanical specimen.
collection-of-beauty-monet-ice-floes-on-: A frigid winter scene of the Seine clogged with drift ice near Bougival, where Monet lived in straitened circumstances. Pale lavenders and chalky greys describe the freeze, his brush dragged dryly to suggest the glassy plates of broken ice.
collection-of-beauty-monet-the-bridge-at: Painted during Monet's lean Bougival period alongside Renoir, the composition hinges on the wooden river bridge cutting across the Seine. The handling is still relatively tight, but already shows the rapid notation of reflections that would soon define Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-monet-yellow-irises: A late Giverny study of yellow flag irises, painted as Monet's eyesight clouded with cataracts and his palette grew correspondingly hot and saturated. The flowers swim in loose, near-abstract bands of green and gold, anticipating the decorative scale of the Grandes Décorations.
collection-of-beauty-monet-1401: A view of the fjord at Christiania (now Oslo), painted on Monet's 1895 Norwegian campaign while visiting his stepson. Snow-bound water and shoreline are rendered in cool blues and pinks, his northern palette pushed toward near-monochrome refinement.
collection-of-beauty-monet-exterieur-de-: Part of Monet's 1877 Gare Saint-Lazare series, the first concerted Impressionist meditation on industrial modernity. Steam, sunlight, and iron architecture dissolve into one another, the locomotive yard reframed as a study in coloured vapour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-houses-of-par: From the London series begun in 1899 from a balcony at the Savoy, the Palace of Westminster looms as a violet silhouette through Thames fog. Monet pursued the same view across hours and weathers, finishing many canvases later in the Giverny studio.
collection-of-beauty-monet-lavacourt: A panoramic view of the Seine at Lavacourt, opposite his Vétheuil home, submitted to the 1880 Salon as a calculated bid for official recognition. The handling is unusually controlled, the river a broad mirror of pearly cloud.
collection-of-beauty-monet-luncheon: An intimate domestic interior showing the remains of a meal at the Monets' Argenteuil house, with the artist's first wife Camille and son Jean implied in the staging. Light pours across linen, glassware, and fruit in a quietly Manet-inflected still life.
collection-of-beauty-monet-meadow-with-p: Poplars near Argenteuil, painted in the suburban Seine landscape that nourished Monet's early Impressionist breakthrough. Vertical trunks divide the field into rhythmic intervals, a compositional device he would push further in the 1891 Poplars series.
collection-of-beauty-monet-pointe-de-la-: An early Channel-coast view of the headland at Sainte-Adresse, painted when Monet was still working through the lessons of Boudin and the Barbizon school. Wide sky and tide-flat foreground show his lifelong fascination with the meeting of sea and weather.
collection-of-beauty-monet-veduta-di-rou: A youthful landscape of Rouelles in the Le Havre hinterland, often cited as Monet's earliest surviving oil. The careful, almost Corot-like handling of trees and stream marks the threshold before his conversion to outdoor, sketch-based painting.
collection-of-beauty-monet-view-of-bordi: Painted on Monet's 1884 Italian Riviera campaign at Bordighera, where the unfamiliar light and exotic vegetation pushed his palette toward hot pinks, oranges, and turquoise. The Mediterranean produced what he called colours he had to invent.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w108: A tender domestic study of the artist's infant son Jean asleep in his cradle, dating from the precarious early years of Monet's family life. The handling is broad and affectionate, sheets and shadow simplified into planes of cool grey.
collection-of-beauty-monet-barques-w-137: A study of moored rowing boats, the kind of riverside motif Monet repeatedly mined along the Seine. Water is rendered in horizontal slabs of broken colour, the hulls anchoring an otherwise nearly abstract surface.
collection-of-beauty-monet-grainstacks-a: An early grainstack study from the fields around Giverny, the motif Monet would canonise in his 1890–91 series. Even here the haystack functions as a coloured mass dissolving into ambient sunset light rather than as agricultural fact.
collection-of-beauty-monet-trouville: A breezy Norman seaside view from Monet's 1870 Trouville stay with Camille, days before the Franco-Prussian War sent him into London exile. Sand grains are reportedly embedded in the paint, evidence of true plein-air execution.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w0145: A snow-bound landscape at sunset, a subject Monet returned to repeatedly through his career to test the chromatic limits of white. Pale pinks and lilacs warm the drifts as the low sun stains the horizon.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1001: From the mid-1880s, when Monet was extending his repertoire of coastal and rural motifs after settling at Giverny. The brushwork is densely woven, building landscape from short, directional strokes of contrasting hue.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1003: A mid-1880s landscape from Monet's expanded raisonné, painted as he consolidated the rapid, plein-air manner into a more deliberate decorative surface. Form yields to a tapestry of small coloured touches.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1010: Catalogued in Wildenstein's raisonné among Monet's Giverny-era studies, the canvas works through his recurring concerns of water, foliage, and reflected sky. Pigment is laid in short broken strokes that read as both atmosphere and surface pattern.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1016: A landscape entry in Monet's catalogue raisonné showing his characteristic divisionist-leaning brushwork of the period. Local colour is broken across complementary touches so the eye reassembles the scene at viewing distance.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1018: An undated landscape from Monet's mature Impressionist output, distinguished by its all-over weave of small, nervous strokes. The motif is subordinated to the choreography of light playing across surface.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1032: A coastal view of sailboats near the Aiguille (Rock Needle) at Étretat, the Norman landmark Monet painted obsessively through the 1880s. Sail and chalk arch are reduced to bright shapes against a sea of broken blue-greens.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1035: From Monet's Étretat campaigns of the 1880s, when he braved cliff ledges and rising tides to capture the chalk arches in changing weather. The handling marries graphic structure to looser, atmospheric water.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1042: A Normandy coast subject from Monet's prolific Étretat-era output, where rock, sea, and sky are unified by a high-keyed palette of blue, lilac, and creamy white. Brushwork follows the contours of swell and stone.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1048: Étretat's Porte d'Amont arch under storm, painted from a clifftop vantage in heavy weather. Wind and surf are rendered in slashing diagonals of green and slate, the chalk headland barely holding its silhouette.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1050: A Belle-Île seascape from Monet's 1886 Brittany campaign, where he confronted the Atlantic's dark, jagged cliffs under shifting weather. The palette tightens to slate, violet and bottle-green, with broken brushwork registering the swell against rock.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1054: Painted during the artist's ten-week stay on Belle-Île-en-Mer, the canvas wrestles with pyramidal sea-stacks and pounding surf. Monet worked in oilskins on the cliffs, layering short, scumbled strokes to capture spray and the heavy Atlantic light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1056: Another study from the Belle-Île series, in which Monet returned again and again to the same outcrops to track changing weather. Cool blues and bruised purples dominate, the sea built up in dense, choppy strokes that mimic the swell itself.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1061: A panoramic view of Vernon, the small Norman town just downriver from Giverny, with its church tower mirrored in the Seine. Monet flattens the composition into horizontal bands of water, masonry and sky, dissolving architecture into reflective light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1065: A motif drawn from Monet's Belle-Île campaign on Brittany's wild south coast, where granite needles meet a relentless sea. The painter mixed lead white into deep ultramarines to render foam, working in the open air against squalls that often interrupted the sittings.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1075: From the artist's mid-1880s output, this canvas shows the loosened, near-abstract handling Monet was developing on the Channel coast. Surface texture takes priority over contour, with the brushstroke itself conveying wind, weather and tide.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1089: A landscape from Monet's most prolific decade at Giverny, where the garden and surrounding meadows became his open-air laboratory. Light is registered in feathered, comma-shaped strokes that trade modeling for vibration of colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w109: An early work from the period before Monet settled into the Impressionist idiom, when he was still absorbing lessons from Boudin and the Barbizon school. The handling is firmer and the tonal range narrower than in his mature plein-air practice.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1120: Painted in the late 1880s, the canvas belongs to Monet's increasing tendency to dissolve subject into atmosphere. Broken touches of complementary colour set the picture humming, anticipating the serial method of the haystacks and poplars.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1121: Rain sweeping across the cliffs of Belle-Île, the sea and sky fused by a grey veil. Monet rendered the downpour with diagonal scumbles laid over a darker ground, an Atlantic counterpart to the foggier studies he would later make in London.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1135: Part of Monet's poplar series on the banks of the Epte, painted from a flat-bottomed boat moored among the trees. The verticals march across the canvas in a near-decorative rhythm, sunlight filtered into juxtaposed greens, lavenders and rose.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1138: Iris beds in flower at Giverny, where the artist deliberately planted in painterly bands of colour. Violet and yellow notes are stitched together with rapid strokes, prefiguring the immersive perspectives of the later Water Lilies.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1155: A Giverny landscape from the years when Monet was systematizing his serial practice, returning to the same view under successive lights. Foliage is broken into flickering touches that read as foliage only at a distance.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1179: An earlier landscape predating the high Impressionist style, with a more traditional, tonal handling than the artist's later work. Drawing remains visible beneath the paint, and the palette stays close to a Barbizon range of earths and greens.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1200: Morning light over a Giverny meadow, painted in the soft, iridescent register Monet reserved for daybreak. Pinks and pale blues are stippled into the grasses, the horizon line lifted high to flatten the field into a tapestry of colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1245: A spring effect in the Giverny garden, where Monet tracked the season's earliest blossom against fresh foliage. The surface is a dense weave of rose, cream and acid green, the structure of trees nearly absorbed into pure colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1296: An early landscape from Monet's formative years, before the Impressionist breakthrough of the 1870s. The composition retains traditional perspectival depth, with paint laid more thinly than in the textured works of his maturity.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1336: Ice floes drifting on the Seine near Bennecourt, a subject Monet had treated since the great freeze of 1880. Cold violets and pale ochres render the half-frozen water, the bare bankside trees handled in spare vertical accents.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1370: From the mid-1890s, when Monet was consolidating his serial method and refining his Giverny motifs. The handling is patient and tonal, with closely keyed colours building atmosphere through accumulation rather than contrast.
