collection-of-beauty-monet-w148: An early work from Monet's apprentice years, when his practice was still indebted to plein-air predecessors like Eugène Boudin. The brushwork is tighter and the subject more conventionally framed than in the mature Impressionist canvases.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1524: From the Charing Cross Bridge series painted on visits to London between 1899 and 1904. Monet worked from a balcony at the Savoy, watching trains cross the Thames as fog and smoke turned bridge and water into shimmering bars of mauve and gold.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1558: Part of the Waterloo Bridge cycle observed from the Savoy Hotel, in which Monet pursued the Thames under every gradation of London weather. Here the overcast light flattens architecture and water into closely keyed greys and lilacs.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1563: A landscape held within Monet's Impressionist repertoire, where the optical mixing of small coloured strokes carries the work. Atmosphere takes precedence over drawing, the eye assembling the scene from a tissue of brushmarks.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w159: Camille Doncieux on the beach at Trouville during the family's stay in the summer of 1870, painted in the open air with sand reportedly still embedded in the paint. The handling is rapid and sketch-like, capturing wind, light and the moment of leisure.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1636: From Monet's later Giverny output, where landscape gives way to immersive fields of colour and reflection. The painter's brushwork loosens further, sacrificing legibility of motif to a unified surface vibration.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1656: A late work from the Giverny garden, painted as Monet's failing eyesight pushed his colour into hotter, more arbitrary registers. Form softens into broad, kneaded strokes that read as foliage and water rather than describing them.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1668: From the painter's final year, when cataracts and a programme of cataract surgery transformed his colour. The picture belongs to the freer, near-abstract late manner that prefigures mid-century painting and crowns the lifelong project of the Giverny garden.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1675: A Water Lilies canvas from Monet's long meditation on the lily pond he engineered at Giverny in 1893. Sky and pads share a single reflective surface, the eye pulled into a horizonless field of greens, blues and pink blossom.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1695: Another study from the Nymphéas, the cycle that occupied the artist for the last three decades of his life. The pond's surface dissolves boundary between water and sky, painted in long, drifting strokes of mauve, jade and cream.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1708: An early canvas from the Water Lilies series begun in earnest after 1903, when Monet shifted from views of the Japanese bridge to the pond's surface alone. Floating pads anchor a shimmering ground in which clouds and willows are fused as reflection.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1725: A late canvas from Monet's Giverny years, one of the water-garden compositions in which the pond surface dissolves into a near-abstract field of reflected sky and floating blooms. Loose, layered brushwork builds colour without a fixed horizon, anticipating the immersive Grandes Decorations.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1726: One of the water-lily paintings made in the studio at Giverny, where Monet returned obsessively to the pond he had dug himself. The viewpoint is tilted down so the surface fills the frame, broken only by floating pads and the soft mirror of overhanging willows.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1781: A Giverny pond study built up in long, weighted strokes of violet, green and pink. Monet works without a horizon line, letting reflected light and lily pads alone organise the picture plane.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1786: Part of the sustained water-lily project that occupied Monet's final two decades. The handling is broader and more gestural than his earlier Impressionist work, the pigment scrubbed and dragged to evoke shifting water and weather.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w182: An early-1870s landscape from Monet's Argenteuil period, when he settled on the Seine and refined the broken-stroke technique that would define Impressionism. Light is registered in short touches of unmixed colour laid wet-in-wet.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1883: A canvas from the Japanese-bridge and water-garden series of Monet's last years, painted as his cataracts deepened and his palette warmed toward reds and oranges. Form gives way almost entirely to colour and reflected light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1884: A late Giverny study in which the lily pond becomes a near-flat tapestry of floating discs and submerged shadow. Monet builds the surface in restless, overlapping touches that carry no firm contour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1897: From the long water-lily campaign begun once Monet diverted a branch of the Epte to feed his pond. The composition omits sky and bank entirely, training the eye on the drift of pads and the cool glaze of reflection.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1900: A turn-of-the-century pond view, made when Monet first showed the water lilies as a unified series at Durand-Ruel. The Japanese footbridge or its absence anchors a study of green-on-green reflection.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w197: An Argenteuil-era landscape from the years Monet shared the riverbank with Manet and Renoir. The brushwork is quick and divisible, the palette tuned to outdoor daylight rather than studio convention.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w1976: A late work in Monet's water-garden idiom, where pond, foliage and reflected sky merge into a single chromatic field. The surface is heavily worked, each pass of the brush adjusting temperature rather than drawing form.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w199: Springtime at Argenteuil, painted soon after Monet moved downriver from Paris in 1871. Blossoming trees and sunlit grass are caught in flickering strokes, the figures held lightly within an atmosphere of dappled spring light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w222: An Argenteuil scene from the year of the first Impressionist exhibition's gestation. Monet works en plein air, registering reflections on water and the play of leaves with rapid, divided touches.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w224: A riverbank composition from Monet's Argenteuil years, where the Seine and its boats supplied an inexhaustible motif. The horizon sits low, leaving most of the canvas to sky and shimmering water.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w24: A road by the Saint-Simeon farm near Honfleur, from Monet's earliest Normandy campaigns. The handling still carries the firm tonal modelling of Boudin and Jongkind, his coastal mentors, before the broken Impressionist stroke took hold.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w265: A landscape from the years Monet rented the house at Argenteuil that became a hub for his circle. Light is treated as the true subject, with grass, water and sky fused by a single weather and hour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w288-the-shel: A wooded path enclosed by foliage, painted in the green-saturated register Monet favoured for shaded interiors of the landscape. Sunlight reaches the ground only in scattered patches, scumbled in pale yellow over deeper greens.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w327: A mid-1870s landscape from the Argenteuil years, painted as Monet pushed his palette toward purer, lighter chromatic notes. The brushwork is short and patchy, more interested in vibration than in contour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w357: An outdoor canvas from 1875, when Monet was at the technical heart of his Argenteuil period. Forms are dissolved into juxtaposed strokes of warm and cool, with shadow rendered in colour rather than black.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w368: A landscape made the year Monet's family was forced from their Argenteuil house by mounting debts. The picture nevertheless shows the confident high-key palette he had perfected on the Seine.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w386: A garden or riverbank motif from 1875, executed in the loose, sun-flecked manner Monet had refined at Argenteuil. Pigment is laid in short directional strokes that read as foliage at viewing distance.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w392: Monet's floating studio, a converted boat moored on the Seine that let him paint from the water itself. Manet had recorded him at work in it; here the vessel becomes the subject, half-shaded under a bank of trees.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w397: A canvas from the year of the second Impressionist exhibition, where Monet showed eighteen works. The brushwork is confidently shorthand, atmosphere carried by colour temperature rather than line.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w419: The Pond at Montgeron, painted at the country estate of the collector Ernest Hoschede, who commissioned a group of decorative panels from Monet. Reflections on still water already prefigure the Giverny obsession to come.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w421: A landscape in Monet's mature Argenteuil-into-Vetheuil manner, with foliage rendered as a mosaic of small directional touches. Local colour is constantly inflected by reflected light from sky and ground.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w429: Evening at Argenteuil, in which the Seine carries the warm pinks and lilacs of a fading sky. Sailboats are noted with a few economical strokes, the picture organised entirely by horizontal bands of reflected light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w433: A hunting scene from 1876, an unusual subject for Monet, who almost never painted narrative motifs. It belongs to the decorative panels commissioned by Ernest Hoschede for the chateau de Rottembourg at Montgeron.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w472: A bouquet of gladioli, from the brief stretch of still life Monet produced after settling at Vetheuil. The blossoms are massed in vertical accents of red and pink, paint laid on thickly to register petal against petal.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w502: A landscape from the Vetheuil years, when Monet, recently widowed, painted the small Seine village and its surrounding fields in all weathers. The handling is rougher and more searching than his Argenteuil work.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w526: A riverside motif in Monet's Vetheuil-period idiom, with broken touches of green, ochre and blue building both bank and water. The picture is held together by a single envelope of weather rather than firm drawing.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w579-springti: A blossoming orchard at Vetheuil, the Seine village where Monet lived after Argenteuil. Loose feathered brushwork builds the white spring foliage against soft greens, the palette typical of his early move toward broken colour and atmospheric light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w58: An early landscape from Monet's pre-Impressionist years, before the broken-colour technique fully emerged. The handling still carries traces of Boudin and the Barbizon plein-air tradition that shaped his apprenticeship along the Normandy coast.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w593-1: A field of red poppies set against summer greens, the kind of motif Monet revisited near Vetheuil and Giverny. Dabs of vermilion are scattered with calligraphic ease across a haze of grass, demonstrating his interest in saturated complementary colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w601: A landscape from Monet's Vetheuil period, when he worked obsessively along the Seine's bends. The brushwork is rapid and atmospheric, recording weather rather than topography in the manner that defines his mature outdoor practice.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w611: A study of willows along the riverbank, a recurring Vetheuil motif. Trailing branches are stroked in vertical green dashes against reflective water, the composition built almost entirely from flickering verticals and horizontals of broken colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w646-jetty-at: The Norman fishing port of Fecamp under storm conditions, painted on one of Monet's coastal campaigns. Choppy water and a bleached sky are scumbled with quick palette-knife passages, recording the weather as much as the harbour's architecture.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w647: A view near Fecamp on the Normandy coast, where Monet painted from the chalk cliffs in early 1881. Salt air and grey-green sea are rendered through agitated, short brushstrokes, the palette muted toward the silver tones of the Channel.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w648: An undated landscape from the Normandy or Vetheuil years, showing the artist's signature open-air handling. The composition relies on broken colour and atmospheric perspective rather than drawn detail, a hallmark of his mature Impressionist practice.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w649: A coastal or riverside study from 1881, the year Monet divided his time between Vetheuil and the Normandy cliffs. Quick wet-on-wet strokes capture transient light, with no preparatory drawing visible beneath the painted surface.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w651-morning-: Dawn light over the Channel, painted during one of Monet's Normandy seasons. Pearly pinks and pale blues are layered in horizontal sweeps, anticipating the obsessive serial study of changing light that would define his later Rouen and Houses-of-Parliament campaigns.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w653: A landscape from Monet's intensely productive 1881 season, when he worked between Vetheuil and the coast. The picture relies on tonal harmonies rather than line, the surface built up from short, dragged strokes of broken colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w658: A landscape from 1881, the year Monet was preparing to leave Vetheuil for Poissy. The brushwork remains feathery and fragmented, recording the play of light over foliage and water in the manner he had refined through the previous decade.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w662: An outdoor study from Monet's 1881 campaign, painted with the rapid, broken touch he had perfected at Argenteuil and Vetheuil. Saturated greens and lavender shadows show his consistent attention to the temperature of light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w663: A landscape painted during Monet's last summer at Vetheuil, before the move to Poissy. Loose vertical brushwork describes trees and reflections together, characteristic of his interest in dissolving form into atmosphere.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w664: A Vetheuil-era landscape worked entirely en plein air. Monet builds the scene from short, parallel strokes of broken colour, layering complementary hues to register the vibration of sunlight rather than to outline objects.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w668: A landscape from the early 1880s, when Monet was still working the Seine valley around Vetheuil. The handling favours atmospheric unity over topographic detail, with soft transitions between sky, foliage and water.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w678-lane-in-: A path on the Ile Saint-Martin near Vetheuil, a small island in the Seine where Monet often set up his easel. Dappled summer light filters through trees in flickering touches of yellow and green, the composition framed by the receding lane.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w679: An outdoor landscape from Monet's Vetheuil years. The picture is constructed from short, parallel strokes that knit foliage, sky and water into a single shimmering field, a technique central to Impressionist landscape practice.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w683: A landscape from 1881, when Monet was producing canvases at high speed for the dealer Durand-Ruel. The handling is quick and confident, with thin scumbles allowing the light ground to glow through the upper layers of paint.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w684: A landscape from Monet's final Vetheuil season, painted with the rapid, fragmented touch he had honed throughout the 1870s. The surface registers atmosphere as a single tonal envelope rather than a sequence of described objects.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w691: A landscape from 1881, the year Monet worked between the Seine valley and the Normandy coast. Broken brushwork and a high-keyed palette dissolve the motif into pure light, in the spirit of the earlier Argenteuil pictures.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w694: An outdoor study from 1881, made during one of Monet's most prolific years. The handling shows the mature Impressionist economy: no preliminary drawing, only short, directional strokes of complementary colour worked wet into wet.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w700-near-vet: A view of the countryside surrounding Vetheuil, the Seine village Monet had occupied since 1878. Soft greens and lavender shadows are stroked in feathery, parallel touches, the composition closer to a tonal study than a topographic record.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w706: A landscape from 1882, when Monet had relocated to Poissy and was making frequent trips to the Normandy coast. The brushwork remains rapid and fragmentary, the palette tuned to the silver-grey light of the lower Seine.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w712-low-tide: Exposed beach and chalk cliffs at Pourville, the small Normandy resort where Monet worked through the spring of 1882. Glistening wet sand is stroked in pale lavenders and ochres, capturing the broad horizontal sweep of the Channel coast at low water.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w717: A coastal scene from Monet's 1882 Normandy campaign at Pourville and Varengeville. Sea, sky and cliff are unified by a silvery palette and short, directional strokes, evidence of his close attention to the changing light of the Channel.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w724: A landscape from the 1882 Normandy season, when Monet was shuttling between Pourville and his rented house at Poissy. Cliff and sea are simplified to broad zones of broken colour, the handling more atmospheric than descriptive.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w726-the-chur: The medieval clifftop church of Saint-Valery at Varengeville, silhouetted against the Channel sky. Monet returned to this motif repeatedly in 1882, treating it as a study in tonal envelope, here flushed with the warm rose and orange of evening.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w736: A coastal subject from 1882, the year of Monet's intensive Pourville and Varengeville campaigns. Broken brushwork and a cool, salt-bleached palette register the particular light of the Normandy cliffs above the Channel.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w752: A landscape from 1882, painted with the swift, confident touch Monet had refined over a decade of plein-air work. The surface is built up from short directional strokes that suggest weather and time of day before they describe place.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w753: A coastal study from Monet's prolific Normandy campaign, executed with the broken-touch brushwork that defines his early-1880s plein-air practice. Cliffs, sea, and weather are reduced to chromatic vibrations, the palette tilted toward cool greens and pearly greys.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w757: Painted during Monet's repeated stays on the Channel coast, the canvas tracks the play of light across rock and water with rapid, scumbled strokes. Form gives way to atmosphere, an approach that anticipates the artist's later serial investigations.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w759: One of dozens of seascapes Monet produced from rented quarters along the Normandy shore, where weather shifts dictated his working day. The composition relies on a high horizon and a textured, comma-stroke sea typical of his Pourville and Étretat campaigns.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w764: A Channel-coast motif handled with the loose, weather-driven facture Monet refined in the early 1880s. Sky and sea are knit together by repeated short strokes, the pigment kept high-keyed to register passing light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w767: From Monet's Normandy production, the scene captures the headlands and shifting maritime light he chased obsessively that decade. The handling is brisk and granular, with chalky lilacs and greens laid wet-into-wet.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w772: Another Channel-coast view from Monet's 1882 campaign, with cliffs cropped tight and the sea pushed nearly flat against the picture plane. The brushwork remains unblended, each stroke registering a separate observation of colour and weather.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w776: A coastal subject from Monet's intensive Normandy season, painted in the open air against quickly changing conditions. Greens, mauves, and cream whites are dragged across the surface in the loose impasto he had by then made his signature.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w778a: Painted on the Normandy shore, the work belongs to a string of cliff and sea studies in which Monet tested how far light could dissolve solid form. The stroke is rapid, the palette cool and slightly chalky.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w787: From Monet's Pourville-Varengeville orbit, where he repeatedly framed the Channel through cliffs and scrubby vegetation. The surface is built up in short, directional strokes that encode wind, haze, and tide.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w807: A Normandy seascape carrying the high-keyed, broken-touch language Monet had perfected by the early 1880s. Distance is implied not by linear perspective but by graded warmth and saturation across the brushwork.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w81: An early canvas predating Impressionism's public arrival, in which Monet is still negotiating his debt to Boudin and Jongkind. Already evident is his appetite for outdoor light and a loosened, atmospheric handling of sky and water.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w812-vase-of-: A still life of chrysanthemums massed in a vase, treated with the same broken-touch brushwork Monet brought to landscape. The flowers register as bursts of pigment rather than carefully drawn forms, the background kept tonal so colour can dominate.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w816: A Channel-coast study from the run of seascapes Monet produced in 1882, painted on site through bracing weather. Sea, cliff, and sky are unified by a quick, granular touch and a restricted, salt-bleached palette.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w822: The pierced cliff at Étretat, a motif Monet returned to repeatedly, framed here through the natural arch and seething water below. The paint is laid in directional strokes that follow the swell, the chalk faces caught in mauve and ochre half-light.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w835: From Monet's continued exploration of the Channel coast in 1883, the work pushes light and atmosphere over topography. Strokes remain short and unblended, the palette pitched to the silvery weather of Normandy.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w844: Painted during one of Monet's working trips along the Normandy shore, where he developed his habit of carrying multiple canvases for changing conditions. The handling is rapid and additive, building form from layered colour rather than line.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w849: A vase of poppies treated as a study in pure chromatic punch, the scarlet blossoms blazing against a muted ground. Monet handles the bouquet with the same plein-air immediacy he brought to landscape, letting brushwork stand for petal and leaf.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w867: A landscape from Monet's mid-1880s output, when he moved freely between the Normandy coast and the Mediterranean. The surface is densely worked in short strokes that hold light without resolving into precise contour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w868: Painted in a year split between Étretat and the Riviera, the canvas carries the loose, weather-attuned facture Monet trusted in any setting. Form is sketched only as far as light requires, the rest given to colour and texture.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w871: A study of olive trees made during Monet's stay at Bordighera on the Italian Riviera, where he marvelled at light he found nearly impossible to render. The silvery foliage is broken into nervous, overlapping strokes against intense Mediterranean blues.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w875: Palms at Bordighera, painted on Monet's 1884 trip to the Riviera, where he wrote of needing a palette of diamonds and gemstones to match the light. The fronds are rendered as quick, calligraphic flicks against saturated southern hues.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w885: From Monet's Bordighera and Cap Martin campaigns, the canvas pursues the strong colour and clarity of the Mediterranean coast. Pigment is laid in vivid, separated strokes, a deliberate departure from the cooler Norman palette.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w891a: A Riviera subject from Monet's 1884 sojourn in Bordighera and Menton, painted in heightened colour to register subtropical light. The brushwork is brisk and the palette unusually saturated for the artist, full of pinks, oranges, and intense blues.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w891b: Companion to its near twin, this Riviera canvas chases the same southern light with the comma strokes Monet relied on in the field. Foliage and rock are dissolved into vibrating colour with little tonal modelling.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w892: A southern landscape from Monet's Mediterranean campaign, where he tested how vivid he could push his palette without losing atmospheric truth. Strokes are short and directional, building form through colour contrast rather than line.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w904: The chalk cliffs at Les Petites-Dalles on the Normandy coast, viewed across an unsettled sea. Monet articulates the cliff face with cool mauves and creams while the water carries his characteristic broken, comma-shaped touch.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w922: A close study of jonquils, the yellow blooms set against muted greens to make colour the principal subject. Monet treats the flowers with quick, loaded strokes, the kind of small still life he often painted between larger landscape campaigns.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w928: A second take on jonquils from the same period, the bouquet handled with rapid, plein-air directness. The bright trumpets read as concentrated daubs of pigment rather than drawn forms, the ground kept loose and tonal.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w948: A very early work from Monet's teenage years in Le Havre, predating his formal training under Boudin and his move toward Impressionism. The handling is more conventional than his later production but already shows an interest in outdoor observation.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w961: An adolescent piece from Monet's Le Havre period, made before he had committed to landscape painting in the open air. Drawing and tonal modelling still carry the work, with only hints of the looser facture he would soon develop.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w962: An early Monet landscape painted while the artist was still a teenager working from nature in Normandy. The brushwork shows the influence of Boudin and the Barbizon school, foreshadowing the loose handling that would later define Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w965: A snow-blanketed view of the village Monet had recently made his home, painted with muted pinks and blues that register the cold light of a winter afternoon. Such effets de neige let him test how a single subject changes under shifting weather and atmosphere.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w977: A Norman cliffside cottage dissolves into a wash of grey-violet fog, the architecture barely legible against the saturated air. Monet painted the Falaise coast repeatedly in the mid-1880s, fascinated by how mist erases line and reduces form to colour.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w982: Slender trees line the riverbank in a tapestry of green and lavender, their reflections broken into short rhythmic strokes. The motif belongs to the long sequence of Giverny river scenes that anchored Monet's late practice in his own garden landscape.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w986: A Giverny meadow caught at first light, with dew and pale sun softening the field into pastel veils of pink and green. Monet's preoccupation with the morning effect produced canvases in which the subject is essentially the quality of the air itself.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w996: A flowering field rendered as a dense weave of dabbed blossoms, the eye coaxed across the surface rather than into deep space. Such meadow paintings exemplify Monet's flattening of landscape into a tapestry of pure colour notes.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-106: A coastal scene from Monet's formative years on the Channel, when he was working alongside Boudin and Jongkind. The handling is broader than academic landscape of the period and already shows his interest in transient light over fixed topography.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-208: Painted in the early 1870s as Monet established the visual language of Impressionism, the picture trades finish for immediacy, applying paint in mosaic-like touches. Works of these years preceded and prepared the 1874 group exhibition that gave the movement its name.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-235: A river or harbour subject from 1872, the year of the famous Impression, Sunrise, sharing its concern with reflections, vapour, and the dissolution of form on water. The vibrating shorthand of small coloured strokes marks Monet's break with conventional finish.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-272-apple-t: Apple trees in full blossom, their pinkish-white canopies set against fresh spring green and dabbed in short broken strokes. The subject was a favourite of the early Impressionists, who valued orchards as outdoor studios where colour and light could be studied together.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-290-autumn-: A view along the Seine at Argenteuil, where Monet was based through much of the 1870s, painted at the moment when foliage flares into copper and gold. The water carries the autumn colour back as a lattice of broken reflections, a hallmark of his river pictures from these years.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-338: Painted during the year of the first Impressionist exhibition, this work shows Monet's mature open-air method fully formed. Pigment is laid down in distinct touches that resolve into atmosphere only at viewing distance, refusing the smooth surface prized by the Salon.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-42: An early landscape from Monet's mid-twenties, when he was painting outdoors in the forest of Fontainebleau and along the Norman coast. The dark tonal range and tighter handling reflect his Barbizon-school roots before the lighter Impressionist palette emerged.
collection-of-beauty-monet-w-763-the-pat: A garden or woodland path drawing the viewer between flanking masses of foliage, painted with the interwoven greens and lilacs typical of Monet's Giverny work. The motif reduces landscape to a corridor of pure colour and dappled shade.
collection-of-beauty-moreno-garden-bordi: Painted on a winter trip to the Italian Riviera, the picture revels in the unfamiliar palette Monet found in Francesco Moreno's exotic garden, with its palms, citrus and Mediterranean blue. The artist wrote home that the light at Bordighera demanded tones of diamond and gemstone.
collection-of-beauty-mortlake-terrace-a1: A Thames-side terrace bathed in the dissolving glare of low evening sun, with a small black dog famously cut out and pasted onto the foreground lawn. The picture is among Turner's most radical experiments in painting directly into the light source.
collection-of-beauty-mother-of-pearl-and: A standing female figure rendered in the pale, harmonised tones the title evokes, in keeping with Whistler's musical naming and his pursuit of arrangement over narrative. The subject becomes a vehicle for tonal close-keying rather than character study.
collection-of-beauty-mr-carlyle-deliveri: A wood-engraved illustration documenting Thomas Carlyle's rectorial address at Edinburgh, reproduced for a mass readership in the Illustrated London News. Such press engravings were the period's chief vehicle for circulating images of public events before photographic reproduction took over.
collection-of-beauty-mu-ch-i-001: A section of the celebrated handscroll cycle Eight Views of the Xiao and Xiang Rivers, in which Muqi distils river, mist and distant peak into broad ink washes. The Chan-Buddhist economy of means deeply influenced later Japanese ink painting.
collection-of-beauty-mucha-apoteoza: The closing canvas of the Slav Epic, in which Mucha gathers the history of the Slavic peoples into an allegorical apotheosis culminating in a youthful figure of independence. The cycle of twenty monumental paintings was his self-declared life's work, given to the city of Prague.
collection-of-beauty-mucha-seasons-1897-: Spring from Mucha's second Four Seasons set, personified as a young woman tuning a lyre strung between blossoming branches. The decorative panel format, sinuous contour and floral framing typify the Art Nouveau idiom Mucha helped to define in Paris.
collection-of-beauty-munch-self-portrait: A late self-portrait in which Munch shows himself frontally before the easel, palette in hand, the face hollowed by age and frank scrutiny. Painting himself was a lifelong project for the artist, charting the shifts of his temperament as openly as any of his symbolic figures.
collection-of-beauty-musizierender-einsi: A hermit plays an instrument outside his rocky cell in a sun-drenched mountain niche, a quintessentially Biedermeier vignette of contemplative solitude. Spitzweg specialised in such gently humorous, finely observed eccentrics set within picturesque German landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-1301809-utag: A snow scene at Ueno from a nishiki-e album by Kunisada, the leading designer of the late ukiyo-e school. The print uses the muted palette and silent atmosphere appropriate to the bosetsu, or evening snow, theme inherited from classical Chinese poetry.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-1302763-tsuk: From Yoshitoshi's series Mirror of Warriors of Our Country, depicting the young Minamoto no Ushiwakamaru confronting the bandit Kumasaka Chōhan. The composition typifies the artist's dramatic late-Edo musha-e, with intricate armour patterning and a charged sense of action.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-03-k: Daybreak over the Daikon vegetable wharf on the Sumida, drawn from Hasui's series Twenty Views of Tōkyō. The print is characteristic shin-hanga: traditional ukiyo-e workshop methods adapted to atmospheric, Western-influenced views of a modernising city.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-04-k: The Kiyomizu Hall in Ueno Park seen across blossoming trees, from Hasui's Twenty Views of Tōkyō. The shin-hanga design preserves the planar colour fields of the woodblock tradition while introducing softened, plein-air light.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-10-k: Rain falls across Shinobazu Pond in Ueno, the lotus leaves and far shore softened to muted greys and greens. Hasui's mastery of bokashi gradation and overprinted rain-lines made him the foremost printmaker of weather in twentieth-century Japan.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-13-k: A sea cave on the Oga Peninsula renders blue-green water against weathered rock, from Hasui's third Souvenirs of Travel series. The artist toured rural Japan compulsively, and these prints document a coastline that motorised tourism was just beginning to reach.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-14-k: Karasu Marsh in Akita rendered as still water and reed beds under a quiet sky, from Hasui's third Souvenirs of Travel set. The reduced palette and emphasis on horizon embody the contemplative restraint that defines his shin-hanga landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-15-k: A view of Akita's vast Hachirōgata Lagoon from the shin-hanga series Souvenirs of Travel III. Hasui's quiet woodblock composition relies on flat washes of muted color and a low horizon to evoke the stillness of the inland water at dusk.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-18-k: From the shin-hanga series Souvenirs of Travel III, this nocturne shows the floating torii of Itsukushima beneath a star-pricked sky. Hasui pairs deep indigo bokashi gradations with sparse highlights to register the hush of Miyajima after dark.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-19-k: A scene of the towering Hōrai Rock rising from the Kiso River, drawn from Hasui's Souvenirs of Travel III. The print balances the cliff's bulk against soft atmospheric washes, a hallmark of the shin-hanga revival of traditional landscape woodblock.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-20-k: A coastal view of the fishing port of Kamezaki in former Owari Province. Hasui frames boats and shoreline buildings under wide skies, using restrained palette and bokashi shading to render the still atmosphere of an Aichi inlet.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-22-k: Fukuoka's Nishi Park rendered in the shin-hanga manner, with figures threading through trees and lanterns. Hasui's design uses overlapping woodblock impressions to suggest dappled light and the casual pace of urban leisure in early Shōwa Japan.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-23-k: Morning rises over the hot-spring town of Beppu in Kyushu, with steam and hills pressed into a tight horizon. Hasui's bokashi gradations carry the cool light of dawn, a signature effect of his shin-hanga landscapes.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-25-k: From the series Twenty Views of Tōkyō, this print follows the Takinogawa River as it winds through suburban greenery. Hasui captures a quieter periphery of the modernizing capital, layering subtle woodblock washes for foliage and reflected water.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-26-k: Evening settles over the Beppu coast in this Souvenirs of Travel III sheet. Hasui anchors the composition with darkened silhouettes against a dusky sky, exploiting the shin-hanga technique of bokashi to dissolve land into water.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586549-31-k: Part of Twenty Views of Tōkyō, the print shows the moon hanging over the rural fringes of Magome. Hasui builds the night scene from layered indigo blocks and a single luminous disc, demonstrating shin-hanga's debt to classical Edo landscape traditions.
collection-of-beauty-ndl-dc-2586550-07-k: A view of the Ōmori coast on Tokyo Bay, drawn from Twenty Views of Tōkyō. Hasui registers the meeting of urban edge and water in soft, overcast tones, the shin-hanga answer to Hokusai and Hiroshige's earlier capital views.
collection-of-beauty-nacimiento-de-san-j: An interior crowded with attendants tends to the newborn John the Baptist while Elizabeth rests nearby. Gentileschi treats the sacred subject as a domestic event, modelling figures in the warm tenebrist light typical of her Neapolitan period.
collection-of-beauty-nakahara-in-the-sag: From Hokusai's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, the print situates the sacred peak behind travelers and scenery in the provinces. Hokusai's signature flat planes of color and bold contour line organize the landscape into the layered recession the series made famous.
collection-of-beauty-narcissus-caravaggi: The youth Narcissus leans over a dark pool, his lit shoulder mirrored by his reflected face. Caravaggio reduces the Ovidian myth to a single closed circle of body and image, with the harsh tenebrist contrast that defined early Baroque painting in Rome.
collection-of-beauty-natoire-sibylle: A French Rococo study of a sibyl, the prophetess of antique tradition shown in a moment of inspired contemplation. The drawing employs the fluid contours and idealized features that characterized academic figure work in mid-eighteenth-century Paris.
collection-of-beauty-natoire-adam-et-eve: Adam and Eve are driven from Eden by an admonishing angel in this Rococo treatment of Genesis. Natoire softens the moralizing subject with flowing draperies, pastel colour and graceful attitudes drawn from his work as director of the French Academy in Rome.
collection-of-beauty-neapolitan-woman-18: A bust-length portrait of a young Neapolitan woman, painted during Repin's travels in southern Italy. The Russian realist favours direct observation over picturesque costume drama, registering complexion and gaze with a swift, loaded brush.
collection-of-beauty-nesterovmv-narusi20: An expansive procession of Russian people - peasants, monks, soldiers and intelligentsia - moves across a northern landscape in search of spiritual truth. Nesterov treats his pre-revolutionary allegory in luminous, restrained colour, blending Symbolist mood with Orthodox iconographic gravity.
collection-of-beauty-nesterov-florensky-: A double portrait of theologians Pavel Florensky and Sergei Bulgakov walking in conversation through a quiet wooded landscape. Nesterov's pared composition and contemplative palette frame the encounter as an icon of Russian religious philosophy on the eve of revolution.
collection-of-beauty-newton-williamblake: Blake's monoprint shows the natural philosopher hunched naked on a sea-floor rock, drawing geometric figures with a compass. The image reads as a critique of pure reason: Newton, absorbed in measurement, becomes blind to the imaginative cosmos beyond his diagram.
collection-of-beauty-niccolopaganini: A pencil portrait of the virtuoso violinist captured during Ingres's years in Rome. The drawing distils Paganini to crisp contour and minimal shading, exemplifying the precise linear style for which Ingres's portrait sketches of musicians and patrons remain prized.
collection-of-beauty-nichiren-bezweert-d: The Buddhist reformer Nichiren stands in a pitching boat off Kakuda, calming the waves with the Lotus Sutra during his exile to Sado. Kuniyoshi's woodblock print sets red robes against churning blue surf in the dramatic warrior-print idiom of late Edo Utagawa school design.
collection-of-beauty-nicolas-poussin-fre: Venus rises from the sea attended by tritons, nereids and putti in a Baroque treatment of the Anadyomene theme. Poussin organizes the marine cortege into a frieze-like rhythm, marrying Ovidian mythology with the cool classical structure he distilled from antique relief sculpture.

A serene Italianate landscape with shepherds, livestock and a distant fortified town beneath still water and clear sky. Poussin's late landscapes treat nature as a measured, almost stoic order, with each motif placed for compositional balance rather than picturesque incident.
collection-of-beauty-nicolas-poussin-l-e: Roman soldiers seize Sabine women in a violent twist of bodies, framed by classicizing architecture. Poussin choreographs the abduction as a sculptural relief of opposing diagonals, applying his rigorous study of antique gesture to the legendary founding episode of Rome.
collection-of-beauty-nicolas-poussin-le-: Compressed to its essential cruelty, the painting isolates a single soldier pinning a mother and child as Herod's slaughter unfolds. Poussin condenses the biblical episode into a sculptural triangle of figures, prefiguring later modern responses to depictions of state violence.
collection-of-beauty-nicolas-poussin-sto: Storm clouds boil over a vast Italianate landscape as the Ovidian lovers meet their tragedy beside a reflective lake. Poussin uses meteorological drama to magnify human catastrophe, with sheet lightning, fleeing horses and bowed trees orchestrated into a single classical machine.
collection-of-beauty-nicolas-poussin-the: Christ hands the keys of the Church to a kneeling Peter, with the apostles ranged in a measured Roman setting. From Poussin's second series of the Seven Sacraments, the painting translates the institutional moment into the artist's mature, archaeologically informed classicism.
collection-of-beauty-night-bivouac-of-gr: Soldiers of Napoleon's Grande Armée huddle around guttering fires in a frozen Russian field during the catastrophic 1812 retreat. Vereshchagin, a battle painter known for unsparing realism, treats the scene with documentary cold rather than heroic rhetoric.
collection-of-beauty-nihon-fukei-senshu-: The cliffside Kannon Hall at Abuto, perched above the Inland Sea, drawn from Hasui's Selected Views of Japan. The shin-hanga print exploits a high horizon and bokashi water gradations to set the small temple against a wide expanse of tide.

A coastal view of Goryō on the Amakusa islands of Kyushu, from Hasui's Selected Views of Japan. The shin-hanga design pairs distant headlands with quiet anchorage, demonstrating the careful balance of woodblock colour and reserve that defined Hasui's travel sheets.

A woodblock print from Hasui's Selected Views of Japan, depicting Mount Onsengadake on the Shimabara Peninsula as seen across the water from Amakusa. Hasui captures the silhouetted volcano in the soft tonal washes that defined his shin-hanga landscapes.

Morning light over the hot-spring town of Beppu, rendered in the muted blues and greys typical of Hasui's shin-hanga manner. Part of the Selected Views of Japan series, the print balances atmospheric mist against the orderly geometry of harbor architecture.

A coastal view of Iwafune in the former Bingo Province, in which Hasui sets fishing vessels against weathered shoreline rocks. The print belongs to the Selected Views of Japan series and exemplifies his preference for unpeopled, contemplative landscape.

Cherry trees in bloom at Kakize in former Bungo Province, treated with the soft pinks and grey greens of early spring. Issued within the Selected Views of Japan series, it joins Hasui's broader survey of Japan's regional natural calendar.

A view of the seaside district of Hakozaki in former Chikuzen Province, near present-day Fukuoka. Hasui places the famous shrine precinct within a low coastal panorama, using flat color planes characteristic of his shin-hanga collaborations with publisher Watanabe Shozaburo.

Hasui depicts the Kayagafuchi rapids deep in Chomonkyo Gorge, a famous Yamaguchi ravine of sheer rock walls and clear water. The print belongs to Selected Views of Japan and shows his characteristic handling of river surfaces in graded blue.

A quiet hot-spring scene at Tochinoki in former Higo Province, set among Kumamoto's mountain forests. Hasui frames the bathhouse village with overhanging trees, employing the soft graded tones that became his signature within the shin-hanga movement.

An island view of Kabeshima off the Hizen coast in present-day Saga Prefecture, shown with low cliffs meeting still water. The print is part of Hasui's Selected Views of Japan, his sustained traveler's record of regional scenery.

A view of Kanahama on the Hizen coast, where Hasui balances rocky foreground shore against a broader open seascape. Issued in the Selected Views of Japan series, it reflects his interest in the quieter shorelines of Kyushu.

The fishing village of Kyodomari in former Hizen Province, set into a sheltered cove of the Kyushu coast. Hasui uses softly registered color blocks and a low horizon line, in keeping with the contemplative pace of the Selected Views of Japan series.

Dawn over the harbor of Mihogaseki in former Izumo Province on the Sea of Japan, rendered in cool grey-blues warming toward the horizon. From Selected Views of Japan, the print typifies Hasui's attention to specific times of day.

A view of Kiyomizu Temple at Yasugi in former Izumo Province, the wooden hall set among tall cedars. Within Selected Views of Japan, Hasui treats the shaded temple precinct with the carefully bokashi-graded skies of shin-hanga printmaking.

The Kotsuki River winding through Kagoshima city, with the volcanic outline of Sakurajima implied in the distance. Part of Selected Views of Japan, the print weighs urban riverbank against open water in Hasui's characteristic flat color planes.

A view of Sakurajima, the active volcano rising from the bay opposite Kagoshima, here seen across calm water. From Selected Views of Japan, Hasui simplifies the mountain into a tonal silhouette in the manner of the shin-hanga landscape.

A street scene at Kasuga-machi in Kumamoto, with low wooden buildings receding along a quiet thoroughfare. Issued in Selected Views of Japan, it shows Hasui turning from grand scenery to the modest texture of a provincial town.

Nezame-no-Toko, the celebrated stretch of pale, water-sculpted rock along the Kiso River in Nagano. Hasui treats the famous gorge with crisp green water and bleached stone, placing the site within his Selected Views of Japan.

A view of Suhara, a post-station village along the old Nakasendo highway in the Kiso valley. Within Selected Views of Japan, Hasui sets clustered roofs against the surrounding mountains in his customary flat planes of color.

Miyuki Bridge below the towering walls of Kumamoto Castle, with figures crossing in front of the fortress's distinctive curved stonework. The print belongs to Hasui's Selected Views of Japan and pairs civic architecture with everyday passage.

Lake Ezu on the outskirts of Kumamoto, its still water spreading toward distant mountains. From Selected Views of Japan, the print exemplifies Hasui's preference for water surfaces graded in blue bokashi to suggest depth and weather.

The vermilion Daigokuden, main hall of the Heian Shrine in Kyoto, modeled on the imperial palace of the ancient Heian capital. Hasui sets the bright lacquered architecture against open gravel courtyard and a soft sky in this Selected Views of Japan plate.

Evening along the Kamo River in Kyoto, with low riverside buildings silhouetted against a graded dusk sky. Part of Selected Views of Japan, the print pairs Hasui's interest in city water with the long twilight of a Kyoto summer.

Nonomiya Shrine in the bamboo groves of Sagano, west of Kyoto, traditionally the site where imperial princesses purified themselves before service at Ise. Hasui treats the dim grove and torii in greens and greys for Selected Views of Japan.

A view of Kanaya-machi, a hillside neighborhood overlooking the harbor of Nagasaki. Issued within Selected Views of Japan, the print sets stepped streets and tile roofs against the city's sheltered bay in Hasui's flat shin-hanga palette.

Sofuku-ji, an Obaku Zen temple in Nagasaki founded by Chinese merchants in the seventeenth century, recognizable by its red Ming-style gate. Hasui frames the precinct within Selected Views of Japan, marking Nagasaki's distinctive Sino-Japanese heritage.

A view of Okayama Castle, the sixteenth-century black-walled keep known as the Crow Castle, mirrored in the Asahi River. Hasui treats the silhouette in deep tonal blocks for his Selected Views of Japan.

The steep slope leading up to Senko-ji Temple in the port town of Onomichi, with rooftops descending toward the Inland Sea. From Selected Views of Japan, the print captures Onomichi's celebrated layered terrain.

A modest hot-spring village at Anraku in Osumi, southern Kyushu, set among low hills. The print is part of Hasui's Selected Views of Japan and reflects his persistent interest in regional bathing towns rendered in muted tones.

The volcanic Mount Mayuyama rising behind the harbor of Shimabara on Kyushu's Ariake coast. Within Selected Views of Japan, Hasui pairs working harbor and looming peak in a composition typical of his coastal landscapes.

Tsukumojima, the cluster of small islets scattered through the bay near Shimabara on Kyushu. From Selected Views of Japan, Hasui arranges the pine-tufted islands against still water in graded blue bokashi.

The Golden Pavilion of Kinkaku-ji in Kyoto under fresh snow, its gilded upper stories set against bare branches and a frozen pond. From Selected Views of Japan, this winter scene is among Hasui's most familiar treatments of a celebrated subject.
collection-of-beauty-nikko-kaido-hasui-k: A shin-hanga woodblock print depicting a quiet stretch of the Nikkō Kaidō, the historic highway leading to the Tokugawa mausoleum. Hasui captures the avenue's towering cryptomeria with the soft atmospheric tonality and precise gradation that define his landscape work.
collection-of-beauty-nikolay-ii-of-russi: A formal portrait of Tsar Nicholas II, the last emperor of Russia, painted shortly after his coronation. Repin balances state-portrait dignity with the loose, vigorous brushwork that distinguished him among the Peredvizhniki realists.
collection-of-beauty-nitta-yoshisada-tsu: A historical woodblock print showing the medieval samurai Nitta Yoshisada at the Battle of Ikuta Forest, where his vassal Oyamada Takaie sacrificed himself in his lord's place. The composition follows the dramatic narrative conventions of late Meiji musha-e warrior prints.
collection-of-beauty-nocturne-by-james-a: Part of Whistler's celebrated Nocturne series, in which the painter pared the Thames down to washes of dusky blue and pewter. The work treats landscape as a tonal arrangement, anticipating the abstract sensibilities Whistler championed in his Aesthetic writings.
collection-of-beauty-northeaster-by-wins: A lone outcrop of the Maine coast pummeled by a winter storm, rendered with the muscular realism Homer developed at Prout's Neck. The painting strips the marine subject to surf, rock, and spray, a meditation on raw natural force.
collection-of-beauty-nuremberg-chronicle: A celebrated woodcut from Hartmann Schedel's Nuremberg Chronicle of 1493, showing capering skeletons in the late-medieval danse macabre tradition. Wolgemut's workshop produced one of the most ambitious printed-image programs of the incunabula period.
collection-of-beauty-nympheas-71293-3: One of the water-lily compositions Monet painted at his Giverny pond, dissolving the surface into floating pads, reflected sky, and submerged depths. The late series pushed Impressionism toward an immersive, near-abstract vision of light on water.
collection-of-beauty-o-soldado-esquecido: A monumental anti-war canvas by the Russian battle painter Vereshchagin, showing a fallen soldier abandoned on the battlefield as carrion birds gather. The work is part of the artist's unflinching cycle exposing the human cost of imperial conflict.
collection-of-beauty-ochanomizu-57588716: From Hasui's Twenty Views of Tokyo, this woodblock print shows wet snow muffling the rooftops along the Kanda River at Ochanomizu. The artist was renowned for his subtle differentiation of snowfall, here rendered as soft cotton-like accumulation.
collection-of-beauty-oeillets-et-clemati: A late still life painted in Manet's final months, when illness confined him to small flower pieces brought by visitors. Carnations and clematis are set in a faceted crystal vase, the brushwork economical yet luminous against a darkened ground.
collection-of-beauty-on-the-beach-two-ar: A wood engraving after Winslow Homer published in Harper's Weekly, depicting a flirtation interrupted on a New England beach. Homer's Reconstruction-era illustrations chronicled American leisure with a wry observational eye.
collection-of-beauty-ono-shindon-in-the-: From Hokusai's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, this print shows farmers tending paddy fields at Ōno in Suruga Province. The flattened planes of green rice terraces lead the eye toward Fuji's familiar silhouette in the distance.
collection-of-beauty-p-1948-sc-276-scale: A landscape from Monet's mature Impressionist period, catalogued as Wildenstein 1192. The painter's broken touch and high-keyed palette translate atmosphere and shifting light into the building blocks of the picture.
collection-of-beauty-paisaje-con-san-jer: Patinir places the penitent Saint Jerome amid a sweeping panoramic landscape of crags, rivers, and distant towns. The Flemish painter is credited with elevating landscape to a primary subject in Western art, the figure dwarfed by the world he renounces.
collection-of-beauty-pallas-athena-by-re: A figure in gleaming helmet and gorget, traditionally identified as the goddess of wisdom and war. Rembrandt models the armour with thick, gilded impasto against deep shadow, a hallmark of his late chiaroscuro.
collection-of-beauty-pallasetlecentaure: Botticelli pairs the chaste, armoured Pallas with a subdued centaur in a tempera allegory of reason mastering brute instinct. The work was made for the Medici and is rich with humanist symbolism, including the entwined diamond rings of Lorenzo's emblem.
collection-of-beauty-pan-syrinx-rubens: Rubens stages the Ovidian myth of Pan pursuing the nymph Syrinx, who becomes a stand of reeds at the moment of capture. The figures and lush riverside vegetation were painted in collaboration with Jan Brueghel the Elder.
collection-of-beauty-paolo-uccello-heili: Uccello's late panel of the chivalric saint lancing the dragon, with a tethered princess and a stylised storm gathering overhead. The artist's lifelong fascination with linear perspective is visible in the geometric grid of the cave and patterned ground.
collection-of-beauty-paolo-veronese-the-: An early devotional canvas in which Christ raises the kneeling Magdalene from her former life. Veronese's Venetian instinct for sumptuous fabric, architectural staging, and silvery light is already evident in this youthful composition.
collection-of-beauty-paolo-veronese-juno: A ceiling allegory painted for the Doge's Palace, in which Juno rains crowns, jewels, and ducal regalia onto a personified Venice. Veronese exploits the di sotto in sù viewpoint to send the goddess plunging through illusory clouds.
collection-of-beauty-paolo-veronese-lucr: Veronese's Lucretia presses the dagger to her breast in the Roman matron's act of self-destruction after her violation by Tarquin. The painter dresses the heroine in Venetian silks, fusing classical exemplum with sixteenth-century courtly elegance.
collection-of-beauty-paolo-veronese-the-: The defeated Persian queen and her household kneel before Alexander the Great after Issus, in a vast frieze-like composition acquired by London's National Gallery in 1857. Veronese fills the picture with contemporary Venetian costume, courtly architecture, and a noble gesture of clemency.
collection-of-beauty-paolo-veronese-008: A reproduction documenting a painting by the Venetian master Paolo Veronese, photographed without its frame for archival study. Veronese's output spans grand religious cycles, mythologies, and ceiling allegories executed across sixteenth-century Venice.
collection-of-beauty-parmigianino-madonn: Known as the Madonna of the Long Neck, this unfinished altarpiece is a touchstone of Italian Mannerism. Parmigianino elongates the Virgin's anatomy and tilts the spatial logic of the column and miniature prophet for an unsettling, refined elegance.
collection-of-beauty-parmigianino-004: Parmigianino's celebrated late altarpiece, in which the serpentine Madonna cradles an unusually elongated Christ Child. The exaggerated proportions and ambiguous architecture exemplify the Mannerist taste for grace pushed beyond classical norm.
collection-of-beauty-parting-spring-by-k: The left panel of a pair of folding screens by Kawai Gyokudō, designated an Important Cultural Property of Japan. Working in the nihonga tradition, the artist evokes the melancholy of late spring through misty mountains and falling cherry blossoms.

The right panel of Gyokudō's Parting Spring screens, completing a continuous landscape of waterfalls, drifting petals, and veiled peaks. The nihonga master fuses traditional ink technique with subtle modern atmospheric effect.
collection-of-beauty-paul-cezanne-1892-9: Two Provençal labourers face off across a wine bottle in Cézanne's most distilled treatment of the card-players theme. The Courtauld version reduces the cast to its essential pair, the figures built from the planar, faceted strokes that prefigured Cubism.
collection-of-beauty-paul-cezanne-montag: Cézanne returned obsessively to the limestone ridge near his native Aix-en-Provence, structuring its slopes through patches of constructive brushwork. The painting treats landscape as an architecture of colour planes, foundational for early modernism.
collection-of-beauty-paul-gauguin-001: An academic life study from Gauguin's early years, predating the Tahitian primitivism for which he is now remembered. The figure is modelled with conventional tonal shading, evidence of the studio training he would soon discard for synthetism.
collection-of-beauty-paul-gauguin-085: A view of the ancient Roman necropolis at Arles, painted during Gauguin's stay with Van Gogh in autumn 1888. The avenue of stone sarcophagi recedes between flame-orange trees, the foreground simplified into broad zones of saturated color that anticipate the cloisonnist style.
collection-of-beauty-paul-gauguin-087: An early Martinique canvas from Gauguin's 1887 stay near Saint-Pierre, showing local women gathering fruit beneath dense tropical foliage. Painted before his Tahitian voyages, the work already shows his shift away from Impressionist brushwork toward flatter, decorative arrangements of figure and landscape.
collection-of-beauty-paul-gauguin-113: Mahana no atua, painted in Paris between Gauguin's two Tahitian sojourns, presents an idol of the moon goddess Hina flanked by ritual bathers. The lower foreground dissolves into pools of pure, mirror-bright color, an experiment in flat decorative abstraction that would influence Nabi and Fauve painters.
collection-of-beauty-paul-gustave-dore-a: Gustave Dore's vision of Andromeda, the Ethiopian princess chained to a sea cliff awaiting Perseus's rescue. The composition exploits Dore's signature contrast of luminous flesh against turbulent rock and sky, a Romantic theatricality refined through years of book illustration. This file represents a digitally restored version of the image.
collection-of-beauty-paul-de-vos-jan-wil: A Flemish Baroque collaboration in which Jan Wildens supplied the wooded paradise landscape and Paul de Vos populated it with the assembled animals of creation. Such division of labor was standard practice in Antwerp's specialist workshops, here producing a dense menagerie set within a luminous park-like clearing.
collection-of-beauty-paul-de-vos-bull-su: A bull-baiting scene of the kind de Vos perfected in Rubens's Antwerp circle, with mastiffs lunging at a tethered bull's flanks. The diagonal tangle of muscle, horn and fang demonstrates the Flemish taste for animal combats as showcases of anatomical drama and tonal bravura.
collection-of-beauty-paul-de-vos-cats-fi: A pantry overturned by brawling cats among hanging game, fish and copper vessels, a kitchen-piece genre popularized by Frans Snyders and continued by his brother-in-law de Vos. The hissing animals serve as a moralized emblem of greed and chaos disrupting domestic order.
collection-of-beauty-paul-de-vos-still-l: A monumental Flemish pronk still life heaped with fruit, game and silver, attended by a servant figure likely contributed by a collaborating figure painter. The composition exemplifies the Antwerp specialty of combining a Snyders-school animal painter with a portraitist for sumptuous decorative ensembles.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-k: An allegory of the four continents personified as river gods reclining beside their female consorts, traditionally identified with the Danube, Nile, Ganges and Rio de la Plata. Tigers, a crocodile and lush vegetation underscore the cosmographic ambition of the Vienna canvas.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-f: The Titan, chained to a Caucasian crag, writhes as the eagle of Zeus tears at his liver in a horizontal composition Rubens developed with Frans Snyders, who painted the bird. The torsion of the body shows Rubens's debt to Michelangelo and the Laocoon, fused with North-Italian colorism.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-k: A spare, intimate Lamentation in which the Virgin and Saint John bend over Christ's pale body laid against a darkened ground. Rubens reduces the cast to its essential mourners, concentrating Counter-Reformation devotional intensity in the contrast of cool flesh and ash-gray drapery.

A storm-drenched Ovidian landscape in which Jupiter and Mercury, sheltered by the pious Philemon and Baucis, unleash a deluge upon the surrounding valley. The boiling sky, falling trees and rain-swollen torrent show Rubens absorbing Venetian and Adam Elsheimer landscape models for cosmic moral drama.

Bishop Ambrose bars Emperor Theodosius from Milan Cathedral after the massacre at Thessalonica, a Counter-Reformation parable of clerical authority over secular power. Rubens stages the confrontation on the church steps with massed retinues, reworking a composition first treated by his Venetian model Veronese.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-t: Venus rushes to the dying Adonis as he collapses beside the boar that has gored him, while putti mourn and his hounds bay. The sweeping diagonal of the lovers' bodies and the velvet handling of flesh against thunderous sky exemplify Rubens's mature Ovidian manner.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-d: A monumental Saint Jerome at his desk, lion at his feet, translating the Vulgate by lamplight. Rubens emphasizes the hermit-scholar's powerful aged anatomy and the deep red drapery of the cardinal, a saint frequently invoked in Counter-Reformation imagery as a model of biblical orthodoxy.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-1: Hercules grapples the invulnerable Nemean lion in a knot of straining limbs, the hero's first labor reduced to its essential close-quarters violence. The Apsley House version follows a Rubens prototype that translates antique sculptural sources, especially the Belvedere Torso, into Baroque flesh and sinew.

A bust-length portrait of a bearded gentleman in the stiff white ruff fashionable in early seventeenth-century Antwerp. The Colchester picture is an after-Rubens version, its frontal pose and economical handling typical of workshop replicas produced for civic and merchant patrons.

After the Old Testament episode in Numbers, Moses raises a bronze serpent on a pole to heal the Israelites bitten by venomous snakes, a type of the Crucifixion. The Shipley version follows a Rubens design of swirling, fallen and pleading bodies that emphasizes Counter-Reformation themes of intercession.

A workshop-tradition Madonna and Child in which the Virgin cradles a robust infant Christ against a dark ground. The composition follows one of Rubens's many devotional formulas, refined in his Antwerp shop for export across Catholic Europe.

A school-of-Rubens Lamentation in which the dead Christ is supported by Mary, John and the holy women in a tightly knit pyramid of grief. The piled drapery and waxen flesh follow patterns Rubens established in altarpieces for Antwerp's churches after his return from Italy.

Cain raises a jawbone-club above his fallen brother in a violent, sculpturally compressed composition modelled on Michelangelo's Sistine ignudi. The Courtauld panel is a small but ferocious meditation on humanity's first murder, its diagonal locked into the surface like a relief.

Christ confers the keys of binding and loosing on Saint Peter, the foundational image of papal primacy revived for Counter-Reformation polemic. Rubens isolates the two figures against an open sky, emphasizing the gesture's solemnity and the apostle's age-roughened hands.

Diana and her companions recoil from leering satyrs at a forest pool in a late mythological invention treating chastity disturbed. Rubens entrusted much of the landscape and animal staffage to specialist collaborators, while reserving the warm, dimpled female nudes for himself.

The huntress goddess in red, drawing back her bow as hounds drive a stag through dense woodland. The picture belongs to Rubens's late series of aristocratic chase subjects, where landscape, animals and figure painting were typically distributed among studio specialists.

An oil head study, painted directly from a Antwerp model, of the kind Rubens kept on file for reuse as apostles, philosophers and patriarchs in larger compositions. The rapid wet-into-wet handling and ruddy flesh tones show his working method at its most direct.

The sea-god enthrones his Nereid bride amid a tumult of tritons, sea-horses and rolling waves. Rubens treats the marine pageantry as a fleshy Baroque counterpart to ancient sea-thiasos sarcophagi, the muscular Neptune contrasted with Amphitrite's pearly skin.

A bust portrait of an aged sitter in dark robes and a white collar, his face recorded with the unsentimental specificity of Rubens's mature portraiture. Studies of this kind doubled as preparatory material for biblical and historical figures requiring weathered authority.

The Carmelite reformer kneels in ecstasy as the Holy Spirit descends in the form of a dove, an episode central to her Vida and to Counter-Reformation devotional propaganda. Rubens renders the moment as a swift, fluid oil sketch built around upward-spiralling drapery and light.

The drunken tutor of Bacchus staggers forward, supported by satyrs and maenads in a frieze of overripe flesh, grapes and laughter. Rubens treats Silenus as a Christianized parable of intemperance, the figures drawn from antique sources but warmed by Venetian color.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-f: Venus and her attendants strip the war-god of his armor while putti tug at his weapons, an allegory of love's pacifying power over violence. Painted in collaboration with Jan Brueghel the Elder, who supplied the rich still life of arms, garlands and shimmering accoutrements.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-k: A bound and crowned Christ is presented to the crowd, his torso lit against a brooding ground in the manner Rubens used to dramatize Passion scenes. The taut musculature and fall of light over the shoulders intensify the Counter-Reformation appeal to compassion.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-s: A workshop image of Democritus, the Greek thinker known in antiquity as the laughing philosopher, mocking the follies of humankind. His broad smile and weathered features draw on the Northern tradition of philosopher portraits revived by Rubens and his circle.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-a: An expansive panorama of the artist's country estate near Antwerp, the chateau set among fields, hedgerows and a low morning sun that throws long shadows across the foreground. Painted on multiple oak panels joined together, it stands among the great late landscapes of the Northern Baroque.

Rubens's homage to Titian, copied during his stay in Madrid where he studied the royal collection. The serpent-child reaches down to Eve as Adam grasps her arm; Rubens warms the Venetian palette and softens the contours toward his own fleshier ideal.

Shepherds press in around the manger, faces lit from below by the Christ child as Mary draws back the cloth. The diagonal massing of figures and theatrical lighting reflect Rubens's recent absorption of Caravaggio during his Italian years.

An ecorche drawing exposing the muscles of the male torso and limbs, made for the artist's own study of human structure. Such sheets fed the powerfully built figures of his history paintings and were later engraved as a teaching aid.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-c: The first murder rendered as violent close-quarters struggle, Cain's club raised above his fallen brother. Rubens compresses the action into a tight pyramidal group, exploiting contorted limbs and chiaroscuro for moral force.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-d: Painted on the outer wing of the Antwerp Cathedral triptych, this panel forms part of one of the central Counter-Reformation altarpieces of the Low Countries. The weight of Christ's body, lowered along a white shroud, anchors a tightly knit group of mourners.

Diana and her companions, returning from the hunt with hounds and game, are accosted by lecherous satyrs at the edge of a wood. The collision of nude bodies, fur and dead quarry typifies Rubens's late mythological mode.

The huntress goddess strides forward with her nymphs, bow in hand and hounds at her heels, fresh game slung from her shoulder. The composition celebrates the chase as a pretext for studies of the female nude in motion.

The Stoic philosopher stands in a basin while a physician opens his veins, his upturned head and outstretched arms recalling the antique sculpture Rubens had drawn in Rome. The picture distills the Baroque taste for Stoic exemplars of virtuous death.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-e: A peasant cart laden with felled timber rumbles down a rutted track as the day's last light catches clouds and treetops. The naturalistic Flemish countryside and warm low sun place this among the personal landscapes Rubens painted for his own pleasure.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-f: Four oil sketches of the same Black sitter, observed from different angles and in varied light, made as working material for finished compositions including the Adoration of the Magi. The studies are notable for their direct, dignified attention to an individual model.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-h: Mary, the infant Christ, Joseph and the young Saint John gather in a domestic interior with a parrot perched nearby, a bird traditionally read as a symbol of the Virgin's purity. The warmly lit grouping shows Rubens reworking a Renaissance devotional formula in his own fleshy idiom.
Hercules wraps the invulnerable lion in a strangling embrace, the hero's bunched muscles answered by the beast's twisting flank. The motif derives from antique sarcophagi Rubens had studied in Italy.

An allegorical Hercules, club raised, tramples a writhing female personification of Discord underfoot. The image was conceived as a ceiling design celebrating princely virtue triumphing over civil strife.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-i: Rubens's first wife is portrayed in the guise of Glycera, the ancient Greek flower seller, garlanded and surrounded by blooms. The conceit fuses tender domestic likeness with classical learning.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-j: Judith holds aloft the severed head of the Assyrian general while her maidservant looks on in the half-dark of the tent. Rubens treats the Old Testament heroine with the heightened drama and tactile flesh of his early Italianate manner.

A bust-length portrait of a young man in a black flat cap, copied after a sixteenth-century original by the Antwerp painter Willem Key. The grave, even lighting and restrained palette preserve the sober character of the Northern Renaissance prototype.

Juno gathers the hundred eyes of the slain giant Argus from his severed head and sets them into the tail of her peacock. The Ovidian subject is staged as a brilliantly coloured open-air tableau of nymphs, birds and rich textiles.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-l: The dead Christ lies on a sheaf of straw, mourned by the Virgin, John and the Marys at the foot of the Cross. The pallid body, sharply foreshortened, recalls Rubens's debt to Italian models of the Pieta.

A nocturnal Lamentation in which torchlight rakes across Christ's body and the bowed faces of his mourners. The compressed grouping and dark surround intensify the emotional pitch in the Italianate Baroque manner.

A broad Flemish countryside opens out under a sky cleared by a passing storm, a rainbow arching over fields, milkmaids and grazing cattle. Often paired with the View of Het Steen, it expresses Rubens's late identification with the land around his country house.

Roman ruins on the Palatine hill rise above figures and herds in a panoramic landscape conceived from sketches made during the artist's years in Italy. Antiquity is set in dialogue with a fertile, lived-in countryside.

Christ raises the bread at the centre of the long table while the apostles react in agitation; Judas turns toward the viewer with averted eyes. The picture concentrates on the moment of institution rather than the announcement of betrayal, in line with Counter-Reformation Eucharistic devotion.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-m: The Virgin is borne aloft by a swirl of angels while the apostles cluster around her empty tomb below. The upward spiralling composition is a hallmark of Rubens's altarpieces for the Antwerp churches of the early seventeenth century.

Mercury, sent by Jupiter, lulls the hundred-eyed guardian Argus to sleep with his pipes before drawing his sword. The Ovidian story is staged in a wooded landscape with the white heifer Io tethered to one side.

Moses raises the bronze serpent on a pole as stricken Israelites writhe in the foreground, their wounds healed at a glance. The crowded, twisting figures provide a typological prefiguration of the Crucifixion familiar in Counter-Reformation imagery.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-n: A red chalk drawing of the painter's young son, his round face and tousled hair caught with the directness of family observation. Such intimate studies of his children are among the most personal works in Rubens's draughtsmanship.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-o: An aged woman shields a candle flame as a boy leans in to light his own taper from hers, the two faces modelled by the small light source. The picture belongs to the Northern tradition of nocturnal genre studies descending from Caravaggio and the Bassano workshop.

An aged peasant woman bent beneath a heavy basket of coal, her weathered face caught in firelit chiaroscuro. Painted in Rubens's late manner, the unsentimental study of labor and age departs from his courtly subjects, prizing physiognomic truth and tactile flesh.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-p: Perseus, fresh from slaying the sea-monster, unbinds the nude Andromeda as putti and Pegasus crowd the rocky shore. The composition pairs heroic male armor with luminous female flesh in a swirling Baroque celebration of rescue and erotic deliverance.

The apostle Peter, instructed by Christ to pay the Temple tax, retrieves a silver coin from a fish's mouth (Matthew 17:27). Rubens stages the miracle as a robust half-length figure study, the fisherman's tanned forearms and concentrated gaze given Caravaggesque weight.

The German Catholic polemicist Kaspar Schoppe sits at his writing table, pen in hand, returning the viewer's gaze with scholarly directness. Painted during Rubens's Italian years, the portrait fuses Venetian color with the sober psychological scrutiny of Northern humanist likenesses.

The Genoese-born Habsburg general Ambrogio Spinola, victor at Breda, appears in burnished black armor with a crisp lace collar and the red sash of command. Rubens, who knew the marquess personally during the Twelve Years' Truce, distills authority into a steady, weary gaze.

An intimate likeness of the artist's daughter Clara Serena, who died at twelve, her round cheeks flushed and chestnut hair loosely brushed. The brisk, affectionate handling, more sketch than commission, ranks among the most tender child portraits of the seventeenth century.
collection-of-beauty-peter-paul-rubens-s: Saint Francis of Assisi kneels in ecstatic communion, his gaunt features and brown habit picked out against a darkened ground. The economy of means and devotional intensity reflect Counter-Reformation taste for affective images of penitent saints.

The painter, near sixty, presents himself in broad-brimmed hat, sword, and gloves — courtier and diplomat as much as artist. The dignified late self-portrait, withholding palette and brushes, asserts his ennoblement and standing among Europe's rulers.

The drunken satyr Silenus, tutor of Bacchus, lurches forward supported by his bacchic retinue, his bloated torso pinkly luminous against shadow. Rubens revisits the antique theme with characteristic relish for unidealized flesh and the comic gravity of intoxication.

Silenus sprawls in heavy slumber while satyrs, nymphs and a leopard wreath him with grapes in a leafy clearing. Painted soon after Rubens's return from Italy, the canvas distills Bacchic mythology into a pulsing arrangement of intertwined nudes and animal life.

An oil sketch of a bearded male head, turned in three-quarter view and lit from above. Such tronies served as workshop reference for apostles and biblical elders, and show Rubens's quick, fluent brushwork unburdened by narrative finish.
