collection-of-beauty-edouard-manet-kears: The Confederate raider Alabama lists and burns under fire from the Union sloop Kearsarge off Cherbourg, smoke staining a blue Channel sea. Manet, who was reportedly close enough to watch French pilot boats return from the engagement, treats this American Civil War moment with a high horizon and broadly brushed water that anticipates marine Impressionism.
collection-of-beauty-edouard-manet-der-f: A bouquet of white lilacs leans from a clear glass vase against a darkened ground, the flowers worked in loaded, gestural strokes. The picture belongs to the small floral still lifes Manet painted at the very end of his life, when illness had reduced his ambitions to flowers brought by visitors.
collection-of-beauty-edouard-manet-le-ch: Two angels support the slumped body of the dead Christ, his pallid torso lit against shadow. Manet borrows a traditional Lamentation type but flattens modeling and exposes brushwork in a way that scandalized the 1864 Salon, where critics also faulted the placement of the side wound on the wrong flank.
collection-of-beauty-edouard-manet-by-he: Manet stands three-quarter length in dark coat and top hat, gloves and cane in hand, framed against an austere studio ground. Fantin-Latour's portrait, the basis for his role in the group canvas Hommage à Manet, presents the Impressionist forerunner with the dandified self-possession his contemporaries described.
collection-of-beauty-elisabeth-louise-vi: The painter of picturesque ruins, Hubert Robert, is shown half-length in painter's smock, palette in hand, his unruly hair and quick-eyed glance conveying improvisatory energy. Vigée Le Brun's portrait of her colleague is a touchstone of late Ancien Régime artistic friendship, executed shortly before the Revolution dispersed both sitter and painter.

A bust-length portrait of the marine painter Joseph Vernet, posed in a powdered wig and dark coat against a neutral ground. Vigée Le Brun renders the older painter with the soft modeling and warm flesh tones that defined her late-Ancien Régime studio practice, treating him as a colleague rather than a patron.
collection-of-beauty-elisabeth-vigee-leb: Marie-Jeanne Bécu, the former royal mistress of Louis XV, is shown in a relaxed garden setting with loose curls and a simple white gown. The image belongs to a series of informal portraits painted at Louveciennes, exchanging court formality for the pastoral neoclassical mode then fashionable in Paris.

Emma Hart, later Lady Hamilton, sits in three-quarter view with cascading dark hair and an open shawl, her gaze turned outward. The portrait reflects Vigée Le Brun's Italian period and her interest in the antique 'attitudes' for which Hamilton was already celebrated in Naples.

The Russian countess reclines in a white empire-line gown trimmed with gauze, painted during Vigée Le Brun's emigration years among the European aristocracy. The picture's smooth porcelain finish and pearly tonality typify her courtly manner adapted to Saint Petersburg taste.
collection-of-beauty: Victorious Central Asian troops stand among severed heads and abandoned weapons after the action, the celebration unsettling rather than triumphant. Paired with 'After a failure', the picture insists that battlefield outcomes share a single moral landscape of slaughter.
collection-of-beauty-2: A Kyrgyz family is on the move across the steppe with camels, horses, and bundled yurt poles, the procession strung out under a wide sky. Vereshchagin records the seasonal migration with documentary attention to harness, load, and the choreography of nomadic transport.
collection-of-beauty__selling-a-child-sl: A Central Asian slave market scene, with a turbaned merchant inspecting a naked boy offered for sale. Vereshchagin's Turkestan series used such unflinching ethnographic detail as moral indictment, the cool light and tile-work setting heightening the transactional cruelty.
collection-of-beauty__prokhod-barskaun: A view of the Barskoon gorge in the Tian Shan mountains, painted during Vereshchagin's travels through Russian Turkestan. Rocky cliffs and a thin descending road register his documentary interest in the terrain of the empire's southern frontier.
collection-of-beauty__razvaliny-chugucha: Crumbling walls and abandoned masonry mark the ruins of Chuguchak (Tacheng), destroyed during the Dungan Revolt shortly before Vereshchagin's visit. The painter renders the desolation with a topographer's precision, treating wreckage as both landscape and historical witness.
collection-of-beauty__ruins-of-chinese-s: The shattered remains of a Chinese temple at Ak-Kent, recorded during Vereshchagin's first Turkestan expedition. Broken roof tiles and exposed beams testify to the violence of the Dungan uprising that swept the region in the 1860s.
collection-of-beauty__ukrainian-woman: A young Ukrainian peasant woman in embroidered dress, painted with Repin's characteristic warmth of touch and earth-toned palette. The study reflects his recurring engagement with rural Ukrainian subjects and the ethnographic realism of the Peredvizhniki circle.
collection-of-beauty__a-garden-gate-in-c: A weathered wooden gate set into a sun-bleached adobe wall, sketched on Vereshchagin's Central Asian journey. The painter's eye for vernacular architecture turns a quiet doorway into a record of Chuguchak's domestic fabric in the wake of regional war.
collection-of-beauty__by-the-fortress-wa: From the Turkestan series, the canvas commemorates the Russian defense of Samarkand in 1868: soldiers crouch behind a defensive wall, awaiting the order to admit attackers within firing range. The terse caption carries the painting's pent-up tension.
collection-of-beauty__fakir: A solitary Indian ascetic, painted after Vereshchagin's travels through India in the early 1870s. The figure's gaunt frame and bright cloth set against a plain ground typify the artist's documentary approach to religious and ethnographic subjects abroad.
collection-of-beauty__yaponka: A young Japanese woman in kimono, made during Vereshchagin's 1903 journey to Japan, his last major travel project. The bright textiles and frontal pose continue his lifelong practice of ethnographic portraiture across Asia.
collection-of-beauty-finches-and-bamboo-: A handscroll of finches perched on slender bamboo, attributed to the Northern Song emperor and devoted painter Huizong. The work exemplifies the courtly bird-and-flower idiom: minute feather observation paired with the calligraphic restraint of monochrome bamboo.
collection-of-beauty-9: A Southern Song album leaf depicting a spring tide laden with rain, attributed to the court painter Li Di. Soft ink wash and atmospheric distance reflect the lyrical small-format landscape tradition cultivated at the Hangzhou academy.
collection-of-beauty__snowscape-ma-yuan: A winter landscape by the Southern Song master Ma Yuan, working in the spare 'one-corner' composition for which he is named. Bare trees and rocky outcrops occupy a fraction of the silk while open mist suggests the cold expanse beyond.
audubon-birds-100-marsh-wren: Plate 100 of The Birds of America shows the Marsh Wren clinging among reedy stems of its nesting habitat. Engraved and aquatinted by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's watercolor, the life-size composition pairs species portrait with carefully observed wetland setting.
audubon-birds-101-raven: Plate 101 presents the Common Raven at full life size, a composition that pushed the limits of the double-elephant folio sheet. Glossy black plumage is rendered through the hand-coloring of Havell's aquatint, the bird shown with branch and prey in characteristic Audubon staging.
audubon-birds-102-blue-jay: Plate 102 depicts three Blue Jays raiding eggs from another bird's nest, a tableau emphasizing Audubon's interest in behavior as well as form. Havell's aquatint preserves the iridescent barring of the wings and the dynamic diagonal grouping.
audubon-birds-103-canada-warbler: Plate 103 shows the Canada Warbler among the foliage of a flowering plant, the yellow breast and necklace of streaks rendered with diagnostic precision. Audubon's habit of pairing each species with botanically accurate vegetation is on full display.
audubon-birds-104-chipping-sparrow: Plate 104 depicts the Chipping Sparrow perched on a flowering branch, its rufous crown and clean breast carefully delineated. The aquatint preserves the soft gradations of the small songbird's plumage achieved in Audubon's original watercolor.
audubon-birds-105-red-breasted-nuthatch: Plate 105 shows the Red-breasted Nuthatch in its characteristic head-down posture on a conifer trunk. Havell's aquatint renders the slate-blue back, rust underparts, and bold facial striping at life size.
audubon-birds-106-black-vulture-or-carri: Plate 106 presents two Black Vultures at a carcass, a subject that gave Audubon the chance to depict carrion-feeding behavior alongside the species' bare gray head and broad wings. The composition fills the double-elephant sheet with its scavenging pair.
audubon-birds-107-canada-jay: Plate 107 depicts the Canada Jay (now Gray Jay) among coniferous branches of the northern forests it inhabits. Soft gray and white plumage is conveyed through subtle aquatint tones and selective hand-coloring.
audubon-birds-108-fox-coloured-sparrow: Plate 108 shows the Fox Sparrow, its rich rufous tail and heavily streaked breast set against a sprig of ground vegetation. The aquatint captures the species' stout bunting-like build and warm coloration.
audubon-birds-109-savannah-finch: Plate 109 depicts the Savannah Sparrow, the small grassland songbird Audubon named for the Georgia city where he first collected it. The bird is shown perched among slender grass stems appropriate to its open-country habitat.
audubon-birds-10-brown-titlark: Plate 10 depicts the Brown Titlark, now known as the American Pipit, a slim ground-dwelling songbird of open country. Havell's aquatint records the streaked breast and slender bill against a simple botanical sprig.
audubon-birds-110-hooded-warbler: Plate 110 shows the Hooded Warbler, the male's black hood framing a vivid yellow face. Audubon places the bird among the foliage of an understory plant, in keeping with the species' preference for shady eastern woodlands.
audubon-birds-111-pileated-woodpecker: Plate 111 fills the double-elephant sheet with a pair of Pileated Woodpeckers excavating a tree trunk, their flaming red crests a striking accent against the bark. The composition showcases Audubon's signature pairing of life-size scale with active behavior.
audubon-birds-112-downy-woodpecker: Plate 112 depicts the Downy Woodpecker, North America's smallest woodpecker, clinging to a slender branch. Havell's aquatint preserves the crisp black-and-white pattern and the male's small red nape patch.
audubon-birds-113-blue-bird: Plate 113 shows the Eastern Bluebird (Sialia sialis), a male, female, and juvenile arranged on a single branch to display the species' sexual and age variation. The vibrant blue back, achieved through hand-coloring over the aquatint, made the plate a perennial favorite.
audubon-birds-114-white-crowned-sparrow: Plate 114 depicts the White-crowned Sparrow, its boldly striped head crisply rendered above a clean gray breast. The bird is set among slender stems suited to the sparrow's brushy habitat.
audubon-birds-115-wood-pewee: Plate 115 shows the Eastern Wood-Pewee perched among the blossoms of swamp azalea (then Azalea viscosa, now Rhododendron viscosum). The pairing of small flycatcher and flowering branch is typical of Audubon's union of ornithology and botanical record.
audubon-birds-116-ferruginous-thrush: Plate 116 depicts the Ferruginous Thrush, now known as the Brown Thrasher, its long tail and rich rusty back rendered at life size. Audubon shows the bird in a dynamic pose against vegetation, capturing the secretive thicket-dweller in characteristic alertness.
audubon-birds-117-mississippi-kite: An aquatint engraving of the Mississippi Kite, a slate-grey raptor of the southern wetlands. Audubon places the bird in characteristic mid-flight pose, fanned tail and pointed wings rendered with the precise feather work that defined the double-elephant folio.
audubon-birds-118-warbling-flycatcher: A study of the small olive-toned songbird known today as the warbling vireo, perched amid foliage. The plate exemplifies Audubon's habit of pairing each species with a botanical setting drawn from direct field observation.
audubon-birds-119-yellow-throated-vireo: The yellow-throated vireo is shown among leafy branches, its lemon breast and white spectacles picked out in fine line and watercolour wash. Engraved by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's life-size original.
audubon-birds-11-bird-of-washington: Audubon's so-called Bird of Washington, depicted as an immense brown eagle on a rocky perch. The species was claimed as a discovery by Audubon but is now generally regarded as a misidentified juvenile bald eagle, and the plate has become a touchstone in debates over his ornithological accuracy.
audubon-birds-120-pewit-flycatcher: Two eastern phoebes, here under their older common name, perched at a nest tucked into a rocky crevice. The composition reflects Audubon's interest in nesting behaviour and his early banding experiments with this species.
audubon-birds-121-snowy-owl: A pair of snowy owls posed on a wind-swept outcrop, the male and female differentiated by the density of their dark barring. The aquatint exploits velvet blacks and reserved white paper to evoke arctic plumage against a low northern sky.
audubon-birds-122-blue-grosbeak: Male and female blue grosbeaks set among grain stalks, the cobalt male contrasted with the warm cinnamon female. The plate shows Audubon's preference for placing songbirds within the agricultural margins where he encountered them.
audubon-birds-123-black-yellow-warblers: A study of magnolia warblers, formerly called black and yellow warblers, arranged on a flowering branch. Audubon's compositional habit of stacking multiple individuals across a single bough allowed him to display plumage variation within a species.
audubon-birds-124-green-black-capt-flyca: The Wilson's warbler, here under an obsolete name, perched on a slender stem. The black-capped male and plainer female demonstrate the sexual dimorphism Audubon was careful to record.
audubon-birds-125-brown-headed-nuthatch: A southern pine specialist shown clinging head-down to bark in the species' typical pose. The plate captures the small nuthatch's sharp bill and stubby tail, characters Audubon emphasised through tight, descriptive line work.
audubon-birds-126-white-headed-eagle: The bald eagle, here under its older descriptive name, gripping a freshly caught catfish on a riverside log. The plate is among the most dramatic in the folio, asserting the species as an emblem of American wilderness.
audubon-birds-127-rose-breasted-grosbeak: Rose-breasted grosbeaks shown amid the foliage of a fruiting branch, the male's crimson bib set against bold black and white plumage. The composition pairs adult and juvenile to record seasonal change.
audubon-birds-128-cat-bird: A pair of grey catbirds among bramble, named for their mewing call. Audubon situates the birds within thorny undergrowth typical of their breeding habitat, a recurring strategy for grounding species in ecological context.
audubon-birds-129-great-crested-flycatch: The great crested flycatcher poised on a forked branch, its rufous tail and lemon belly delicately laid in. Audubon notes the species' habit of weaving shed snake skins into its nest, an ethological detail typical of his accompanying writings.
audubon-birds-12-baltimore-oriole: Baltimore orioles arrayed on a tulip tree branch, the pendulous woven nest the species is famed for visible among the leaves. The plate combines ornithological and botanical observation in a single, balanced composition.
audubon-birds-130-yellow-winged-sparrow: Grasshopper sparrows, under an older name, posed low among grass stems. Audubon's spare composition reflects the cryptic, ground-dwelling nature of the bird, whose flat-headed silhouette he renders with characteristic precision.
audubon-birds-131-american-robin: American robins shown on a fruiting branch, an adult feeding alongside speckled juveniles. The plate documents the species across age classes, a comparative approach Audubon employed for common North American thrushes.
audubon-birds-132-three-toed-woodpecker: Black-backed and American three-toed woodpeckers gathered on a beetle-bored conifer trunk, their yellow caps clearly distinguished. The plate combines several northern boreal species into one ecological tableau.
audubon-birds-133-black-poll-warbler: Blackpoll warblers perched on a flowering branch, the male's black cap and white cheek set against streaked flanks. Audubon's tight stippling captures the fine plumage detail of this long-distance migrant.
audubon-birds-134-hemlock-warbler: Now considered a plumage variant of the yellow-rumped or Townsend's warbler, the hemlock warbler appears here on a coniferous spray. The plate reflects the unsettled state of warbler taxonomy in the early nineteenth century.
audubon-birds-135-blackburnian-warbler: Blackburnian warblers, the male's flame-orange throat ablaze against black and white, set within hemlock foliage. Audubon's controlled palette concentrates colour at the throat to mirror the bird's own visual emphasis.
audubon-birds-136-meadow-lark: Eastern meadowlarks posed in tall prairie grass, the yellow breast and black V boldly displayed. The composition links the species firmly to the open agricultural country in which Audubon most often observed it.
audubon-birds-137-yellow-breasted-chat: Yellow-breasted chats among tangled vines and blooming trumpet flowers. Audubon plays the chat's lemon breast against the vivid orange-red of the blossoms, integrating bird and habitat in a single decorative scheme.
audubon-birds-138-connecticut-warbler: A pair of Connecticut warblers posed low among foliage, the male's grey hood and bold eye-ring carefully delineated. The species was first described by Alexander Wilson in Connecticut, hence the name retained by Audubon.
audubon-birds-139-field-sparrow: Field sparrows on a low briar, the pink bill and rusty crown rendered against a soft palette. The plate's modest scale and quiet pose suit the unassuming character of this old-field songbird.
audubon-birds-13-snow-bird: The dark-eyed junco, then called the snow bird, depicted in slate and white plumage on a sprig of greenery. Audubon's terse staging suits the species' winter ubiquity at northern doorsteps.
audubon-birds-140-pine-creeping-warbler: Pine warblers, under an older common name, posed on a pine bough heavy with cones. Audubon ties the species inseparably to its host tree, reflecting the strict habitat fidelity that gives the warbler its name.
audubon-birds-141-i-goshawk-2-stanley-ha: A composite plate showing the northern goshawk together with the Stanley hawk, now identified as the Cooper's hawk. The grouping allowed Audubon to compare two large accipiters of similar markings within a single sheet.
audubon-birds-142-american-sparrow-hawk: American kestrels, formerly called sparrow hawks, shown with prey at a rocky perch. The plate captures the falcon's distinctive facial pattern and slate-blue wings of the male, the rufous back of the female.
audubon-birds-143-golden-crowned-thrush: The ovenbird, under its older name, walking the forest floor among fallen leaves. Audubon registers the bird's olive back and bold black-bordered crown stripe with the close observation typical of ground-dwelling warblers in the folio.
audubon-birds-144-small-green-crested-fl: A small olive-toned flycatcher poses among slender branches in this hand-coloured aquatint from the double-elephant folio Birds of America. Audubon presents the bird life-size and in characteristic alert posture, his foliage rendered with the botanical precision that distinguished the project from earlier ornithological prints.
audubon-birds-145-yellow-red-poll-warble: A pair of warblers, their crowns flushed with rufous, perch among delicate leafing twigs. The plate exemplifies Audubon's working method of arranging freshly collected specimens on a wire grid to capture lifelike attitudes for the engraver.
audubon-birds-146-fish-crow: The Fish Crow is shown perched among coastal vegetation, its glossy black plumage rendered through layered aquatint tones. Audubon distinguished this species from the larger American Crow chiefly by voice and habitat, observations recorded alongside the plate in his Ornithological Biography.
audubon-birds-147-night-hawk: Nighthawks wheel and rest in this composition, their long pointed wings and cryptic mottled plumage shown in multiple attitudes. Audubon characteristically combined several individuals on a single sheet to display sex differences and flight posture in one image.
audubon-birds-148-pine-swamp-warbler: A small warbler clings to a budding branch in this hand-coloured aquatint engraving. The plate forms part of the Havell edition of Birds of America, etched and aquatinted in London by Robert Havell Jr. after Audubon's watercolour studies.
audubon-birds-149-sharp-tailed-finch: A sharp-tailed sparrow is shown among marsh grasses, its streaked breast and ochre face pattern emphasised against muted reeds. Audubon associated the species with Atlantic salt-meadows, a habitat he sketched on field expeditions along the eastern seaboard.
audubon-birds-14-prairie-warbler: Two prairie warblers perch among flowering branches, their yellow underparts and side streaking carefully delineated. The plate is typical of the early Havell numbers, in which Audubon paired songbirds with the specific vegetation in which he had observed them feeding.
audubon-birds-150-red-eyed-vireo: A red-eyed vireo is shown gleaning among broad green leaves, the diagnostic eye-stripe and pale ruby iris picked out by the colourist. Audubon depicted the bird in its preferred deciduous canopy, where its persistent song was familiar throughout eastern American summers.
audubon-birds-151-turkey-buzzard: The Turkey Vulture stands with wings half-spread, its bare red head and ragged plumage rendered in close anatomical detail. Audubon's notes on this species fuelled his celebrated dispute with Charles Waterton over whether vultures locate carrion by sight or smell.
audubon-birds-152-white-breasted-black-c: Nuthatches descend a tree trunk head-first in their characteristic posture, the bark texture engraved with fine aquatint grain. The composition records the bird's habitual foraging behaviour rather than presenting it as a static museum specimen.
audubon-birds-153-yellow-crown-warbler: Small warblers with bright yellow crown-patches are arranged on a flowering branch, the colourist's washes giving warmth to the breast streaking. Audubon often grouped several individuals to economise on plates while showing plumage variation.
audubon-birds-154-tennessee-warbler: A Tennessee Warbler perches among slender twigs, its olive-and-grey palette modelled by selectively applied aquatint. The species was named by Alexander Wilson from a migrant collected on the Cumberland River, and Audubon retained the regional epithet.
audubon-birds-155-black-throated-blue-wa: Male and female Black-throated Blue Warblers appear together, the marked sexual dimorphism between slate-blue male and olive female deliberately contrasted on a single branch. The pairing reflects Audubon's pedagogical impulse to depict both sexes for naturalists' reference.
audubon-birds-156-american-crow: An American Crow grasps an ear of maize, the glossy black plumage built from layered aquatint and selective burnishing. The image, like much of Audubon's corvid imagery, registers the crow's reputation as an agricultural pest while affording it a dignified portrait.
audubon-birds-157-rusty-grakle: Rusty Blackbirds are shown in autumn plumage edged with cinnamon, the colourist working warm washes over the matte black underdrawing. Audubon observed the species in flooded woodland on its migration south through the Mississippi valley.
audubon-birds-158-american-swift: Chimney Swifts are caught in rapid flight, their stiff bowed wings and short tails sketched in several positions. The plate departs from Audubon's usual perched compositions, attempting to render an aerial bird that almost never alights in plain view.
audubon-birds-159-cardinal-grosbeak: A pair of Northern Cardinals are arranged on a fruiting branch, the male's vermilion plumage carried by a saturated red wash applied over the engraved ground. The plate's vivid colouring relies on the brilliant cochineal- and madder-based reds available to the Havell workshop.
audubon-birds-15-blue-yellow-backed-warb: The diminutive Northern Parula is shown working through lichen and blossom, its blue-grey back and yellow throat picked out in delicate washes. Audubon's botanical setting reflects the bird's preference for Spanish-moss-laden southern woodlands.
audubon-birds-160-black-capped-titmouse: A Black-capped Chickadee hangs from a slender twig, its bold cap and bib reduced to crisp shapes against the understated background. Audubon often illustrated tits and chickadees in acrobatic feeding postures observed at close range.
audubon-birds-161-brasilian-caracara-eag: The Crested Caracara is shown standing over carrion, its bare facial skin and barred breast carefully delineated. Audubon encountered the species in Florida and Texas, expanding the geographic scope of Birds of America to the subtropical edge of the continent.
audubon-birds-162-zenaida-dove: A pair of Zenaida Doves perch on a fruiting branch, their warm cinnamon plumage and iridescent neck patches finely modelled. Audubon studied this West Indian species on his 1832 expedition to the Florida Keys, the principal source for several plates in this section.
audubon-birds-163-palm-warbler: Palm Warblers are placed among low foliage with their characteristic tail-pumping posture suggested by the bird's downward-curved attitude. The plate reflects Audubon's habit of incorporating diagnostic behaviour into otherwise still compositions.
audubon-birds-164-tawny-thrush: A Veery is shown among understorey foliage, the warm cinnamon back and faintly spotted breast realised through restrained washes. Audubon followed nineteenth-century usage in calling this woodland thrush the Tawny Thrush.
audubon-birds-165-bachmans-finch: Bachman's Sparrow is shown among grass stems in a sparse composition, the streaked back and plain breast typical of the genus. The species honours Audubon's friend and collaborator John Bachman, with whom he later co-authored the Viviparous Quadrupeds of North America.
audubon-birds-166-rough-legged-falcon: The Rough-legged Hawk is depicted in dark and light morphs, the feathered tarsi that give the species its name carefully engraved. Audubon's plate documents the broad plumage variation within a single Arctic-breeding raptor.
audubon-birds-167-key-west-dove: The Key West Quail-Dove is shown amid tropical foliage, its iridescent nape and rufous flanks rendered in unusually saturated hues. Audubon collected the species on the Florida Keys, where it has since become a vanishingly rare visitor.
audubon-birds-168-fork-tailed-flycatcher: A Fork-tailed Flycatcher is shown with its dramatically elongated outer tail-feathers fanned across the page, the design exploiting the double-elephant folio's exceptional sheet size. Audubon included this South American vagrant on the strength of stray records reaching the eastern United States.
audubon-birds-169-mangrove-cuckoo: The Mangrove Cuckoo perches in dense coastal foliage, its buff underparts and black ear-patch picked out against tropical leaves. Audubon recorded the species during his Florida Keys expedition, where he distinguished it from the more familiar Yellow-billed Cuckoo.
audubon-birds-16-great-footed-hawk: A Peregrine Falcon stands over a duck prey-item, blood-flecked feathers strewn around its talons. The plate, here under Audubon's preferred name of Great-footed Hawk, is among the most dramatic predator images in Birds of America.
audubon-birds-170-gray-tyrant: A Gray Kingbird perches alertly on a slender stem, its pale grey upperparts and stout black bill rendered with restrained tonal washes. Audubon associated the species with the Florida coast, where it occupies the same exposed perches as its more northerly relative the Eastern Kingbird.
audubon-birds-171-barn-owl: A pair of barn owls perched in a darkened nocturnal scene, one grasping a captured ground squirrel in its talons. Audubon's hand-coloured aquatint emphasises the species' heart-shaped facial disc and softly mottled plumage, rendered with the dramatic lighting typical of his Havell-engraved double elephant folio.
audubon-birds-172-blue-headed-pigeon: Plate from Birds of America showing the blue-headed quail-dove, a ground-feeding species native to Cuba. The bird is set against tropical foliage, its iridescent crown and white throat-stripe rendered through layered aquatint and watercolour finishing.
audubon-birds-173-barn-swallow: Barn swallows shown in flight and at rest, their forked tails and rust-throated faces sharply observed. The composition typifies Audubon's preference for dynamic poses over the stiff specimen-style of earlier ornithological illustration.
audubon-birds-174-olive-sided-flycatcher: An olive-sided flycatcher perched on a slender branch, captured mid-watch in the alert posture characteristic of aerial insectivores. The plate combines etching, aquatint and hand colouring produced for the double elephant folio.
audubon-birds-175-nuttall-s-lesser-marsh: Marsh wrens perched among reedy stalks, named here for the naturalist Thomas Nuttall. The plate places the small passerines within a habitat vignette, a hallmark of Audubon's life-size approach to ornithological portraiture.
audubon-birds-176-spotted-grous: A spotted (or Canada) grouse rendered in profile with closely observed plumage barring and crimson eye comb. The aquatint engraving reflects Audubon's collaboration with Robert Havell Jr., whose London workshop translated the watercolours into print.
audubon-birds-177-white-crowned-pigeon: White-crowned pigeons depicted among glossy fruiting branches of a tropical tree, evoking the species' Caribbean and Floridian range. The white cap and slate-grey body are set against rich foliage typical of Audubon's habitat backgrounds.
audubon-birds-178-orange-crowned-warbler: An orange-crowned warbler poised on a flowering twig, its olive plumage and faint head-stripe carefully delineated. The plate is engraved, etched and aquatinted, with watercolour applied by hand in Havell's London studio.
audubon-birds-179-wood-wren: A wood wren among woodland branches, rendered at life-size as required by Audubon's double elephant folio format. The aquatint preserves the soft barring of the plumage and the alert tail-cocked posture typical of the family.
audubon-birds-17-carolina-turtle-dove: Carolina (mourning) doves grouped on a flowering branch, their pinkish-buff plumage set against delicate blossoms. The composition uses overlapping figures to create the multi-bird tableaux Audubon favoured for sociable species.
audubon-birds-180-pine-finch: Pine finches (now pine siskins) perched among conifer foliage, with streaked plumage and yellow wing-flashes precisely registered. The plate exemplifies Audubon's dual interest in scientific accuracy and decorative botanical setting.
audubon-birds-181-goldon-eagle: A golden eagle in full predatory descent, gripping a white hare against a mountainous backdrop. One of Audubon's most theatrical compositions, the plate showcases the raptor's broad wingspan and golden nape feathers.
audubon-birds-182-ground-dove: A pair of common ground-doves on a flowering branch, the species' diminutive scale and scaled breast accurately rendered. The plate balances ornithological detail with Audubon's characteristic floral staging.
audubon-birds-183-goldon-crested-wren: Golden-crested wrens (kinglets) shown among coniferous sprigs, their flame-coloured crown stripes carefully highlighted. The miniature scale of the species allowed Audubon to compose multiple birds within a single intimate vignette.
audubon-birds-184-mangrove-humming-bird: A mangrove hummingbird hovering at tropical blossoms, its iridescence captured through the layered aquatint process. The plate reflects the broader nineteenth-century fascination with the dazzling plumage of New World hummingbirds.
audubon-birds-185-bachman-s-warbler: Bachman's warbler depicted on a flowering branch, named after Audubon's friend and collaborator the Rev. John Bachman. Once a southern wetland species, the bird is now feared extinct, lending the plate added documentary weight.
audubon-birds-186-pinnated-grous: A male pinnated grouse (greater prairie chicken) in courtship display, neck-sacs inflated and pinnae raised. The plate documents a once-abundant prairie species whose lekking behaviour Audubon observed firsthand.
audubon-birds-187-boat-tailed-grackle: Boat-tailed grackles displayed in pairs, the long keel-shaped tail of the male contrasted with the smaller browner female. The composition captures the species' coastal marsh habitat through suggestive vegetation.
audubon-birds-188-tree-sparrow: American tree sparrows arrayed on a slender bare branch, their rufous caps and distinctive breast-spot precisely drawn. The simplicity of the staging foregrounds the careful study of plumage that underpins the entire Birds of America project.
audubon-birds-189-snow-bunting: Snow buntings in winter plumage, rendered against a sparse Arctic-suggestive ground. The black-and-white males and warmer-toned females are positioned to record both seasonal phases of this circumpolar species.
audubon-birds-18-bewick-s-wren: A pair of Bewick's wrens with cocked tails and finely barred plumage, named for the English wood engraver Thomas Bewick. The plate emphasises their habit of foraging through dense thickets, suggested here by an entwined branch.
audubon-birds-190-yellow-bellied-woodpec: Yellow-bellied sapsuckers shown clinging to a tree trunk, their characteristic horizontal sap-well rows implied beneath them. The plate combines etching and aquatint to capture the strongly patterned head and red crown.
audubon-birds-191-willow-grous-or-large-: Willow grouse, or willow ptarmigan, depicted in transitional plumage between summer rufous and winter white. The image documents the species' Arctic and sub-Arctic adaptation through carefully observed feathered legs and feet.
audubon-birds-192-great-american-shrike-: A northern shrike perched with prey impaled on a thorn, illustrating the larder behaviour that earned the species its common name. The plate combines naturalistic observation with the narrative drama Audubon favoured.
audubon-birds-193-lincoln-finch: Lincoln's sparrow depicted on a flowering branch, named for Audubon's young Labrador-expedition companion Thomas Lincoln. Subtly streaked plumage and a buff malar stripe distinguish the species from related sparrows.
audubon-birds-194-canadian-titmouse: Boreal chickadees, then known as Canadian titmice, perched among coniferous twigs. The plate records a northern forest species whose brown cap distinguishes it from its more familiar black-capped relative.
audubon-birds-195-ruby-crowned-wren: Ruby-crowned kinglets shown among slender sprigs, the male displaying the scarlet crown-patch usually concealed beneath olive feathers. The miniature scale required especially fine aquatint work in the Havell engraving.
audubon-birds-196-labrador-falcon: A Labrador (gyr) falcon perched in profile, the species' powerful build and barred plumage rendered with raptorial gravitas. The plate reflects Audubon's 1833 Labrador expedition and its Arctic ornithological discoveries.
audubon-birds-197-american-crossbill: Red crossbills feeding among conifer cones, the unique mandible crossing essential to their seed-extraction technique clearly visible. The composition unites diet, habitat and anatomy in a single life-size study.
audubon-birds-198-brown-headed-worm-eati: A worm-eating warbler perched on slender vegetation, the bold dark head-stripes that identify the species precisely registered. The plate shows the typical aquatint-and-watercolour layering of the Havell-engraved double elephant folio.
audubon-birds-199-little-owl: A small Athene noctua perched in alert profile, rendered in hand-coloured aquatint after Audubon's life-size watercolour. Sharp etched feather striations across the crown and mantle convey the species' compact, ground-hunting build, while the dark eye-mask anchors the composition.
audubon-birds-19-louisiana-water-thrush: Parkesia motacilla shown probing along a streamside branch, rendered with the characteristic streaked underparts and bold supercilium. Engraved by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's double-elephant folio watercolour, with delicate aquatint tonal washes evoking damp woodland habitat.
audubon-birds-1-wild-turkey: The opening plate of Birds of America presents a male Meleagris gallopavo striding through cane, painted at Beechwoods Plantation in West Feliciana Parish. The towering double-elephant folio format permitted Audubon to render the bird at life size, with iridescent body plumage built up in layered aquatint.
audubon-birds-200-shore-lark: Eremophila alpestris depicted on tundra-like ground, the small black facial pattern and yellow throat carefully etched. The plate captures the species' open-country posture, with sparse plant detail emphasising the bird's preference for bare, gravelly terrain.
audubon-birds-201-canada-goose: A pair of Branta canadensis arranged across the sheet to fit the folio dimensions, exhibiting Audubon's habit of bending necks and limbs to maintain life-size proportions. Aquatint grain models the soft grey body plumage against the crisply etched black neck and white chinstrap.
audubon-birds-202-red-throated-diver: Gavia stellata in breeding plumage, the rust-red throat patch contrasting with finely engraved silver-grey neck stripes. The low waterline composition and distant horizon situate the diver in its northern coastal habitat.
audubon-birds-203-fresh-water-marsh-hen: King rail, Rallus elegans, drawn skulking among reeds with neck extended in characteristic cautious gait. Fine etched lines build the barred flank pattern, while aquatint shading establishes the dim, vegetated marsh interior.
audubon-birds-204-salt-water-marsh-hen: Rallus crepitans set within tidal cordgrass, the long bill and laterally compressed body conveying its furtive habits in salt marshes. Subtle tonal aquatint distinguishes the muted ochre and grey plumage suited to coastal camouflage.
audubon-birds-205-virginia-rail: Rallus limicola depicted in a low, alert stance with the long reddish bill prominent. The plate's restrained palette and tightly etched feather barring reflect Audubon's effort to render small marsh species with the same dignity afforded to larger birds.
audubon-birds-206-summer-or-wood-duck: Aix sponsa shown in arboreal habit suggested by overhanging foliage, the drake's iridescent crest and bold facial striping rendered in saturated hand-applied watercolour over aquatint. A favoured Audubon subject for its colour and behaviour.
audubon-birds-207-booby-gannet: Sula leucogaster perched in profile, the wedge-shaped bill and dark mantle contrasted against pale underparts. The composition reflects observations from Audubon's Florida and Gulf coast voyages aboard the Marion in the 1830s.
audubon-birds-208-esquimaux-curlew: Numenius borealis shown probing tundra ground, the slender decurved bill carefully traced. The species was already in steep decline during Audubon's lifetime and is now likely extinct, lending the plate documentary value.
audubon-birds-209-wilson-s-plover: Charadrius wilsonia rendered on a beach margin, the heavy black bill and single breast band etched with precision. The sparse coastal setting and shell fragments locate the bird in its preferred Atlantic shoreline habitat.
audubon-birds-20-blue-winged-yellow-warb: Vermivora cyanoptera arranged on a leafy branch to display the lemon underparts and slate wing edging. The pose reflects Audubon's practice of wiring fresh specimens into life-like attitudes for direct watercolour study.
audubon-birds-210-least-bittern: Ixobrychus exilis shown in classic vertical reed-grasping pose, body elongated and bill pointed skyward in the species' camouflage stance. Etched striations on the breast and crown convey the bird's cryptic plumage.
audubon-birds-211-great-blue-heron: Ardea herodias rendered at full life size by bending the long neck and legs to fit the double-elephant folio sheet. Aquatint and watercolour build the slate-grey body and shaggy plumes, with marshland horizon barely indicated.
audubon-birds-212-common-gull: Larus canus depicted in flight or standing profile, the soft grey mantle and yellow bill described in muted aquatint washes. A relatively restrained composition typical of plates devoted to widespread coastal species.
audubon-birds-213-puffin: Atlantic puffin, Fratercula arctica, shown with breeding-season bill ornament and orange feet. The plate likely draws on observations from Audubon's 1833 expedition to Labrador, where he studied seabird colonies first-hand.
audubon-birds-214-razor-bill: Alca torda set on a rocky ledge, the deep laterally flattened bill with its white vertical stripe carefully etched. Aquatint tonality conveys the velvety black mantle against the cliff-side seabird habitat.
audubon-birds-215-hyperborean-phalarope: Red-necked phalarope, Phalaropus lobatus, shown swimming with the species' lobed feet and slender needle bill. The compositional emphasis on aquatic habit reflects the bird's distinctive pelagic life cycle.
audubon-birds-216-wood-ibiss: Wood stork, Mycteria americana, given full folio scale with bare scaly head and heavy decurved bill. The towering pose and southern wetland setting reflect Audubon's Florida fieldwork in the 1830s.
audubon-birds-217-louisiana-heron: Tricolored heron, Egretta tricolor, captured in a sinuous wading pose with white belly stripe set against slate body. The dynamic curve of the neck typifies Audubon's preference for active, narrative postures over static specimen views.
audubon-birds-218-foolish-guillemot: Common murre, Uria aalge, shown in profile with the pointed bill and dark upperparts of breeding plumage. The plate reflects observations from Audubon's Labrador voyage among dense seabird cliffs.
audubon-birds-219-black-guillemot: Cepphus grylle in breeding plumage, the sooty body relieved by a striking white wing patch and red feet rendered in saturated watercolour. The cliff-perched composition evokes North Atlantic colony habitat.
audubon-birds-21-mocking-bird: Mimus polyglottos engaged with a coiled rattlesnake amid yellow jessamine vines, one of Audubon's most narrative compositions. The dramatic encounter drew scientific criticism over snake-climbing accuracy but established the plate's enduring fame.
audubon-birds-220-piping-plover: Charadrius melodus on pale sand, the bird's sandy-buff back and partial neck band described with minimal etched line. The sparse beach setting reflects the species' preference for open Atlantic shoreline.
audubon-birds-221-mallard-duck: Anas platyrhynchos drake and hen arranged within marsh vegetation, the male's bottle-green head built up in iridescent layered washes. A characteristic Audubon pairing of sexes within a single life-size plate.
audubon-birds-222-white-ibis: Eudocimus albus shown probing with the long red decurved bill, white plumage punctuated by black wingtips. The composition reflects Audubon's southern Florida expeditions among coastal mangrove and estuarine habitats.
audubon-birds-223-pied-oyster-catcher: American oystercatcher, Haematopus palliatus, on a shell-strewn shore with the long red bill specialised for prying bivalves. Bold tonal contrast between black hood and white belly suits the bird's striking field appearance.
audubon-birds-224-kittiwake-gull: Rissa tridactyla rendered with the species' clean grey mantle and black wingtips, perched on rocks suggestive of ocean cliffs. The plate's restrained palette emphasises the kittiwake's pelagic, colony-nesting life.
audubon-birds-225-kildeer-plover: A pair of killdeer arranged in alert postures along a stretch of bare sand, their double black breast bands rendered through Havell's hand-coloured aquatint. The plate showcases the species' upright stance and the distinctive russet rump that flashes in flight, presented life-size on double elephant folio paper.
audubon-birds-226-hooping-crane: A whooping crane bows its long neck to seize a young alligator from a marshy Louisiana shoreline, with additional hatchlings scattering through the grass. Composed in stages between 1821 and 1822, the plate combines on-the-spot field study with a later background, and depicts the bird at full life-size by twisting its neck downward.
audubon-birds-227-pin-tailed-duck: A drake northern pintail and his mate are paired against a watery margin, the male's chocolate head and slender tail-spike contrasted with the hen's softer scalloping. The aquatint records the species' elegant body line and the fine vermiculation across the flanks that Audubon studied closely from skinned specimens.
audubon-birds-228-american-green-winged-: Drake and hen green-winged teal cluster on a marshy bank, the male's chestnut head crossed by an iridescent green eye-mask and the speculum picked out in metallic green ink. Aquatint and stipple together register the dense barring across the flanks of North America's smallest dabbling duck.
audubon-birds-229-scaup-duck: A drake and hen lesser scaup float on rippled water, the male's blue-grey bill and finely vermiculated back set against the female's brown plumage and white facial patch. The plate compresses two views of the species into a single composition, a device Audubon often used to convey sexual dimorphism.
audubon-birds-22-purple-martin: Several purple martins gather around a hollow gourd nest, the glossy blue-black males and duller females shown in the cooperative groupings typical of the colony-nesting swallow. The plate documents an early form of artificial nest box used by southern planters, embedding ethnography within ornithological observation.
audubon-birds-230-ruddy-plover: A trio of sanderlings, here called Ruddy Plover, are shown along a shoreline in transitional plumage, their reddish summer wash giving way to the pale winter dress. The aquatint captures the species' restless run-and-stop foraging at the surf line through tightly composed silhouettes.
audubon-birds-231-long-billed-curlew: A long-billed curlew stands above a panoramic view of Charleston harbour, its enormous decurved bill nearly touching the cinnamon underwing. Audubon's choice of city skyline as backdrop is unusual for the series, situating the shorebird within a specific Atlantic seaport context.
audubon-birds-232-hooded-merganser: A drake hooded merganser raises his fan-shaped white crest while the hen swims behind, her shaggier rust-coloured tuft trailing in the water. The aquatint emphasises the saw-edged bill of this fish-eating diving duck and the bold pied pattern of the breeding male.
audubon-birds-233-sora-or-rail: A sora picks its way through a tangle of marsh stems, the short yellow bill and barred flanks rendered against the vertical reeds. Audubon used the dense vegetation both as habitat documentation and as a compositional device to suggest the secretive bird's preference for dense cover.
audubon-birds-234-tufted-duck: A tufted duck paddles on open water, the drake's drooping black crest and golden eye contrasted with the brown hen alongside. The plate is among the rarer Eurasian vagrants in the series, included after Audubon obtained specimens from the European edge of the species' range.
audubon-birds-235-sooty-tern: A sooty tern banks above a tropical sea, its dark upperparts and white forehead resolved against pale sky. Audubon studied the species during his 1832 voyage to the Florida Keys, where vast breeding colonies on the Tortugas supplied much of his field data.
audubon-birds-236-night-heron-or-qua-bir: Adult and immature black-crowned night herons share a single branch, the sleek slate-and-white adult above and the streaky brown juvenile below. The plate compresses the species' dramatic age-related plumage change into one frame, a strategy Havell's aquatint handled through tonal gradation.
audubon-birds-237-great-esquimaux-curlew: An Eskimo curlew stands amid coastal vegetation, its slender decurved bill and cinnamon underwing visible at full life-size. The plate has acquired posthumous weight as a record of a species that was abundant in Audubon's day and is now feared extinct following relentless market hunting.
audubon-birds-238-great-marbled-godwit: A marbled godwit probes a tidal flat with its long, slightly upturned, bicoloured bill. The aquatint emphasises the cinnamon wash across the underparts and the heavy barring on the back that gives the species its name, presented against a pared-back shoreline.
audubon-birds-239-american-coot: An American coot swims among aquatic vegetation, the white frontal shield and chalky bill standing out against slate-black plumage. Audubon shows the lobed toes splayed beneath the water, a structural detail that distinguishes coots from the superficially similar ducks.
audubon-birds-23-yellow-breasted-warbler: A small yellow-breasted warbler perches among slim foliage, its citron underparts and olive back rendered with fine stipple. The plate reflects Audubon's effort to document the still-unsettled nomenclature of North American wood-warblers, several of which were known by names that have since been revised.
audubon-birds-240-roseate-tern: A roseate tern hovers with deeply forked tail streaming behind, its pale grey mantle and faintly pink-flushed breast captured through Havell's careful stipple. The plate distinguishes this slender species from the commoner common and Arctic terns by the long, almost wholly black bill.
audubon-birds-241-black-backed-gull: A great black-backed gull stands at full life-size on a rocky shore, the slate-black mantle contrasted with a bright pink leg and a heavy yellow bill marked by a red gonys spot. The plate registers the formidable scale of the world's largest gull, a predator of seabird colonies along the North Atlantic.
audubon-birds-242-snowy-heron-or-white-e: A snowy egret strides through shallows with golden feet outstretched, the long aigrette plumes that nearly drove the species to extinction cascading from nape and back. A small hunter crouches in the distance, an unusual narrative inclusion that links the bird to the plume trade beginning to thin its colonies.
audubon-birds-243-american-snipe: A Wilson's snipe crouches in marsh tussocks, its cryptic striped head pattern and long flexible bill visible above the grasses. Audubon shows the bird in the freezing posture used to evade detection, illustrating the camouflage that makes the species so difficult to flush.
audubon-birds-244-common-gallinule: A common gallinule walks across floating vegetation, the bright red frontal shield and yellow-tipped bill catching the eye against slate plumage. The aquatint records the long, splayed toes adapted for distributing the bird's weight across lily pads.
audubon-birds-245-thick-billed-murre: A thick-billed murre rides choppy water in upright posture, the heavy black bill with its pale tomial line set against a glossy black hood. Audubon studied the species during his 1833 expedition to Labrador, where breeding colonies on the cliffs supplied specimens for the plate.
audubon-birds-246-eider-duck: A drake common eider swims with the brown hen behind, his bold black-and-white plumage and pastel green nape divided sharply along the flank. The plate documents the marine duck whose down has been harvested for centuries from nests in northern colonies.
audubon-birds-247-velvet-duck: A white-winged scoter, here called Velvet Duck, floats on coastal water, the drake's velvety black plumage broken by a white wing patch and a knobbed orange bill. Aquatint reproduces the matte quality of the breeding plumage that gave the species its old name.
audubon-birds-248-american-pied-bill-dob: A pied-billed grebe sits on calm water, the short dark-banded ivory bill and stub-tailed silhouette giving the species its archaic name. The plate emphasises the lobed feet barely visible below the surface, the same structural feature shared with coots and other grebes.
audubon-birds-249-tufted-auk: A crested auklet rests on a rocky ledge with its forward-curving black crest and bright orange bill plate prominent. Audubon worked from specimens collected in the Bering Sea region, an area he never visited, making this plate one of his more reconstructive compositions.
audubon-birds-24-roscoe-s-yellow-throat: A small yellow-throated warbler perches on a slender twig, the throat and breast a saturated lemon yellow against an olive back. The name Roscoe's Yellow-throat reflects nineteenth-century nomenclatural confusion that ornithology later resolved by folding the bird into established species.
audubon-birds-250-arctic-tern: An Arctic tern hovers with deeply forked tail and short red legs trailing, the blood-red bill held downward as it scans for fish. The plate captures the proportions of the famously long-distance migrant, whose annual journey between polar oceans Audubon could only have suspected.
audubon-birds-251-brown-pelican: A brown pelican perches on a mangrove branch, its huge gular pouch slack and the chestnut-and-cream nape of breeding plumage clearly registered. Audubon studied the species at close range during his 1832 Florida voyage, allowing the life-size composition to bend the bird into a natural resting posture.
audubon-birds-252-florida-cormorant: A Florida cormorant rendered in hand-coloured aquatint, its glossy black plumage and hooked bill set against a coastal backdrop. The plate exemplifies Audubon's life-size convention, with the bird's posture suggesting a moment of rest between dives along the southern shore.
audubon-birds-253-jager: A jaeger captured mid-flight, the slender wings and elongated central tail feathers stretched across the double-elephant sheet. Audubon pairs anatomical precision with a windswept marine setting, evoking the bird's piratical habit of harassing terns and gulls for food.
audubon-birds-254-wilson-s-phalarope: Wilson's phalarope shown in breeding plumage, with the chestnut neck stripe and needle-fine bill carefully detailed. The composition includes a wading pose typical of the species, which spins on shallow water to stir up invertebrates from the muddy bottom.
audubon-birds-255-red-phalarope: Red phalaropes depicted in their rust-coloured summer dress, slender bills probing at the water's edge. Hand-coloured aquatint by Robert Havell Jr. brings out the contrast between the rich plumage and the muted tones of the Arctic-tinged habitat.
audubon-birds-256-purple-heron: A long-necked heron poised among reed-fringed waters, its slate and chestnut plumage rendered through layered aquatint washes. The bird is shown in the patient, statuesque stance characteristic of ambush-feeding herons in marshland environments.
audubon-birds-257-double-crested-cormora: A double-crested cormorant displaying the wispy nuptial tufts that give the species its name, perched with throat sac visible. The aquatint plate emphasises the iridescent black plumage and the sharp, hooked bill used to seize fish underwater.
audubon-birds-258-hudsonian-godwit: A Hudsonian godwit shown with its long, slightly upturned bill and cinnamon underparts of the breeding season. The shorebird is set against a sparse shoreline, a nod to its tundra-to-pampas migration that takes it across the entire length of the Americas.
audubon-birds-259-horned-grebe: A horned grebe in breeding plumage, the namesake golden ear tufts flared against a chestnut neck. The composition conveys the bird's compact, low-floating posture, supported by lobed feet that propel it on diving forays for small fish.
audubon-birds-25-song-sparrow: Song sparrows perched on a flowering branch, their streaked breasts and warm brown backs picked out in fine engraved line. Audubon places the birds in active poses that suggest the territorial singing for which the species is named across North America.
audubon-birds-260-fork-tail-petrel: A fork-tailed petrel skimming low over the open ocean, the deeply notched tail and dark plumage rendered in tonal aquatint. The plate captures the seabird's pelagic life, only briefly touching down on water to pick at planktonic prey.
audubon-birds-261-hooping-crane: A whooping crane bent into the folio's outsized format, its tall white frame and crimson crown filling the page. Audubon shows the bird in a feeding pose along a marsh edge, a record of an animal already growing scarce in his lifetime.
audubon-birds-262-tropic-bird: A tropicbird rendered with elongated tail streamers trailing behind a stout white body. The plate evokes the species' offshore wanderings across warm seas, where it plunge-dives for squid and flying fish far from any coast.
audubon-birds-263-pigmy-curlew: A pygmy curlew, today known as the curlew sandpiper, depicted with its decurved bill probing at the tide line. Audubon's aquatint records a Eurasian visitor to North American shores, in transitional plumage between the rufous breeding dress and pale winter coat.
audubon-birds-264-fulmar-petrel: A fulmar shown in stiff-winged glide, the tube-nosed bill and pale grey plumage finely etched. The composition reflects this seabird's life on Atlantic swells, where it feeds on fish offal and was long associated with whaling fleets.
audubon-birds-265-buff-breasted-sandpipe: A buff-breasted sandpiper rendered in soft ochre tones, the upright stance typical of this dry-grassland shorebird. The plate emphasises the plain, dove-like face and yellow legs that distinguish it from the wetter-loving members of its family.
audubon-birds-266-common-cormorant: A great cormorant standing with wings half-spread to dry, its glossy black plumage offset by a yellow gular patch. Audubon's hand-coloured aquatint captures the heavy, prehistoric silhouette familiar from rocky coasts on both sides of the Atlantic.
audubon-birds-267-arctic-yager: A long-tailed jaeger shown over open water, the slender central rectrices streaming behind a sharply patterned body. The plate documents a high-Arctic breeder that follows seabird colonies south, robbing them of their catch in piratical chases.
audubon-birds-268-american-woodcock: An American woodcock half-hidden in leaf litter, the long bill and large eyes set high on the head rendered with anatomical care. Audubon's woodland setting reflects the bird's crepuscular habits and probing search for earthworms in damp forest floors.
audubon-birds-269-greenshank: A greenshank standing tall on its namesake olive-green legs, slightly upturned bill held alert. The aquatint records a Eurasian wader that occasionally appears on American shores, with the soft grey-and-white winter plumage carefully picked out.
audubon-birds-26-carolina-parrot: Carolina parakeets clustered on a cocklebur branch, their green bodies and yellow-and-orange heads brilliantly hand-coloured. Audubon's plate is now bittersweet documentation of the only parrot native to the eastern United States, driven to extinction by the early twentieth century.
audubon-birds-270-stormy-petrel: A storm petrel skimming low over the waves, its small dark form and white rump patch finely engraved. The plate captures the swallow-like flight by which these tiny seabirds patter across the surface, picking at oily morsels in open water.
audubon-birds-271-frigate-pelican: A magnificent frigatebird with deeply forked tail and angular wings, rendered against an oceanic sky. Audubon emphasises the long hooked bill that the species uses to harry boobies and tropicbirds into giving up their catch in mid-air.
audubon-birds-272-richardson-s-jager: A parasitic jaeger, here under its older name for Sir John Richardson, shown in the dark morph that occurs across the species. The plate captures the falcon-like wings and pointed central tail feathers used in agile aerial pursuits of terns and small gulls.
audubon-birds-273-cayenne-tern: A Cayenne tern shown with its yellow-tipped bill and shaggy black crest, perched along a sandy shore. The aquatint records a southern subspecies of the Sandwich tern, a slender seabird that hovers and plunges for small fish in coastal shallows.
audubon-birds-274-semipalmated-snipe-or-: A willet in breeding plumage, the long straight bill and grey-brown body opening to reveal the striking black-and-white wing pattern in flight. Audubon's plate shows both standing and flying postures to record the field marks that emerge only when the wings unfold.
audubon-birds-275-noddy-tern: A brown noddy with its sooty body and pale cap, perched on a tropical branch. The plate reflects the species' colonial nesting on warm-water islands, where birds build flat platforms in low trees rather than scraping in the sand like most terns.
audubon-birds-276-king-duck: A king eider drake displaying its extraordinary head, with slate-blue crown, pearly cheeks and bulbous orange forehead shield rendered in detail. Audubon pairs the male with a more soberly mottled female, contrasting the species' dramatic sexual dimorphism on Arctic seas.
audubon-birds-277-hutchins-s-barnacle-go: A small Canada goose form, then named for naval officer Thomas Hutchins, depicted standing alert on tundra ground. The plate captures the compact body and short neck that distinguish this northern subspecies, now generally treated as a cackling goose by ornithologists.
audubon-birds-278-schinz-s-sandpiper-res: A small sandpiper attributed to Schinz, with the streaked breast and decurved bill that fit it equally to a dunlin or white-rumped sandpiper. The plate stands at the centre of a long taxonomic confusion stretching from Brehm's 1822 description through Bonaparte and Coues.
audubon-birds-279-cabot-s-tern: A Cabot's tern with shaggy black crest and yellow bill tip, rendered in clean profile against the page. The aquatint marks this American population of what was long considered a Sandwich tern, whose taxonomic status has shifted between subspecies and full species over time.
audubon-birds-27-red-headed-woodpecker: A boldly patterned woodpecker with a crimson head, white belly, and ink-black wings, shown clinging to bark in characteristic vertical poise. Audubon's hand-coloured aquatint captures the bird's habit of caching acorns in tree crevices across the open oak woodlands of eastern North America.
audubon-birds-280-black-tern: A small marsh tern in sooty breeding plumage, depicted in mid-flight over inland wetlands. The aquatint emphasises the bird's buoyant, dipping foraging style as it plucks insects from the water surface, a behaviour distinguishing it from its coastal relatives.
audubon-birds-281-great-white-heron: A towering all-white wading bird with a heavy yellow bill, stalking shallows of the Florida Keys. Audubon treated the form as a separate species, and the plate's life-size composition required folding the long neck to fit the double-elephant folio sheet.
audubon-birds-282-white-winged-silvery-g: A pale-mantled gull rendered in soft greys against a marine horizon. The plate belongs to Audubon's later seabird sequence, drawn during voyages along the Labrador coast where he documented species new to North American ornithology.
audubon-birds-283-wandering-shearwater: A long-winged pelagic seabird shown gliding low across an Atlantic swell. The aquatint conveys the stiff-winged shearing flight that gives the family its name, with subtle tonal washes describing the dark upperparts and pale underbody.
audubon-birds-284-purple-sandpiper: A stocky shorebird in winter plumage, picking through wave-washed rocks. Audubon's etched line and watercolour wash describe the bird's slate-grey back and yellow legs, and place it in the rugged tidal habitat it favours along North Atlantic coasts.
audubon-birds-285-fork-tailed-gull: A graceful gull with a deeply forked tail and dark hood, shown banking over open ocean. The plate documents a high-Arctic pelagic species rarely encountered ashore, drawn from specimens collected during the northern expeditions of Audubon's contemporaries.
audubon-birds-286-white-fronted-goose: A grey-brown goose with a distinctive white facial blaze and barred belly, set in a marshland vignette. Audubon's composition pairs adult and juvenile to show plumage development, a comparative device he used throughout the waterfowl plates.
audubon-birds-287-ivory-gull: An all-white high-Arctic gull with jet-black legs, perched on pack ice. The aquatint relies on minimal tonal modelling to render the pure plumage, a technical challenge Robert Havell Jr. solved with the lightest of aquatint grounds.
audubon-birds-288-yellow-shank: A slender wading sandpiper with bright yellow legs, shown in a brackish pool. The plate corresponds to what is now called the Lesser Yellowlegs, captured mid-stride to show the bird's restless, high-stepping foraging gait.
audubon-birds-289-solitary-sandpiper: A dark-backed inland sandpiper bobbing along a wooded stream edge. Unlike its flock-forming relatives, the species nests alone in old songbird nests in northern conifer forests, a life history Audubon noted in the accompanying Ornithological Biography.
audubon-birds-28-solitary-flycatcher-or-: A small olive-and-white songbird with bold white spectacles, perched among foliage. Now known as the Blue-headed Vireo, the plate captures the deliberate, gleaning movements that distinguish vireos from the more aerial true flycatchers.
audubon-birds-290-red-backed-sandpiper: A small shorebird in rich breeding plumage with a black belly patch, the form now called Dunlin. Audubon's aquatint shows the species probing wet mudflats, the long down-curved bill rendered with delicate engraved line.
audubon-birds-291-herring-gull: A large grey-mantled gull with a yellow bill and pink legs, shown standing on a rocky shore. The plate pairs adult and mottled juvenile to show the four-year plumage progression typical of the larger gulls.
audubon-birds-292-crested-grebe: A diving waterbird with elaborate rufous and black head plumes raised in display. The composition places the bird low on a glassy lake surface, emphasising the lobed feet and almost tail-less profile that adapt the species for underwater pursuit.
audubon-birds-293-large-billed-puffin: A black-and-white seabird with a deep, brightly banded breeding bill, perched on a sea cliff. The plate documents what Audubon proposed as a distinct species; later ornithologists reclassified the form within the Atlantic Puffin's variation.
audubon-birds-294-pectoral-sandpiper: A medium-sized sandpiper with a sharply demarcated streaked breast, foraging in wet grass. Aquatint stippling describes the cryptic plumage that camouflages the species on the Arctic tundra where it breeds.
audubon-birds-295-manks-shearwater: A black-above, white-below pelagic seabird, depicted skimming the trough of a wave. Audubon based the plate on specimens taken offshore of New England, documenting a transatlantic species whose nocturnal burrow-nesting habits were then poorly understood.
audubon-birds-296-barnacle-goose: A boldly patterned goose with a white face, black neck, and silvery barred back, shown in a coastal marsh. Medieval naturalists believed the species hatched from goose-barnacles, a folk-tale Audubon's text in the Ornithological Biography quietly displaces.
audubon-birds-297-harlequin-duck: A small sea duck with intricate slate-blue, chestnut, and white markings on the male, paired with a soberly mottled female. Audubon places the pair on rocky water to evoke the fast mountain streams and surf zones the species inhabits.
audubon-birds-298-red-necked-grebe: A diving waterbird with a chestnut neck and pale cheek patches in breeding dress, set on still water. The plate's low waterline and reflected plumage show Havell's skill at adapting Audubon's watercolours into atmospheric aquatint.
audubon-birds-299-dusky-petrel: A small dark seabird with a slim bill, shown pattering across the ocean surface. The aquatint depicts a tropical-Atlantic shearwater rare in northern waters, drawn from specimens collected during Gulf Stream voyages.
audubon-birds-29-towhe-bunting: A long-tailed ground-dwelling sparrow with black hood, rufous flanks, and white belly, scratching among leaf litter. Audubon's vignette shows the species' two-footed backward kick foraging behaviour, a habit unusual among North American passerines.
audubon-birds-2-yellow-billed-cuckoo: A slender, long-tailed cuckoo with a curved yellow bill, perched among the foliage of a flowering pawpaw. Audubon's botanical accuracy and elongated S-curve composition became signatures of the early Birds of America plates engraved by Robert Havell Jr.
audubon-birds-300-golden-plover: A medium plover in dark-bellied breeding plumage spangled with gold, shown on open ground. Audubon documents a long-distance migrant whose autumn flocks once darkened American skies before market hunting reduced the population.
audubon-birds-301-canvas-backed-duck: A large diving duck with a chestnut head and pale grey back, paired with a brown female on still water. The plate references the species' association with wild celery on Chesapeake Bay, the staple food that gave its flesh a celebrated flavour in nineteenth-century markets.
audubon-birds-302-dusky-duck: A dark, mottled-brown dabbling duck with a paler head, set among emergent marsh plants. Now known as the American Black Duck, the plate captures the cryptic plumage that distinguishes the species from its closest relative, the Mallard.
audubon-birds-303-bartram-sandpiper: A long-necked grassland shorebird with a small dove-like head, shown standing tall in open prairie. The species, now called Upland Sandpiper, was named by Audubon in honour of the Philadelphia naturalist William Bartram.
audubon-birds-304-turn-stone: A short-legged shorebird with a harlequin orange, black, and white pattern, shown levering a pebble with its wedge-shaped bill. The plate illustrates the species' eponymous foraging method of flipping stones and shells along the tideline.
audubon-birds-305-purple-gallinule: A vivid waterbird with iridescent violet-blue plumage, a red-and-yellow bill, and yellow legs, walking across floating leaves. The plate places the species in southern swamp vegetation where its disproportionately long toes spread its weight over lily pads.
audubon-birds-306-great-northern-diver-o: A Common Loon glides low across glassy water, the checkerboard of white spots on its black back rendered in the precise hand-coloured aquatint that defined the double-elephant folio. Audubon arranged the bird life-size, the long body stretching nearly the full sheet to convey its diving build.
audubon-birds-307-blue-crane-or-heron: A Little Blue Heron stands in a hunched stalking pose, slate plumage washed with the soft purple cast of its breeding crest. The composition crops marsh grasses tightly around the legs, a typical Havell-engraved aquatint with watercolour finish.
audubon-birds-308-tell-tale-godwit-or-sn: The Greater Yellowlegs, then called Tell-tale Godwit for its alarm calls that betrayed hunters, is shown mid-stride on a tidal flat. Fine etched stippling defines the speckled back while flat washes carry the pale belly and yellow legs.
audubon-birds-309-great-tern: A Common Tern banks against an open sky, wings spread to display the dark primary wedge and forked tail. Audubon favoured these dynamic flight studies for seabirds, drawn from freshly shot specimens wired into lifelike postures.
audubon-birds-30-vigor-s-warbler: A small wood warbler, named by Audubon for the British zoologist Nicholas Aylward Vigors, perches among slender twigs. The species is now considered a probable juvenile or hybrid, making the plate an artefact of early American ornithological taxonomy.
audubon-birds-310-spotted-sandpiper: Two Spotted Sandpipers occupy a rocky shoreline, their breeding plumage carrying the heavy round spots that vanish in winter. Audubon often paired adults to compress life history into a single sheet of the Birds of America.
audubon-birds-311-american-white-pelican: An American White Pelican fills the folio sheet, its vast bill resting along the breast and the breeding-season horn rising from the upper mandible. The compressed pose was Audubon's standard solution for fitting outsized waterbirds within the double-elephant format.
audubon-birds-312-long-tailed-duck: Long-tailed Ducks, then called Old-squaws, are shown in winter pied plumage with the drake's slender tail trailing behind. Audubon studied them on the Atlantic coast where they wintered in noisy rafts.
audubon-birds-313-blue-winged-teal: A pair of Blue-winged Teal rest on a quiet bank, the drake's white facial crescent crisply lifted from the cinnamon head. The pale blue forewing patch that names the species is folded just visible along the flank.
audubon-birds-314-black-headed-gull: A Black-headed Gull, a chiefly Old World species recorded as a North American vagrant, hovers above water with wings cocked. The aquatint captures the dark chocolate hood that gives the bird its name only in summer dress.
audubon-birds-315-red-breasted-sandpiper: The Red Knot, here under its older common name, is shown in the rust-coloured breeding plumage that earns the species its alternative title. Audubon paired it with a winter-plumaged bird to record the dramatic seasonal change.
audubon-birds-316-black-bellied-darter: An Anhinga perches with wings spread to dry, the long serpentine neck and silver-streaked scapulars defining the cormorant-like swimmer. Audubon worked the bird from southern swamp habitat, then a frontier subject for natural history.
audubon-birds-317-black-or-surf-duck: Surf Scoters ride a low swell, the drake's piebald head with its multicoloured bill rendered in dense aquatint stipple. The plate represents Audubon's interest in coastal sea ducks gathered along the New England shore.
audubon-birds-318-american-avocet: An American Avocet wades on stilt-like blue legs, the upturned bill sweeping water for invertebrates. The cinnamon head and neck mark breeding plumage; the plate uses a low horizon to emphasise the bird's improbable proportions.
audubon-birds-319-lesser-tern: Least Terns hover and dive over a stretch of sand, their black-capped, yellow-billed forms cropped close to the waterline. The colony-nesting seabird remains one of the smallest terns in the Americas.
audubon-birds-31-white-headed-eagle: A Bald Eagle stands over a freshly killed catfish, gripping the pale belly of the prey with one taloned foot. The grim, unsentimental treatment of carrion and predation is characteristic of Audubon's approach to North American raptors.
audubon-birds-320-little-sandpiper: The Least Sandpiper, North America's smallest shorebird, is shown probing wet mud with its short downcurved bill. Audubon recorded the yellowish legs that distinguish it from the otherwise similar Semipalmated Sandpiper.
audubon-birds-321-roseate-spoonbill: A Roseate Spoonbill bends its spatulate bill to feed, the deep pink wing coverts and orange tail set against a Florida backwater. The plate is among the most flamboyant in Birds of America and helped fix the species in the public imagination.
audubon-birds-322-red-headed-duck: A Redhead drake floats with its chestnut head and pale grey back distinct from the closely related Canvasback. Audubon's diving ducks were drawn from market-gunned specimens shot in Chesapeake Bay.
audubon-birds-323-black-skimmer-or-shear: A Black Skimmer cuts low over calm water, its outsized lower mandible slicing the surface for small fish. The asymmetrical bill, longer below than above, is unique among birds and was carefully measured in Audubon's drawing.
audubon-birds-324-bonapartian-gull: A Bonaparte's Gull, named by Audubon for the ornithologist Charles Lucien Bonaparte, hovers in light tern-like flight. The slate hood of breeding plumage and clean white wedge along the primaries are precisely registered in aquatint.
audubon-birds-325-buffel-headed-duck: A Bufflehead drake displays the puffy white-and-iridescent head pattern that gives the species its old name. Audubon paired the small diver with a more sober female to document the strong sexual dimorphism.
audubon-birds-326-gannet: A Northern Gannet stands on a rocky ledge, the cream-washed head and pale eye marking adult plumage. Audubon visited Bird Rock in the Gulf of St Lawrence in 1833 and based later studies on observations from that breeding colony.
audubon-birds-327-shoveller-duck: Northern Shovelers swim with the drake's iridescent green head leading and the broad spatulate bill held just above the water. The bill's lamellae, used to filter feed, are detailed in the aquatint as fine parallel hatching.
audubon-birds-328-long-legged-avocet: The Black-necked Stilt, here under an older common name, balances on improbably long pink legs, its black-and-white plumage held against a thin marsh horizon. Audubon emphasises the slender form by giving the figure almost the entire vertical sheet.
audubon-birds-329-yellow-breasted-rail: A Yellow Rail crouches in dense sedges, the buff-and-black striping providing camouflage that made the bird notoriously hard to flush. Audubon's plate is among the earliest detailed depictions of this secretive marsh species.
audubon-birds-32-black-billed-cuckoo: A Black-billed Cuckoo perches among magnolia leaves and large pink blooms, its long graduated tail and slender bill set against the foliage. Audubon's botanical backgrounds were typically painted by his assistants Joseph Mason or George Lehman.
audubon-birds-330-ring-plover: The Semipalmated Plover, formerly grouped with the Eurasian Ringed Plover, runs along wet sand in its breeding necklace of black. Audubon worked from coastal birds and recorded the partial webbing between the toes that gives the species its modern name.
audubon-birds-331-goosander: A Common Merganser, called Goosander in Britain, swims with the drake's dark green head and white flanks stretched along the surface. The serrated bill used to grasp slippery fish is drawn in fine etched detail.
audubon-birds-332-pied-duck: The Labrador Duck, extinct since around 1878, appears here in piebald drake plumage and brown female dress. Audubon's plate is now a historical document of one of the first North American birds lost to human pressure.
audubon-birds-333-green-heron: A small wading heron shown in profile beside reedy water, rendered in the hand-coloured aquatint technique that defines the double-elephant folio. The compressed pose accommodates the life-size format Audubon insisted upon for every species in the project.
audubon-birds-334-black-bellied-plover: A coastal shorebird depicted in breeding plumage, its black underparts and silvery dappled back picked out in fine engraved line and aquatint wash. The low marshland setting reflects Audubon's habit of placing each species in its working habitat.
