audubon-birds-335-red-breasted-snipe: Long-billed dowitchers, then known as red-breasted snipe, probe a shoreline in postures observed during Audubon's wetland field studies. The plate uses Robert Havell Jr.'s aquatint and engraving to render the streaked, cinnamon-toned breeding dress.
audubon-birds-336-yellow-crowned-heron: A yellow-crowned night heron stalks a crab among coastal vegetation, the recurved plumes and slate body tones built up through layered aquatint. The composition uses the bend of the heron's neck to fit the bird at full scale within the folio sheet.
audubon-birds-337-american-bittern: The cryptic marsh heron is shown in its characteristic upright freeze pose, neck extended skyward to mimic surrounding reeds. Audubon's plate captures the streaked brown camouflage in the fine tonal gradations possible through aquatint.
audubon-birds-338-bemaculated-duck: A diving duck of the goldeneye group, the so-called bemaculated duck is now considered a variant rather than a distinct species. The plate pairs a swimming bird with a preening companion to record both head pattern and scapular detail.
audubon-birds-339-little-auk: The dovekie, a stub-billed pelagic auk of the North Atlantic, appears swimming and in flight against an open sea. The black-and-white plumage is articulated through dense aquatint stippling rather than line, a Havell speciality.
audubon-birds-33-american-goldfinch: Goldfinches in summer yellow perch among thistle, the seedhead Audubon repeatedly used to characterise this species' diet. The plate is among the earlier engravings in the series, executed initially by W. H. Lizars before Havell took over production.
audubon-birds-340-least-stormy-petrel: A tiny pelagic petrel skims a wave trough, wings raised in the fluttering flight that earned the family its name. Audubon shows the species over open ocean, a setting rare in the folio and demanding extensive aquatint tone for the swell.
audubon-birds-341-great-auk: The flightless North Atlantic auk is shown swimming with a fish, drawn from specimens since the bird was already vanishing toward its 1844 extinction. The plate is now treated as a key visual record of a species Audubon never observed alive.
audubon-birds-342-golden-eye-duck: A drake and hen common goldeneye are arranged on water, the drake's white cheek-spot and bottle-green head set against finely aquatinted ripples. The pair format allowed Audubon to register sexual dimorphism within a single plate.
audubon-birds-343-ruddy-duck: A stiff-tailed diving duck shown with its tail cocked vertically, a courtship posture characteristic of the species. The chestnut body and bright blue bill of the breeding male required carefully registered hand colouring over the aquatint base.
audubon-birds-344-long-legged-sandpiper: The stilt sandpiper, here under its older name, stands in shallow water in worn breeding plumage. Havell's aquatint renders the barred underparts with a dotted texture distinct from the smoother washes used for the back.
audubon-birds-345-american-widgeon: A drake American wigeon displays the white forecrown and green eye-stripe that distinguish it from its Eurasian cousin. The bird is set on still water, with reflections built up by selective biting of the aquatint plate.
audubon-birds-346-black-throated-diver: The black-throated loon, called diver in British usage, is shown in breeding plumage with its striped neck patch. The vermiculated back pattern is achieved by very fine engraved cross-hatching beneath the aquatint tone.
audubon-birds-347-smew-or-white-nun: A drake smew, a small Eurasian sawbill that strays to North America, is rendered in his crisp white plumage with black eye-mask. The plate's negative-space approach leaves the paper itself to carry most of the bird's body.
audubon-birds-348-gadwall-duck: A pair of gadwall, a quietly patterned dabbling duck, are placed in shallow marsh water. The intricate vermiculation of the drake's flanks is one of Havell's more demanding aquatint passages in the waterfowl series.
audubon-birds-349-least-water-hen: The diminutive black rail, here as least water-hen, is shown in dense marsh vegetation that nearly conceals the bird. The plate reflects how rarely Audubon could observe a species that remains among North America's most secretive rails.
audubon-birds-34-worm-eating-warbler: A buff-toned warbler with bold black head stripes clings to woodland foliage, holding the larvae from which it takes its name. The plate is from the early Lizars-engraved portion of the folio, later reissued by Havell.
audubon-birds-350-rocky-mountain-plover: The mountain plover stands on dry shortgrass terrain rather than the shoreline typical of its family. Audubon emphasised the unmarked breast and pale legs that distinguish this prairie species from its coastal relatives.
audubon-birds-351-great-cinereous-owl: A great grey owl perches on a broken branch, the concentric facial discs rendered through circular sweeps of aquatint tone. The plate captures the disproportion between the species' enormous head and its relatively slight body.
audubon-birds-352-black-winged-hawk: The white-tailed kite, then called black-winged hawk, hovers with raised wings above grassland. Audubon often drew raptors in active flight rather than at rest, requiring Havell to model the underwing through graduated aquatint.
audubon-birds-353-i-chesnut-backed-titmo: Three small chickadees and titmice are arranged on a single branch as a comparative study, a format Audubon used for closely related songbirds. The plate sets chestnut-backed and boreal forms beside a more familiar black-capped relative.
audubon-birds-354-i-louisiana-tanager-2-: A western tanager, then called Louisiana tanager, shares foliage with a scarlet tanager, contrasting two of North America's most saturated red plumages. The double subject required careful registration of two distinct red mixtures in the hand colouring.
audubon-birds-355-macgillivray-s-finch: A coastal seaside sparrow named for the Scottish naturalist William MacGillivray, Audubon's London editor. The streaked olive plumage and yellow lore are placed against salt marsh grasses to reflect the species' restricted habitat.
audubon-birds-356-marsh-hawk: The northern harrier, here as marsh hawk, glides low with wings held in the species' characteristic shallow dihedral. The plate emphasises the white rump patch that remains the field mark used to identify the bird in flight.
audubon-birds-357-american-magpie: A black-billed magpie perches with its long graduated tail extended, the iridescent blue-green wing and tail tones laid in over a black aquatint base. Audubon encountered this corvid during his western travels along the upper Missouri.
audubon-birds-358-pine-grosbeak: A male pine grosbeak in rosy breeding plumage feeds among conifer needles, the heavy bill characteristic of the boreal finches. The plate uses a vertical pine sprig to anchor a relatively quiet, single-bird composition.
audubon-birds-359-i-arkansaw-flycatcher-: Three western tyrant flycatchers are grouped on a single branch: western kingbird, scissor-tailed flycatcher and Say's phoebe. The arrangement compares tail shape and underpart colour across species Audubon studied during his expeditions west of the Mississippi.
audubon-birds-35-children-s-warbler: A small wood-warbler named after John George Children of the British Museum, now considered a juvenile yellow warbler rather than a distinct species. The plate is a record of the taxonomic uncertainty that surrounded many small migrants in Audubon's day.
audubon-birds-360-i-winter-wren-2-rock-w: Two diminutive North American wrens share a single sheet, the Winter Wren perched among forest debris while the Rock Wren clings to broken stone. Hand-coloured aquatint after watercolour, with double-strike etched lines giving the plumage its fine barred texture.
audubon-birds-361-long-tailed-or-dusky-g: A male Dusky Grouse displayed on a Rocky Mountain branch, its long tail spread to show the slate barring and yellow comb above the eye. The plate relies on Robert Havell Jr.'s aquatint ground to render the soft, smoke-grey body feathers in graduated tone.
audubon-birds-362-i-yellow-billed-magpie: A composite plate of four western corvids gathered on a single oak branch, including the California-endemic Yellow-billed Magpie and the crested Steller's Jay. Such grouped plates condensed Audubon's later Pacific-coast specimens, drawn from skins forwarded by Townsend and Nuttall.
audubon-birds-363-bohemian-chatterer: A pair of Bohemian Waxwings perched on a fruiting branch, their silky cinnamon plumage punctuated by red sealing-wax tips on the secondaries. The aquatint tone captures the smooth, almost powdered quality of waxwing feathers.
audubon-birds-364-white-winged-crossbill: Several White-winged Crossbills work cones on a spruce sprig, the crossed mandibles drawn in profile to show their seed-extracting function. Crimson males and olive females are arranged to display sexual dimorphism on a single page.
audubon-birds-365-lapland-long-spur: Lapland Longspurs in breeding and winter plumage on a tussock of grass, illustrating the seasonal change between a black-faced summer male and the streaked, sparrow-like winter bird. Drawn from specimens taken on Audubon's Labrador expedition.
audubon-birds-366-iceland-or-jer-falcon: Two Gyrfalcons stand over prey, one in the white Greenland morph and the other in darker plumage, emphasising the wide colour range of this circumpolar raptor. The white bird is rendered almost in reserve against the paper, with aquatint shadow giving the body modelling.
audubon-birds-367-band-tailed-pigeon: A western Band-tailed Pigeon perched among oak foliage, the broad terminal tail-band and iridescent neck patch carefully delineated. The species was newly described to eastern audiences when this plate appeared in the double elephant folio.
audubon-birds-368-rock-grous: A Rock Ptarmigan on tundra rubble, shown in the mottled summer plumage that camouflages it among lichen-covered stones. Audubon worked from skins collected on the Arctic coast, since the species does not occur in the lower United States.
audubon-birds-369-i-mountain-mocking-bir: A Sage Thrasher and a Varied Thrush share a sheet of Pacific Northwest specimens, both birds based on study skins from the Wyeth-Townsend expedition. The plate pairs a desert-sagebrush songbird with a humid coniferous-forest thrush.
audubon-birds-36-stanley-hawk: A Cooper's Hawk, here under its old vernacular Stanley Hawk, drops on a covey of Northern Bobwhite, talons spread. The dynamic prey-strike composition is one of the recurring set-pieces Audubon used to differentiate accipiters from buteos.
audubon-birds-370-american-water-ouzel: An American Dipper perched on a wet stone in a fast stream, the stocky grey body and short cocked tail noted for its underwater foraging habit. Audubon arranged the bird against splashing water to allude to the species' diving behaviour.
audubon-birds-371-cock-of-the-plains: A male Greater Sage-Grouse in lek display, air sacs inflated over a white ruff while the spike-tailed female crouches nearby. The plate documents a sagebrush species Audubon never saw alive, drawn from Townsend specimens.
audubon-birds-372-common-buzzard: A Common Buzzard perched on a stump with a captured snake, included in the American series on the basis of a single questionable specimen. The bird is Eurasian and the record is now considered erroneous, but the figure remains in the folio sequence.
audubon-birds-373-i-evening-grosbeak-2-s: Evening Grosbeak and Black-headed Grosbeak share a flowering branch, contrasting the heavy yellow western finch with the cinnamon-breasted Black-headed. The composition stresses the disproportionately massive seed-cracking bills of both species.
audubon-birds-374-sharp-shinned-hawk: A pair of Sharp-shinned Hawks attacks smaller songbirds in mid-air, the slate-backed adult and brown juvenile shown together. The plate exemplifies Audubon's preference for raptors caught in the act of predation rather than at static rest.
audubon-birds-375-lesser-red-poll: Common Redpolls feed on a slender branch of birch, the small streaked finches with rosy crowns rendered in delicate engraved line. Their fondness for catkin seeds, suggested in the foliage choice, is a hallmark of Audubon's habitat-aware staging.
audubon-birds-376-trumpeter-swan: An adult Trumpeter Swan arches its neck to drink from a marsh pool, the long body filling almost the full sheet of the double elephant folio. The plate is one of the largest single-bird compositions in the series, exploiting the format for North America's heaviest waterfowl.
audubon-birds-377-scolopaceus-courlan: A Limpkin wades among aquatic vegetation, the long decurved bill specialised for extracting apple snails from their shells. Audubon observed the species in Florida and rendered the shaggy brown plumage with white drop-shaped streaks.
audubon-birds-378-hawk-owl: A Northern Hawk Owl perches upright on a dead conifer stub, the long tail and falcon-like silhouette distinguishing it from typical owls. The diurnal hunting habit is suggested by the open daylight setting, unusual for owl plates.
audubon-birds-379-ruff-necked-humming-bi: Several Rufous Hummingbirds hover at a flowering branch, the male's iridescent gorget shifting from copper to flame depending on angle. The plate gathers multiple postures to compensate for the difficulty of catching the bird at rest.
audubon-birds-37-golden-winged-woodpecke: A family group of Northern Flickers, then known as Golden-winged Woodpeckers, arranged on a branch to show the bright yellow shafts of the wing and tail feathers. The composition presents male, female and juveniles together in the manner of a comparative life history.
audubon-birds-380-tengmalm-s-owl: A Boreal Owl perched against dark conifer foliage, the squarish facial disc and white-spotted forehead drawn from a northern specimen. Its scarcity in the lower latitudes made the species a rarity in the folio.
audubon-birds-381-snow-goose: An adult white-morph Snow Goose stands beside a darker first-year bird, contrasting the species' plumage stages. The black wing tips on the white adult are a recurring identification cue Audubon emphasised through aquatint shading.
audubon-birds-382-sharp-tailed-grous: Sharp-tailed Grouse perch on a prairie shrub, the pointed central tail feathers and pale spotted underparts characterising the species. Drawn from upper Missouri specimens, the plate represents the open-country grouse of the northern plains.
audubon-birds-383-long-eared-owl: A Long-eared Owl turns its tufted head from a perch among dense branches, its mottled brown plumage breaking up against the bark. The composition shows the cryptic roosting posture by which the bird disappears into wooded cover during the day.
audubon-birds-384-black-throated-bunting: A pair of Dickcissels on grain stalks, the male's yellow breast crossed by a black bib reminiscent of a meadowlark in miniature. Audubon set them in cultivated grasses to allude to the species' preference for grain fields and old prairie.
audubon-birds-385-i-bank-swallow-2-viole: A Bank Swallow at a sandy burrow entrance is paired with the western Violet-green Swallow, contrasting brown plumage against iridescent green and purple. The grouping aligns nesting habit with geographical range across one sheet.
audubon-birds-386-white-heron: A Great Egret in breeding plumage stands amid coastal vegetation, long aigrette plumes cascading down its back. The plate documents the species before late nineteenth-century plume hunting nearly drove it to extinction.
audubon-birds-387-glossy-ibis: A Glossy Ibis probes a wetland margin, the curved bill and mahogany body washed with green-bronze iridescence rendered through layered aquatint and watercolour. Audubon based the figure on a Florida specimen of this then-uncommon coastal wader.
audubon-birds-388-i-nuttall-s-starling-2: Three western blackbirds and an oriole share a slender branch, their black, yellow, and orange plumages set off against bare paper. The aquatint and engraved line, hand-coloured under Audubon's direction in Robert Havell's London workshop, register every barb of the wing feathers.
audubon-birds-389-red-cockaded-woodpecke: A pair of pine-dwelling woodpeckers cling to a longleaf trunk, the male's tiny scarlet cockade barely visible behind the eye. Audubon's double elephant folio fits the birds at life size, and the etched bark conveys the resin-streaked pines of the southern coastal plain.
audubon-birds-38-kentucky-warbler: Two olive-and-yellow warblers with bold black sideburns perch on a flowering magnolia branch. The composition pairs an eastern songbird with a southern bloom, a habit Audubon used to compress habitat and season into one engraved sheet.
audubon-birds-390-i-lark-finch-2-prairie: Three sparrow-like birds of the Great Plains and Pacific slope are arranged on a low rock with a sprig of grass. The plate gathers species Audubon worked up from western specimens supplied by Townsend and Nuttall, drawn at life size for the engraved folio.
audubon-birds-391-brant-goose: A small dark sea goose stands on a shoreline strewn with eelgrass, its head and neck inky against a pale tidal sky. Audubon shows the bird in the low, ground-hugging pose typical of brants on the Atlantic coast, where they overwinter on the grass beds they feed on.
audubon-birds-392-louisiana-hawk: A broad-shouldered raptor of the southern marshes grips its prey on a low perch, dark wings half-mantled. The plate, etched and aquatinted by Havell, preserves Audubon's habit of staging hawks mid-action rather than as static specimens.
audubon-birds-393-i-townsend-s-warbler-2: Three small western birds, recently described from Pacific Northwest collections, share a flowering branch. Audubon assembled the plate from skins forwarded by John Kirk Townsend, allowing him to add Pacific species to a work otherwise built on his own field studies.
audubon-birds-394-i-chestnut-coloured-fi: Four small seed-eaters from the western interior are stacked on twigs and a low stone, their ruddy, black, and streaked plumages contrasted side by side. Combination plates like this allowed Audubon to keep the folio's species count climbing as new western specimens reached him.
audubon-birds-395-i-audubon-s-warbler-2-: Three western wood-warblers, all named from Townsend and Nuttall's expedition skins, gather on a single conifer spray. The plate is one of several near the end of the folio that introduced Pacific species to European subscribers for the first time.
audubon-birds-396-burgomaster-gull: A heavy pale-mantled gull stands on a rocky shore, its hooked yellow bill and cold pink legs picked out against the engraved water. Now called the glaucous gull, the bird was given its old Dutch nickname for the way it lords over Arctic colonies, robbing eggs and chicks.
audubon-birds-397-scarlet-ibis: A vivid red wading bird probes a coastal shallow, its curved bill and long neck tracing an S against pale sky. Audubon based the plate on birds taken in the Gulf of Mexico, the northernmost edge of a species otherwise tied to South American mangroves.
audubon-birds-398-i-lazuli-finch-2-clay-: Three small western buntings and sparrows share a sprig of seeding grass, the lazuli's blue head set against the duller browns of its neighbours. The grouping is one of Audubon's late composite plates assembled from skins rather than live observation.
audubon-birds-399-i-black-throated-green: Three eastern wood-warblers, including the flame-throated Blackburnian, perch among hemlock needles and a curling vine. Audubon worked the species into a single branch to pack his northeastern songbirds into the folio's later plates.
audubon-birds-39-crested-titmouse: A pair of tufted titmice work over an oak twig hung with acorns, the upright crest and dark eye giving each a sharp profile. The plate compresses the bird's preferred habitat — eastern hardwood mast — into a single engraved branch.
audubon-birds-3-prothonotary-warbler-res: Brilliant golden-yellow warblers move through a tangle of cane and creeper at the edge of a southern swamp. Audubon drew the species in the bottomland habitat where it nests in flooded tree cavities, a detail he stressed in the accompanying Ornithological Biography.
audubon-birds-400-i-arkansaw-siskin-2-me: Five small finches and a tanager are stacked across a pine spray, the western tanager's red head burning above the duller seed-eaters. The plate marks Audubon's centenary number and shows his late reliance on study skins for species he never observed alive.
audubon-birds-401-red-breasted-merganser: A drake and hen sawbill ride a low Atlantic swell, the male's ragged green crest and rust collar laid against a pale wash of horizon. Audubon paired the sexes to show the merganser's marked dimorphism in a single image.
audubon-birds-402-i-black-throated-guill: Four small North Pacific auks and auklets are arranged on bare rock, their stub bills and ornamented faces shown in profile. The plate, drawn from skins collected on the Northwest Coast, brought species new to Atlantic ornithology into the folio.
audubon-birds-403-golden-eye-duck: A drake and hen common goldeneye dive in a winter bay, the male's white cheek-spot bright against his green-glossed head. Audubon's plate captures the abrupt forward thrust of a diving duck just before it submerges.
audubon-birds-404-eared-grebe: A slender black-necked grebe in summer dress rides a shaded pool, its golden ear-tufts fanned behind a red eye. The bird's long neck and lobed feet are drawn with the wiry precision Havell's aquatint allowed for water birds.
audubon-birds-405-semipalmated-sandpiper: A small grey-and-white sandpiper, one of the abundant peeps of the Atlantic flats, picks across wet sand. Audubon includes the partly webbed toes that give the species its name, a detail more often described than drawn.
audubon-birds-406-trumpeter-swan: A massive white swan arches its neck along the full height of the double elephant folio sheet, snapping at a small moth. Audubon bent the bird's neck into a tight curl so it could stand at life size on a page just over a metre tall — one of his most-discussed compositional solutions.
audubon-birds-407-dusky-albatros: A long-winged southern seabird banks over an open ocean horizon, its sooty plumage dark against a pale wash. Audubon worked the bird up from a specimen rather than field encounter, and the plate is among the few in the folio devoted to a Southern Hemisphere species.
audubon-birds-408-american-scoter-duck: A jet-black sea duck with an orange-knobbed bill rides a low swell, a hen tucked behind. The black scoter winters in rafts along the North American coasts, and Audubon's etching keeps the matte plumage from going flat by varnishing the highlight on the bill.
audubon-birds-409-i-havell-s-tern-2-trud: Two slender terns wheel across a coastal sky, their forked tails and slate-tipped wings set against bare paper. Audubon named one of the birds for his London engraver Robert Havell, the first such naming in the folio.
audubon-birds-40-american-redstart: Black-and-orange flycatching warblers spread their tails through a tangled branch, flashing the pigment patches that give the species its name. Audubon shows the male in the fan-tailed display posture used to startle insects from leaves.
audubon-birds-410-marsh-tern: A pale tern with black cap and forked tail courses low over a salt marsh, snapping at insects above the cordgrass. Now identified with the gull-billed tern, the species hawks for prey rather than plunge-diving like most of its relatives.
audubon-birds-411-common-american-swan: A tundra swan stands on a quiet shore, the small yellow lore-spot at the base of the bill picked out in colour. Audubon distinguished the species from the larger trumpeter on the basis of voice and bill markings, an early American contribution to swan taxonomy.
audubon-birds-412-i-violet-green-cormora: Two Pacific cormorants perch on a sea stack, their iridescent throats and slim crests catching a low light. The plate draws on skins gathered by John Kirk Townsend on the Columbia River, where these species nest on coastal cliffs.
audubon-birds-413-california-partridge: A covey of crested California quail forages through low chaparral, the male's curling topknot tipped forward. Audubon worked the plate from skins sent east from the Pacific coast, introducing one of the West's most distinctive game birds to his subscribers.
audubon-birds-414-i-golden-winged-warble: Two small wood-warblers are arranged on slender branches, the Golden-winged showing its black throat patch and yellow wing bars beside the streaked, chestnut-cheeked Cape May. Engraved by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's life-size watercolour, then hand-coloured in aquatint for the double-elephant folio.
audubon-birds-415-i-brown-creeper-2-cali: A Brown Creeper spirals up a furrowed trunk while the small pygmy nuthatch (here labelled Californian) perches head-down nearby, contrasting two bark-foraging strategies. The plate uses Havell's combined etching, engraving and aquatint, with watercolour applied by hand to the printed sheet.
audubon-birds-416-i-hairy-woodpecker-2-r: A composite study of five woodpecker species posed on dead snags, comparing crown patterns, barred backs and tail bracing across eastern and western North America. Such grouped plates appeared in the later supplementary numbers of the folio, accommodating species Audubon received from western expeditions.
audubon-birds-417-i-maria-s-woodpecker-2: Six woodpecker forms are stacked on a single trunk, several of them now treated as regional races of the Hairy and American Three-toed Woodpeckers. The plate reflects the taxonomic optimism of 1830s ornithology, when subtle plumage variants were routinely described as full species.
audubon-birds-418-i-american-ptarmigan-2: A pair of high-latitude grouse pose against tundra plants, the Rock Ptarmigan in mottled summer plumage beside the alpine White-tailed Ptarmigan. Audubon worked from skins supplied by the Hudson's Bay Company and from Thomas Nuttall's western specimens.
audubon-birds-419-i-little-tawny-thrush-: Three forest birds share a sprig of foliage: the russet Hermit Thrush form, Townsend's Solitaire with its pale eye-ring, and the soft grey Canada Jay. The grouping pulls together species new to science from John Kirk Townsend's Columbia River collections.
audubon-birds-41-ruffed-grouse: A male Ruffed Grouse fans his black-banded tail and erects the dark neck ruff in courtship display, while females and a chick feed in autumn underbrush. Audubon shows the bird amid grapevine and dogwood, signalling the eastern hardwood habitat he knew from Pennsylvania.
audubon-birds-420-prairie-starling: The Yellow-headed Blackbird, here under an obsolete name, is shown on a stalk of prairie grass, the male's golden hood set against jet plumage. Audubon based the figure on specimens from the upper Missouri, a region he reached in 1843.
audubon-birds-421-brown-pelican: A Brown Pelican stands on a mangrove branch, the long pouched bill folded against the breast and the breeding-season chestnut nape clearly rendered. Audubon studied the species along the Florida Keys in 1832, and the plate is among the largest aquatints in the folio.
audubon-birds-422-rough-legged-falcon: A Rough-legged Hawk grips prey on a low perch, feathered tarsi and dark belly band visible against a pale, lightly washed sky. The species winters on open North American country after breeding in the Arctic, a range Audubon noted from northern correspondents.
audubon-birds-423-i-plumed-partridge-2-t: Two western quail are figured together: the Mountain Quail with its long straight head plume, and the Scaled Quail showing its distinctive crescent-edged feathering. The plate draws on specimens from Townsend and Nuttall's overland expedition to the Pacific.
audubon-birds-424-i-lazuli-finch-2-crims: Six finch-like passerines crowd a single branch, ranging from the turquoise Lazuli Bunting to the heavy-billed Evening Grosbeak. Such crowded composite sheets close the folio, making efficient use of the costly copper plates as Audubon hurried to incorporate western discoveries.
audubon-birds-425-columbian-humming-bird: A pair of Rufous Hummingbirds, named for the Columbia River where Townsend collected them, hover at trumpet-shaped flowers. Audubon renders the male's coppery gorget with the metallic shimmer the aquatint medium handles unusually well.
audubon-birds-426-californian-vulture: An immense California Condor is shown perched, its bare orange head and white underwing linings displayed at near life size. Audubon never saw the bird alive, working from a skin in the museum at Fort Vancouver brought by David Douglas.
audubon-birds-427-i-white-legged-oyster-: Two oystercatcher forms stand on a rocky shore with bright orange-red bills lowered to bivalves, one pied and one nearly all-black. Both names are now subsumed into the American Oystercatcher and Black Oystercatcher of the Pacific coast.
audubon-birds-428-townsend-s-sandpiper: A small shorebird in autumn plumage probes a wet shoreline, the species first described from a single specimen collected by John Kirk Townsend. Modern ornithologists treat it as an aberrant or hybrid bird rather than a valid taxon, leaving the plate a record of a vanished name.
audubon-birds-429-western-duck: The Steller's Eider, called Western Duck here, drifts on calm water in the male's striking black, white and chestnut breeding pattern. Audubon worked from Bering Sea specimens supplied by Russian-American Company contacts.
audubon-birds-42-orchard-oriole: Several Orchard Orioles, including a yellow-green female and a brick-red adult male, animate a flowering locust branch with a pendulous woven nest. Audubon emphasises the species' close association with farmsteads and orchard trees of the eastern states.
audubon-birds-430-slender-billed-guillem: A pair of Pigeon Guillemots in summer dress sit on a sea-cliff ledge, the slim red feet and wing patches set against sooty plumage. The plate depends on specimens from Townsend's North Pacific collecting along the Oregon coast.
audubon-birds-431-american-flamingo: A single flamingo bows its long neck almost to the ground, its body filling the double-elephant sheet vertically so the entire bird could be shown life-size. Audubon studied the species in the Florida Keys in 1832 and the plate is among the most graphically daring of the folio.
audubon-birds-432-i-burrowing-owl-2-larg: Five small owls are arrayed against a sparse landscape, including the long-legged Burrowing Owl at its prairie-dog hole and the buoyant Short-eared Owl quartering open ground. The composition compares head shape, leg length and wing form across diurnal and nocturnal hunters.
audubon-birds-433-i-bullock-s-oriole-2-b: An assortment of bright passerines crowds a leafy branch: two oriole species, a Lesser Goldfinch, a Varied Thrush and a Northern Waterthrush. Such mixed plates were a practical way to publish western specimens that reached Audubon late in the project.
audubon-birds-434-i-little-tyrant-fly-ca: Six small insect-eaters share a single twig, several of them species known only from a handful of skins and never reliably observed since. The plate has long fascinated ornithologists as a possible record of birds now extinct or never validly described.
audubon-birds-435-i-columbian-water-ouze: Two American Dippers cling to mossy stones in a fast stream, one upright and one diving for aquatic insects. The plate documents the only North American songbird that hunts under flowing water, with western specimens supplied by Townsend.
audubon-birds-43-cedar-bird: A flock of Cedar Waxwings perches in a fruiting branch, the silky fawn plumage, black mask and red wing tips carefully differentiated. Audubon notes their fondness for berries by surrounding them with the very fruit on which they feed.
audubon-birds-44-summer-red-bird: A male Summer Tanager in saturated rose-red and a duller olive female pose in a flowering branch with insect prey in the male's bill. Audubon's text emphasises the species' fondness for hornets and bees, behaviour the figures hint at directly.
audubon-birds-45-traill-s-flycatcher: A small olive-brown Empidonax flycatcher named after the Edinburgh naturalist Thomas Stewart Traill perches alertly on a sprig. Modern taxonomy splits the original Traill's into the Alder and Willow Flycatchers, two near-identical species separable mainly by song.
audubon-birds-46-barred-owl: A Barred Owl stares forward with dark liquid eyes, vertical breast streaks contrasting with horizontal barring across the throat, gripping a captured grey squirrel. Audubon's typically narrative composition presents predator and prey on a single branch.
audubon-birds-47-ruby-throated-humming-b: Multiple Ruby-throated Hummingbirds hover and perch around trumpet-creeper blossoms, the iridescent throats catching light at different angles. Audubon used the plate to advertise the species' status as the only hummingbird breeding east of the Mississippi.
audubon-birds-48-azure-warbler: A small blue-and-white warbler, now usually identified with the Cerulean Warbler, perches in tender spring foliage. Audubon worked from limited material for this species and his figure remained the standard reference for decades.
audubon-birds-49-blue-green-warbler: A small olive-green songbird perches among the slender shoots and ripening fruit of a wild plant, drawn life-size in the format that defined the double-elephant folio. Hand-coloured aquatint over copper engraving renders the warbler's pale underparts with the soft tonal washes characteristic of the Havell workshop in London.
audubon-birds-4-purple-finch: A pair of finches, the male flushed raspberry-red and the streaked brown female, cling to a flowering branch in mirrored poses. The composition follows the field-observed habit of pairing male and female on a single botanical specimen, a hallmark of the Birds of America project drawn from skins and live study.
audubon-birds-50-black-yellow-warbler: Now identified as the magnolia warbler, the bird is shown twisting through foliage with its yellow underparts and bold black streaks turned toward the viewer. The aquatint plate combines etched contour lines with hand-applied watercolour to capture the warbler's distinctive tail-spot pattern.
audubon-birds-51-red-tailed-hawk: Two hawks grapple over a hare in a dramatic mid-air struggle, talons interlocked and wings braced against one another. The double-elephant folio's full sheet is exploited to show the raptors at life size, a scale unprecedented in ornithological publishing of the period.
audubon-birds-52-chuck-will-s-widow: A nightjar pair confronts a coiled snake among oak foliage, the birds' camouflaged plumage rendered in finely stippled aquatint. The narrative encounter reflects the artist's preference for staging behaviour rather than presenting specimens against blank ground.
audubon-birds-53-painted-finch: The painted bunting, called the Nonpareil for its blue, green and crimson plumage, is shown with several individuals on a sprig of wild almond. The plate demonstrates the colourists' ability to register multiple unmixed pigments through the aquatint ground without muddying the saturated hues.
audubon-birds-54-rice-bird: Bobolinks gather among the seed-heads of a cultivated rice plant, the breeding males in their inverted black-and-buff plumage contrasted with the streaked females. The plate documents the species under the agricultural name applied to migrating flocks that fed on Carolina rice fields.
audubon-birds-55-cuvier-s-regulus: A tiny crested kinglet sits on a leafy branch, its identity now considered a probable hybrid or unrepeatable observation. The plate honours Georges Cuvier and exemplifies the speculative species occasionally included from single field encounters.
audubon-birds-56-red-shouldered-hawk: A red-shouldered hawk stoops with talons spread, banded tail flared as it descends on prey. The flight pose required the engraver Robert Havell Jr. to translate rapid wash drawing into the layered tonal bitings of aquatint without losing the gesture.
audubon-birds-57-loggerhead-shrike: A pair of shrikes occupies the spiny branches of a green-briar, the predatory songbird's hooked bill and black mask carefully delineated. The botanical setting accurately records a thicket habitat where shrikes impale prey on thorns.
audubon-birds-58-hermit-thrush: A hermit thrush stands on the forest floor among fallen leaves and a sprig of partridgeberry, the rufous tail set against cool grey-brown upperparts. The understated composition reflects the bird's retiring habits in eastern woodland.
audubon-birds-59-chestnut-sided-warbler: Two warblers move through a flowering shrub, their yellow crowns and chestnut flanks picked out against fresh green leaves. The aquatint records a species that became more common as nineteenth-century clearing created the second-growth scrub it favours.
audubon-birds-5-bonaparte-s-flycatcher: A small flycatcher named for the ornithologist Charles Lucien Bonaparte perches among slender branches. The bird is no longer recognised as a distinct species and is generally considered a misidentified or aberrant specimen, surviving only in this plate.
audubon-birds-60-carbonated-warbler: Two yellow-and-black warblers occupy a branch of a flowering tree, depicting a species known only from the artist's own report. No other naturalist has documented it, and the plate stands as the sole record of a bird that may have been a hybrid or extinct form.
audubon-birds-61-great-horned-owl: A pair of great horned owls perches in heavy darkness, ear tufts raised and yellow eyes facing the viewer. The deep blacks were achieved through repeated aquatint bitings, a technique that gave the Havell workshop its reputation for nocturnal subjects.
audubon-birds-62-passenger-pigeon: A male and female passenger pigeon exchange a beakful of food on a branch, the male's slate-blue head turned to the rosy female. The plate documents flock behaviour of a species then numbered in billions but driven to extinction by 1914.
audubon-birds-63-white-eyed-flycatcher-o: A white-eyed vireo sits on a sprig with prominent fruit, the pale iris that gives the bird its name carefully picked out. The plate reflects the unsettled early-nineteenth-century classification that grouped vireos with flycatchers.
audubon-birds-64-swamp-sparrow: A swamp sparrow clings to a stalk of marsh vegetation, its rusty cap and grey breast set against the muted greens of wetland reed. The composition keeps the bird at eye level among emergent stems rather than placing it in open view.
audubon-birds-65-rathbone-warbler: A small warbler named in honour of the Rathbone family of Liverpool, patrons during the artist's English subscription drive. Now considered an immature or variant yellow warbler rather than a distinct species, the plate preserves a name otherwise lost from ornithology.
audubon-birds-66-ivory-billed-woodpecker: Three ivory-billed woodpeckers cling to a dead trunk, the male's vermilion crest and the species' pale chisel-bill rendered at life size. The aquatint preserves a bird widely considered extinct, with the social group depicted reflecting the family parties once seen in southern bottomland forest.
audubon-birds-67-red-winged-starling-or-: Red-winged blackbirds in graded plumages occupy a wild oat plant, the male's scarlet-and-yellow epaulet flashed in display. The plate uses the older common names current before the species was universally classed among American blackbirds.
audubon-birds-68-republican-or-cliff-swa: Cliff swallows are shown alongside their gourd-shaped mud nests built under a rocky overhang, several birds entering and leaving the colony. The plate documents the colonial nesting biology that earned the bird its alternative name of Republican Swallow.
audubon-birds-69-bay-breasted-warbler: Two bay-breasted warblers move through the foliage of a flowering branch, the male's chestnut throat and crown contrasted with creamy neck patches. The plate captures spring breeding plumage rarely encountered south of the boreal forest.
audubon-birds-6-wild-turkey-2: A male wild turkey strides across the ground, iridescent body feathers and bare blue head turned in profile. The bird occupies almost the full sheet of the double-elephant folio, a deliberate scaling that opens the publication's prospectus and signals American subject matter.
audubon-birds-6-wild-turkey: A male wild turkey strides across the ground, iridescent body feathers and bare blue head turned in profile. The bird occupies almost the full sheet of the double-elephant folio, a deliberate scaling that opens the publication's prospectus and signals American subject matter.
audubon-birds-70-henslow-s-bunting: Henslow's sparrow, then placed among the buntings, rests on a stalk in a grassy field setting. The plate honours the Cambridge botanist John Stevens Henslow, who supported the artist during his English campaign for subscribers.
audubon-birds-71-winter-hawk: A perched raptor identified as the Winter Hawk, now recognised as the immature plumage of the red-shouldered hawk. The plate records a separate species name once thought to apply, before juvenile and adult plumages were correctly correlated.
audubon-birds-72-swallow-tailed-hawk: A swallow-tailed kite banks on long pointed wings, its forked tail spread to steer in flight. The aerial pose, set against open sky, exploits the format of the folio sheet to display a raptor that hunts insects on the wing over southern wetlands.
audubon-birds-73-wood-thrush: A wood thrush stands on a branch of flowering dogwood, its bold round breast spots set against rich brown upperparts. The pairing of the bird with this characteristic spring-flowering tree of eastern deciduous forest follows the project's commitment to ecological context.
audubon-birds-74-indigo-bird: An indigo bunting sings from a sprig of wild grape, the male's saturated blue plumage achieved through layered washes over the aquatint ground. The plate uses the common name in circulation before the species was reclassified among the cardinal-grosbeak family.
audubon-birds-75-le-petit-caporal: A small falcon known under a French common name and now generally identified as an immature merlin or American kestrel variant. The plate preserves a vernacular term used by Louisiana hunters that did not enter standard ornithological nomenclature.
audubon-birds-76-virginian-partridge: A covey of Virginian (Northern Bobwhite) Partridges scatters in panic as a hawk strikes from above, the dynamic composition exemplifying the dramatic narrative staging that distinguishes Birds of America from earlier ornithological plates. Hand-coloured aquatint after a watercolour drawn from life-size specimens.
audubon-birds-77-belted-kingfisher: A pair of Belted Kingfishers perches on a riverside branch, the female distinguished by her chestnut breast band. The hand-coloured aquatint captures the slate-blue plumage and shaggy crest, with a subtle aquatic background hinting at the species' habitat along North American waterways.
audubon-birds-78-great-carolina-wren: Two Carolina Wrens animate a tangled vine, rendered with the close botanical observation typical of the double-elephant folio. The hand-coloured aquatint emphasizes the warm cinnamon plumage and prominent white eye-stripe, presenting the birds at life-size on the unusually large 39-inch sheet.
audubon-birds-79-tyrant-fly-catcher: An Eastern Kingbird is shown defending its territory, the species' aggressive behaviour toward larger raptors having earned it the name Tyrant Fly-catcher. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving by Robert Havell Jr. of London, working from the original watercolour study.
audubon-birds-7-purple-grakle-or-common-: Common Grackles cluster on a maize stalk, ears split open to expose the kernels they raid. The composition links the species directly to its reputation as an agricultural pest, with iridescent plumage rendered through layered hand-colouring over an aquatint base.
audubon-birds-80-prairie-titlark: Two American Pipits, then called Prairie Titlarks, are shown on the ground among low vegetation, reflecting their terrestrial foraging habits. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving from the double-elephant folio, with the streaked buff plumage carefully differentiated to aid field identification.
audubon-birds-81-fish-hawk-or-osprey: An Osprey clutches a freshly caught weakfish in its talons, mid-flight against a coastal sky. The plate emphasizes the raptor's specialized fishing anatomy, including the reversible outer toe, and is among the more dramatic action compositions in the folio.
audubon-birds-82-whip-poor-will: Three Eastern Whip-poor-wills perch on an oak branch among red-flowered foliage, the cryptic plumage demonstrating the bird's reliance on camouflage during daylight roosting. Hand-coloured aquatint conveying the nocturnal nightjar's enormous gape and bristled rictus.
audubon-birds-83-house-wren: House Wrens animate a glass bottle and old hat repurposed as nesting cavities, a domestic vignette underscoring the species' adaptability to human structures. Hand-coloured aquatint engraved by Robert Havell Jr., with characteristically lively posing of the small brown birds.
audubon-birds-84-blue-grey-fly-catcher: A pair of Blue-grey Gnatcatchers tends a lichen-covered cup nest, the delicate construction rendered in fine detail to show the spiderweb binding. Hand-coloured aquatint demonstrating the natural history precision that supplemented Audubon's more theatrical bird studies.
audubon-birds-85-yellow-throated-warbler: Yellow-throated Warblers explore pine boughs in a composition reflecting the species' preferred coniferous habitat in the southern United States. Hand-coloured aquatint with the bold yellow throat patch and bold facial striping rendered from Audubon's life-size watercolour study.
audubon-birds-86-black-warrior: A dark-morph Red-tailed Hawk, which Audubon described as a distinct species he named Black Warrior in honour of an Alabama river, is shown in commanding profile. Now classified as Harlan's Hawk, the plate documents an important regional colour variant of the widespread raptor.
audubon-birds-87-florida-jay: A pair of Florida Scrub-Jays perches on a flowering persimmon branch, the plate documenting a species endemic to the oak-scrub habitats of peninsular Florida. Hand-coloured aquatint engraved by Robert Havell Jr. after Audubon's watercolour from his Florida expedition.
audubon-birds-88-autumnal-warbler: What Audubon described as the Autumnal Warbler is now recognized as the non-breeding plumage of the Bay-breasted Warbler, an instructive example of the seasonal variation that confused early American ornithology. Hand-coloured aquatint from the double-elephant folio.
audubon-birds-89-nashville-warbler: Nashville Warblers move among the leaves and yellow blooms of a flowering plant, captured in the active foraging poses Audubon favoured over rigid museum-style profiles. Hand-coloured aquatint engraved in London by Robert Havell Jr.
audubon-birds-8-white-throated-sparrow: White-throated Sparrows perch among the broad green leaves and dangling pods of a dogwood, with the boldly striped head and bright white throat patch rendered through layered watercolour over engraving. Aquatint hand-coloured under Robert Havell Jr.'s London supervision.
audubon-birds-90-black-white-creeper: Black-and-white Warblers ascend a tree trunk in the nuthatch-like fashion that gives the species its common name, with the bold zebra striping carefully rendered. Hand-coloured aquatint demonstrating Audubon's interest in characteristic posture as a diagnostic field mark.
audubon-birds-91-broad-winged-hawk: An adult and juvenile Broad-winged Hawk perch together on a branch, paired to show the contrasting plumages of the small forest raptor known for its enormous autumnal kettling migrations. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving from the double-elephant folio.
audubon-birds-92-pigeon-hawk: A Merlin, formerly called the Pigeon Hawk, grips a freshly killed Northern Bobwhite, the prey lying breast-up to display its plumage. The compact, agile falcon is shown in the predatory tableau Audubon used to convey both species' identity and ecological role.
audubon-birds-93-sea-side-finch: Seaside Sparrows perch among the salt-marsh grasses that define their narrow coastal range from New England to the Gulf. Hand-coloured aquatint with the dingy olive-grey plumage and yellow lores rendered with the precision required for distinguishing closely related Ammospiza sparrows.
audubon-birds-94-grass-finch-or-bay-wing: A Vesper Sparrow stands among low grasses, the chestnut shoulder patch and white outer tail feathers carefully rendered as field marks. Hand-coloured aquatint from the double-elephant folio, executed by Robert Havell Jr. after Audubon's life-size watercolour.
audubon-birds-95-yellow-poll-warbler: Yellow Warblers animate a flowering branch, with the male's chestnut breast streaks distinguishing him from the unstreaked female. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving in which the bird's lemon-yellow plumage is set against soft botanical illustration to enhance contrast.
audubon-birds-96-columbia-jay: A pair of Black-throated Magpie-Jays, native to western Mexico, flaunts elongated tail feathers far exceeding their body length. The plate is unusual within Birds of America for depicting a species outside North American territory, drawn from skins rather than field encounter.
audubon-birds-97-little-screech-owl: Three Eastern Screech-Owls illustrate the species' striking colour polymorphism, with red-morph and grey-morph individuals shown together on a single branch. Hand-coloured aquatint engraved by Robert Havell Jr., emphasizing the small owl's prominent ear tufts and yellow eyes.
audubon-birds-98-white-bellied-swallow: Tree Swallows wheel near a flowering plant, the iridescent blue-green dorsal plumage contrasted against the clean white underparts. Hand-coloured aquatint from the double-elephant folio, demonstrating Audubon's effort to depict aerial insectivores in characteristic flight rather than at rest.
audubon-birds-99-cow-pen-bird: Brown-headed Cowbirds, the first North American brood parasite formally documented, are shown on a flowering branch. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving in which the male's warm brown head contrasts with the glossy black body, executed by Robert Havell Jr. in London.
audubon-birds-9-selby-s-flycatcher: What Audubon named Selby's Flycatcher in honour of British ornithologist Prideaux John Selby is now generally regarded as a Hooded Warbler. Hand-coloured aquatint from the double-elephant folio, recording one of several taxonomic redeterminations made after the work's initial publication.
kunstformen-images-assortment-of-hepatic: Plate 82 arranges liverworts (Hepaticae) into a symmetrical mosaic showcasing the bilateral and radial geometries of Marchantia, Asterella and related thalloid genera. Lithograph from Kunstformen der Natur, where Haeckel's bilateral plate design was meant to advertise nature as an artist following the same compositional rules as architects.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-acanthometra: Lithographic plate gathering Acantharian protozoa whose star-shaped strontium-sulfate skeletons radiate twenty spicules from a central point. Haeckel arranged the microscopic single-celled marine organisms in a cathedral-like symmetry, advancing his argument that morphological law unites the smallest plankton with the greatest works of design.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-acanthophract: Plate of Acanthophractan radiolarians whose silica lattices form polyhedral and bell-shaped tests under the microscope. Drawn from specimens collected on the HMS Challenger expedition, the lithograph distils Haeckel's belief that the geometry of marine plankton offered a kind of natural Jugendstil ornament.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-acephala: Plate of headless bivalve molluscs arrayed for symmetry: Venus clams, cockles, and related shells shown in opening, valve, and hinge views. Haeckel exploits the radiating ribs and growth rings to draw the eye outward from each pair, treating malacology as ornamental design.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-actiniae: Sea anemones rendered with their tentacle crowns spread in jewel-toned discs, including Cereus pedunculatus and Aiptasia mutabilis. The plate compresses several species into a single bouquet, using the column striations and radial muscle bands as decorative geometry.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ammonitida: Fossil ammonite shells from the Mesozoic, shown in lateral and ventral views to display sutured chambers and ribbed coiling. The composition makes the logarithmic spiral and ornamentation of Cardioceras and Schloenbachia legible at a glance, treating extinct cephalopods as design specimens.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-amphoridea: Paleozoic carpoid and cystoid echinoderms with their armored plates flattened across the page. Haeckel arranges the polygonal calyx tessellations of Enoploura and Pleurocystites to highlight the irregular, almost crystalline plating of these early stalked relatives of starfish.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-anthomedusae: Hydromedusae of the Anthomedusae order, shown from above, below and in lateral section to expose the gonads and gastrovascular canals. Trailing tentacles fall in glassy curtains around bell-shaped umbrellas, a study in translucent radial symmetry.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-antilopina: Comparative heads of antelopes and related bovids: four-horned antelope, gnu, sitatunga, pronghorn, and waterbuck. The plate functions as a horn-shape catalogue, each profile placed to compare spiral, lyrate, and forked configurations.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-arachnida: Mites and amblypygids drawn at high magnification, their dorsal armor and segmented legs picked out against neutral grounds. Haeckel emphasizes the sculpted cuticle of cepheid mites and the flattened, crab-like body of the whip spider Phrynichus.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ascidiae: Sea squirts presented as solitary forms, in section, and as colonial aggregates. Boltenia echinata is shown bristling with spicules above a longitudinal cut that reveals the U-shaped gut, while compound ascidians cluster like ripening fruit on the lower register.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-aspidonia: Horseshoe crabs and their extinct sea-scorpion kin, including Tachypleus gigas in adult and larval form alongside the Silurian eurypterid Eurypterus tetragonophthalmus. The plate stages a deep-time comparison between living and fossil chelicerates.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-asteridea: Developmental sequence of the common starfish Asterias rubens, from bipinnaria and brachiolaria larvae to a juvenile adult. Haeckel charts the metamorphosis from bilateral plankton to pentaradial echinoderm in a single ascending series.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-basimycetes: Stinkhorn fungi arranged like floral specimens: the latticed Clathrus ruber, the veiled Phallus indusiatus, and the starfish-armed Aseroe rubra. Cross-sectioned volvas at the base reveal the gelatinous egg stage from which each fruiting body emerges.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-batrachia: Tropical tree frogs and marsupial frogs grouped for skin-pattern variety, including Hyla meridionalis, Rhacophorus pardalis, and the egg-brooding Gastrotheca. Splayed toe pads and warted backs are emphasized to register adhesive and camouflage adaptations.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-blastoidea: Extinct Paleozoic blastoids with budlike calyces drawn from above and the side, including Pentremites pyriformis. The five-petaled ambulacral fields are featured prominently, and one specimen appears with its delicate brachioles extended, folded, and removed.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-bryozoa: Freshwater and marine moss animals centered on Cristatella mucedo, traced through statoblast, juvenile, and mature colony stages. Haeckel draws the gelatinous mat with its lophophore crowns extended in a feathered ring around each zooid.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-bryozoa-33: Calcified bryozoan skeletons enlarged so that individual zooecia read as tiled honeycomb. Phaeostachys, Cribrilina, and Umbonula appear as porous lacework, with detail shifting between whole colonies and single chambered cells.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-calcispongiae: Calcareous sponges of the Leucosolenia and Guancha groups, shown as branching tubular colonies anchored by spiculate roots. Haeckel sets habitus drawings beside isolated triradiate spicules to expose the skeletal architecture beneath the soft tissue.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-campanariae: Hydroid colonies of the family Campanulariidae, slender stems bearing bell-shaped hydranths and reproductive gonophores. The plate threads the colonies like vines across the page, foregrounding the hydrothecae as cup-on-stalk ornaments.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-chaetopoda: Marine bristle worms with their feeding crowns expanded: feather duster Sabellastarte indica, the calcareous-tubed Hydroides, and the spiraled fan of Sabella spallanzanii. Tube architecture and feathered radioles are treated as the principal motif.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-chelonia: Comparative tableau of turtles and tortoises, ranging from the leatherback Dermochelys coriacea and hawksbill to the matamata-like Hydromedusa, geometric tortoise, and Aldabra giant. Carapace pattern is the organizing logic, set against contrasting head and limb forms.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-chiroptera: Bat heads in frontal view, from the long-eared Plecotus auritus to the leaf-nosed Megaderma and Chrotopterus. The plate lines up specialized noseleaves and ear funnels to show the morphological diversity behind echolocating sensory equipment.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ciliata: Tintinnid ciliates magnified so that their vase-shaped loricae read as miniature urns and bells. Codonella, Dictyocysta, and Tintinnopsis shells are arranged with their cellular occupants extending crowns of cilia, microscopic protozoa rendered as architecture.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-cirripedia: Goose barnacles in habit and dissection: Lepas anatifera intact, then with one valve cut away to reveal the feathered cirri and curled body. Conchoderma colonies are shown encrusting the whale-barnacle Coronula, a study in commensal stacking.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-coniferae: Conifer cones and foliage from monkey puzzle, Norway spruce, Santa Lucia fir, hinoki cypress, and Japanese thujopsis. Haeckel arranges scale-by-scale dissections beside intact cones to compare the geometry of seed-bearing structures across the group.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-copepoda: Planktonic copepods drawn with antennae and setae fanned out to fill the page, including the iridescent Calocalanus pavo with its peacock-tail furca. The plate celebrates ornamental setal arrays that drag through seawater for propulsion and sensing.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-crinoidea: Stalked sea lilies of the genera Endoxocrinus and Saracrinus, drawn full-length with feathery arms unfurled above jointed columns. Detail studies of stem ossicles emphasize the segmented, almost machined construction of these living fossil echinoderms.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ctenophorae: Comb jellies suspended in implied current, their eight ciliated bands shown as iridescent ribbons running down translucent bodies. Haeckelia rubra appears from above and side, joined by Hormiphora and the winged Callianira bialata.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-cubomedusae: Box jellyfish in lateral view, the cuboidal bell of Chirodropus and Carybdea hung over trailing tentacle bundles. The plate isolates the rigid cubic geometry that distinguishes cubozoans from softer bell-shaped medusae.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-cyrtoidea: Radiolarian shells of the Cyrtoidea group, eleven mineralized lattices arranged in three rows. Helmet, bell, and tower forms display the silica skeletons that fascinated Haeckel from his Challenger expedition reports onward.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-cystoidea: Paleozoic cystoid echinoderms shown from above to display the polygonal calyx plating and pore-rhomb respiratory structures. Staurocystis, Glyptosphaerites, and Cystoblastus are arranged as a comparative key to early stalked echinoderm armor.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-decapoda: Decapod crustaceans in dorsal view, from the spiky Daldorfia horrida and long-eyed Podophthalmus vigil to the banded coral shrimp Stenopus hispidus. The plate revels in carapace ornament, spines, and elongated chelae across crab and shrimp morphologies.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-desmidiea: A plate of desmid green algae, single-celled freshwater organisms whose cell walls split into mirror-image semicells. Haeckel arranges Staurastrum and related genera to highlight their geometric symmetry, including views of mating cells in which two halves conjugate to form a zygospore.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-diatomea: An assembly of marine and freshwater diatoms, microscopic algae enclosed in silica frustules. Top and side views display the intricate punctate ornament of genera such as Auliscus, Cymbella and Campyloneis, drawn at a scale that turns optical microscopy into ornamental design.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-diatomea-4: Triangular and lyre-shaped diatom valves arranged in radial symmetry, including Triceratium and Lyrella species seen in valve view. The plate emphasises the lattice of areolae perforating the silica shell, a feature only resolvable under high magnification.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-discoidea: Radiolarians of the order Discoidea, single-celled marine plankton with star- and disc-shaped silica skeletons. Most figures derive from Haeckel's own 1887 Challenger Expedition monograph, where he described thousands of species from deep-sea sediment samples.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-discomedusae-: Studies of small disc jellyfish, with Linantha lunulata shown from above and in side profile alongside developmental stages of Palephyra. The plate documents both the radial gastrovascular canals visible from above and the bell musculature seen during swimming.

Multiple views of rhizostome jellyfish in the Toreuma group, dissecting a single animal into side, oral and aboral perspectives. The arrangement shows mouth-arms, gonad ribbons and the gelatinous bell as a coordinated anatomical study.

Desmonema annasethe, a jellyfish Haeckel named after his late wife Anna Sethe, who died the year before he encountered the species. The trailing oral arms and tentacles, drawn in cascading filaments, are often read as a personal memorial within the scientific atlas.

Large rhizostome medusae including Pilema and Rhopilema shown from side, aboral and oral aspects. The plate captures the eight branched mouth-arms that replace a single central mouth in this group, filtering plankton through countless secondary openings.

Aurelia and related semaeostome jellyfish presented alongside the polyp stage budding off juvenile medusae by strobilation. Pairing adult and larval forms on one sheet illustrates the alternation of generations central to scyphozoan life cycles.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-echinidea: Sand dollars and sea biscuits including Clypeaster rosaceus and Encope emarginata, drawn with and without spines. Aboral views expose the five-petalled ambulacral pattern of the test, a hallmark of the irregular echinoids adapted to burrowing in sediment.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-echinidea-60: Cidaroid sea urchins shown stripped of their long primary spines so the tubercles and plate sutures of the test are legible. Mouth and periproct regions are isolated to compare the apical and oral systems across Cidaris and Prionocidaris species.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-filicinae: Staghorn ferns of the genus Platycerium, traced through successive frond stages from young plant to mature epiphyte. The shield-like sterile basal fronds and forked fertile blades are drawn to emphasise the dimorphism characteristic of the genus.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-filicinae-92: A composition of tropical ferns including tree ferns of Cyatheaceae, the bird's-nest fern Asplenium nidus and the eusporangiate Angiopteris. Habit drawings juxtapose growth forms ranging from arborescent trunks to ribbon-leaved Monogramma.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-flagellata: Choanoflagellates and related flagellate protists arranged as colonial branched assemblages, including Anthophysa, Cephalothamnium and Codonocladium. The collared cells on stalked rosettes anticipate the body plan of sponge choanocytes.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-florideae: Red algae of the Florideophyceae, with Chondrus crispus, the Irish moss of north Atlantic shores, given prominence. The plate displays the cartilaginous, repeatedly dichotomous thalli that produce carrageenan and were already commercially harvested in the nineteenth century.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-fucoideae: Brown seaweeds drawn at habit scale, including the giant Pacific kelp Nereocystis luetkeana, Cutleria and Cystoseira. The selection traces the morphological range from pneumatocyst-bearing kelps to bushy littoral species of the order Fucales.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-gamochonia: Coleoid cephalopods including Chiroteuthis, Histioteuthis and the common octopus, drawn from below and above. The plate sets pelagic deep-sea squids with hooked arms beside benthic octopuses to compare arm crowns, fins and chromatophore patterning.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-gorgonida: Gorgonian octocorals with skeletal spicules detailed beside whole polyps and branch fragments. Eunicella and Primnoella illustrate the eight-tentacled polyps and calcareous sclerites that stiffen the proteinaceous gorgonin axis of the colony.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-hexacoralla: Solitary and colonial stony corals of the Hexacorallia, including the cold-water reef-builder Lophelia prolifera. Skeletal views from above and the side reveal the six-fold symmetry of septa within each calcified corallite.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-hexacoralla-6: Reef corals from the genera Turbinaria, Pavona and Hydnophora arranged as a compositional bouquet. Lobate and frond-like growth forms are contrasted with branching morphologies to display the architectural variety produced by colonial scleractinian polyps.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-hexactinellae: Glass sponges of the class Hexactinellida, with the Venus's flower basket Euplectella aspergillum at the centre. Whole-body drawings are paired with magnified detail of the six-rayed siliceous spicules that fuse into a rigid lattice.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-lacertilia: A gathering of lizards including the flying dragon Draco volans, the Texas horned lizard Phrynosoma cornutum and a Trioceros chameleon. The plate compares cranial casques, dermal spines and gliding membranes as variations on a single squamate body plan.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-leptomedusae: Hydrozoan medusae of the order Leptothecata, including Aequorea and Zygocanna, drawn from above and the side. The radial canals and gonadal ribbons of the bell are emphasised, structures that bioluminesce in living Aequorea through the green fluorescent protein.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-lichenes: Cup and reindeer lichens of the genus Cladonia, with branched podetia and squamulose primary thalli. The plate documents the symbiotic body in growth habit rather than dissection, recording species that dominate boreal heaths and Antarctic ground cover.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-melethallia: Coenobial green algae of the genus Pediastrum, whose flat plate-shaped colonies of polygonal cells float in fresh water. Arranged in concentric rings, the species illustrate how a fixed cell number per colony produces star-, wheel- and disc-shaped geometries.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-muscinae: A garden of mosses including Polytrichum commune, Sphagnum palustre and the dung moss Splachnum luteum with its inflated apophysis. Capsules, setae and leafy gametophytes are drawn at habit scale to compare bryophyte sporophyte architecture.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-mycetozoa: Slime moulds shown across their full life cycle, from amoeboid swarm cells emerging from spores to mature plasmodia of Badhamia and Trichia. The plate treats Mycetozoa as protists rather than fungi, reflecting Haeckel's broader reorganisation of the kingdoms of life.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-narcomedusae: Narcomedusan hydrozoans, deep-water jellyfish whose bell margin is scalloped into discrete lobes. Pegantha, Aeginura and Solmaris are shown from below to display the peripheral tentacles arising directly from the exumbrella, a defining feature of the order.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-nepenthaceae: The tropical pitcher plant Nepenthes gymnamphora, drawn with its modified leaves forming fluid-filled traps. Carnivorous insectivory had been confirmed only decades earlier, and the plate emphasises the lid, peristome and tendril that suspend each pitcher.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-nudibranchia: Sea slugs of multiple aeolid and dorid lineages, including the orange-tipped Facelina auriculata and the spotted Tritonia hombergii. Cerata, rhinophores and branchial plumes are rendered in colour to record pigmentation that fades from preserved specimens.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ophiodea: Brittle stars arranged in radial symmetry across the plate, their slender, articulated arms fanning outward from small central discs. Haeckel emphasized the spiny ornamentation and pentaradial geometry of these echinoderms, drawing on specimens collected by the Challenger expedition.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ophiodea-70: Basket stars and brittle stars displayed from multiple angles, including the branching, fractal-like arms of Astrophyton at center. The composition contrasts dense knotted limbs with the more sparse, whip-like rays of smaller ophiuroids to convey echinoderm diversity.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-orchidae: Tropical orchids rendered as a dense bouquet, including Odontoglossum and Oncidium species with their lipped flowers and trailing column structures. The plate stresses the asymmetric, almost zoomorphic petals that fascinated nineteenth-century naturalists studying floral mimicry.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-ostraciontes: Boxfish and cowfish drawn from multiple angles to highlight their rigid carapace of fused scale plates. Haeckel sets the angular, polyhedral bodies against one another to underscore how these tropical reef fish trade flexibility for armored defense.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-pennatulida: Sea pens and sea pansies displayed as feather-like and disc-shaped colonies, with enlargements of individual polyps along the rachis. The plate documents the colonial architecture of these soft corals, showing how a single founding polyp gives rise to a polymorphic colony.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-peridinea: Marine dinoflagellates magnified to reveal their armored cellulose plates, flagellar grooves, and projecting horns. Species like Ceratium tripos and Ornithocercus magnificus dominate the composition, their delicate sail-like extensions translated into precise lithographic line.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-peromedusae: Crown jellies of the genus Periphylla shown from side and underside, the conical bell ringed by deep grooves and trailing tentacles. The plate captures the bioluminescent deep-sea medusae described from Challenger expedition hauls.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-phaeodaria-1: Microscopic radiolarians with siliceous skeletons constructed in geometric forms, including the icosahedral lattice of Circogonia and the radial spines of Haeckeliana. Haeckel renders these planktonic single-celled organisms as if they were architectural blueprints.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-phaeodaria-61: Detailed terminations of radiolarian spicules from the Aulographis group, each tip elaborated into candelabras, asterisks, or forks. The plate isolates these microscopic ornament details to show the structural inventiveness of phaeodarian skeletons.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-platodes: Cercaria and trematode larvae presented with their characteristic forked tails and suckers visible. The plate documents parasitic flatworm life stages, drawing on Johannes Müller's and others' microscopic studies of digenean development.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-polycyttaria: Colonial radiolarians shown as gelatinous spheres studded with individual cells and symbiotic zooxanthellae. The plate documents social plankton, where dozens of single-celled organisms cohabit in a shared transparent matrix.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-prosobranchia: Marine snails arranged as a still-life of shells, including the long-spired Murex pecten, the cone of Conus imperialis, and the harp-ribbed Harpa ventricosa. The plate functions as a survey of gastropod ornament, from spines to glossy enamel.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-rotatoria: Microscopic rotifers shown with their ciliated coronas and transparent bodies, including the trumpet-shaped Stephanoceros and the colonial Lacinularia. The plate freezes the spinning corona that gives these freshwater animals their name.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-sertulariae: Hydroid colonies branching like miniature ferns, with magnified hydrothecae and reproductive gonangia along each stem. The plate emphasizes how these cnidarians blur the line between plant-like growth form and animal anatomy.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-siphoneae: Caulerpa green algae rendered as climbing tendrils, with grape-cluster, peltate, and feathered varieties of a single giant unicell. The composition emphasizes how one continuous cytoplasm can mimic the form of an entire higher plant.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-siphonophorae: Porpema colonies shown with disc-like floats and trailing sexual polyps, set against magnified gas bladders. The plate documents how a siphonophore is not a single jelly but a coordinated colony of specialized zooids.

Physophora hydrostatica colonies arranged with central swimming bells and a skirt of stinging tentacles. Haeckel pairs an overall view with anatomical details of the gas float and individual nectophore to dissect the colonial body plan.

A Forskalia-like siphonophore dissected layer by layer, from intact colony to stripped polyp clusters. The sequence reveals the modular construction of a pelagic cnidarian, with each zooid specialized for swimming, feeding, or reproduction.

Mature and immature Epibulia and Rhizophysa colonies shown alongside cross-sections of their gas bladders. The plate emphasizes the apical pneumatophore that allows these chains of polyps to drift just below the sea surface.

Praya and Bassia colonies displayed as chains of paired swimming bells with trailing reproductive medusae. Haeckel uses the bilateral nectophores to convey the streamlined hydrodynamics of calycophoran siphonophores.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-spirobranchia: Brachiopod shells from the inside, with internal spiral lophophore supports visible in fossils such as Spirifer and Cyrtina. The plate doubles as a paleontological reference, juxtaposing Paleozoic forms to show the calcified scaffolding of the feeding organ.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-spumellaria: Spherical and spindle-shaped radiolarian skeletons with concentric latticework and radiating spines. The plate isolates the polycystine forms whose silica architecture inspired Haeckel's broader argument that nature is intrinsically geometric.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-spyroidea: Two-chambered radiolarian skeletons rendered as miniature cages, each with a sagittal constriction and ornamental feet. The composition catalogs the helmet-like Nassellaria forms drawn from Challenger expedition microfossil samples.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-stauromedusae: Stalked jellyfish rendered as goblet-shaped medusae anchored by adhesive feet, including Haliclystus auricula clinging to algae. The plate captures these unusual sessile cnidarians from side and base views.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-stephoidea: Ring-shaped radiolarian skeletons with single, double, or triple silica hoops, presented as crowns and lattices. The plate centers on simple-ringed Nassellaria, showing how minimal skeletal motifs can scaffold a living single cell.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-teleostei: Bizarrely formed bony fish, including seahorses, the leafy sea dragon Phycodurus eques, and the antennated frogfish Antennarius striatus. The plate emphasizes camouflage and modified fins that disguise these teleosts as drifting weed.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-tetracoralla: Solitary and colonial rugose corals shown as polished cross-sections and side views, including the horn-shaped Omphyma turbinata. The plate is essentially paleontological, displaying the four-fold septal symmetry of an extinct Paleozoic order.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-thalamophora-: Miliolid foraminifera arranged as porcelain-like shells with looping chambers, including Quinqueloculina and Cornuspira. The plate magnifies these microscopic shelled protists to reveal the sequential coiling that defines the order.

Lagenid foraminifera shown as flask- and bottle-shaped tests with single apertures, ornamented by ribs, spines, and squamose plates. Haeckel groups the variety to highlight how a basic vase form can be elaborated into dozens of species.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-thalamphora: Elongate foraminifera with stacked or alternating chambers, including Nodosaria, Uvigerina, and Bolivina. The plate concentrates on multilocular benthic forms whose calcareous tests accumulate to build deep-sea sediments.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-thoracostraca: A symmetrical plate of shrimp-like crustaceans, juxtaposing transparent adult Lucifer and Penaeus species with their nauplius and mastigopus larval stages. Haeckel uses the layout to argue visually for embryological recapitulation, treating the developmental sequence as ornamental composition.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-thuroidea: Sea cucumbers and their larvae arranged on a dark ground, showing Phyllophorus and Synapta alongside successive auricularia stages. The plate pairs adult body plans with their drifting planktonic young to emphasize life-cycle continuity within Holothuroidea.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-tineida: A study of plume and leaf-mining moths in which Alucita and Pterophorus species spread their feathered wings into near-radial patterns. Magnified wing-scale details at the margins underscore the geometric microstructure beneath the apparently dusty surfaces.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-trachomedusae: Bell-shaped trachymedusae are drawn from above, beside, and beneath, with the long manubrium of Geryonia trailing like a clapper. The transparent tissues let internal gonads and radial canals show through, turning anatomy into a chandelier-like ornament.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-trochilidae: Twelve hummingbird species, including the Sword-billed and Booted Racket-tail, are arrayed against black to display iridescent gorgets, crests, and tail streamers. Drawn from millinery skins, the postures are decorative rather than observational, foregrounding plumage geometry over flight behavior.
kunstformen-images-haeckel-tubulariae: Solitary and budding hydromedusae such as Sarsia and Coryne float across the page, several shown in the act of releasing free-swimming medusae from polyp colonies. The plate compresses an entire alternating life cycle into a single ornamental field.
